topics.title: Earth Pressure - At Rest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Earth Pressure - At Rest # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Earth Pressure at Rest** is the lateral (horizontal) pressure exerted by a mass of soil on a retaining structure that is **absolutely rigid** and **does not undergo any lateral displacement or rotation** relative to the soil mass. The state of stress developed in the soil under this zero-deformation condition is termed the **"At-Rest State"** (denoted by the subscript **0**, as in $K_0$). Mathematically, the coefficient of earth pressure at rest is defined as the ratio of the **effective horizontal (lateral) stress** to the **effective vertical (overburden) stress**: $$K_0 = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v} = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\gamma' \cdot z}$$ where: - $K_0$ = Coefficient of earth pressure at rest (dimensionless) - $\sigma'_h$ = Effective horizontal stress - $\sigma'_v$ = Effective vertical stress - $\gamma'$ = Effective unit weight of soil - $z$ = Depth below ground surface > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "At-Rest" condition is a **fundamental boundary state** between the Active state ($K_a$) and Passive state ($K_p$). It represents the **in-situ lateral stress condition** before any excavation or construction activity disturbs the soil. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Other States:** > - **At-Rest State ($K_0$):** No lateral strain permitted ($\varepsilon_h = 0$) > - **Active State ($K_a$):** Wall moves **away** from soil (lateral extension) > - **Passive State ($K_p$):** Wall moves **towards** soil (lateral compression) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you are standing in a crowded railway compartment during peak hours. People (representing **soil particles**) are packed tightly against you from all sides. The vertical pressure you feel from the weight of people standing above is analogous to the **vertical overburden pressure** ($\sigma_v$). The sideways push you feel from neighboring passengers who have nowhere to go is analogous to the **lateral earth pressure at rest** ($\sigma_h$). Now, consider what happens if: - The compartment walls suddenly **move outward** (wall moves away from soil) → people "relax" and lean forward → **Active State** - The compartment walls suddenly **push inward** (wall pushes into soil) → people are crushed against you → **Passive State** - The compartment walls **remain perfectly still** (rigid wall) → equilibrium is maintained as it is → **At-Rest State** This perfectly captures the essence of $K_0$: the soil is in its **natural, undisturbed state** where no lateral yielding has occurred. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Examples of At-Rest Conditions:** > 1. Basement walls of rigid buildings founded on stiff soils > 2. Earth pressure on bridge abutments with restrained movement > 3. Long, rigid retaining walls with adequate bracing > 4. Soil pressure on anchored sheet pile walls (near anchor level) > 5. Pressure on tunnel linings in competent rock/soil ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Range of $K_0$ for cohesionless soils:** **0.4 to 0.6** (typically ~0.5 for loose sands) - **Range of $K_0$ for overconsolidated clays:** **1.0 to 3.0+** (can exceed 1.0) - **Range of $K_0$ for normally consolidated clays:** **0.5 to 0.7** - **Standard unit weight of water ($\gamma_w$):** **9.81 kN/m³** (or **10 kN/m³** for simplification) - **Poisson's ratio ($\mu$) range for soils:** **0.2 to 0.5** > [!CAUTION] > **Common Misconception:** $K_0$ is **NOT** equal to 1.0 (which would imply hydrostatic conditions). For most soils, $K_0 < 1.0$ because soil grains cannot sustain unlimited horizontal stress without lateral expansion. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind $K_0$ The at-rest condition arises from the **geological deposition process** of soils. When soil particles are deposited naturally over geological time, each new layer exerts a vertical stress on the layers below, but the soil mass responds by developing an internal structure that resists lateral deformation. This natural self-equilibrium produces a horizontal stress component that is typically **less than** the vertical stress (for normally consolidated soils). ### 2.1.1 Physical Interpretation When a soil element at depth $z$ is isolated: - The **vertical plane** is acted upon by the overburden pressure $\sigma_v = \gamma z$ - The **horizontal plane** develops a reaction $\sigma_h$ to maintain zero lateral strain - The relationship between these two stresses is governed by the soil's **elastic properties** (for theoretical derivation) or by **empirical correlations** (for practical applications) ### 2.1.2 Stress Distribution in At-Rest Condition The horizontal stress increases **linearly with depth**, similar to the vertical stress: $$\sigma'_h(z) = K_0 \cdot \sigma'_v(z) = K_0 \cdot \gamma' \cdot z$$ The **lateral earth pressure at rest** on a vertical wall of height $H$ is: $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2} K_0 \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2$$ This is the **total thrust per unit length** of the wall (kN/m). ## 2.2 Theoretical Methods to Determine $K_0$ ### Method 1: Elastic Theory (Poisson's Ratio Approach) For a linearly elastic, isotropic soil mass, the relationship between horizontal and vertical stress under zero lateral strain is: $$K_0 = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}$$ where $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio of the soil. **Derivation Logic:** Under the condition of zero lateral strain, the application of Hooke's law in 3D yields the above relationship. This is **theoretically valid** but **practically limited** because real soils are not perfectly elastic. ### Method 2: Jaky's Empirical Formula (1894) — Most Widely Used For **normally consolidated soils**, Jaky proposed: $$K_0(NC) = 1 - \sin(\phi)$$ where $\phi$ = effective angle of internal friction of the soil. **Why this works:** Extensive experimental data showed that for freshly deposited (normally consolidated) soils, the lateral stress coefficient correlates remarkably well with the soil's frictional properties. ### Method 3: Overconsolidated Soils (Mayne & Kulhawy, 1982) For overconsolidated clays, the $K_0$ value depends on the **Overconsolidation Ratio (OCR)**: $$K_0(OC) = (1 - \sin\phi) \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi}$$ This accounts for the "memory" of past stress history in overconsolidated deposits. ### Method 4: Schmidt's Simplified Formula for OC Soils $$K_0(OC) = K_0(NC) \cdot OCR$$ This is a **conservative** approximation often used in preliminary design. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | Variables | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-----------|-------| | 1 | $K_0 = \sigma'_h / \sigma'_v$ | Definition of at-rest coefficient | $\sigma'_h, \sigma'_v$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | $K_0 = \mu / (1 - \mu)$ | Elastic theory (ideal soils) | $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio | Dimensionless | | 3 | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | Jaky's formula (NC soils) | $\phi$ = friction angle | Dimensionless | | 4 | $K_0(OC) = (1 - \sin\phi) \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi}$ | Overconsolidated soils | OCR = overconsolidation ratio | Dimensionless | | 5 | $K_0(OC) \approx K_0(NC) \cdot OCR$ | Simplified OC estimate | OCR | Dimensionless | | 6 | $\sigma'_h = K_0 \cdot \gamma' \cdot z$ | Horizontal stress at depth z | $z$ = depth | kN/m² | | 7 | $P_0 = \frac{1}{2} K_0 \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2$ | Total thrust per unit length | $H$ = wall height | kN/m | | 8 | $\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z$ | Vertical overburden stress | $z$ = depth | kN/m² | | 9 | $\sigma'_v = \sigma_v - u$ | Effective vertical stress | $u$ = pore water pressure | kN/m² | | 10 | $OCR = \sigma'_p / \sigma'_v$ | Overconsolidation ratio | $\sigma'_p$ = preconsolidation pressure | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder for KTU Exams:** Always use **effective stresses** (denoted by prime ') when computing $K_0$ in saturated soils. Pore water pressure acts equally in all directions and must be subtracted first. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice The at-rest earth pressure concept is **critically important** in the following applications: 1. **Design of Rigid Retaining Walls:** When walls are stiff enough (e.g., massive gravity walls, well-braced basement walls) to prevent lateral movement, $K_0$ conditions prevail. 2. **Tunnel & Shaft Design:** The initial stress state around underground openings is governed by $K_0$. 3. **Pile Foundation Analysis:** The lateral capacity of piles is influenced by the initial $K_0$ of the surrounding soil. 4. **Braced Excavations:** Strut loads in deep excavations often correlate with $K_0$ pressures. 5. **Slope Stability Analysis:** Initial stress conditions affect the factor of safety calculations. 6. **Finite Element Modeling:** The initial stress state in numerical simulations of geotechnical problems is set using $K_0$ values. ## 2.5 Factors Affecting $K_0$ | Factor | Effect on $K_0$ | |--------|-----------------| | **Soil Density** | Denser packing → higher $K_0$ | | **Stress History (OCR)** | Higher OCR → significantly higher $K_0$ | | **Friction Angle ($\phi$)** | Higher $\phi$ → lower $K_0$ (NC case) | | **Poisson's Ratio ($\mu$)** | Higher $\mu$ → higher $K_0$ (elastic case) | | **Soil Type** | Clays > Sands (typical $K_0$ values) | | **Cementation/Bonding** | Increases $K_0$ due to inter-particle bonds | | **Geological Age** | Older deposits → higher $K_0$ (secondary compression) | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: $K_0$ from Elastic Theory ### Starting Point: Generalized Hooke's Law in 3D For a linearly elastic, isotropic, homogeneous material, the strains in the three principal directions are related to the stresses through: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$ $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \mu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$ $$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \mu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$ where $E$ = Young's Modulus, $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio. ### Step 1: Apply the At-Rest Condition For a soil element under the at-rest condition, the lateral strains are constrained to zero (i.e., $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = 0$). Setting $\varepsilon_x = 0$: $$0 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$ Multiplying both sides by $E$: $$0 = \sigma_x - \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$ Rearranging: $$\sigma_x = \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$ ### Step 2: Introduce the Convention for Soil Mechanics In soil mechanics, we adopt the following convention for a soil element at depth $z$: - Vertical direction (z-axis): $\sigma_z = \sigma_v$ (vertical stress) - Horizontal directions (x and y axes): $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_h$ (horizontal stress, equal due to symmetry) Substituting into the strain equation: $$\sigma_h = \mu(\sigma_h + \sigma_v)$$ ### Step 3: Expand and Isolate $\sigma_h$ Expanding the right-hand side: $$\sigma_h = \mu \cdot \sigma_h + \mu \cdot \sigma_v$$ Moving the term $\mu \cdot \sigma_h$ to the left side: $$\sigma_h - \mu \cdot \sigma_h = \mu \cdot \sigma_v$$ Factoring $\sigma_h$ on the left: $$\sigma_h(1 - \mu) = \mu \cdot \sigma_v$$ ### Step 4: Solve for the Ratio $\sigma_h / \sigma_v$ Dividing both sides by $\sigma_v \cdot (1 - \mu)$: $$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}$$ ### Step 5: Define the Coefficient $K_0$ By definition, $K_0 = \sigma_h / \sigma_v$, therefore: $$\boxed{K_0 = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}}$$ This is the **theoretical relationship** between the at-rest pressure coefficient and Poisson's ratio, valid for idealized elastic soil behavior. ### Verification with Typical Values For $\mu = 0.3$ (typical for sands): $$K_0 = \frac{0.3}{1 - 0.3} = \frac{0.3}{0.7} = 0.429$$ For $\mu = 0.4$ (typical for clays): $$K_0 = \frac{0.4}{1 - 0.4} = \frac{0.4}{0.6} = 0.667$$ For $\mu = 0.5$ (theoretical maximum, undrained conditions): $$K_0 = \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.5} = 1.0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** As Poisson's ratio approaches 0.5 (incompressible material), $K_0$ approaches 1.0, meaning the horizontal and vertical stresses become equal — analogous to hydrostatic conditions in a fluid. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Jaky's Empirical Formula ### Background and Experimental Basis Jaky (1894, published in Hungarian; re-evaluated by Széchy, 1957) analyzed experimental data from oedometer tests on granular soils and found a consistent relationship between $K_0$ and the friction angle for normally consolidated sands. ### Logical Derivation Path While Jaky's formula is empirical, it can be justified through the following conceptual reasoning: **Step 1:** Consider a cohesionless soil element at the verge of failure along a plane. The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion gives: $$\sin\phi = \frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)}{(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)}$$ **Step 2:** For a soil element in the at-rest state, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$ (vertical, major) and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h$ (horizontal, minor). $$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{\sigma_v + \sigma_h}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting $K_0 = \sigma_h / \sigma_v$: $$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_v - K_0 \cdot \sigma_v}{\sigma_v + K_0 \cdot \sigma_v} = \frac{1 - K_0}{1 + K_0}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $K_0$: $$\sin\phi \cdot (1 + K_0) = 1 - K_0$$ $$\sin\phi + K_0 \cdot \sin\phi = 1 - K_0$$ $$K_0 \cdot \sin\phi + K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$$ $$K_0(1 + \sin\phi) = 1 - \sin\phi$$ $$K_0 = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$ **Step 5:** Jaky's observation that the actual $K_0$ is approximately **double** the failure-state value led to the simplified empirical formula: $$K_0 \approx 1 - \sin\phi$$ This can be interpreted as a **conservative average** between the theoretical elastic and limit equilibrium values. ### Verification with Typical Values For loose sand: $\phi = 30°$, so $K_0 = 1 - \sin(30°) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$ For dense sand: $\phi = 40°$, so $K_0 = 1 - \sin(40°) = 1 - 0.643 = 0.357$ For soft clay: $\phi = 20°$, so $K_0 = 1 - \sin(20°) = 1 - 0.342 = 0.658$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: At-Rest Pressure Calculation ### Problem Statement A 6 m high rigid retaining wall supports a cohesionless soil backfill with the following properties: - Unit weight: $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$ - Friction angle: $\phi = 32°$ - Groundwater table is at the base of the wall **Find:** (a) The coefficient of earth pressure at rest using Jaky's formula (b) The lateral pressure distribution behind the wall (c) The total thrust on the wall per unit length ### Solution #### Part (a): Coefficient of Earth Pressure at Rest Applying Jaky's empirical formula: $$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi = 1 - \sin(32°) = 1 - 0.5299 = 0.470$$ Therefore, $K_0 \approx 0.47$ #### Part (b): Lateral Pressure Distribution Since the groundwater table is at the base, the soil is dry (or moist) throughout. The total unit weight can be used directly. The vertical stress at depth $z$: $$\sigma_v(z) = \gamma \cdot z = 18 \cdot z \text{ kN/m}^2$$ The horizontal (lateral) stress at depth $z$: $$\sigma_h(z) = K_0 \cdot \sigma_v(z) = 0.47 \cdot 18 \cdot z = 8.46 \cdot z \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **At the top (z = 0):** $\sigma_h = 0 \text{ kN/m}^2$ **At the base (z = 6 m):** $\sigma_h = 8.46 \cdot 6 = 50.76 \text{ kN/m}^2$ #### Part (c): Total Thrust on the Wall The total thrust per unit length of the wall is the area of the triangular pressure distribution: $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2} K_0 \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.47 \cdot 18 \cdot 6^2$$ $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.47 \cdot 18 \cdot 36 = 152.28 \text{ kN/m}$$ The resultant acts at $H/3$ from the base (centroid of the triangle). **Point of application:** $z = H/3 = 6/3 = 2 \text{ m}$ from the base ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of $K_0$ Determination Methods | Method | Applicable Soil Type | Required Parameters | Accuracy | KTU Exam Preference | |--------|---------------------|---------------------|----------|---------------------| | $K_0 = \mu / (1 - \mu)$ | Idealized elastic soils | Poisson's ratio | Low (theoretical) | Moderate (conceptual) | | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | Normally consolidated cohesionless & cohesive soils | Friction angle | High (empirical) | **Highest (most used)** | | $K_0(OC) = (1 - \sin\phi) \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi}$ | Overconsolidated clays | $\phi$, OCR | Very High | High (advanced problems) | | $K_0(OC) = K_0(NC) \cdot OCR$ | Overconsolidated clays (preliminary) | $\phi$, OCR | Moderate | Moderate | | Laboratory Oedometer Test | All soil types | Measured lateral stress | Highest (direct) | Low (rare in exams) | | In-situ Tests (SPT, CPT, DMT) | All soil types | Correlated parameters | Variable | Low (advanced) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Strategy:** For numerical problems, **default to Jaky's formula** ($K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$) unless the problem explicitly specifies Poisson's ratio or an overconsolidated condition. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Flow Diagram: States of Earth Pressure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Soil Mass at Depth z] --> B{Wall Displacement Condition} B -->|No Movement| C[At-Rest State] B -->|Wall Moves Away| D[Active State] B -->|Wall Moves Toward| E[Passive State] C --> F["K0 Condition
sigma_h equals K0 times sigma_v
Lateral strain equals 0"] D --> G["Ka Condition
sigma_h equals Ka times sigma_v
Lateral extension occurs"] E --> H["Kp Condition
sigma_h equals Kp times sigma_v
Lateral compression occurs"] F --> I["Typical K0 equals 0.4 to 0.6
Jaky K0 equals 1 minus sin phi"] G --> J["Ka less than K0
Ka equals tan squared 45 minus phi over 2"] H --> K["Kp greater than K0
Kp equals tan squared 45 plus phi over 2"] I --> L[End State Analysis] J --> L K --> L ``` ## 4.2 Stress Distribution Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SurfaceLayer["Ground Surface"] GS1[Vertical Stress equals 0 at z equals 0] end subgraph MidDepth["At Depth z equal to 3 m"] MD1[Vertical Stress sigma_v equals gamma z] MD2[Horizontal Stress sigma_h equals K0 sigma_v] MD3[Effective Stress Relation sigma prime equals sigma minus u] end subgraph BaseLayer["At Base z equals 6 m"] BL1[Maximum Vertical Stress sigma_v max] BL2[Maximum Horizontal Stress sigma_h max] BL3[Resultant Thrust P0 acts at H over 3] end GS1 --> MD1 MD1 --> MD2 MD2 --> MD3 MD3 --> BL1 BL1 --> BL2 BL2 --> BL3 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: $K_0$ Determination Methodology ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["Input Parameters"] I1[Friction Angle phi] I2[Poisson Ratio mu] I3[OCR Value] I4[Soil Type] end subgraph Methods["K0 Calculation Methods"] M1[Method 1: Elastic Theory] M2[Method 2: Jaky Formula] M3[Method 3: Mayne Kulhawy] M4[Method 4: Laboratory Test] end subgraph Output["Output Result"] O1[K0 Coefficient Value] O2[Lateral Stress sigma_h] O3[Total Thrust P0] end I1 --> M2 I2 --> M1 I1 --> M3 I3 --> M3 I4 --> M4 M1 --> O1 M2 --> O1 M3 --> O1 M4 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Analysis Diagram: $K_0$ vs $K_a$ vs $K_p$ ```mermaid graph LR subgraph StateComparison["Earth Pressure States Comparison"] A1[Active State Ka] -->|Smallest| A2[sigma_h smallest value] B1[At-Rest State K0] -->|Intermediate| B2[sigma_h intermediate value] C1[Passive State Kp] -->|Largest| C2[sigma_h largest value] end A2 --> D1[Wall moves away from soil] B2 --> D2[No wall movement] C2 --> D3[Wall moves into soil] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams use the Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow approach to represent the conceptual relationships between the different earth pressure states and calculation methods, as required for topics involving physical soil-structure interactions that are best conveyed through process flows. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual Question **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]** **Q1.** Define the term "Coefficient of Earth Pressure at Rest" ($K_0$). State Jaky's empirical formula for $K_0$ for normally consolidated soils and explain its physical significance. #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The coefficient of earth pressure at rest ($K_0$) is defined as the ratio of effective horizontal stress ($\sigma'_h$) to effective vertical stress ($\sigma'_v$) in a soil mass when no lateral deformation or yield is permitted: $$K_0 = \frac{\sigma'_h}{\sigma'_v}$$ **Jaky's Formula (1 Mark):** For normally consolidated soils: $$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$$ **Physical Significance (0.5 Marks):** This formula indicates that $K_0$ decreases with increasing friction angle, reflecting the fact that denser, more frictional soils develop lower lateral stresses in their natural undisturbed state. --- ### Question 2: Short-Answer Conceptual Question **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Question Bank]** **Q2.** Differentiate between Active, Passive, and At-Rest earth pressure states. Under what field conditions does the at-rest condition prevail? #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | State | Wall Movement | Strain Condition | Coefficient | |-------|---------------|------------------|-------------| | **Active ($K_a$)** | Wall moves **away** from soil | Lateral **extension** | $K_a < K_0$ | | **At-Rest ($K_0$)** | **No** wall movement | Lateral strain = **0** | Intermediate value | | **Passive ($K_p$)** | Wall moves **into** soil | Lateral **compression** | $K_p > K_0$ | **Field Conditions for At-Rest (1 Mark):** The at-rest condition prevails when: 1. Retaining walls are **rigid** and **restrained** against movement 2. **Braced basement walls** with adequate strutting 3. **Anchored sheet pile walls** at the anchor level 4. Soils around **tunnels** in competent strata --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** **Q.A.** A 7 m high rigid retaining wall retains a dry cohesionless backfill with unit weight $\gamma = 17 \text{ kN/m}^3$ and angle of internal friction $\phi = 34°$. The wall is founded on rock and does not yield laterally. **(a)** Determine the coefficient of earth pressure at rest using Jaky's empirical formula. **[4 Marks]** **(b)** Calculate the magnitude and point of application of the total active earth pressure (at-rest condition) on the wall. **[7 Marks]** **(c)** Comment on the assumption of "at-rest" condition and its validity for the given problem. **[3 Marks]** #### Model Solution: **Part (a): Coefficient of Earth Pressure at Rest (4 Marks)** Using Jaky's formula for normally consolidated soils: $$K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$$ $$K_0 = 1 - \sin(34°)$$ $$K_0 = 1 - 0.5592$$ $$\boxed{K_0 = 0.4408 \approx 0.441}$$ **Part (b): Magnitude and Point of Application (7 Marks)** **Vertical stress distribution:** At depth $z$ below the surface: $$\sigma_v(z) = \gamma \cdot z = 17 \cdot z \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Horizontal (at-rest) stress distribution:** $$\sigma_h(z) = K_0 \cdot \sigma_v(z) = 0.441 \cdot 17 \cdot z = 7.497 \cdot z \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Pressure at key depths:** - At top ($z = 0$): $\sigma_h = 0 \text{ kN/m}^2$ - At base ($z = 7$ m): $\sigma_h = 7.497 \cdot 7 = 52.48 \text{ kN/m}^2$ **Total thrust per unit length of wall:** The pressure distribution is triangular (linear with depth), so the total thrust equals the area of the triangle: $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_h(\text{base}) \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 52.48 \cdot 7$$ $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_0 \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.441 \cdot 17 \cdot 7^2$$ $$P_0 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.441 \cdot 17 \cdot 49 = 183.69 \text{ kN/m}$$ $$\boxed{P_0 = 183.69 \text{ kN/m}}$$ **Point of application:** For a triangular pressure distribution, the resultant acts at the centroid of the triangle, which is at $H/3$ from the base: $$\bar{z} = \frac{H}{3} = \frac{7}{3} = 2.33 \text{ m from the base}$$ **Part (c): Validity of At-Rest Assumption (3 Marks)** The assumption of at-rest condition is **valid** for this problem because: 1. The wall is founded on **rock**, which provides a **rigid, unyielding base** 2. The wall is described as **rigid** (not susceptible to rotation or translation) 3. The problem explicitly states the wall "does not yield laterally" **However, in practice:** - Even small displacements (as low as **0.001H to 0.002H**) can mobilize active conditions - Long-term creep in soils may reduce $K_0$ towards $K_a$ - Designers should verify the wall's actual stiffness before assuming $K_0$ conditions --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Bank]** **Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the theoretical relationship between the coefficient of earth pressure at rest ($K_0$) and Poisson's ratio ($\mu$) for an elastic soil mass. Derive the relationship step by step. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** An overconsolidated clay deposit has the following properties: Friction angle $\phi = 26°$, OCR = 4. Compute the coefficient of earth pressure at rest using (i) the simplified Schmidt approach, and (ii) the Mayne and Kulhawy (1982) relationship. Compare your results. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution: **Part (a): Theoretical Relationship Between $K_0$ and $\mu$ (7 Marks)** **Statement of the relationship (0.5 Marks):** For an elastic, isotropic soil mass under zero lateral strain: $$K_0 = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}$$ **Step-by-step Derivation (6 Marks):** **Step 1:** For a linearly elastic, isotropic material, the strain in the x-direction is given by generalized Hooke's law: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$ **Step 2:** Under at-rest conditions, there is no lateral deformation, so $\varepsilon_x = 0$: $$0 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$ **Step 3:** Since $E \neq 0$, we have: $$\sigma_x = \mu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$ **Step 4:** For a soil element with vertical stress $\sigma_z = \sigma_v$ and equal horizontal stresses $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_h$ (by symmetry): $$\sigma_h = \mu(\sigma_h + \sigma_v)$$ **Step 5:** Expanding: $$\sigma_h = \mu \cdot \sigma_h + \mu \cdot \sigma_v$$ **Step 6:** Isolating $\sigma_h$: $$\sigma_h - \mu \cdot \sigma_h = \mu \cdot \sigma_v$$ $$\sigma_h(1 - \mu) = \mu \cdot \sigma_v$$ **Step 7:** Dividing by $\sigma_v(1 - \mu)$: $$K_0 = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}$$ **Part (b): Comparison of Methods for Overconsolidated Clay (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $\phi = 26°$, OCR = 4 **Preliminary Calculation:** $$\sin\phi = \sin(26°) = 0.4384$$ For normally consolidated soil: $$K_0(NC) = 1 - \sin\phi = 1 - 0.4384 = 0.5616$$ **Method (i): Schmidt's Simplified Approach (3 Marks)** $$K_0(OC) = K_0(NC) \times OCR$$ $$K_0(OC) = 0.5616 \times 4 = 2.2464$$ $$\boxed{K_0(OC) = 2.25}$$ **Method (ii): Mayne and Kulhawy (1982) Relationship (3 Marks)** $$K_0(OC) = (1 - \sin\phi) \times OCR^{\sin\phi}$$ $$K_0(OC) = 0.5616 \times 4^{0.4384}$$ Computing $4^{0.4384}$: $$\ln(4^{0.4384}) = 0.4384 \times \ln(4) = 0.4384 \times 1.3863 = 0.6077$$ $$4^{0.4384} = e^{0.6077} = 1.8363$$ Therefore: $$K_0(OC) = 0.5616 \times 1.8363 = 1.0312$$ $$\boxed{K_0(OC) = 1.03}$$ **Comparison (1 Mark):** | Method | $K_0$ Value | Remarks | |--------|-------------|---------| | Schmidt (Simplified) | **2.25** | Overestimates; non-conservative | | Mayne & Kulhawy | **1.03** | More accurate; widely accepted | **Conclusion:** The Mayne and Kulhawy relationship gives a **lower, more realistic** value because it accounts for the diminishing effect of OCR at high overconsolidation ratios. The Schmidt method is conservative and may be used for preliminary design only. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse $K_0$ with $K_a$ or $K_p$ in the exam. Remember: $K_a < K_0 < K_p$ (for cohesionless soils with $\phi > 0$). > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to use **effective stresses** when groundwater is present. **Always** compute $\sigma'_v = \gamma' z$ below the water table using buoyant unit weight. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Using Jaky's formula for **overconsolidated** soils without OCR correction. For OC soils, you **MUST** use the Mayne-Kulhawy or Schmidt modification. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Reporting units for $K_0$ (it is **dimensionless**). Marks may be deducted if units are incorrectly shown. > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Computing the point of application as $H/2$ instead of $H/3$ for a triangular distribution. The resultant of a triangular pressure distribution acts at $H/3$ from the base. > 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Not stating the assumptions clearly (e.g., level ground, dry/moist soil, vertical wall, no surcharge). Examiners look for explicit assumption statements. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Earth Pressure at Rest** - [x] **$K_0$ is the ratio** of effective horizontal stress to effective vertical stress when **no lateral strain** occurs - [x] **Jaky's Formula:** $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ (for **normally consolidated** soils) - [x] **Elastic Theory:** $K_0 = \mu / (1 - \mu)$ (theoretical, limited practical use) - [x] **OC Soils (Mayne-Kulhawy):** $K_0(OC) = (1 - \sin\phi) \cdot OCR^{\sin\phi}$ - [x] **OC Soils (Schmidt):** $K_0(OC) = K_0(NC) \cdot OCR$ (conservative, simplified) - [x] **At-rest pressure intensity:** $\sigma_h = K_0 \cdot \gamma' \cdot z$ - [x] **Total thrust (triangular distribution):** $P_0 = \frac{1}{2} K_0 \cdot \gamma \cdot H^2$ - [x] **Point of application:** $H/3$ from the **base** of the wall - [x] **Typical $K_0$ range:** **0.4 to 0.6** for NC cohesionless soils; **1.0 to 3.0+** for OC clays - [x] **Order of magnitudes:** $K_a < K_0 < K_p$ (for $\phi > 0$) - [x] **Use effective stresses** below water table: $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ - [x] **At-rest conditions** exist when walls are **rigid, restrained, or anchored** - [x] **Jaky's formula is the most commonly tested** in KTU examinations - [x] **Always state assumptions** before solving numerical problems - [x] **Units of $K_0$:** Dimensionless (no units!) - [x] **Real-world examples:** Rigid basement walls, tunnel linings, anchored sheet piles, braced excavations > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When a problem gives only the friction angle $\phi$ and asks for $K_0$, **immediately** write down $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ (Jaky's formula) unless the problem specifies Poisson's ratio, OCR, or other special conditions. This single formula is the **gateway to most KTU earth pressure problems**.

topics.title: Active and Passive Earth Pressures - Rankine's Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Active and Passive Earth Pressures - Rankine's Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earth Pressure** is the lateral pressure exerted by soil mass on a retaining structure (such as a retaining wall, sheet pile, or basement wall). It is fundamentally different from the vertical stress (overburden pressure) acting on a horizontal plane.

In **Rankine's Theory (1857)**, the soil mass is considered to be in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (limiting equilibrium), meaning the soil is on the verge of failure at every point within the stressed zone. Based on the relative movement between the wall and the soil, two extreme states exist:

**1. Active State (Minimum Principal Stress Condition):**
> The wall moves **away** from the soil backfill. The soil mass expands laterally, mobilizing its full shear strength. In this state, the horizontal stress becomes the **minor principal stress** ($\sigma_3$), and the vertical stress remains the major principal stress ($\sigma_1$).

**2. Passive State (Maximum Principal Stress Condition):**
> The wall is pushed **towards** the soil backfill. The soil mass is compressed laterally. In this state, the horizontal stress becomes the **major principal stress** ($\sigma_1$), and the vertical stress becomes the minor principal stress ($\sigma_3$).

**3. At-Rest State (Elastic / $K_0$ Condition):**
> The wall is perfectly stationary with no lateral movement. The horizontal stress is related to vertical stress by the **coefficient of earth pressure at rest**, $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ (for normally consolidated soils, Jaky's formula).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Squeezing Lemon vs. The Pushed Bookshelf"**

Imagine a tall bookshelf full of heavy books (**the soil mass**), resting against a thin wooden partition (**the retaining wall**).

*   **Active State (The Squeezing Lemon):** If the wooden partition is **slowly tilted away** from the bookshelf, the books lean back and a small triangular wedge of books slides downward. The books are now "relaxed" and push the wall with only a **minimum** sideways force. The wall is in tension; the soil has expanded.

*   **Passive State (The Pushed Bookshelf):** Now imagine the partition is **pushed strongly into** the bookshelf. The books are crushed, compressed, and pile up against the wall. They resist with their **maximum** possible lateral force. The wall feels squeezed; the soil is in a state of compression.

*   **At-Rest State:** If the partition is glued in place and never moves, the books simply rest in their natural position and exert a "natural" sideways push.

The **Active Pressure** is always much smaller than the **Passive Pressure** (typically $K_a \approx 0.3$ versus $K_p \approx 3.0$ for $\phi = 30°$), because soil is far weaker in tension (pulling away) than in compression (being squeezed).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

*   **Unit weight of soil ($\gamma$)** — measured in **kN/m³** (typically 16–20 kN/m³ for most soils).
*   **Angle of internal friction ($\phi$)** — measured in **degrees** (typically $0°$ to $45°$ for different soils).
*   **Cohesion ($c$)** — measured in **kN/m²** or **kPa** (zero for purely cohesionless soils like sand, non-zero for clay).
*   **Coefficients of Earth Pressure**: $K_a$ (active), $K_p$ (passive), $K_0$ (at-rest) — **dimensionless** numbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 syllabus specifically targets the derivation of Rankine's active and passive earth pressure coefficients for both **cohesionless** ($c = 0$) and **cohesive** ($c-\phi$) soils, including the **effect of inclined backfill** and **submerged/water table conditions**. Wall friction is **assumed to be zero** in Rankine's theory (a key limitation compared to Coulomb's theory).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rankine's Assumptions (Bulleted Logic)

1.  **Homogeneous, Isotropic Soil Mass:** The soil properties ($c$, $\phi$, $\gamma$) are uniform throughout the backfill.
2.  **Semi-Infinite Backfill:** The soil extends infinitely behind the wall (no surcharge or finite wedge effects).
3.  **Smooth, Vertical Wall:** The wall provides **no frictional resistance** to the soil. This is the most restrictive assumption; the resultant earth pressure acts **parallel to the ground surface**.
4.  **Plane Strain Condition:** A long wall is considered, so end effects are negligible (2D analysis).
5.  **State of Plastic Equilibrium:** The soil is on the verge of failure everywhere in the failure zone, and **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** is fully mobilized.
6.  **The Failure Surface is a Plane:** Rankine assumed a planar failure surface at angles $(45° + \phi/2)$ for active and $(45° - \phi/2)$ for passive states with respect to the major principal plane.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — The Failure Mechanism

> **Why do we get a planar failure surface?**
> Because the wall is smooth (no friction), the principal stress directions remain vertical and horizontal everywhere in the soil mass. Mohr's circle can be drawn directly, and the planes of maximum shear stress (the failure planes) are inclined at $\pm(45° + \phi/2)$ to the major principal plane.

> **How do we obtain $K_a$ and $K_p$?**
> We draw the Mohr's circle for the soil in active and passive states, then enforce the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope ($\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$) as a tangent. From the geometry of the resulting circle, we extract the ratio of minor-to-major (or major-to-minor) principal stresses.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **Design of Retaining Walls** (gravity, cantilever, counterfort walls).
*   **Sheet Pile Walls** for deep excavations, basements, and cofferdams.
*   **Braced Cuts and Anchored Sheet Piling.**
*   **Calculating Lateral Loads on Bridge Abutments.**
*   **Stability Analysis of Earth Dams and Embankments** (where inclined backfill is common).
*   **Underground Structures:** Lateral pressure on culverts, tunnels, and pipelines.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Formulas for Rankine Earth Pressure Coefficients (Horizontal Backfill)**

| # | Parameter | Cohesionless Soil ($c=0$) | Cohesive Soil ($c-\phi$) | Units |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | At-rest coefficient | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Active coefficient | $K_a = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Same as cohesionless | dimensionless |
| 3 | Passive coefficient | $K_p = \dfrac{1+\sin\phi}{1-\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Same as cohesionless | dimensionless |
| 4 | Active pressure at depth $z$ | $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | kN/m² |
| 5 | Passive pressure at depth $z$ | $p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | $p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ | kN/m² |
| 6 | Total Active Thrust (height $H$) | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ | $P_a = \dfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$ | kN/m |
| 7 | Total Passive Thrust (height $H$) | $P_p = \dfrac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2$ | $P_p = \dfrac{1}{2} K_p \gamma H^2 + 2cH\sqrt{K_p}$ | kN/m |
| 8 | Point of Application | $H/3$ from base | $H/3$ from base (after deducting tension zone) | m |
| 9 | Depth of Tension Crack ($z_c$) | Not applicable | $z_c = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$ | m |
| 10 | Critical Height (no tension) | Not applicable | $H_{cr} = \dfrac{4c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \dfrac{2 z_c}$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Inclined Backfill (Slope angle $\beta$ with horizontal), the modified coefficients are:**
> $$K_a = \cos\beta \cdot \frac{\cos\beta - \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}{\cos\beta + \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}$$
> $$K_p = \cos\beta \cdot \frac{\cos\beta + \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}{\cos\beta - \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For Submerged Backfill (with water table at depth $H_1$):**
> *   Effective unit weight $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ is used below the water table.
> *   Hydrostatic water pressure $p_w = \gamma_w \cdot z_w$ is added separately to the lateral pressure.
> *   Total lateral pressure = (Effective lateral earth pressure) + (Hydrostatic water pressure).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Active Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_a$)

**Step 1: Set up the stress state at depth $z$ in the active condition.**

Consider a point at depth $z$ below the horizontal ground surface. The vertical stress (overburden) is:
$$\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z$$

This is the **major principal stress** in the active state because the wall has moved away, reducing the horizontal stress:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$$

The horizontal stress $\sigma_h$ is the **minor principal stress** $\sigma_3$ and is what we need to find.

**Step 2: Apply Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion.**

The Mohr-Coulomb envelope in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane is:
$$\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$

At plastic equilibrium, the Mohr's circle representing the active state is **tangent** to this failure envelope.

**Step 3: Geometry of Mohr's Circle.**

The center of Mohr's circle is at:
$$\sigma_c = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_v + \sigma_h}{2}$$

The radius of Mohr's circle is:
$$R = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{2}$$

**Step 4: Tangency condition.**

For the circle to be tangent to the line $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$, the perpendicular distance from the center of the circle to the failure line must equal the radius.

Mathematically, this gives:
$$\sin\phi = \frac{R}{\sigma_c + c \cot\phi} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3) + 2c \cot\phi}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\sigma_3$ (active horizontal stress).**

For **cohesionless soil** ($c = 0$):
$$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}$$

Rearranging:
$$\sigma_1(1 - \sin\phi) = \sigma_3(1 + \sin\phi)$$

$$\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

Since $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$:
$$\boxed{K_a = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 6: For cohesive (c-φ) soil:**

Re-solving the tangency equation with $c \neq 0$:
$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} - 2c \cdot \frac{\cos\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

Using $\sqrt{K_a} = \tan(45° - \phi/2)$ and the identity $\cos\phi/(1+\sin\phi) = \sqrt{K_a}$:
$$\boxed{\sigma_h = K_a \cdot \sigma_v - 2c\sqrt{K_a}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_p$)

**Step 1: Stress state inversion.**

In the passive state, the wall moves toward the soil. The horizontal stress now becomes the **major** principal stress:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_h \quad \text{(unknown, what we want)}$$

The vertical overburden stress becomes the **minor** principal stress:
$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$$

**Step 2: Apply tangency condition again.**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3 + 2c\cot\phi}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\sigma_1$.**

For **cohesionless soil** ($c = 0$):
$$\sigma_1 (1 - \sin\phi) = \sigma_3 (1 + \sin\phi)$$

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_3} = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$$

Since $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_v$:
$$\boxed{K_p = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 4: For cohesive (c-φ) soil:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = K_p \cdot \sigma_v + 2c\sqrt{K_p}}$$

---

### 3.3 Total Active Thrust and Point of Application

For a wall of height $H$ retaining cohesionless soil with horizontal backfill, the active pressure varies linearly from **0 at the top** to $K_a \gamma H$ **at the base**.

The total active thrust per unit length of wall is the **area of the triangular pressure diagram**:
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (K_a \gamma H) \cdot H$$

$$\boxed{P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2}$$

The **centroid** of this triangular pressure distribution is at a height of $H/3$ from the base.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Verification Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A 6 m high retaining wall retains a cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Calculate active thrust, passive thrust, and their ratio.

**Solution:**

$$K_a = \tan^2(45° - 15°) = \tan^2(30°) = (0.5774)^2 = 0.3333$$

$$K_p = \tan^2(45° + 15°) = \tan^2(60°) = (1.7321)^2 = 3.0000$$

Active thrust:
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 6^2 = 108.0 \text{ kN/m}$$

Passive thrust:
$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.0000 \times 18 \times 6^2 = 972.0 \text{ kN/m}$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{P_p}{P_a} = \frac{972.0}{108.0} = 9.0 \quad \left(\text{i.e., } \frac{K_p}{K_a}\right)$$

> **Insight:** The passive resistance is **9 times** the active thrust for $\phi = 30°$. This dramatic difference is why retaining walls can be relatively slender (designed for active pressure) yet are extremely stable when passively resisting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Active vs Passive Failure States

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Retaining Wall Problem] --> B{Wall Movement Direction}
    B -- Moves AWAY from soil --> C[ACTIVE STATE]
    B -- Moves TOWARD soil --> D[PASSIVE STATE]
    B -- NO Movement --> E[AT-REST STATE]
    
    C --> C1[Horizontal stress = sigma3, MINOR principal]
    C --> C2[Vertical stress = sigma1, MAJOR principal]
    C --> C3[Failure plane angle: 45 + phi/2 from horizontal]
    C --> C4[Ka = tan squared 45 minus phi/2]
    C --> C5[Low lateral pressure - soil expands]
    
    D --> D1[Horizontal stress = sigma1, MAJOR principal]
    D --> D2[Vertical stress = sigma3, MINOR principal]
    D --> D3[Failure plane angle: 45 minus phi/2 from horizontal]
    D --> D4[Kp = tan squared 45 plus phi/2]
    D --> D5[High lateral pressure - soil compresses]
    
    E --> E1[K0 = 1 minus sin phi, Jaky formula]
    E --> E2[Elastic equilibrium, no failure]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rankine Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Input: Soil Properties c, phi, gamma] --> stepB[Input: Wall Geometry H, Backfill Slope beta]
    stepB --> stepC{Soil Type?}
    stepC -- Cohesionless c=0 --> stepD[Compute Ka and Kp]
    stepC -- Cohesive c-phi --> stepE[Compute Ka, Kp, Tension Crack Depth zc]
    stepC -- Inclined Backfill --> stepF[Compute modified Ka and Kp using beta]
    stepD --> stepG[Compute Active and Passive Thrusts Pa, Pp]
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH[Compute Point of Application H/3]
    stepH --> stepI[Check Stability and Final Wall Design]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Active and Passive Pressure Distribution on a Cohesionless Wall

```
        Active State (c=0)                Passive State (c=0)
        
   Soil |    |                            Soil |    |
   |    |    |                            |    |    |
   |    |    | pa = 0                     |    |    | pp = 0
   |    |    |                            |    |    |
   |    |    | Increasing                 |    |    | Increasing
   |    |    | pa = Ka·gamma·z            |    |    | pp = Kp·gamma·z
   |    |    |                            |    |    |
   |    |    |                            |    |    |
   |    |    | pa = Ka·gamma·H (max)      |    |    | pp = Kp·gamma·H (max)
   |    |    v                            |    |    v
   +----+--------+                        +----+--------+
   |    Wall     |                        |    Wall     |
   |             | Pa = 0.5·Ka·gamma·H^2  |             | Pp = 0.5·Kp·gamma·H^2
   |             |  acts at H/3           |             |  acts at H/3
   +-------------+                        +-------------+
   
   Triangle opens TO THE RIGHT           Triangle opens TO THE LEFT
   (small, low magnitude)                (large, high magnitude)
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Active Pressure Distribution on a Cohesive Soil Wall (with Tension Crack)

```
   Soil |    |           Tension Crack
   |    |    |           (depth zc, no
   |    |    |            lateral pressure
   |    |    v            in this zone)
   |    |   ---           
   |    |    |  Negative pa
   |    |    |  (tension, neglected
   |    |    |   in design)
   |    |    |
   |    |    | pa = 0  (zero pressure point)
   |    |    |
   |    |    | Positive pa
   |    |    | (compression)
   |    |    | pa = Ka·gamma·z - 2c·sqrt(Ka)
   |    |    v
   +----+--------+
   |    Wall     |  Pa = 0.5·Ka·gamma·H^2 - 2c·H·sqrt(Ka) + (2c·H·sqrt(Ka))
   |             |     = 0.5·Ka·gamma·H^2 - 2c·H·sqrt(Ka) + 0
   |             |  (Note: 2c·H·sqrt(Ka) on RHS is REPLACED by 0.5·Ka·gamma·(H-zc)^2)
   |             |  
   +-------------+  Simplified: Pa = 0.5·Ka·gamma·(H-zc)^2  acting at (H-zc)/3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define the terms **Active Earth Pressure** and **Passive Earth Pressure**. State the values of $K_a$ and $K_p$ in terms of the angle of internal friction $\phi$ as per Rankine's theory.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*   **Active Earth Pressure (1 Mark):** Active earth pressure is the lateral pressure exerted by the soil mass on a retaining wall when the wall **moves away** from the backfill, allowing the soil to expand laterally. In this state, the soil is in plastic equilibrium with the horizontal stress as the minor principal stress.
*   **Passive Earth Pressure (1 Mark):** Passive earth pressure is the lateral pressure exerted by the soil mass on a retaining wall when the wall is **pushed into** the backfill, causing the soil to be compressed laterally. In this state, the horizontal stress becomes the major principal stress.
*   **Coefficients (1 Mark):**
    $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$
    $$K_p = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
What is a **tension crack** in cohesive soils? Derive the expression for its depth $z_c$ in terms of cohesion $c$, unit weight $\gamma$, and $\phi$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*   **Tension Crack (1 Mark):** In cohesive soils, the active earth pressure equation $p_a = K_a \gamma z - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ yields a **negative (tensile) value** near the top of the wall. Since soil cannot sustain tension, a vertical crack called a **tension crack** forms, of depth $z_c$, where lateral pressure is taken as zero.
*   **Derivation (2 Marks):** Setting $p_a = 0$:
    $$0 = K_a \gamma z_c - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$
    $$z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2c}{\gamma} \tan\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

A 7 m high retaining wall with a **vertical back face and horizontal cohesionless backfill** retains a sand with the following properties:
$$\phi = 35°, \quad \gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**(a)** Determine the **Rankine's active earth pressure coefficient** $K_a$ and the **total active thrust** $P_a$ on the wall. State the **point of application** of this thrust. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the water table rises to a height of **3 m below the top of the wall**, calculate the **revised active thrust** taking pore water pressure into account. Use $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{35°}{2}\right) = \tan^2(27.5°)$$

$$\tan(27.5°) = 0.5206 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_a = 0.2710$$

Active pressure at base ($z = H = 7$ m):
$$p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H = 0.2710 \times 19 \times 7 = 36.04 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Total active thrust per metre run:
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2710 \times 19 \times 7^2$$

$$P_a = 126.16 \text{ kN/m}$$

Point of application: $H/3 = 7/3 = 2.33$ m from the base. **(Full 7 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Above water table (0 to 4 m, since WT is 3 m below top, so above WT depth is 4 m):
At $z = 4$ m, $p_{a,1} = 0.2710 \times 19 \times 4 = 20.60$ kN/m²
Thrust above WT: $P_{a,1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 20.60 \times 4 = 41.20$ kN/m **(2 marks)**

Below water table (4 m to 7 m, i.e., 3 m of submerged soil):
Effective unit weight: $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19$ kN/m³
At base ($z = 7$ m), effective active pressure from soil:
$p'_{a,2} = K_a \cdot \gamma' \cdot z = 0.2710 \times 10.19 \times 7 = 19.33$ kN/m²
At WT level ($z = 4$ m), $p'_{a,2} = 0.2710 \times 10.19 \times 4 = 11.05$ kN/m²

Increment in effective earth pressure from 4 m to 7 m:
$\Delta p'_a = 19.33 - 11.05 = 8.28$ kN/m²
Thrust from effective stress in submerged zone:
$P_{a,2} = 11.05 \times 3 + \frac{1}{2} \times 8.28 \times 3 = 33.15 + 12.42 = 45.57$ kN/m **(2 marks)**

Hydrostatic water pressure at base: $p_w = \gamma_w \times 3 = 9.81 \times 3 = 29.43$ kN/m²
Water thrust: $P_w = \frac{1}{2} \times 29.43 \times 3 = 44.15$ kN/m **(2 marks)**

**Total revised active thrust:**
$$P_{a,total} = 41.20 + 45.57 + 44.15 = 130.92 \text{ kN/m} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**

A retaining wall of height 6 m retains a **cohesive backfill** with the following properties:
$$c = 20 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad \phi = 20°, \quad \gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**(a)** Calculate the **total active thrust** on the wall. Show the **pressure distribution diagram** and locate the **point of application** of the resultant thrust. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **depth of the tension crack** and the **critical height** of the wall for which the tension crack just reaches the base. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{20°}{2}\right) = \tan^2(35°) = (0.7002)^2 = 0.4903$$

$$\sqrt{K_a} = 0.7002$$

Active pressure at base ($z = H = 6$ m):
$$p_a = K_a \gamma H - 2c\sqrt{K_a} = 0.4903 \times 18 \times 6 - 2 \times 20 \times 0.7002$$
$$p_a = 52.95 - 28.01 = 24.94 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Active pressure at top ($z = 0$):
$$p_a = 0 - 28.01 = -28.01 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{(tensile, neglected)} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

Depth of tension crack: $z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{40}{18 \times 0.7002} = 3.17$ m **(1 mark)**

Total active thrust (area of the positive triangle from $z_c$ to $H$):
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot p_a \cdot (H - z_c) = \frac{1}{2} \times 24.94 \times (6 - 3.17)$$
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 24.94 \times 2.83 = 35.29 \text{ kN/m} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

Point of application: $(H - z_c)/3 = 2.83/3 = 0.943$ m above the base. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Tension crack depth (already computed):
$$z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2 \times 20}{18 \times 0.7002} = 3.17 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(3 marks)}$$

Critical height $H_{cr}$ occurs when the tension crack reaches the base, i.e., $H = z_c$:
$$H_{cr} = \frac{4c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2 \times 2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = 2 z_c$$
$$H_{cr} = 2 \times 3.17 = 6.34 \text{ m} \quad \text{...(4 marks)}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Loss of Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up $K_a$ and $K_p$:** Active coefficient uses $\tan^2(45 - \phi/2)$, passive uses $\tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$. Reversing these gives 9× wrong answers for $\phi = 30°$.
> 2. **Forgetting to add water pressure in submerged cases:** When a water table exists, the **effective** earth pressure uses $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$, but a **separate hydrostatic water thrust** must be added. Students often miss this, losing 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Neglecting the tension crack in cohesive soils:** Always check if $z_c < H$. If yes, the **active thrust formula simplifies to** $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H - z_c)^2$ — not $\frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$. The two expressions are mathematically equivalent only when $z_c$ is properly used.
> 4. **Wrong units:** $\gamma$ is in kN/m³, $H$ in m, so $K_a \gamma H^2$ has units of kN/m (thrust per unit length). Always state units in answers.
> 5. **Point of application error:** Active thrust on cohesionless soil acts at $H/3$ from the base. For cohesive soil with tension crack, it acts at $(H - z_c)/3$ from the base, not $H/3$.
> 6. **Confusing Rankine with Coulomb:** Rankine assumes a **smooth wall** and a **planar failure surface**. Do not add wall friction angle $\delta$ to Rankine equations.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

*   **Rankine Theory** is based on **Mohr-Coulomb** failure criterion and the assumption of a **smooth vertical wall** with **horizontal (or inclined) backfill**.
*   **Active state** = soil expands laterally = **minimum** lateral pressure = $K_a$ is **small** (typically 0.25–0.50).
*   **Passive state** = soil is compressed laterally = **maximum** lateral pressure = $K_p$ is **large** (typically 2.0–10.0).
*   The relationship $K_a \times K_p = 1$ (for cohesionless soil) is a useful check.
*   **Failure plane inclination** is $45° + \phi/2$ (active) and $45° - \phi/2$ (passive) with respect to the **horizontal** ground surface.
*   **For cohesive soils**, the active thrust formula is $P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 - 2cH\sqrt{K_a}$, and there exists a **tension crack** of depth $z_c = 2c/(\gamma\sqrt{K_a})$.
*   **Critical height** $H_{cr} = 4c/(\gamma\sqrt{K_a})$ — at this height, the wall theoretically requires no lateral support.
*   For **inclined backfill** at angle $\beta$, the modified Rankine coefficients involve $\cos\beta$ and $\sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}$ terms.
*   For **submerged backfill**, use effective unit weight below the water table and **add** the hydrostatic water thrust separately.
*   **Point of application** of total active thrust on a cohesionless wall: $H/3$ from base; on a cohesive wall (with tension crack): $(H - z_c)/3$ from base.
*   Rankine's theory is **conservative** for active pressure (overestimates $P_a$ slightly) and **unconservative** for passive pressure (significantly underestimates $P_p$ in real soils with wall friction).
*   **Limitation:** Rankine's theory cannot handle wall friction ($\delta$), non-vertical walls, or broken backfill profiles — for these, **Coulomb's Wedge Theory** must be used.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth Pressure and Point of Application for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Earth Pressure and Point of Application for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Earth Pressure** is defined as the lateral (horizontal) pressure exerted by soil mass on a retaining structure (such as a retaining wall, sheet pile, or basement wall). It arises because soil, when unsupported laterally, tends to mobilize its **shear strength** to achieve a state of plastic equilibrium.

The **point of application** of this resultant earth thrust is the location (height above the base) at which the entire lateral pressure distribution can be assumed to act as a single equivalent concentrated force, used for structural design of the wall (overturning, sliding, and stability checks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Limiting States of Earth Pressure (KTU 2024 Module 1 Core):**
> 1. **At-Rest Pressure (K₀)** — Wall does not move; soil mass is in elastic equilibrium.
> 2. **Active Pressure (Kₐ)** — Wall moves **away** from the backfill; soil expands laterally and fails by shearing.
> 3. **Passive Pressure (Kₚ)** — Wall moves **towards** the backfill; soil is compressed and fails by shearing in the opposite sense.

The relative magnitude of the lateral movement (δ) controls which state is mobilized:

$$
\delta_{\text{active}} \approx 0.001 H \quad \ll \quad \delta_{\text{passive}} \approx 0.01 H \text{ to } 0.05 H
$$

where $H$ is the height of the wall.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **🍎 The Apple-in-a-Box Analogy**
>
> Imagine packing **sand (cohesionless soil)** in a cardboard box. If you **leave the box alone**, the sand pushes against the side walls gently — this is **At-Rest Pressure (K₀)**.
>
> Now, suppose you **slowly pull one side of the box outward** (like opening a drawer). The sand barely has to hold itself up because gravity is doing most of the work — the lateral push on the wall **drops** to its minimum. This minimum push is the **Active Pressure (Kₐ)**. The sand is "trying to escape" by sliding downward.
>
> Conversely, if you **push the wall into the sand** (like closing a drawer), the sand is now compressed and resists hard — the push on the wall shoots up to a very high value. This maximum push is the **Passive Pressure (Kₚ)**. The sand is "fighting back".
>
> For **clay (cohesive soil)**, think of sticky wet mud. Even if you remove one wall, the mud may **stand on its own** for a short while (up to a critical depth called the **tension crack depth**, $z_c$) due to cohesion. Below $z_c$, it pushes laterally. This is why **clay is the trickier of the two** for retaining wall design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit weight of soil | $\gamma$ | **16 – 22** | kN/m³ |
| Angle of internal friction | $\phi$ | **0° – 45°** | degrees |
| Cohesion | $c$ | **0 – 100** | kN/m² |
| Surcharge load | $q$ | **0 – 100** | kN/m² |
| Wall height | $H$ | **2 – 10** | m |
| At-rest coefficient | $K_0$ | **0.4 – 0.6** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For **cohesionless soils** ($c = 0$), the analysis is purely frictional. For **cohesive soils** ($c > 0,\ \phi = 0$ or $c > 0,\ \phi > 0$), the analysis must account for the tensile strength (cohesion), which introduces the concept of **tension cracks** and **equivalent fluid pressure** (Terzaghi's concept).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine's Theory of Earth Pressure — Underlying Assumptions

**William John Macquorn Rankine (1857)** proposed the classical theory based on the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion** and the concept of plastic equilibrium. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires the following assumptions to be stated explicitly:

1. The soil mass is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**.
2. The backfill surface is **plane (horizontal or inclined)**.
3. The wall is **smooth and vertical** (no wall friction is considered).
4. The wall yields sufficiently to mobilize the full shear strength of the soil.
5. Failure occurs **simultaneously at every point** along a planar rupture surface.
6. The soil is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (Mohr circle touches the failure envelope).

## 2.2 At-Rest Earth Pressure ($K_0$)

When the wall is **rigid and does not move** laterally, the lateral stress is related to the vertical effective stress by the **coefficient of earth pressure at rest**, $K_0$:

$$
\sigma_h' = K_0 \, \sigma_v'
$$

For a normally consolidated cohesionless soil (Jaky, 1944):

$$
K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi
$$

For overconsolidated soils (Mayne & Kulhawy, 1982):

$$
K_0 = (1 - \sin\phi)\,\text{OCR}^{\sin\phi}
$$

where **OCR** is the overconsolidation ratio.

## 2.3 Active Earth Pressure — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why** does active pressure develop? Because when the wall moves away, the soil mass extends laterally. The vertical stress remains $\gamma z$, but the horizontal stress **drops** until the Mohr circle touches the failure envelope on the **dry side** of the Mohr-Coulomb line.

**How** does Rankine derive the active pressure coefficient?

- The principal stresses in the active state: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$ (vertical), $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h = p_a$ (horizontal).
- Mohr-Coulomb failure: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2) + 2c \tan(45^\circ + \phi/2)$.

Solving for the minor principal stress (active pressure):

$$
p_a = \sigma_3 = \sigma_1 K_a - 2c \sqrt{K_a}
$$

where

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}
$$

For **cohesionless soil** ($c = 0$):

$$
p_a = \gamma z \, K_a
$$

For **cohesive soil** ($c > 0$):

$$
p_a = \gamma z \, K_a - 2c \sqrt{K_a}
$$

## 2.4 Passive Earth Pressure — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why** does passive pressure develop? Because when the wall pushes into the soil, the soil is compressed laterally. The horizontal stress becomes the **major principal stress**, and the vertical stress is the **minor principal stress**.

**How** does Rankine derive the passive pressure coefficient?

- Principal stresses: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = p_p$ (horizontal), $\sigma_3 = \sigma_v = \gamma z$ (vertical).
- Mohr-Coulomb: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 K_p + 2c \sqrt{K_p}$.

Solving:

$$
p_p = \sigma_3 K_p + 2c \sqrt{K_p}
$$

where

$$
K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \frac{1}{K_a}
$$

For **cohesionless soil** ($c = 0$):

$$
p_p = \gamma z \, K_p
$$

For **cohesive soil** ($c > 0$):

$$
p_p = \gamma z \, K_p + 2c \sqrt{K_p}
$$

## 2.5 Point of Application of Earth Pressure

The **point of application** is the centroid of the pressure distribution diagram.

| Soil Type | Pressure Distribution Shape | Point of Application (from base) |
|---|---|---|
| Cohesionless, dry | Triangular | $H/3$ |
| Cohesionless, submerged | Trapezoidal | Depends on $\gamma'$ vs. $\gamma_{\text{sat}}$ |
| Cohesive ($c > 0,\ \phi = 0$) | Triangular (net), with tension zone ignored | $H/3$ of effective height $(H - 2z_c)$ |
| Surcharge + cohesionless | Trapezoidal | Centroid of trapezoid |
| Submerged + surcharge | Composite | Centroid of composite area |

For a **cohesionless** backfill with **horizontal surface** and **triangular** active pressure distribution:

$$
P_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \gamma H^2 K_a, \quad \text{acting at } \overline{z} = \frac{H}{3} \text{ from the base}
$$

For a **cohesive** soil ($c > 0$), the **net** active pressure is zero at the **tension crack depth** $z_c$:

$$
z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2c}{\gamma} \tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

The effective pressure distribution is **triangular** from $z = z_c$ to $z = H$, with:

$$
P_a^{\text{net}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \gamma (H - z_c)^2 K_a, \quad \text{acting at } \overline{z} = \frac{H - z_c}{3} \text{ from the base}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** When asked for "point of application", always quote the height measured **from the base of the wall upward**. A common student error is to quote it from the top.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Soil |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | At-rest coefficient (Jaky) | $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ | — | Cohesionless, NC |
| 2 | At-rest lateral pressure | $p_0 = K_0 \sigma_v'$ | kN/m² | All |
| 3 | Active coefficient | $K_a = \tan^2(45^\circ - \phi/2) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ | — | All |
| 4 | Passive coefficient | $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2) = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$ | — | All |
| 5 | Active pressure (general) | $p_a = \gamma z K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ | kN/m² | Cohesive |
| 6 | Active pressure (cohesionless) | $p_a = \gamma z K_a$ | kN/m² | Cohesionless |
| 7 | Passive pressure (general) | $p_p = \gamma z K_p + 2c\sqrt{K_p}$ | kN/m² | Cohesive |
| 8 | Passive pressure (cohesionless) | $p_p = \gamma z K_p$ | kN/m² | Cohesionless |
| 9 | Tension crack depth | $z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma\sqrt{K_a}}$ | m | Cohesive |
| 10 | Active thrust (cohesionless) | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a$ | kN/m | Cohesionless |
| 11 | Passive thrust (cohesionless) | $P_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_p$ | kN/m | Cohesionless |
| 12 | Net active thrust (cohesive) | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma(H - z_c)^2 K_a$ | kN/m | Cohesive |
| 13 | Point of application (cohesionless) | $H/3$ from base | m | Cohesionless |
| 14 | Point of application (cohesive, net) | $(H - z_c)/3$ from base | m | Cohesive |
| 15 | Surcharge effect (cohesionless) | $\Delta p_a = q K_a$ | kN/m² | Cohesionless |
| 16 | Active thrust with surcharge | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a + q H K_a$ | kN/m | Cohesionless |
| 17 | Active thrust with submergence | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a + \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma' H^2 K_a + \gamma_w \tfrac{H_w^2}{2}$ | kN/m | Submerged backfill |
| 18 | Saturated unit weight | $\gamma_{\text{sat}} = \gamma' + \gamma_w$ | kN/m³ | All |
| 19 | Rankine active angle (failure plane) | $45^\circ + \phi/2$ with horizontal | degrees | All |
| 20 | Rankine passive angle (failure plane) | $45^\circ - \phi/2$ with horizontal | degrees | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (≈ 10 kN/m³) in all KTU numerical problems** unless otherwise stated.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of retaining walls** for highways, railways, and bridge abutments (active side governs stability).
- **Design of basement walls** and **sheet pile bulkheads** in marine construction (active + passive both relevant).
- **Stability of cuts and excavations** in urban infrastructure.
- **Earth pressure on pipelines, tunnels, and culverts.**
- **Anchored bulkheads and braced excavations** in deep basements (apparent pressure diagrams, e.g., Terzaghi & Peck, are used in KTU Module 3).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Active Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_a$)

### Step 1 — Elemental Soil Element

Consider an elemental soil element at depth $z$ in a semi-infinite backfill. Under Rankine active conditions:

- **Vertical stress** (major principal, since $K_a < 1$): $\sigma_v = \sigma_1 = \gamma z$
- **Horizontal stress** (minor principal, equal to the lateral pressure on the wall): $\sigma_h = \sigma_3 = p_a$

The element is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** — i.e., on the verge of failure.

### Step 2 — Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion

The Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope in terms of principal stresses is:

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c \tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

We define the **passive pressure coefficient** (which appears in the failure equation) as:

$$
K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

And recognize that $\sqrt{K_p} = \tan(45^\circ + \phi/2)$. Substituting into the failure equation:

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 K_p + 2c\sqrt{K_p}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Minor Principal Stress

Rearranging for $\sigma_3$:

$$
\sigma_3 K_p = \sigma_1 - 2c\sqrt{K_p}
$$

$$
\sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_1}{K_p} - \frac{2c}{\sqrt{K_p}}
$$

Define the **active pressure coefficient** as:

$$
K_a = \frac{1}{K_p} = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_3 = \sigma_1 K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}
$$

Substituting $\sigma_1 = \gamma z$ and $\sigma_3 = p_a$:

$$
\boxed{p_a = \gamma z \, K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}}
$$

For cohesionless soil ($c = 0$):

$$
p_a = \gamma z \, K_a
$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Identity $K_a K_p = 1$

We can verify this:

$$
K_a K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

Using the identity $\tan(45^\circ - \theta) = \cot(45^\circ + \theta)$:

$$
K_a K_p = \cot^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = 1
$$

Hence $K_a = 1/K_p$ and $K_p = 1/K_a$. This identity is used extensively in KTU problems to cross-check answers.

## 3.2 Derivation of Rankine's Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient ($K_p$)

### Step 1 — Stress Reversal in the Passive State

When the wall moves **towards** the backfill, the soil is compressed. The horizontal stress becomes the **major principal stress** and the vertical stress becomes the **minor principal stress**:

- Major principal: $\sigma_1 = p_p$ (horizontal)
- Minor principal: $\sigma_3 = \gamma z$ (vertical)

### Step 2 — Apply Mohr-Coulomb

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 K_p + 2c\sqrt{K_p}
$$

Substituting $\sigma_1 = p_p$ and $\sigma_3 = \gamma z$:

$$
\boxed{p_p = \gamma z \, K_p + 2c\sqrt{K_p}}
$$

For cohesionless soil ($c = 0$):

$$
p_p = \gamma z \, K_p
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Tension Crack Depth ($z_c$)

The active pressure at depth $z$ for a cohesive soil is:

$$
p_a = \gamma z K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}
$$

Setting $p_a = 0$ to find the depth at which the **net** lateral stress becomes zero (above this, soil is in tension and a crack forms):

$$
\gamma z_c K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a} = 0
$$

Solving for $z_c$:

$$
\gamma z_c K_a = 2c\sqrt{K_a}
$$

$$
z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}
$$

Using the identity $\sqrt{K_a} = \tan(45^\circ - \phi/2) = 1/\tan(45^\circ + \phi/2)$:

$$
\boxed{z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma}\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Total Active Thrust ($P_a$) and Point of Application

### Case A: Cohesionless Soil, Horizontal Backfill, No Surcharge

The lateral pressure distribution is a **triangle** with maximum value $p_{a,\text{max}} = \gamma H K_a$ at depth $H$ and zero at $z = 0$.

The total active thrust per unit length of wall is the **area of the triangle**:

$$
P_a = \int_0^H \gamma z K_a \, dz = \gamma K_a \left[\frac{z^2}{2}\right]_0^H = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a
$$

The **point of application** is the centroid of the triangle, located at $H/3$ from the base (i.e., $2H/3$ below the top).

### Case B: Cohesive Soil ($\phi = 0$, $c > 0$)

With $K_a = 1$ and $\sqrt{K_a} = 1$, the active pressure is:

$$
p_a = \gamma z - 2c
$$

The tension crack depth is $z_c = 2c/\gamma$. The **net** pressure distribution (ignoring the tension zone) is triangular from $z = z_c$ to $z = H$, with:

- Maximum net pressure at $z = H$: $p_{a,\text{net,max}} = \gamma H - 2c = \gamma (H - z_c)$

Total net active thrust:

$$
P_a^{\text{net}} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma (H - z_c)^2
$$

Point of application: $(H - z_c)/3$ from the base.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Special Case — Pure Clay with $\phi = 0$ and $c_u$:**
> The result simplifies to the **"equivalent fluid pressure"** concept of Terzaghi:
> $p_a = \gamma z - 2 c_u$ with $K_a = 1$ and $\sqrt{K_a} = 1$. The tension crack depth becomes $z_c = 2c_u/\gamma$.

### Case C: Cohesionless Soil with Uniform Surcharge $q$

The surcharge adds a **uniform** lateral pressure of magnitude $q K_a$ over the full height. The total active thrust is:

$$
P_a = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a}_{\text{triangular part}} + \underbrace{q H K_a}_{\text{rectangular part}}
$$

The two components act at $H/3$ and $H/2$ from the base respectively. The **combined** point of application is found by taking moments about the base:

$$
\overline{z} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a \cdot \tfrac{H}{3} + q H K_a \cdot \tfrac{H}{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a + q H K_a} = \frac{\tfrac{\gamma H^3}{6} + \tfrac{q H^2}{2}}{\tfrac{\gamma H^2}{2} + q H}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A retaining wall 6 m high retains a cohesionless backfill with $\phi = 30^\circ$ and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The backfill carries a uniform surcharge of $q = 20 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Determine the active earth thrust and its point of application.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $K_a$.

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{30^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(30^\circ) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

**Step 2:** Triangular component (due to soil weight only).

$$
P_{a1} = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a = \tfrac{1}{2}(18)(6)^2(0.333) = \tfrac{1}{2}(18)(36)(0.333) = 107.89 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Acts at $H/3 = 2 \text{ m}$ from the base.

**Step 3:** Rectangular component (due to surcharge).

$$
P_{a2} = q H K_a = (20)(6)(0.333) = 40.00 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Acts at $H/2 = 3 \text{ m}$ from the base.

**Step 4:** Total active thrust.

$$
P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 107.89 + 40.00 = 147.89 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 5:** Point of application (taking moments about the base).

$$
\overline{z} = \frac{(107.89)(2) + (40.00)(3)}{147.89} = \frac{215.78 + 120.00}{147.89} = \frac{335.78}{147.89} = 2.27 \text{ m from base}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** $P_a = 147.89 \text{ kN/m}$, acting at $\overline{z} = 2.27 \text{ m}$ from the base of the wall.

## 3.6 Comparison: Active vs. Passive vs. At-Rest (Tabular Summary)

| Parameter | At-Rest ($K_0$) | Active ($K_a$) | Passive ($K_p$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wall movement | Zero | Away from soil | Towards soil |
| Stress state | Elastic | Plastic (extension) | Plastic (compression) |
| $\sigma_h$ relative to $\sigma_v$ | $\sigma_h < \sigma_v$ | $\sigma_h \ll \sigma_v$ | $\sigma_h \gg \sigma_v$ |
| Typical coefficient (for $\phi = 30^\circ$) | 0.50 | 0.333 | 3.000 |
| Failure plane angle with horizontal | None | $45^\circ + \phi/2 = 60^\circ$ | $45^\circ - \phi/2 = 30^\circ$ |
| Wall movement needed | 0 | ~$0.001H$ | ~$0.01H$ to $0.05H$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note the asymmetric magnitudes:** $K_p$ is always much larger than $K_a$ for the same $\phi$. For $\phi = 30^\circ$, $K_p = 9 K_a$. This is why **passive pressure is the designer's friend** — even a small embedment of a wall can mobilize a very large passive resistance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Earth Pressure States

The following block-level flow maps the **decision logic** that a geotechnical engineer follows to determine the appropriate earth pressure state for design. This conforms to the KTU 2024 requirement for schematic illustration of earth pressure concepts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Retaining Structure] --> B{Soil Type?}
    B -- Cohesionless, c equals 0 --> C[Compute Ka from phi]
    B -- Cohesive, c greater than 0 --> D[Compute Ka and c term]
    C --> E{Wall Movement Direction?}
    D --> E
    E -- No movement --> F[At-Rest: K0 equals 1 minus sin phi]
    E -- Wall moves away --> G[Active: Pa equals half gamma H squared Ka]
    E -- Wall moves towards soil --> H[Passive: Pp equals half gamma H squared Kp]
    F --> I[Point of Application: H over 2 from base triangular]
    G --> J[Pressure Distribution Triangular: Pa at H over 3 from base]
    H --> K[Pressure Distribution Triangular: Pp at H over 3 from base]
    D --> L{Tension Crack Check}
    L -- zc greater than 0 --> M[Effective Height H minus zc, Net triangular from zc to H]
    L -- phi equals 0 pure clay --> N[Equivalent Fluid: Pa equals gamma z minus 2cu]
    M --> O[Point of Application: H minus zc over 3 from base]
    N --> P[Point of Application: H minus zc over 3 from base]
    J --> Q[Stability and Design Checks for Wall]
    K --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    I --> Q
    Q --> R[End: Final Wall Section]
```

## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Schematic (Textual Rendering)

The following is a **functional block schematic** of the lateral pressure distribution on a retaining wall for various soil and loading conditions. The diagrams are described block-wise so that the reader can reconstruct them mentally or sketch them on paper.

### 4.2.1 Cohesionless Backfill, No Surcharge, No Water Table

```
   |  WALL                          |
   |                                |
   |  z = H    |<--  pa,max = gamma H Ka -->|  ← triangular distribution
   |           |                          |
   |           |                          |
   |           |                          |
   |  z = 0    |__________________________|
   |              <----- H ----->
   |                                    Pa = ½ γ H² Ka
   |                                    Acts at H/3 from base
```

### 4.2.2 Cohesive Backfill ($\phi = 0$, Pure Clay)

```
   |  WALL                                     |
   |          [TENSION ZONE - IGNORED]         |
   |  z = H   |                                |  pa,max = γ H − 2cu
   |          |      pa = γ z − 2cu             |
   |  z = zc  |--------*  (zero pressure)      |
   |          |       /|                       |
   |          |      / |                       |
   |  z = 0   |_____/__|_______________________|
   |                ^   ^   ^
   |               zc   H   H-2zc (effective height)
   |           Pa,net = ½ γ (H − zc)²
   |           Acts at (H − zc)/3 from base
```

### 4.2.3 Cohesionless Backfill with Surcharge

```
   |  WALL                                          |
```
