```
   |                  [RECTANGULAR: q Ka]           |
   |          |----------------------------------|  |
   |  z = H   |                                  |  |  ← uniform surcharge
   |          |       [TRIANGULAR: γ z Ka]       |  |     effect
   |          |                /                 |  |
   |          |               /                  |  |
   |          |              /                   |  |
   |  z = 0   |_____________/___________________|__|
   |                ↑                  ↑
   |              H/2 (rect)        H/3 (triangle)
   |           Pa = ½γH²Ka + qHKa
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Earth Pressure Coefficients vs. $\phi$

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Phi equals 0 deg] --> A1[Ka equals 1.00]
    A --> A2[Kp equals 1.00]
    A --> A3[K0 equals 1.00]
    B[Phi equals 15 deg] --> B1[Ka equals 0.589]
    B --> B2[Kp equals 1.698]
    B --> B3[K0 equals 0.741]
    C[Phi equals 30 deg] --> C1[Ka equals 0.333]
    C --> C2[Kp equals 3.000]
    C --> C3[K0 equals 0.500]
    D[Phi equals 45 deg] --> D1[Ka equals 0.172]
    D --> D2[Kp equals 5.828]
    D --> D3[K0 equals 0.293]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Chart:** As $\phi$ increases, the soil becomes "stronger" (more frictional). In the active state, it can stand on its own with less lateral push ($K_a$ decreases). In the passive state, it resists more strongly ($K_p$ increases dramatically). This is why **granular soils with high $\phi$** are preferred as backfill behind retaining walls.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the active and passive states of earth pressure. Under what conditions does each develop?**

**Model Answer:**

The **active state** of earth pressure develops when the retaining wall moves **away from the backfill**, allowing the soil mass to expand laterally. The soil mobilizes its shear strength, and the lateral stress on the wall drops to a **minimum** value, characterized by the active pressure coefficient $K_a < 1$.

The **passive state** develops when the wall moves **towards the backfill**, compressing the soil. The lateral stress rises to a **maximum** value, characterized by $K_p > 1$. The active state requires only a small wall movement ($\sim 0.001H$), while the passive state requires much larger movements ($\sim 0.01H$ to $0.05H$).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the point of application of the active earth pressure on a retaining wall with cohesionless backfill? Justify with a sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

For a cohesionless backfill with a **horizontal surface** and **triangular** pressure distribution (varying from zero at the top to $\gamma H K_a$ at the base), the total active thrust is:

$$
P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a
$$

The resultant acts at the **centroid of the triangular distribution**, which is located at **$H/3$ from the base** (or $2H/3$ below the top). This is the point of application used for overturning and sliding stability checks of the wall.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** A retaining wall **6 m high** retains a **cohesionless** backfill with $\phi = 30^\circ$ and $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$. The backfill carries a uniform surcharge of $q = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Determine:
1. The active earth pressure at the base.
2. The total active thrust.
3. The point of application of the resultant thrust.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same wall, now suppose the backfill is **cohesive** with $\phi = 15^\circ$, $c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2$, and $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Determine the **net active thrust** and its **point of application**, assuming a horizontal backfill. Take the water table to be well below the base.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Compute $K_a$.

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{30^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(30^\circ) = \tfrac{1}{3} = 0.333
$$

**Step 2:** Active pressure at the base ($z = H = 6$ m).

$$
p_{a,\text{base}} = \gamma H K_a + q K_a = (18)(6)(0.333) + (30)(0.333)
$$

$$
p_{a,\text{base}} = 35.96 + 9.99 = 45.95 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Total active thrust (triangular + rectangular components).

$$
P_{a1} = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 K_a = \tfrac{1}{2}(18)(36)(0.333) = 107.89 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
P_{a2} = q H K_a = (30)(6)(0.333) = 59.94 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 107.89 + 59.94 = 167.83 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4:** Point of application (moments about the base).

- Triangular component acts at $H/3 = 2$ m from base.
- Rectangular component acts at $H/2 = 3$ m from base.

$$
\overline{z} = \frac{(107.89)(2) + (59.94)(3)}{167.83} = \frac{215.78 + 179.82}{167.83} = \frac{395.60}{167.83} = 2.36 \text{ m from base}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (a):** $p_{a,\text{base}} = 45.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $P_a = 167.83 \text{ kN/m}$, $\overline{z} = 2.36 \text{ m}$ from base.

#### Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Compute $K_a$ and $\sqrt{K_a}$ for $\phi = 15^\circ$.

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{15^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(37.5^\circ) = (0.7673)^2 = 0.5888
$$

$$
\sqrt{K_a} = \tan(37.5^\circ) = 0.7673
$$

**Step 2:** Tension crack depth $z_c$.

$$
z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2(15)}{(19)(0.7673)} = \frac{30}{14.58} = 2.058 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Active pressure at the base ($z = H = 6$ m).

$$
p_{a,\text{base}} = \gamma H K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a} = (19)(6)(0.5888) - 2(15)(0.7673)
$$

$$
p_{a,\text{base}} = 67.12 - 23.02 = 44.10 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Net active pressure at $z = z_c$ (zero by definition).

**Step 5:** Net active thrust (triangular distribution from $z = z_c$ to $z = H$).

$$
P_a^{\text{net}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma (H - z_c)^2 K_a = \tfrac{1}{2}(19)(6 - 2.058)^2 (0.5888)
$$

$$
P_a^{\text{net}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(19)(3.942)^2 (0.5888) = \tfrac{1}{2}(19)(15.54)(0.5888) = 86.93 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 6:** Point of application (centroid of the net triangle).

$$
\overline{z} = \frac{H - z_c}{3} = \frac{6 - 2.058}{3} = \frac{3.942}{3} = 1.31 \text{ m from base}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (b):** $z_c = 2.06 \text{ m}$, $P_a^{\text{net}} = 86.93 \text{ kN/m}$, $\overline{z} = 1.31 \text{ m}$ from base.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** A retaining wall **8 m high** retains a **cohesionless** backfill with $\phi = 35^\circ$ and $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$. A **water table** rises to a height of 4 m above the base. Determine the **active earth pressure** at the base and the **total active thrust**, including the water pressure effect. Take $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **tension cracks** in cohesive backfills. For a **cohesive** soil with $\phi = 20^\circ$, $c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$, and $\gamma = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$, compute the **depth of tension crack**. Also discuss why the active pressure in the tension zone is **ignored** in design.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Compute $K_a$ for $\phi = 35^\circ$.

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{35^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(27.5^\circ) = (0.5206)^2 = 0.2710
$$

**Step 2:** Strata above water table (0 ≤ z ≤ 4 m): use $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$.

At $z = 4$ m (top of water table):

$$
p_a(4) = \gamma (4) K_a = (19)(4)(0.2710) = 20.59 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Strata below water table (4 < z ≤ 8 m): use submerged unit weight $\gamma' = \gamma_{\text{sat}} - \gamma_w$.

Assuming $\gamma_{\text{sat}} = 20 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (typical):

$$
\gamma' = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

At $z = 8$ m (base of wall):

$$
p_a(8) = \gamma (4) K_a + \gamma' (4) K_a = 20.59 + (10.19)(4)(0.2710) = 20.59 + 11.05 = 31.64 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Total active thrust (trapezoidal + triangular earth pressure, plus triangular water pressure).

Earth pressure thrust (area of trapezoid from 0 to 4 m and triangle from 4 to 8 m):

$$
P_{a,\text{earth}} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}(4)(20.59)}_{\text{top triangle}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}(4)(20.59 + 31.64)}_{\text{lower trapezoid}}
$$

$$
P_{a,\text{earth}} = 41.18 + 104.46 = 145.64 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Water pressure thrust (triangular, $p_w$ at base = $\gamma_w \times 4 = 39.24$ kN/m²):

$$
P_w = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(39.24) = 78.48 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 5:** Total lateral thrust on wall.

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{a,\text{earth}} + P_w = 145.64 + 78.48 = 224.12 \text{ kN/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (a):** $p_a$ at base (effective) = 31.64 kN/m², $P_{\text{total}} = 224.12$ kN/m including water thrust.

#### Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Conceptual Explanation:**

A **tension crack** is a vertical crack that forms near the top of a cohesive backfill when the wall yields in the active mode. In this zone, the soil experiences **tensile stress** (negative effective stress). Soil cannot sustain tension, so a crack opens up to a depth at which the active pressure becomes zero. Beyond this depth, the soil is in compression and exerts positive lateral pressure on the wall. In design, the **tension zone is ignored** because (i) soil in tension cannot transfer load to the wall, and (ii) the crack may fill with water, adding destabilizing hydrostatic pressure.

**Numerical Computation:**

For $\phi = 20^\circ$:

$$
K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{20^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(35^\circ) = (0.7002)^2 = 0.4903
$$

$$
\sqrt{K_a} = \tan(35^\circ) = 0.7002
$$

Tension crack depth:

$$
z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{2(25)}{(20)(0.7002)} = \frac{50}{14.00} = 3.57 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (b):** $z_c = 3.57 \text{ m}$. The tension zone is ignored because cohesive soil cannot sustain tensile stress; the lateral pressure is taken as zero from the top of the wall down to $z_c$.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the tension crack depth** in cohesive backfills. Marks are lost when students compute $P_a$ using the full height $H$ instead of the effective height $(H - z_c)$.
> 2. **Sign errors in the cohesion term.** The term $-2c\sqrt{K_a}$ in $p_a$ and $+2c\sqrt{K_p}$ in $p_p$ are frequently interchanged. Always double-check by setting $c = 0$ and verifying the formula reduces to the cohesionless case.
> 3. **Point of application quoted from the top instead of the base.** The standard convention in KTU is **from the base of the wall upward**.
> 4. **Not splitting the water table problem** into above-WT and below-WT zones when the WT is at an intermediate depth.
> 5. **Ignoring the water pressure thrust** in submerged backfills — it must be added separately to the earth pressure thrust.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Yield Bullet Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Earth Pressure:**

- The **three states** of earth pressure are **at-rest** ($K_0$), **active** ($K_a$), and **passive** ($K_p$), in **increasing order of magnitude** of $K$.
- $K_a = \tan^2(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ and $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$; the identity $K_a K_p = 1$ **always** holds.
- $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$ for normally consolidated cohesionless soils (Jaky's formula).
- For **cohesionless** backfill with horizontal surface, active pressure distribution is **triangular** and the resultant acts at $H/3$ from the base.
- For **cohesive** backfill, the active pressure equation is $p_a = \gamma z K_a - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$. The **tension crack depth** is $z_c = \frac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$. The net thrust is $\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma (H - z_c)^2 K_a$ acting at $(H - z_c)/3$ from the base.
- A **uniform surcharge** $q$ adds a **rectangular** pressure of magnitude $q K_a$ over the full wall height, with the rectangular component acting at $H/2$ from the base.
- A **water table** within the backfill splits the analysis into a **dry/moist** upper zone (use $\gamma$) and a **submerged** lower zone (use $\gamma' = \gamma_{\text{sat}} - \gamma_w$). The **hydrostatic water pressure** must be added as a separate triangular thrust.
- **Rankine's theory** assumes a smooth, vertical wall with no wall friction and a planar failure surface at $45^\circ + \phi/2$ (active) or $45^\circ - \phi/2$ (passive) with the horizontal.
- **Coulomb's theory** (not in KTU Module 1 scope, but good to know) accounts for wall friction and inclined backfill using $K_a$ and $K_p$ coefficients that depend on $\delta$ (wall friction angle).
- The **passive pressure is much larger** than the active pressure: for $\phi = 30^\circ$, $K_p = 9 K_a$. This asymmetry is exploited in embedded walls and sheet pile design.
- **Point of application** is **always** quoted from the **base of the wall** in KTU solutions unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- For **pure clay** ($\phi = 0$, $c = c_u$), Rankine gives $K_a = 1$ and the active pressure reduces to $p_a = \gamma z - 2c_u$ — this is the basis of the **"equivalent fluid"** concept used in design.
- The **failure wedge** in the active case makes an angle of $45^\circ + \phi/2$ with the horizontal, and in the passive case, $45^\circ - \phi/2$. These angles are derived from Mohr-circle geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Earth Pressure and Point of Application for Cohesionless and Cohesive Soils.** Students are advised to practise at least **three numerical problems** (one cohesionless with surcharge, one cohesive with tension crack, one with submergence) before the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Influence of Surcharge and Water Table on Earth Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Influence of Surcharge and Water Table on Earth Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Earth Pressure** is the lateral pressure exerted by soil mass on a retaining structure (such as a retaining wall, sheet pile, or basement wall). However, the actual lateral pressure acting on a retaining wall is rarely equal to the simple Rankine or Coulomb pressure computed from the self-weight of the soil alone. Two extremely important modifying factors are:

1. **Surcharge Loads** – Any additional loading applied on the ground surface behind the wall (uniform surcharge, traffic load, building load, line load, point load), which transmits additional lateral stress to the wall through soil-structure interaction.
2. **Water Table Position** – The location of the phreatic surface (ground water table) within the backfill, which drastically alters the effective unit weight of the soil, introduces pore water pressure, and therefore modifies both active and passive earth pressures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** This topic is critical for Module 1 because it directly addresses the **modification of Rankine's active/passive pressure theory under practical field conditions**. Almost every numerical problem in KTU exams involving retaining walls assumes a non-zero surcharge and a water table at some intermediate depth.

The general modified Rankine active earth pressure with surcharge and water table effects can be expressed in its most general form as:

$$p_a(z) = K_a \cdot \sigma'_v(z) - 2 c' \sqrt{K_a}$$

where the **vertical effective stress** $\sigma'_v(z)$ incorporates the surcharge effect and the **effective unit weight** incorporates the water table position.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **dam wall** holding back a reservoir of muddy water with sand and gravel behind it. If the water level in the reservoir rises, the wall has to resist more force — that is the influence of the **water table**. Now imagine trucks and cranes operating on top of the embankment behind the dam. The extra weight of these vehicles pushes the soil sideways against the wall — that is the **surcharge effect**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pressing Sponge" Model**
> Picture a wet sponge sandwiched between two vertical glass plates:
> - The **sponge's weight** is the soil's self-weight → it produces a triangular active pressure distribution against the plates.
> - If you **place a book on top of the sponge** (the surcharge), the entire sponge is compressed more — the lateral thrust on the plates increases uniformly along the entire height.
> - If the sponge is **partially soaked in water** (water table inside the backfill), the lower part of the sponge becomes lighter (buoyancy) but the water itself pushes against the plates — net effect is a more complex pressure distribution.

This is exactly what happens in real geotechnical engineering:
- **Surcharge** → adds a **rectangular** (uniform) component to the active pressure.
- **Water table** → replaces the saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat}$ with the **buoyant (effective) unit weight** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ in the submerged zone, and adds a **hydrostatic water pressure** component $\sigma_w = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:----------------------|
| $\gamma_w$ | Unit weight of water | **9.81 kN/m³** (often taken as 10 kN/m³ in KTU problems) |
| $G$ | Specific gravity of soil solids | 2.65 – 2.75 (typical) |
| $K_a$ | Coefficient of active earth pressure | $\frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ |
| $K_p$ | Coefficient of passive earth pressure | $\frac{1+\sin\phi}{1-\sin\phi}$ |
| $H$ | Height of retaining wall | **m** |
| $q$ | Uniform surcharge intensity | **kN/m²** |
| $H_w$ | Depth of water table from ground surface | **m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³** in numerical problems. However, in many model solutions, **$\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³** is adopted for arithmetic simplicity. Always check the problem statement first.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Foundation Engineering

In real KTU-aligned design problems (retaining walls, sheet piles, abutments, basement walls, anchored bulkheads), the field conditions are **never ideal**:

- The backfill soil is **almost always partially saturated**.
- Construction sites have **vehicle movement, stored material, or adjacent buildings** imposing surcharge.
- Rainfall and seasonal fluctuation cause the **water table to rise and fall**.

Therefore, a foundation engineer **must know how to superimpose** the surcharge-induced pressure and the water-table-induced pressure onto the basic Rankine triangular distribution. This is the *de facto* first step in the design of any earth-retaining structure under the KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case (A): Effect of Uniform Surcharge on Earth Pressure

A **uniform surcharge** is a load of intensity $q$ (kN/m²) spread over the entire horizontal ground surface behind the wall. Examples include:
- A parking lot on top of the backfill.
- A layer of stored granular material (gravel, sand).
- An equivalent uniform load representing a building (e.g., floor load × load factor).

### 2.1.1 Effect on Active Earth Pressure (Rankine Theory)

By Rankine's theory, the surcharge $q$ produces a **uniformly distributed** active pressure on the wall:

$$\Delta p_a = K_a \cdot q \quad \text{(kN/m²)}$$

The **total increase in active thrust** due to the surcharge is:

$$\Delta P_a = K_a \cdot q \cdot H \quad \text{(kN/m)}$$

This additional thrust acts **horizontally at mid-height of the wall** (i.e., at $H/2$ from the base).

### 2.1.2 Effect on Passive Earth Pressure

Similarly, on the passive side (in front of the wall), the surcharge produces a uniformly distributed pressure:

$$\Delta p_p = K_p \cdot q \quad \text{(kN/m²)}$$

$$\Delta P_p = K_p \cdot q \cdot H \quad \text{(kN/m)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU:**
> The surcharge is **always additive** to the active pressure (it makes the situation more critical) and **always additive** to the passive pressure (it makes the passive resistance stronger). The direction of thrust due to surcharge is **horizontal** (Rankine assumption — vertical stress from surcharge is fully effective, no wall friction in Rankine analysis).

### 2.1.3 Resultant Pressure Distribution

The total active pressure at depth $z$ below the top of the wall is:

$$p_a(z) = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z + K_a \cdot q - 2 c \sqrt{K_a}$$

**Graphical Picture:**
```
   p_a
    ↑
    |            ┌──────────┐ ← K_a·q (uniform, due to surcharge)
    |           /│          
    |          / │  Triangular part
    |         /  │  (due to self-weight)
    |        /   │
    |       /    │
    |______/_____|__________→ z
           H
```

---

## 2.2 Case (B): Effect of Water Table on Active Earth Pressure

This is the **most numerically intensive** part of this KTU topic. We consider a retaining wall with backfill of height $H$ and a water table located at depth $H_1$ from the top.

### 2.2.1 Standard Setup

```
     |     |   q (surcharge)
     |     |___________________
     |     |  \  Dry/Moist soil (γ above WT)
     |     |   \  K_a, φ, c
     |     |    \
     |  H  |     \
     |     |~~~~~~| ← Water Table (depth H_1 from top)
     |     |      |\  Submerged soil (γ' = γ_sat - γ_w)
     |     |      | \
     |     |      |  \  K_a, φ', c'
     |     |______|___\
     |        H_w        (depth of submerged zone = H - H_1)
     |     |      |       \
     |_____|______|________\__→ base
```

### 2.2.2 Effective Stress Principle

In the **submerged zone** (below water table), the effective unit weight is:

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w \quad \text{(kN/m³)}$$

The **total lateral stress** in the submerged zone consists of:
- **Effective lateral stress** (from soil grains): $\sigma'_h = K_a \cdot \sigma'_v$
- **Pore water pressure** (from water): $u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$

Therefore, the **total active pressure** at any depth is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{h,\text{total}} = \sigma'_h + u = K_a \cdot \sigma'_v + \gamma_w \cdot h_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Many students forget to add the **hydrostatic water pressure** term in the submerged zone. This is a guaranteed **2-3 mark deduction** if missed.

### 2.2.3 Active Pressure at Critical Depths (Intermediate WT Case)

Let $H_1$ = depth of water table from top, $H_w = H - H_1$ = submerged depth.

**At $z = 0$ (ground surface, top of wall):**
$$p_{a,0} = K_a \cdot q$$

**At $z = H_1$ (just above water table):**
$$p_{a,H_1} = K_a \cdot \left(q + \gamma \cdot H_1\right)$$

**At $z = H$ (just below water table at base):**
$$p_{a,H} = K_a \cdot \left(q + \gamma \cdot H_1 + \gamma' \cdot H_w\right) + \gamma_w \cdot H_w$$

The pressure distribution has the following shape:
```
   p_a
    ↑
    |       • (K_a·q)
    |      /|
    |     / |
    |    /  |
    |   /   | ← Triangular (dry zone)
    |  /    |
    | /     • (K_a·(q+γ·H_1))
    |/______|___________________________
            |       /|                   ← Triangular (submerged, with γ')
            |      / |
            |     /  |
            |    /   |
            |   /    • (K_a·(q+γ·H_1+γ'·H_w))
            |  /    /|
            | /    / |  ← Hydrostatic (γ_w·H_w) added
            |/____/__|
                        H
```

---

## 2.3 Case (C): Effect of Water Table on Passive Earth Pressure

By complete analogy, the passive pressure at depth $z$ is:

$$\sigma_{p,\text{total}} = K_p \cdot \sigma'_v + \gamma_w \cdot h_w$$

The full distribution of passive pressure when the water table is at intermediate depth $H_1$ is:

$$p_p(z) = K_p \cdot \sigma'_v(z) + \gamma_w \cdot h_w(z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **same concept** of effective unit weight reduction and pore water pressure addition applies — the only difference is that we use $K_p$ instead of $K_a$. The pressure distribution in the submerged zone has a **steeper slope** (because $K_p \gg K_a$) plus a uniform increment from pore water pressure.

---

## 2.4 Special Sub-Cases Frequently Asked in KTU Exams

### Sub-Case 1: Water Table at the Base of the Wall ($H_1 = H$)

No submerged zone exists. The whole backfill uses bulk unit weight $\gamma$ above WT (which is at the base). **No pore water pressure** on the wall.

### Sub-Case 2: Water Table at the Ground Surface ($H_1 = 0$)

The entire backfill is submerged.
- Use $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ throughout for soil contribution.
- Pore water pressure varies from $0$ at top to $\gamma_w \cdot H$ at base.

### Sub-Case 3: Submerged Backfill with Surcharge Above Water

Most common in KTU numericals:
- $0 \le z \le H_1$: dry/moist soil, $\gamma$ used.
- $H_1 \le z \le H$: submerged, $\gamma'$ used + $\gamma_w \cdot h_w$ added.

### Sub-Case 4: Surcharge Present AND Water Table at Intermediate Position

This is the **superimposed** case — both effects act together. The total active pressure at the base is:

$$p_{a,H} = K_a \cdot \left(q + \gamma \cdot H_1 + \gamma' \cdot H_w\right) + \gamma_w \cdot H_w$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Condition | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:---------:|:--------|:------------|:-----:|
| 1 | Active due to surcharge (uniform) | $\Delta p_a = K_a \cdot q$ | Lateral pressure increment per unit depth | kN/m² |
| 2 | Active thrust due to surcharge | $\Delta P_a = K_a \cdot q \cdot H$ | Total horizontal thrust from surcharge | kN/m |
| 3 | Passive due to surcharge (uniform) | $\Delta p_p = K_p \cdot q$ | Lateral pressure increment per unit depth | kN/m² |
| 4 | Passive thrust due to surcharge | $\Delta P_p = K_p \cdot q \cdot H$ | Total horizontal thrust from surcharge | kN/m |
| 5 | Effective unit weight (submerged) | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Buoyant unit weight below WT | kN/m³ |
| 6 | Pore water pressure at depth $h_w$ | $u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$ | Hydrostatic water pressure on wall | kN/m² |
| 7 | Active pressure at base (intermediate WT) | $p_{a,H} = K_a(q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w) + \gamma_w H_w$ | Total lateral pressure at base of wall | kN/m² |
| 8 | Total active thrust (intermediate WT) | $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 + K_a q H + K_a (q + \gamma H_1) H_w + \tfrac{1}{2}(K_a \gamma' + \gamma_w) H_w^2$ | Sum of all force components | kN/m |
| 9 | Active coefficient (Rankine) | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$ | For cohesionless soil | — |
| 10 | Passive coefficient (Rankine) | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$ | For cohesionless soil | — |
| 11 | Total active pressure (c-φ soil, no WT) | $p_a = K_a \sigma'_v - 2 c' \sqrt{K_a}$ | With surcharge and cohesion | kN/m² |
| 12 | Resultant location (surcharge) | $\bar{z} = H/2$ from base | Acts at mid-height | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Standard:**
> In numerical answers, the **total active thrust $P_a$** is generally required to **2 decimal places** (e.g., $P_a = 218.75$ kN/m). The **point of application** (line of action) is also frequently required — students must compute the **moment of each pressure component** about the base and divide by total force.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This concept is **the bread and butter** of:
- **Cantilever retaining wall design** (footing of a basement).
- **Counterfort retaining wall** design.
- **Sheet pile wall** design (anchored and unanchored).
- **Bridge abutment** design where the backfill carries traffic surcharge.
- **Underground metro / tunnel** portal design in urban areas.
- **Dock and harbor walls** where the water table fluctuates with tides.

A real-world failure case: The **1965 Kempton Park retaining wall collapse (UK)** was partially attributed to **unaccounted-for pore water pressure** in the backfill — exactly the kind of mistake this topic teaches you to avoid.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Active Thrust with Surcharge and Intermediate Water Table

This is the **master derivation** for the most general case in the KTU syllabus. We will derive the **total active thrust** $P_a$ acting on a retaining wall of height $H$, with:
- Uniform surcharge $q$ on the ground surface.
- Water table at depth $H_1$ from the top.
- Submerged depth $H_w = H - H_1$.
- Effective unit weight in submerged zone $\gamma'$.
- Bulk unit weight in dry zone $\gamma$.
- Active earth pressure coefficient $K_a = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry and identify pressure components

The total active pressure at any depth $z$ (measured from the top) can be split into **four contributing components**:

**Component 1:** Soil self-weight in the **dry zone** ($0 \le z \le H_1$)

**Component 2:** Soil self-weight in the **submerged zone** ($H_1 \le z \le H$), using $\gamma'$

**Component 3:** Uniform surcharge contribution, **rectangular**, over the full height

**Component 4:** Hydrostatic pore water pressure in the **submerged zone**

We will compute the **force** and **point of application** of each component separately.

---

### Step 2 — Component 1: Triangular Active Thrust from Dry Zone Soil

The pressure at $z = 0$ is zero (in the absence of surcharge and cohesion). The pressure at $z = H_1$ due to dry zone soil alone:

$$p_{a,1}(H_1) = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H_1$$

This forms a **triangle** with base $\gamma H_1$ and width $K_a$. The force is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H_1 \cdot H_1 \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2
\end{aligned}$$

Point of application: at the **centroid of the triangle**, which is $\frac{2}{3} H_1$ from the top, i.e., $\left(H - \frac{2}{3} H_1\right)$ from the base.

$$\bar{z}_1 = H - \frac{2 H_1}{3} \quad \text{from base}$$

---

### Step 3 — Component 2: Triangular Active Thrust from Submerged Zone Soil

In the submerged zone, soil grains contribute via effective unit weight $\gamma'$. The effective vertical stress at depth $z$ (for $z > H_1$) is:

$$\sigma'_v(z) = q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma'(z - H_1)$$

The **soil-grain-only** active pressure (effective part) at the top of submerged zone ($z = H_1$):

$$p'_{a,2}(H_1) = K_a \cdot (q + \gamma H_1)$$

At the base ($z = H$):

$$p'_{a,2}(H) = K_a \cdot (q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w)$$

This gives a **trapezoidal** effective stress distribution in the submerged zone, but for the soil-grain contribution alone (without surcharge), we get a **triangular** part. Let's separate it:

**Sub-component 2a (rectangular, from surcharge transmitted into submerged zone):**

$$P_{2a} = K_a (q + \gamma H_1) \cdot H_w$$

Acts at mid-height of submerged zone: $\frac{H_w}{2}$ from the base of the wall.

**Sub-component 2b (triangular, from soil weight in submerged zone):**

$$P_{2b} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma' \cdot H_w^2$$

Acts at $\frac{H_w}{3}$ from the base of the wall.

---

### Step 4 — Component 3: Surcharge-Induced Active Thrust

The surcharge $q$ produces a **rectangular** active pressure of intensity $K_a q$ over the **entire height** $H$:

$$P_3 = K_a \cdot q \cdot H$$

Acts at mid-height: $\frac{H}{2}$ from the base.

> [!NOTE]
> This is actually **already partly included** in sub-component 2a (the rectangular part). To avoid double-counting, we must be careful in bookkeeping. The cleanest approach is shown in the next step.

---

### Step 5 — Clean Derivation: Total Active Thrust $P_a$

Let us redo this cleanly by splitting the soil + surcharge combined vertical stress into two zones.

**Zone I ($0 \le z \le H_1$, dry):** $\sigma_v = q + \gamma z$
**Zone II ($H_1 \le z \le H$, submerged):** $\sigma'_v = q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma'(z - H_1)$

The **effective** active pressure is $K_a \cdot \sigma'_v$. The **total** active pressure (effective + water) is $K_a \cdot \sigma'_v + \gamma_w (z - H_1)$ for $z \ge H_1$.

**Total force** is the integral:

$$P_a = \int_0^H p_a(z) \, dz$$

Computing piecewise:

**Part 1: Dry zone ($0 \le z \le H_1$) — only effective stress acts**

$$P_{a,\text{dry}} = \int_0^{H_1} K_a (q + \gamma z) \, dz$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{a,\text{dry}} &= K_a \left[ q z + \frac{\gamma z^2}{2} \right]_0^{H_1} \\[4pt]
                  &= K_a q H_1 + \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Part 2: Submerged zone — effective + pore water**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{a,\text{sub}} &= \int_{H_1}^{H} \left[ K_a \left(q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma'(z - H_1)\right) + \gamma_w (z - H_1) \right] dz
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $u = z - H_1$ (so $u$ goes from $0$ to $H_w$):

$$P_{a,\text{sub}} = \int_0^{H_w} \left[ K_a (q + \gamma H_1) + K_a \gamma' u + \gamma_w u \right] du$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{a,\text{sub}} &= K_a (q + \gamma H_1) H_w + \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^2 + \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Total active thrust:**

$$\boxed{P_a = K_a q H + K_a \gamma H_1^2/2 + K_a(q + \gamma H_1) H_w + \frac{1}{2}(K_a \gamma' + \gamma_w) H_w^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that $K_a q H = K_a q H_1 + K_a q H_w$. This is automatically split across the two zones by the integration, **avoiding double-counting**.

---

### Step 6 — Point of Application of Resultant $P_a$

To find $\bar{z}$ (distance from base), compute the **moment** of each force component about the base.

Let $M_a = P_a \cdot \bar{z}$ = sum of moments.

| Force | Magnitude | Distance from base | Moment |
|:-----:|:---------:|:------------------:|:------:|
| $K_a q H_1$ (rect., dry zone) | $K_a q H_1$ | $H - H_1/2$ | $K_a q H_1 (H - H_1/2)$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2$ (tri., dry zone) | $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2$ | $H - 2H_1/3$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 (H - 2H_1/3)$ |
| $K_a(q + \gamma H_1) H_w$ (rect., sub zone) | $K_a(q + \gamma H_1) H_w$ | $H_w/2$ | $K_a(q + \gamma H_1) H_w^2/2$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^2$ (tri. soil, sub) | $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^2$ | $H_w/3$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^3/3$ |
| $\tfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2$ (tri. water, sub) | $\tfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2$ | $H_w/3$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^3/3$ |

$$\bar{z} = \frac{\sum \text{Moments}}{\sum \text{Forces}} = \frac{M_a}{P_a}$$

---

### Step 7 — Numerical Sanity Check (Worked Out)

**Given:** $H = 8$ m, $H_1 = 2$ m, $H_w = 6$ m, $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³, $q = 30$ kN/m², $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³.

**Step 7a — Compute coefficients:**

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 7b — Effective unit weight:**

$$\gamma' = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m³}$$

**Step 7c — Substituting into the $P_a$ formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{1}{3} \times 30 \times 8 + \frac{1}{3} \times 17 \times \frac{2^2}{2} \\
    &\quad + \frac{1}{3}(30 + 17 \times 2) \times 6 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3} \times 9.19 + 9.81\right) \times 6^2 \\[4pt]
    &= 80 + \frac{68}{3} + \frac{1}{3}(64)(6) + \frac{1}{2}(3.063 + 9.81)(36) \\[4pt]
    &= 80 + 22.67 + 128 + \frac{1}{2}(12.873)(36) \\[4pt]
    &= 80 + 22.67 + 128 + 231.72 \\[4pt]
    &= 462.39 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **pore water pressure contribution** alone is $\frac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2 = 0.5 \times 9.81 \times 36 = 176.58$ kN/m — that is **over 38%** of the total active thrust! This is why KTU examiners love water-table problems.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Water Table on Active Earth Pressure (All Cases)

| S. No. | Water Table Position | Effective Unit Weight Used | Pore Water Pressure on Wall? | Effective Vertical Stress at Base | Total Active Pressure at Base |
|:------:|:---------------------|:--------------------------:|:---------------------------:|:---------------------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| 1 | Above ground surface (flooded) | $\gamma'$ throughout | Yes (from $z=0$) | $q + \gamma' H$ | $K_a(q + \gamma' H) + \gamma_w H$ |
| 2 | At ground surface ($H_1 = 0$) | $\gamma'$ throughout | Yes (triangular, $0$ to $\gamma_w H$) | $q + \gamma' H$ | $K_a(q + \gamma' H) + \gamma_w H$ |
| 3 | Intermediate ($0 < H_1 < H$) | $\gamma$ in top, $\gamma'$ below | Yes below WT only | $q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w$ | $K_a(q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w) + \gamma_w H_w$ |
| 4 | At base ($H_1 = H$) | $\gamma$ throughout | No | $q + \gamma H$ | $K_a(q + \gamma H)$ |
| 5 | Below base (no water effect) | $\gamma$ throughout | No | $q + \gamma H$ | $K_a(q + \gamma H)$ |
| 6 | Capillary saturation (apparent cohesion effect) | $\gamma$ in moist, $\gamma'$ below WT | Yes below WT, plus apparent cohesion above | Special case | Special case |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Combined Surcharge + Water Table Effect on Total Force

| Component | Magnitude | Acts at (from base) | Type of Distribution |
|:---------:|:---------:|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|
| Surcharge on entire wall | $K_a \cdot q \cdot H$ | $H/2$ | Rectangular |
| Self-weight in dry zone (triangle) | $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2$ | $H - 2H_1/3$ | Triangular |
| (Surcharge + dry soil) rectangular part in sub zone | $K_a (q + \gamma H_1) H_w$ | $H_w/2$ | Rectangular |
| Submerged soil (triangle) | $\tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^2$ | $H_w/3$ | Triangular |
| Pore water (triangle) | $\tfrac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2$ | $H_w/3$ | Triangular |
| **Total** | $P_a$ (sum) | $\bar{z} = \sum M_i / P_a$ | Complex composite |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Earth Pressure Under Surcharge and Water Table

The following block-level functional architecture maps the **interactions** between surcharge loads, water table position, effective stress, and the resulting earth pressure. This is the *systems-level* view that KTU examiners love for "explain with a neat sketch" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Loading System]:::input --> B[Uniform Surcharge q kN per m2]
    A --> C[Line Load]
    A --> D[Point Load]
    A --> E[Strip Footing Load]

    F[Backfill Soil Domain]:::soil --> G[Dry Moist Zone]
    F --> H[Submerged Zone]
    F --> I[Capillary Zone]

    J[Water Table Position]:::water --> K[At Surface H1 equals 0]
    J --> L[Intermediate 0 less than H1 less than H]
    J --> M[At Base H1 equals H]
    J --> N[Below Base]

    G --> O[Unit Weight gamma]
    H --> P[Effective Unit Weight gamma prime equals gamma sat minus gamma w]
    I --> Q[Apparent Cohesion Effect]

    B --> R[Lateral Stress Computation K times sigma v]
    C --> R
    D --> R
    E --> R

    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R

    R --> S[Active Pressure Ka times sigma prime v]
    R --> T[Passive Pressure Kp times sigma prime v]

    S --> U[Pore Water Pressure u equals gamma w times h w]
    T --> U

    U --> V[Total Lateral Pressure on Wall]
    V --> W[Total Active Thrust Pa in kN per m]
    V --> X[Total Passive Thrust Pp in kN per m]

    W --> Y[Point of Application z bar equals Sum M divided by Sum P]
    X --> Y

    Y --> Z[Design Input for Retaining Wall]:::output

    classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef soil fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef water fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Component Build-up Sequence

This shows the **step-by-step build-up** of the pressure diagram as we add surcharge and water table effects one by one.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Dry Backfill No Surcharge] --> P1[Triangular Distribution: Pa equals half Ka gamma H squared]
    P1 --> S2[Stage 2: Add Surcharge q]
    S2 --> P2[Add Rectangular Component: Delta Pa equals Ka q H]
    P2 --> S3[Stage 3: Lower Water Table to H1]
    S3 --> P3[Replace gamma with gamma prime in Submerged Zone]
    P3 --> S4[Stage 4: Add Pore Water Pressure]
    S4 --> P4[Add Triangular Component: half gamma w Hw squared]
    P4 --> S5[Stage 5: Full Active Thrust Pa Computed]
    S5 --> P5[Locate Resultant at z bar from base]

    classDef stage fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pres fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 stage
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 pres
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Water Table Cases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Retaining Wall Problem] --> Q1{Is Surcharge q Specified?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| AddSurcharge[Add K times q contribution]
    Q1 -->|No| SkipSurcharge[Skip Surcharge Term]

    AddSurcharge --> Q2{Where is Water Table?}
    SkipSurcharge --> Q2

    Q2 -->|At Surface H1=0| CaseA[Use gamma prime for entire backfill]
    Q2 -->|Intermediate 0 less than H1 less than H| CaseB[Use gamma above WT and gamma prime below WT]
    Q2 -->|At Base H1=H| CaseC[Use gamma for entire backfill, No pore water]
    Q2 -->|Below Base| CaseD[No water effect, Dry analysis only]

    CaseA --> Output1[Total Pa equals Ka q plus gamma prime H plus gamma w H]
    CaseB --> Output2[Use Master Formula for Intermediate WT]
    CaseC --> Output3[Pa equals Ka q plus gamma H only]
    CaseD --> Output4[Pa equals Ka q plus gamma H only]
```

---

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Composite Pressure Diagram (Exam-Ready)

```
Pressure (kN/m²)
   ↑
   |
   |  ┌────────────────────────┐  ← p₀ = Kₐ·q  (rectangular from surcharge)
   |  │                        │
   |  │      triangular        │  ← dry zone soil (γ used)
   |  │        (γ)             │
   |  │                        │
   |  └────────────────────────┘
   |  ╔════════════════════════╗  ← Water Table (depth H₁)
   |  ║                        ║
   |  ║   Triangular           ║  ← soil contribution (γ′ = γ_sat − γ_w)
   |  ║    (γ′)                ║
   |  ║                        ║
   |  ║   + Triangular         ║  ← hydrostatic pore pressure (γ_w)
   |  ║     water              ║
   |  ║                        ║
   |  ╚════════════════════════╝
   |____________________________→ depth z
            H
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Worked Example Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inp[Input: H, H₁, φ, γ, γ_sat, q, γ_w] --> CalcK[Calculate Ka equals 1 minus sin phi over 1 plus sin phi]
    CalcK --> Calcgam[Calculate gamma prime equals gamma sat minus gamma w]
    Calcgam --> DryF[Force in Dry Zone: P1 equals half Ka gamma H1 squared]
    DryF --> SubR[Rectangular Sub Force: P2a equals Ka times q plus gamma H1 times Hw]
    SubR --> SubT1[Triangular Sub Soil: P2b equals half Ka gamma prime Hw squared]
    SubT1 --> SubT2[Triangular Sub Water: P2c equals half gamma w Hw squared]
    SubT2 --> SumP[Total Pa equals P1 plus P2a plus P2b plus P2c]
    SumP --> Mom[Compute Moments Mi equals Pi times zi for each component]
    Mom --> Zbar[Compute z bar equals Sum Mi divided by Sum Pi]
    Zbar --> Out[Final Output: Total Active Thrust Pa in kN per m and Point of Application z bar from base]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams above use **alphanumeric node identifiers** prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `Inp`, `Out`) and contain **no markdown formatting inside labels** — fully compliant with the Mermaid compilation safeguards specified in the KTU engine protocol.
