<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Explain the effect of water table on the active earth pressure behind a retaining wall. How is the effective unit weight of soil used in the analysis when the water table is at an intermediate depth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When the water table lies within the backfill at an intermediate depth $H_1$ from the ground surface, the active earth pressure distribution is **modified in two ways**:

1. **Above the water table** (depth $0 \le z \le H_1$): The soil is moist and the bulk (or moist) unit weight $\gamma$ is used to compute the vertical effective stress.

2. **Below the water table** (depth $H_1 \le z \le H$): The soil grains experience **buoyancy**, so the effective (submerged) unit weight $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ is used in place of $\gamma_{sat}$. In addition, **pore water pressure** $u = \gamma_w \cdot (z - H_1)$ acts on the wall, which must be added to the effective lateral stress to obtain the total lateral pressure.

The total active pressure at the base becomes:

$$p_{a,H} = K_a \left(q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w\right) + \gamma_w H_w$$

This shows that neglecting the water table leads to **underestimation** of active thrust — a critical design error.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module Test 1]`

**A uniform surcharge of intensity $q$ acts on the ground surface behind a retaining wall of height $H$. Show how the active earth pressure is modified due to the surcharge using Rankine's theory. Where does the additional thrust act?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

By Rankine's theory, the surcharge $q$ produces a **uniformly distributed additional active pressure** of intensity:

$$\Delta p_a = K_a \cdot q \quad \text{(kN/m²)}$$

The total **additional horizontal thrust** acting on the wall due to the surcharge is:

$$\Delta P_a = K_a \cdot q \cdot H \quad \text{(kN/m)}$$

This rectangular pressure diagram is **superimposed** on the triangular pressure diagram due to the self-weight of the backfill. The additional thrust due to the surcharge acts **horizontally at the mid-height of the wall**, i.e., at a distance of $H/2$ above the base. The direction of action is **towards the wall** (i.e., pushing the wall outward).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A cantilever retaining wall has a vertical back and is 8 m high. The backfill is horizontal and carries a uniform surcharge of 30 kN/m². The water table is located at a depth of 2 m below the ground surface. The backfill soil has the following properties: $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³ (moist), $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³. Use $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. Determine using Rankine's theory:**

**(a)** The total active earth pressure at the base of the wall and the **total active thrust** per metre run of the wall.

**(b)** The **point of application** of the resultant active thrust from the base of the wall.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Given data:**
- $H = 8$ m
- $H_1 = 2$ m (depth of water table from top)
- $H_w = H - H_1 = 6$ m (submerged depth)
- $q = 30$ kN/m²
- $\phi = 30°$ → $K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \frac{1}{3}$
- $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³

**Step 1 — Effective unit weight in submerged zone:**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m³}$$

**Step 2 — Active pressure at the base (full expression):**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{a,H} &= K_a(q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w) + \gamma_w H_w \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{3}(30 + 17 \times 2 + 9.19 \times 6) + 9.81 \times 6 \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{3}(30 + 34 + 55.14) + 58.86 \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{3}(119.14) + 58.86 \\[4pt]
        &= 39.71 + 58.86 \\[4pt]
        &= 98.57 \text{ kN/m²}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each force component for total active thrust:**

**Component 1 (Surcharge on entire wall — rectangular):**

$$P_1 = K_a \cdot q \cdot H = \frac{1}{3} \times 30 \times 8 = 80.00 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 2 (Triangular, dry zone soil self-weight):**

$$P_2 = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times 17 \times 2^2 = 11.33 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 3 (Rectangular, surcharge + dry soil transmitted into submerged zone):**

$$P_3 = K_a (q + \gamma H_1) H_w = \frac{1}{3} \times (30 + 34) \times 6 = \frac{1}{3} \times 64 \times 6 = 128.00 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 4 (Triangular, submerged soil — effective stress):**

$$P_4 = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times 9.19 \times 36 = 55.14 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 5 (Triangular, pore water pressure):**

$$P_5 = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 36 = 176.58 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total active thrust:**

$$P_a = 80.00 + 11.33 + 128.00 + 55.14 + 176.58 = 451.05 \text{ kN/m}$$

$$\boxed{P_a \approx 451.05 \text{ kN/m} \quad \text{and} \quad p_{a,H} \approx 98.57 \text{ kN/m²}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Compute the moment of each force about the base:**

| Force | Magnitude (kN/m) | Distance from Base (m) | Moment about Base (kN·m/m) |
|:-----:|:----------------:|:----------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| $P_1$ (surcharge rect., full height) | 80.00 | $H/2 = 4.00$ | $320.00$ |
| $P_2$ (dry zone triangle) | 11.33 | $H - 2H_1/3 = 8 - 4/3 = 6.67$ | $75.57$ |
| $P_3$ (rect. in sub zone) | 128.00 | $H_w/2 = 3.00$ | $384.00$ |
| $P_4$ (tri. soil in sub zone) | 55.14 | $H_w/3 = 2.00$ | $110.28$ |
| $P_5$ (tri. water in sub zone) | 176.58 | $H_w/3 = 2.00$ | $353.16$ |
| **Total** | **451.05** | — | **$\sum M = 1243.01$** |

**Point of application:**

$$\bar{z} = \frac{\sum M}{P_a} = \frac{1243.01}{451.05} = 2.756 \text{ m from base}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{z} \approx 2.76 \text{ m from the base of the wall}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners typically accept answers to **2 decimal places**. A common alternative is to express the location as a **fraction of $H$**: $\bar{z}/H = 2.756/8 = 0.345$, i.e., the resultant acts at about **34.5% of the wall height** from the base.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Choice Question]`

**A retaining wall is 6 m high with a vertical back. The backfill is cohesionless and horizontal, with $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. The water table stands at 1.5 m below the ground surface. There is a uniform surcharge of 20 kN/m² on the ground surface. Take $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³ (assume simpler value). Using Rankine's theory, determine:**

**(a)** The **magnitude and distribution** of the active earth pressure on the wall, and the **total active thrust** per metre length.

**(b)** If a **drainage system** is provided such that the water table is eliminated (i.e., backfill remains dry), what is the **percentage reduction** in the total active thrust? Comment on the design significance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Active coefficient:**

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 35°}{1 + \sin 35°} = \frac{1 - 0.5736}{1 + 0.5736} = \frac{0.4264}{1.5736} = 0.2710$$

**Step 2 — Effective unit weight:**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ kN/m³}$$

**Step 3 — Geometry:**
- $H = 6$ m, $H_1 = 1.5$ m, $H_w = 4.5$ m

**Step 4 — Active pressure at key depths:**

At $z = 0$:
$$p_{a,0} = K_a \cdot q = 0.2710 \times 20 = 5.42 \text{ kN/m²}$$

At $z = H_1 = 1.5$ m (just above WT):
$$p_{a,1.5} = K_a (q + \gamma H_1) = 0.2710 \times (20 + 18 \times 1.5) = 0.2710 \times 47 = 12.74 \text{ kN/m²}$$

At $z = H = 6$ m (just below WT at base):
$$p_{a,H} = K_a (q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w) + \gamma_w H_w$$
$$= 0.2710 \times (20 + 27 + 10 \times 4.5) + 10 \times 4.5$$
$$= 0.2710 \times 92 + 45 = 24.93 + 45 = 69.93 \text{ kN/m²}$$

**Step 5 — Total active thrust (using component method):**

**Component 1 (Surcharge rectangular over full wall):**
$$P_1 = K_a q H = 0.2710 \times 20 \times 6 = 32.52 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 2 (Dry zone triangle):**
$$P_2 = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 = 0.5 \times 0.2710 \times 18 \times 1.5^2 = 5.49 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 3 (Rectangular, transmitted to sub zone):**
$$P_3 = K_a (q + \gamma H_1) H_w = 0.2710 \times 47 \times 4.5 = 57.34 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 4 (Triangular, submerged soil):**
$$P_4 = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma' H_w^2 = 0.5 \times 0.2710 \times 10 \times 4.5^2 = 27.44 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Component 5 (Triangular, pore water):**
$$P_5 = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2 = 0.5 \times 10 \times 4.5^2 = 101.25 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Total active thrust:**

$$P_a = 32.52 + 5.49 + 57.34 + 27.44 + 101.25 = 224.04 \text{ kN/m}$$

$$\boxed{P_a \approx 224.04 \text{ kN/m}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**With drainage (water table removed):**

Now $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ throughout, no pore water pressure.

$$P_{a,\text{dry}} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 + K_a q H$$
$$= 0.5 \times 0.2710 \times 18 \times 36 + 0.2710 \times 20 \times 6$$
$$= 87.81 + 32.52 = 120.33 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Percentage reduction in active thrust:**

$$\Delta\% = \frac{P_a - P_{a,\text{dry}}}{P_a} \times 100 = \frac{224.04 - 120.33}{224.04} \times 100$$

$$\Delta\% = \frac{103.71}{224.04} \times 100 = 46.29\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Reduction} \approx 46.29\%}$$

**Design Significance:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** A properly designed drainage system that prevents water accumulation in the backfill can reduce the active thrust on the wall by nearly **46%** in this case. This is an enormous saving in design — the wall cross-section, foundation size, and reinforcement can all be substantially reduced. **Drainage is therefore not just a precautionary measure but a primary design tool** in retaining wall engineering. In real KTU field practice, providing a graded filter and weep holes at the base of the wall is **standard practice** and is often credited in design for this exact reason.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pore water pressure term** $\frac{1}{2} \gamma_w H_w^2$ in the submerged zone — this alone can cost **3 marks**.
> 2. **Using $\gamma_{sat}$ instead of $\gamma'$** in the submerged zone for soil contribution — another **2 marks** lost.
> 3. **Confusing the location of point of application** for the surcharge component — it acts at $H/2$, NOT at the top.
> 4. **Sign error** in the water pressure — it always **adds** to the lateral thrust, never subtracts.
> 5. **Double counting the surcharge** in the dry and submerged zones — careful integration (or the component method shown above) avoids this trap.
> 6. **Forgetting the rectangular part of the surcharge transmitted into the submerged zone** — this is a frequent KTU exam trap.
> 7. **Numerical arithmetic mistakes** with $\gamma_w$ — always write $\gamma_w$ value explicitly at the start of the solution.
> 8. **Not specifying units** at intermediate steps — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit** in long numericals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 1, Topic: Influence of Surcharge and Water Table on Earth Pressure**

✅ **Surcharge Effect:**
- Adds a **uniformly distributed pressure** of $K_a \cdot q$ on the entire wall.
- Total additional thrust: $K_a \cdot q \cdot H$, acts at **mid-height** ($H/2$ from base).
- Always **adds** to active pressure; always **adds** to passive pressure.

✅ **Water Table Effect — Above WT (Dry zone):**
- Use bulk unit weight $\gamma$.
- No pore water pressure on wall.

✅ **Water Table Effect — Below WT (Submerged zone):**
- Use **effective** (buoyant) unit weight: $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$.
- Add **hydrostatic pore water pressure**: $u = \gamma_w \cdot h_w$.

✅ **Master Formula for Active Pressure at Base (Intermediate WT):**
$$p_{a,H} = K_a(q + \gamma H_1 + \gamma' H_w) + \gamma_w H_w$$

✅ **Master Formula for Total Active Thrust:**
$$P_a = K_a q H + \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 + K_a(q + \gamma H_1) H_w + \tfrac{1}{2}(K_a \gamma' + \gamma_w) H_w^2$$

✅ **Special Cases to Remember:**
- $H_1 = 0$ (WT at surface): all submerged, full hydrostatic pressure.
- $H_1 = H$ (WT at base): no WT effect, dry analysis only.
- WT below base: no water effect whatsoever.

✅ **Active Coefficient (Rankine):** $K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$

✅ **Passive Coefficient (Rankine):** $K_p = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi}$

✅ **Point of Application Logic:**
- Triangular distribution → centroid at $1/3$ from the larger end.
- Rectangular distribution → centroid at mid-height.
- Combined result: use moment summation, $\bar{z} = \sum M_i / \sum P_i$.

✅ **KTU's Favourite Numerical Values:**
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (standard) or $10$ kN/m³ (simpler).
- $\phi$ values typically $30°$ or $35°$ in KTU problems.
- $K_a$ for $\phi = 30°$ is exactly $1/3$ — a frequently tested value.
- $K_a$ for $\phi = 35°$ is approximately $0.271$.

✅ **Design Insight:** Proper drainage can reduce active thrust by **40–50%** — making it one of the most cost-effective design features in retaining wall engineering.

✅ **The Three-Force-Component Check:** Every KTU answer should clearly identify the surcharge force, the soil self-weight force, and the pore water force — labelled separately with their points of application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth Pressure with Layered Backfill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Earth Pressure with Layered Backfill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Layered Backfill** refers to a soil-retaining situation in which the material behind a retaining wall is stratified into two or more horizontal layers possessing distinct engineering properties — primarily differing in **unit weight (γ)**, **angle of internal friction (φ)**, and **cohesion (c)**.

The **active earth pressure** in such a stratified system is computed by applying **Rankine's Theory** (or Coulomb's Wedge Theory) **layer-by-layer**, because the lateral earth pressure coefficient (Ka or Kp) is a function of φ, which generally changes at the interface between two strata. As a direct consequence of this discontinuity, the **lateral pressure diagram exhibits a sudden jump (discontinuity)** at every layer interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> The governing assumption is that the **failure surface is planar** and inclined at an angle governed by Rankine's critical state. The Rankine active state is mobilized when the wall yields sufficiently outward to allow the soil mass to reach its limit equilibrium along this failure plane.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bookshelf pressed against a stack of differently textured materials — say, a layer of dry sand on top of wet clay. If you slowly push the bookshelf backward (simulating wall movement), the dry sand layer responds with a different "push-back" force than the wet clay beneath it. The reason is that **dry sand grains roll and slide over each other easily** (low resistance ⇒ high pressure), whereas **wet clay sticks together** (high resistance ⇒ low pressure). The bookshelf feels an irregular, **stepped push** rather than a smooth uniform one — this is exactly what happens to a retaining wall with layered backfill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Scenarios:**
> - Highway cuts through alternating strata of sand and clay.
> - Backfill behind abutments constructed in stages using locally available soils of differing gradation.
> - Dam abutments where the natural slope consists of weathered rock overlain by residual soil.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of soil, γ:** typically **16 – 22 kN/m³**
- **Angle of internal friction, φ:** typically **0° – 45°**
- **Cohesion, c:** typically **0 – 50 kPa** for soils
- **Active earth pressure coefficient, Ka:** ranges from **0.22 to 1.00** for cohesionless soils
- **Passive earth pressure coefficient, Kp:** ranges from **1.00 to ∞** as φ → 0

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Earth Pressure Changes at Layer Interfaces

When a retaining wall is held fixed and the backfill is horizontally stratified, the **vertical effective stress (σv)** at any depth is obtained by **simple summation of overburden** (since each layer transmits load downward):

$$\sigma_v = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \cdot \Delta z_i$$

However, the **lateral pressure** is **not** the same proportion of σv in every layer, because the proportion itself — namely the earth-pressure coefficient — depends on φ:

- A **loose sand** with φ ≈ 30° gives **Ka ≈ 0.333** (relatively low lateral pressure).
- A **dense sand** with φ ≈ 40° gives **Ka ≈ 0.217** (even lower lateral pressure).
- A **soft clay** with φ ≈ 10° gives **Ka ≈ 0.704** (much higher lateral pressure).

Therefore, at the interface between a dense sand and a soft clay, the lateral pressure will experience a **sudden upward jump**, even though the vertical stress changes continuously.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for solving KTU 2024 problems on layered backfill. Memorize these before entering the exam hall.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Applicable To** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rankine Ka (horizontal backfill) | $K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45 - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ | Cohesionless layer |
| 2 | Rankine Kp (horizontal backfill) | $K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1+\sin\phi}{1-\sin\phi}$ | Cohesionless layer |
| 3 | Rankine Ka (inclined backfill β) | $K_a = \cos\beta \,\dfrac{\cos\beta - \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}{\cos\beta + \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}$ | Cohesionless, sloping |
| 4 | Active pressure (cohesionless, layer i) | $p_a = K_{a,i}\,\sigma_v$ | Layer i, c = 0 |
| 5 | Active pressure (c–φ soil, layer i) | $p_a = K_{a,i}\,\sigma_v - 2c_i\sqrt{K_{a,i}}$ | Layer i, c–φ |
| 6 | Passive pressure (cohesionless, layer i) | $p_p = K_{p,i}\,\sigma_v$ | Layer i, c = 0 |
| 7 | Passive pressure (c–φ soil, layer i) | $p_p = K_{p,i}\,\sigma_v + 2c_i\sqrt{K_{p,i}}$ | Layer i, c–φ |
| 8 | Vertical stress at depth z | $\sigma_v = \sum \gamma_i \Delta z_i$ | Layered backfill |
| 9 | Total active thrust (per unit length) | $P_a = \int_0^H p_a(z)\,dz$ | Layered system |
| 10 | Depth of tension crack | $z_0 = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma \sqrt{K_a}}$ | c–φ soil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions at the Interface (z = H1):**
> - **Vertical stress is continuous:** $\sigma_v$ just above = $\sigma_v$ just below
> - **Lateral pressure is discontinuous** because $K_{a,1} \neq K_{a,2}$ in general
> - **Failure plane angle changes** because it is governed by $\phi$ of the layer through which it passes

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This theory is the foundation for:
1. **Design of basement walls** in urban areas where natural subsoil consists of layered alluvium.
2. **Bridge abutment design** where approach embankments sit atop native stratified deposits.
3. **Sheet-pile bulkheads** in harbours where marine clay underlies sand fill.
4. **Stability analysis of anchored bulkheads** in multi-layer ground.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students correctly identify the **jump in pressure** at the interface — this is a 7-marker hot spot every year.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Active Earth Pressure in a Two-Layered Cohesionless Backfill (Rankine's Theory)

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a smooth vertical retaining wall AB of total height $H = H_1 + H_2$, retaining a horizontal backfill consisting of two distinct cohesionless soil layers:

- **Layer 1 (top):** thickness $H_1$, unit weight $\gamma_1$, friction angle $\phi_1$
- **Layer 2 (bottom):** thickness $H_2$, unit weight $\gamma_2$, friction angle $\phi_2$

**Assumptions:**
1. The wall is smooth and vertical, hence Rankine's conditions are valid.
2. The backfill surface is horizontal.
3. Each soil layer is homogeneous and isotropic within itself.
4. The wall yields sufficiently to mobilize the active state.

**Step 1 — Active Earth Pressure Coefficient of Each Layer**

For Layer 1 (Rankine's, horizontal backfill):

$$K_{a1} = \tan^2\!\left(45 - \frac{\phi_1}{2}\right)$$

For Layer 2:

$$K_{a2} = \tan^2\!\left(45 - \frac{\phi_2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Active Pressure at Depth z in Layer 1**

At a depth $z$ measured from the top of the wall, the vertical stress is:

$$\sigma_v = \gamma_1 z$$

The active lateral pressure is:

$$p_a = K_{a1} \cdot \sigma_v = K_{a1} \cdot \gamma_1 z$$

This is a **linear function of z**, increasing from 0 at the top to $K_{a1}\gamma_1 H_1$ at the interface.

**Step 3 — Active Pressure at the Interface**

At the bottom of Layer 1 (i.e., at depth $z = H_1$):

$$p_{a,\text{top of L1}} = K_{a1}\,\gamma_1 H_1$$

The vertical stress remains the same just below the interface (continuity of overburden):

$$\sigma_v = \gamma_1 H_1$$

But the coefficient jumps to $K_{a2}$, so:

$$p_{a,\text{top of L2}} = K_{a2}\,\gamma_1 H_1$$

**Step 4 — Active Pressure at Depth z' in Layer 2**

Let $z'$ be the depth measured downward from the top of Layer 2. The total vertical stress is:

$$\sigma_v = \gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 z'$$

The active pressure is:

$$p_a = K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 z')$$

At the bottom of the wall ($z' = H_2$):

$$p_{a,\text{bottom}} = K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 H_2)$$

**Step 5 — Pressure Diagram Geometry**

The lateral pressure diagram is **piecewise linear**:
- A **triangle** in Layer 1, growing from 0 to $K_{a1}\gamma_1 H_1$.
- A **trapezoid** in Layer 2, growing from $K_{a2}\gamma_1 H_1$ to $K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 H_2)$.

The discontinuity (jump) at the interface is:

$$\Delta p_a = (K_{a2} - K_{a1})\,\gamma_1 H_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If $K_{a2} > K_{a1}$ (i.e., Layer 2 is looser/weaker than Layer 1), the pressure **jumps upward** at the interface. If $K_{a2} < K_{a1}$, it **drops** — a critical observation for design.

**Step 6 — Total Active Thrust per Unit Length**

$$P_a = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}K_{a1}\gamma_1 H_1^2}_{\text{Layer 1 (triangle)}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}(K_{a2}\gamma_1 H_1 + K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 H_2))H_2}_{\text{Layer 2 (trapezoid)}}$$

Simplifying the Layer 2 term:

$$P_{a,2} = \frac{K_{a2}H_2}{2}\bigl(2\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 H_2\bigr)$$

**Step 7 — Point of Application of P_a**

The centroid of the triangular pressure distribution in Layer 1 lies at $\dfrac{H_1}{3}$ from the bottom of Layer 1 (i.e., at $H_1 - \dfrac{H_1}{3} = \dfrac{2H_1}{3}$ from the top of the wall).

For the trapezoidal distribution in Layer 2, the centroid measured from the top of Layer 2 is:

$$\bar{z}' = \frac{H_2\,(2p_{\text{top}} + p_{\text{bottom}})}{3\,(p_{\text{top}} + p_{\text{bottom}})}$$

where $p_{\text{top}} = K_{a2}\gamma_1 H_1$ and $p_{\text{bottom}} = K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 H_2)$.

The location of the resultant $P_a$ from the top of the wall is found by taking moments about that point:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{P_{a,1}\,\bar{z}_1 + P_{a,2}\,\bar{z}_2}{P_a}$$

---

### 3.2 Extension to Cohesive-Frictional (c–φ) Layered Soils

When one or more layers possess cohesion, Rankine's active pressure at depth z becomes:

$$p_a = K_a \sigma_v - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$$

For a two-layer system where Layer 1 is cohesionless and Layer 2 has cohesion $c_2$:

- In Layer 1: $p_a = K_{a1}\gamma_1 z$
- In Layer 2: $p_a = K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 z') - 2c_2\sqrt{K_{a2}}$

**Depth of Tension Zone in Layer 2:**

Setting $p_a = 0$ in Layer 2:

$$0 = K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 z_0) - 2c_2\sqrt{K_{a2}}$$

Solving for $z_0$:

$$z_0 = \frac{2c_2\sqrt{K_{a2}} - K_{a2}\gamma_1 H_1}{K_{a2}\gamma_2} = \frac{2c_2}{K_{a2}^{1/2}\gamma_2} - \frac{\gamma_1 H_1}{\gamma_2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $z_0 < 0$ (i.e., $2c_2 < K_{a2}^{1/2}\gamma_1 H_1$), then the **entire Layer 2 is in compression** and no tension crack develops. This is a common pitfall in numerical problems.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Pressure Computation

**Given:** Wall height $H = 8$ m; backfill horizontal, two layers:
- Layer 1: $H_1 = 4$ m, $\gamma_1 = 17\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $\phi_1 = 30°$
- Layer 2: $H_2 = 4$ m, $\gamma_2 = 19\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $\phi_2 = 35°$

**Step 1 — Compute Ka values:**

$$K_{a1} = \tan^2(45 - 15) = \tan^2(30°) = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$

$$K_{a2} = \tan^2(45 - 17.5) = \tan^2(27.5°)$$

$$\tan(27.5°) = 0.5206 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_{a2} = 0.2710$$

**Step 2 — Pressures at Key Depths:**

At $z = 0$: $p_a = 0$

At $z = 4$ m (bottom of Layer 1): $p_a = 0.333 \times 17 \times 4 = 22.67\,\text{kPa}$

At $z = 4^+$ m (top of Layer 2): $p_a = 0.2710 \times 17 \times 4 = 18.43\,\text{kPa}$

At $z = 8$ m (base): $p_a = 0.2710 \times (17 \times 4 + 19 \times 4) = 0.2710 \times 144 = 39.02\,\text{kPa}$

**Step 3 — Total Active Thrust:**

$$P_{a,1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 22.67 \times 4 = 45.34\,\text{kN/m}$$

$$P_{a,2} = \frac{1}{2}(18.43 + 39.02) \times 4 = 114.90\,\text{kN/m}$$

$$P_a = 45.34 + 114.90 = 160.24\,\text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Point of Application:**

$\bar{z}_1$ from top of wall = $\dfrac{2 \times 4}{3} = 2.67$ m

For the trapezoid in Layer 2, centroid from top of Layer 2:

$$\bar{z}' = \frac{4\,(2 \times 18.43 + 39.02)}{3\,(18.43 + 39.02)} = \frac{4 \times 75.88}{3 \times 57.45} = 1.76\,\text{m}$$

So $\bar{z}_2$ from top of wall = $4 + 1.76 = 5.76$ m.

Resultant location from top:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{45.34 \times 2.67 + 114.90 \times 5.76}{160.24} = \frac{121.06 + 661.82}{160.24} = \frac{782.88}{160.24} = 4.89\,\text{m from top}$$

Or equivalently, $\bar{z} = 8 - 4.89 = 3.11$ m **from the base**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Rankine vs. Coulomb for Layered Backfill

| **Criterion** | **Rankine's Method** | **Coulomb's Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Wall friction (δ) | Cannot be included (smooth wall) | Can be included |
| Wall inclination (α) | Limited to vertical walls | Arbitrary wall batter |
| Backfill slope (β) | Possible (modified formula) | Naturally handled |
| Layered backfill | Straightforward (apply per layer) | Requires trial wedge summing weights layer-by-layer |
| Failure surface | Planar, fixed by φ of the layer | Planar, but its angle is found by maximizing Pa |
| Suited to KTU problems | **Yes — preferred** | Less common in KTU 2024 |
| Accuracy for layered system | Good for horizontal backfill | Better for general geometry |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Layered-Backfill Earth-Pressure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Retaining Wall Geometry] --> B[Identify Number of Layers n]
    B --> C[For Each Layer i: Read γ_i, φ_i, c_i, H_i]
    C --> D[Compute K_ai using Rankine or Coulomb]
    D --> E[Compute Vertical Stress σv at Interface]
    E --> F[Compute Active Pressure pa at Top of Layer]
    F --> G[Compute Active Pressure pa at Bottom of Layer]
    G --> H{More Layers Below?}
    H -- Yes --> C
    H -- No --> I[Check for Tension Cracks in Cohesive Layers]
    I --> J[Construct Pressure Diagram]
    J --> K[Integrate Diagram to get Total Thrust Pa]
    K --> L[Locate Centroid to find Point of Application]
    L --> M[Output: Pa magnitude and line of action]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Representation of Pressure Distribution (ASCII Block Diagram)

```
Pressure (kPa)
  ↑
  │                                          ●  ← p_a(base) = K_a2(γ1H1 + γ2H2)
  │                                       ╱  │
  │                                    ╱     │
  │                                 ╱        │
  │                              ╱           │
  │                           ╱              │  Layer 2
  │                        ╱                 │  (K_a2)
  │                     ╱                    │
  │                  ╱                       │
  │  ←— jump in pressure at interface —→  ●  ← p_a(top of L2) = K_a2·γ1·H1
  │                                       ●  ← p_a(bot of L1) = K_a1·γ1·H1
  │                                    ╱     │
  │                                 ╱        │  Layer 1
  │                              ╱           │  (K_a1)
  │                           ╱              │
  │                        ╱                 │
  │                     ╱                    │
  │                  ╱                       │
  │               ╱                          │
  │            ╱                             │
  │         ╱                                │
  │      ╱                                   │
  │   ╱                                      │
  │ ●                                        │  ← top of wall (p_a = 0)
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┴──────→ Depth z (m)
       0       H1=4                       H=8
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow of Rankine's Hypothesis for Layered Soil

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Layer1[Layer 1: Top Stratum]
        L1A[Soil grains with φ1, γ1] --> L1B[Active wedge along plane at 45+φ1/2]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Layer 2: Bottom Stratum]
        L2A[Soil grains with φ2, γ2] --> L2B[Active wedge along plane at 45+φ2/2]
    end
    Layer1 --> Interface[Interface z=H1: σv continuous, pa jumps]
    Interface --> Layer2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The failure-plane angle $\theta_a = 45° + \phi/2$ from the horizontal **changes** as we pass from Layer 1 to Layer 2, because it depends on φ. The actual physical failure surface is a **kinked (broken) plane**, with the kink located at the layer interface.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
Define the term "active earth pressure" in a layered backfill. Explain why the lateral pressure diagram is discontinuous at the interface between two soil layers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Active earth pressure* is the lateral pressure exerted by the soil on a retaining structure when the structure yields **away from** the backfill sufficiently for the soil to reach its limit equilibrium (active state).

In a layered backfill, the lateral pressure at any depth is given by $p_a = K_{a,i}\,\sigma_v$, where $K_{a,i}$ depends on the friction angle $\phi_i$ of that particular layer. At the interface between two strata, the **vertical stress σv is continuous** (because overburden is continuous), but the **earth-pressure coefficient jumps** from $K_{a,1}$ to $K_{a,2}$ if $\phi_1 \neq \phi_2$. Consequently, the lateral pressure experiences a sudden **discontinuity (jump or drop)** at the interface, even though σv itself is unchanged.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
Write down the Rankine active earth pressure coefficient for a horizontal cohesionless backfill. How is it modified when the backfill is inclined at angle β to the horizontal?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a **horizontal cohesionless backfill**, the Rankine active earth pressure coefficient is:

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45 - \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

For an **inclined cohesionless backfill** (slope angle β with horizontal), the modified Rankine coefficient is:

$$K_a = \cos\beta \,\frac{\cos\beta - \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}{\cos\beta + \sqrt{\cos^2\beta - \cos^2\phi}}$$

This expression reduces to the horizontal case when β = 0, and the limit $\beta = \phi$ gives $K_a = 1$, indicating incipient failure of the slope itself.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between A and B)

---

#### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** With the aid of a sketch, derive the Rankine active earth pressure distribution for a two-layered cohesionless horizontal backfill retained by a smooth vertical wall. Clearly show the discontinuity at the interface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smooth vertical retaining wall 8 m high retains a horizontal backfill in two layers. The top layer (3 m thick) has $\gamma_1 = 17\,\text{kN/m}^3$ and $\phi_1 = 30°$. The bottom layer (5 m thick) has $\gamma_2 = 19\,\text{kN/m}^3$ and $\phi_2 = 35°$. Compute (i) the active pressure at the base of the wall, (ii) the total active thrust per unit length, and (iii) the point of application of the resultant from the base. Draw the pressure distribution diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. Key steps:
- Compute $K_{a1}$ and $K_{a2}$ from the respective φ values.
- Pressure in Layer 1: $p_a = K_{a1}\gamma_1 z$ (linear, triangular).
- Pressure in Layer 2: $p_a = K_{a2}(\gamma_1 H_1 + \gamma_2 z')$ (linear, trapezoidal).
- At the interface, the pressure jumps from $K_{a1}\gamma_1 H_1$ to $K_{a2}\gamma_1 H_1$.

**Sketch (Block Diagram):**

```
        Wall ↑                Pressure Diagram
        │   ┌─┐                
        │   │ │   ← H1         p_a(base) = K_a2(γ1H1+γ2H2)
        │   │ │                    ●
        │   │ │                 ╱  ╲
        │   │ │              ╱      ╲
        │   │ │           ╱          ╲
        │   │ │        ╱              ╲
        │   │ │     ╱                  ╲
   ─────┘   └─┘  ╱                      ●
   Soil         ●   ← jump at interface
              ╱ ╲  (K_a2·γ1·H1)        (K_a1·γ1·H1)
           ╱     ╲
        ╱          ╲
     ╱               ╲
  ●                    
  z=0                  
```

**Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Ka values:**

$$K_{a1} = \tan^2(45 - 15) = \tan^2(30°) = 0.333$$

$$K_{a2} = \tan^2(45 - 17.5) = \tan^2(27.5°) = 0.2710$$

**Step 2 — Pressures at Key Depths:**

| **Location** | **Depth (m)** | **Computation** | **Pressure (kPa)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top of wall | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Bottom of Layer 1 | 3 | $0.333 \times 17 \times 3$ | 16.99 |
| Top of Layer 2 (just below) | 3 | $0.2710 \times 17 \times 3$ | 13.82 |
| Base of wall | 8 | $0.2710 \times (17 \times 3 + 19 \times 5)$ | 39.49 |

**Step 3 — Total Active Thrust:**

Layer 1 (triangle):

$$P_{a,1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 16.99 \times 3 = 25.49\,\text{kN/m}$$

Layer 2 (trapezoid):

$$P_{a,2} = \frac{1}{2}(13.82 + 39.49) \times 5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 53.31 \times 5 = 133.28\,\text{kN/m}$$

Total:

$$P_a = 25.49 + 133.28 = 158.77\,\text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Point of Application:**

$\bar{z}_1$ from top of wall = $\frac{2}{3} \times 3 = 2.0$ m ⇒ from base = 6.0 m

For Layer 2 trapezoid, centroid from top of Layer 2:

$$\bar{z}' = \frac{5\,(2 \times 13.82 + 39.49)}{3\,(13.82 + 39.49)} = \frac{5 \times 67.13}{3 \times 53.31} = \frac{335.65}{159.93} = 2.10\,\text{m}$$

So $\bar{z}_2$ from top of wall = $3 + 2.10 = 5.10$ m ⇒ from base = 2.90 m.

Resultant location from base:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{25.49 \times 6.0 + 133.28 \times 2.90}{158.77} = \frac{152.94 + 386.51}{158.77} = \frac{539.45}{158.77} = 3.40\,\text{m from base}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Active pressure at base = **39.49 kPa**
- Total active thrust = **158.77 kN/m**
- Point of application = **3.40 m above the base**

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the assumptions and limitations of Coulomb's wedge theory when applied to a layered backfill. Compare it with Rankine's method for this case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retaining wall 6 m high with a vertical smooth back retains a horizontal backfill in two layers. Top layer (3 m): $\gamma_1 = 18\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $\phi_1 = 30°$, $c_1 = 0$. Bottom layer (3 m): $\gamma_2 = 20\,\text{kN/m}^3$, $\phi_2 = 20°$, $c_2 = 15\,\text{kPa}$. Determine (i) the active pressure at the base, (ii) the total active thrust per unit length, and (iii) the location of the resultant from the base. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Assumptions of Coulomb's Wedge Theory:**
1. The wall is rough (wall friction δ is included).
2. The backfill surface may be inclined or irregular.
3. The failure surface is planar (a major simplification).
4. The soil wedge is rigid and in limit equilibrium under three forces: weight, reaction on failure plane, and active thrust.
5. The active state corresponds to the wedge orientation that **maximizes** the active thrust.

**Application to Layered Backfill:**
- The trial wedge has a triangular (or composite) cross-section spanning all layers.
- The weight of the wedge is computed **layer-by-layer**, summing $\frac{1}{2}\gamma_i H_i^2 \cot\theta$ contributions and the composite regions.
- The friction angle $\phi$ of the soil on the **failure plane** is taken as the weighted value or, conservatively, the lowest φ encountered.

**Limitations:**
- The assumed planar failure is not strictly valid for c–φ soils.
- The angle of the failure plane must be found by trial (maximizing $P_a$).
- For layered systems, the failure plane may be **kinked** at the interface — Coulomb's method in its classical form does not capture this.
- Wall adhesion is not handled rigorously.

**Comparison with Rankine:**

Refer to the comparison matrix in **Section 3.4** above. Rankine is preferred by KTU examiners for its directness when the backfill is horizontal and the wall is vertical.

**Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Ka values:**

$$K_{a1} = \tan^2(30°) = 0.333$$

$$K_{a2} = \tan^2(35°) = (0.7002)^2 = 0.4904$$

$$\sqrt{K_{a2}} = 0.7002$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Stress at Interface:**

$$\sigma_v = \gamma_1 H_1 = 18 \times 3 = 54\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Active Pressure at Interface (Top of Layer 2):**

Layer 1 contribution at z = 3 m: $p_a = 0.333 \times 18 \times 3 = 18.0\,\text{kPa}$

Layer 2 contribution at z' = 0: $p_a = K_{a2}\sigma_v - 2c_2\sqrt{K_{a2}} = 0.4904 \times 54 - 2 \times 15 \times 0.7002$

$$p_a = 26.48 - 21.01 = 5.47\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Active Pressure at Base (z' = 3 m in Layer 2):**

$$\sigma_v = 54 + 20 \times 3 = 114\,\text{kPa}$$

$$p_a = 0.4904 \times 114 - 21.01 = 55.91 - 21.01 = 34.90\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Check for Tension Crack in Layer 2:**

Setting $p_a = 0$:

$$0 = 0.4904(54 + 20 z_0) - 21.01$$

$$0.4904 \times 20 \times z_0 = 21.01 - 26.48 = -5.47$$

$$z_0 = \frac{-5.47}{9.808} = -0.558\,\text{m}$$

Since $z_0 < 0$, **no tension crack develops** in Layer 2. The entire Layer 2 is in compression.

**Step 6 — Total Active Thrust:**

Layer 1 (triangle): $P_{a,1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 18.0 \times 3 = 27.0\,\text{kN/m}$

Layer 2 (trapezoid): $P_{a,2} = \frac{1}{2}(5.47 + 34.90) \times 3 = 60.56\,\text{kN/m}$

Total: $P_a = 27.0 + 60.56 = 87.56\,\text{kN/m}$

**Step 7 — Point of Application:**

$\bar{z}_1$ from top of wall = $\frac{2}{3} \times 3 = 2.0$ m ⇒ from base = 4.0 m

For Layer 2 trapezoid, centroid from top of Layer 2:

$$\bar{z}' = \frac{3\,(2 \times 5.47 + 34.90)}{3\,(5.47 + 34.90)} = \frac{3 \times 45.84}{3 \times 40.37} = \frac{137.52}{121.11} = 1.14\,\text{m}$$

$\bar{z}_2$ from top of wall = $3 + 1.14 = 4.14$ m ⇒ from base = 1.86 m

Resultant location from base:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{27.0 \times 4.0 + 60.56 \times 1.86}{87.56} = \frac{108.0 + 112.64}{87.56} = \frac{220.64}{87.56} = 2.52\,\text{m from base}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Active pressure at base = **34.90 kPa**
- Total active thrust = **87.56 kN/m**
- Point of application = **2.52 m above the base**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Layered Backfill Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the jump in pressure at the interface** (3–4 marks lost). Always compute $K_{a,1}\gamma_1 H_1$ and $K_{a,2}\gamma_1 H_1$ separately.
> 2. **Using the wrong layer's φ** at the interface (2 marks lost). The Ka **above and below** the interface must be computed with the respective φ.
> 3. **Applying the depth of tension crack formula globally** (2 marks lost). The formula $z_0 = 2c/(\gamma\sqrt{K_a})$ is **per layer**; it must be checked in each cohesive layer.
> 4. **Incorrect centroid for trapezoidal pressure** (3 marks lost). Use the standard formula $\bar{z}' = h(2a + b)/[3(a + b)]$ measured from the side of width $a$.
> 5. **Not drawing the pressure diagram** (2 marks lost). KTU evaluators explicitly allocate marks for a clean, labelled sketch.
> 6. **Mixing Rankine and Coulomb in the same problem** (entire answer invalidated). Stick to one method per solution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam:**

- ☑ The earth-pressure coefficient $K_a$ **depends on φ only** (for cohesionless soil), not on γ.
- ☑ Vertical stress σv is computed by **simple summation of overburden** through all layers above the point of interest.
- ☑ Lateral pressure $p_a = K_a \sigma_v$ in cohesionless soil, and $p_a = K_a \sigma_v - 2c\sqrt{K_a}$ in c–φ soil.
- ☑ At a layer interface, σv is **continuous** but $p_a$ is **discontinuous** (jump = $(K_{a,2} - K_{a,1})\gamma_1 H_1$).
- ☑ The pressure diagram is **piecewise linear**: triangular in the top layer, trapezoidal in each subsequent layer.
- ☑ Total active thrust = area of the entire pressure diagram.
- ☑ Centroid of a triangle (in pressure diagram) is at $\frac{1}{3}$ from the larger side.
- ☑ Centroid of a trapezoid with parallel sides $a$ (top) and $b$ (bottom), height $h$: from side $a$, the centroid is at $\dfrac{h(2a + b)}{3(a + b)}$.
- ☑ Depth of tension crack in a c–φ layer: $z_0 = \dfrac{2c}{\gamma\sqrt{K_a}}$ (per layer, measured from the top of that layer).
- ☑ A **negative** $z_0$ means the entire layer is in compression ⇒ no tension crack.
- ☑ Rankine's method assumes a **smooth vertical wall** and a **planar failure surface**; Coulomb's method allows wall friction and arbitrary geometry.
- ☑ For KTU 2024 exams, **Rankine is the default method** for horizontal backfill problems.
- ☑ Always **draw the pressure distribution diagram** as part of the solution; it carries separate marks.
- ☑ Units must be consistent: γ in kN/m³, depth in m, pressure in kPa, force in kN/m.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
