> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coulomb's Theory [Concept Only]

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coulomb's Earth Pressure Theory (1773):** A classical limit-equilibrium method for evaluating the lateral thrust exerted by a cohesionless backfill on a retaining structure. The theory assumes that the retained soil mass fails along a planar slip surface (a trial rupture plane), and the resultant earth thrust is obtained by resolving the weights of the failing wedge together with the frictional reactions mobilized along the wall back-face and the assumed failure plane.

Charles-Augustin de Coulomb formulated this approach while investigating the lateral load exerted by "pulverulent" (granular) materials against fortress revetments. The theory explicitly accommodates:

- **Wall-back inclination** with respect to the horizontal ($\alpha$),
- **Backfill slope** inclination ($\beta$),
- **Angle of internal friction** of the soil ($\phi$),
- **Angle of wall friction** developed at the soil–structure interface ($\delta$),
- **Surcharge** and **cohesion** (as conceptual extensions in the generalized form).

For the **[Concept Only]** module, the focus is on the *mechanics of the wedge*, the *assumptions*, the *force polygon*, and the *philosophy of locating the critical failure plane*, rather than the closed-form derivations of $K_a$ and $K_p$.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Leaning Sand Pile Against a Wooden Board:**
> Imagine pouring dry sand behind a rough wooden plank. As the sand grows, it pushes the plank outward. Now tilt the plank backwards and roughen its surface (sandpaper the back face). The sand stops sliding cleanly and instead grips the board, reducing the push. If you slope the top surface of the sand downward away from the wall, the push reduces even further. Coulomb's Theory is the mathematical framework that *quantifies exactly how much* the wall is being pushed, taking all three of these geometric and frictional effects into account.

The conceptual pillars of the theory are best internalized through three intuitive statements:

1. **The soil wants to slide** along *some* plane — but we do not yet know *which* plane. So we test several.
2. **The wall offers resistance** through friction at its back-face; the rougher the wall, the more the soil "hangs" on it.
3. **The worst-case sliding wedge** dictates design — we search for the wedge that maximizes the active thrust (or minimizes the passive resistance).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard values are conventionally used in KTU-evaluated design problems:
> - **Unit weight of dry sand:** $\gamma_d \approx 16$ kN/m³
> - **Unit weight of saturated sand:** $\gamma_{sat} \approx 18$–$20$ kN/m³
> - **Angle of internal friction ($\phi$):** $28°$–$40°$ for granular soils
> - **Wall friction angle ($\delta$):** typically $\frac{1}{2}\phi$ to $\frac{2}{3}\phi$ (smooth to rough interface)
> - **Limiting equilibrium factor for active case:** the wedge geometry is searched until $P_a$ is **maximum**.
> - **Limiting equilibrium factor for passive case:** the wedge geometry is searched until $P_p$ is **minimum**.
> - **Earth pressure unit (SI):** kN/m (per metre run of wall)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Coulomb's Theory (Step-by-Step)

The analytical philosophy of Coulomb's wedge method can be decomposed into six logical steps:

1. **Identify the geometry of the problem.** The wall back makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal, and the backfill surface makes an angle $\beta$ with the horizontal (sloping downward away from the wall).
2. **Assume a trial failure plane.** A planar surface is postulated, rising from the heel of the wall into the backfill at an angle $\theta$ measured from the horizontal.
3. **Isolate the failing wedge.** The wedge is bounded by the wall back-face, the assumed failure plane, and the sloping ground surface. The wedge is treated as a free body.
4. **Apply equilibrium of forces.** Three forces act on the wedge:
    - **$W$** — Weight of the wedge (acts vertically downward).
    - **$P$** — Reaction from the wall (acts at angle $\delta$ from the normal to the wall back-face).
    - **$R$** — Reaction along the failure plane (acts at angle $\phi$ from the normal to the failure plane).
5. **Construct the force triangle.** Since the wedge is in limiting equilibrium, the three forces must close to form a triangle. From this triangle, $P$ can be expressed as a function of $\theta$.
6. **Search for the critical wedge.** Differentiate $P(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ and set $\frac{dP}{d\theta}=0$. The wedge that yields the **maximum $P$** corresponds to the **active** case; the wedge that yields the **minimum $P$** corresponds to the **passive** case.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why a planar failure surface?** Coulomb *assumed* planar slip because the resulting wedge is statically determinate (only three unknowns and three equilibrium equations). In reality, curved failure surfaces occur in passive cases (especially when $\phi$ is large), but the assumption keeps the math tractable.
> - **Why the angle $\delta$?** Real wall–soil interfaces are never frictionless. The soil drags along the wall face as it descends, generating shear stresses that tilt $P$ away from the wall-normal by $\delta$.
> - **Why differentiate $P$ with respect to $\theta$?** Because the actual failure plane is the one *most likely* to mobilize — and that is the plane for which $P$ is extremal (maximum for active, minimum for passive).
> - **Why is this called a "limit-equilibrium" method?** Because the wedge is assumed to be on the verge of failure (factor of safety = 1), with both the failure plane and the wall–soil interface fully mobilized.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following expressions are presented **conceptually** (closed-form derivations of $K_a$ and $K_p$ are deferred to later modules per the **[Concept Only]** directive). However, students must memorize the *form* of the active wedge and the geometry conventions.

| Symbol | Quantity | Description | Standard Range / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | Wall-back inclination | Angle of wall back with horizontal | $0°$ to $20°$ |
| $\beta$ | Backfill slope | Angle of ground surface with horizontal | $0°$ to $\phi$ |
| $\phi$ | Angle of internal friction | Soil shear strength parameter | $28°$–$40°$ |
| $\delta$ | Wall friction angle | Friction at wall–soil interface | $\frac{1}{2}\phi$ to $\frac{2}{3}\phi$ |
| $\theta$ | Trial failure-plane angle | Angle of slip plane with horizontal | Variable (search variable) |
| $W$ | Wedge weight | Self-weight of the failing soil wedge | kN/m |
| $P_a$ | Active earth thrust | Force from soil onto wall (active case) | kN/m |
| $P_p$ | Passive earth resistance | Resistance offered by soil to wall (passive case) | kN/m |
| $R$ | Reaction on failure plane | Soil–soil reaction along slip surface | kN/m |
| $\gamma$ | Unit weight of soil | Weight per unit volume | kN/m³ |
| $K_a$ | Active earth-pressure coefficient | Dimensionless ratio $\frac{P_a}{\frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2}$ | $\approx 0.2$–$0.4$ |
| $K_p$ | Passive earth-pressure coefficient | Dimensionless ratio $\frac{P_p}{\frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2}$ | $\approx 3$–$10$ |

### 2.3.1 Conceptual Form of the Resultant Thrust

The general expression for the Coulomb active thrust, applicable once the closed-form derivation is studied, follows the canonical form:

$$
P_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,K_a
$$

where $H$ is the vertical height of the wall and $K_a$ is the active earth-pressure coefficient that absorbs all the geometric and frictional complexity of the problem. Analogously, for the passive case:

$$
P_p \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,K_p
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadratic dependence on height:** Doubling the wall height quadruples the thrust. This is why tall retaining structures experience disproportionately large lateral loads in their lower thirds.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Coulomb's theory remains the workhorse for designing:
- **Gravity retaining walls** for highways, bridge abutments, and basement excavations.
- **Cantilever sheet pile walls** in temporary works.
- **Reinforced earth walls** where the back-face inclination is non-zero.
- **Anchored bulkheads** in marine and harbour engineering.

In Kerala's coastal and lateritic terrain, where $\phi$ often ranges between $28°$ and $34°$ and wall friction is conservatively taken as $\delta = \tfrac{2}{3}\phi$, Coulomb's approach gives economical and *conservative* estimates of lateral thrust when correctly applied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The **[Concept Only]** directive restricts the full closed-form derivation of $K_a$ and $K_p$. However, the *conceptual derivation* of the active thrust as a function of the trial wedge angle $\theta$ is fully within scope, and is presented below in exhaustive detail.

## 3.1 Geometry of the Trial Active Wedge

Consider a wall of vertical height $H$, with its back-face inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. The backfill rises at angle $\beta$ above the horizontal, away from the wall. Assume a trial failure plane making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal, originating at the heel of the wall and intersecting the ground surface at some distance from the wall.

The trial wedge is a triangle bounded by:
- The wall back-face (length $L_w$),
- The failure plane (length $L_f$),
- The sloping ground surface (length $L_g$).

Using the law of sines within the wedge triangle, the geometry may be summarized as follows. The interior angle at the wall heel (between the wall and the failure plane) is:

$$
\psi_1 \;=\; 90° - \alpha + \theta
$$

The interior angle at the toe of the failure plane (between the failure plane and the ground) is:

$$
\psi_2 \;=\; 90° - \theta + \beta
$$

The interior angle at the ground surface vertex (between the wall and the ground) is:

$$
\psi_3 \;=\; 90° + \alpha - \beta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification by angle summation:** $\psi_1 + \psi_2 + \psi_3 = 180°$. This must hold for any consistent wedge geometry.

## 3.2 Weight of the Wedge

The weight of the wedge, treated as a two-dimensional problem (force per metre run of wall), is given by the area of the wedge triangle multiplied by the unit weight of the soil $\gamma$.

The length of the failure plane $L_f$ is obtained from the geometry. Drop a perpendicular from the top of the wall to the failure plane; the analysis yields:

$$
L_f \;=\; \frac{H}{\sin\theta}\,\cdot\,\frac{\cos(\alpha - \beta)}{\cos(\theta - \beta)}
$$

The cross-sectional area of the wedge $A_w$ is then:

$$
A_w \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,L_w\,L_f\,\sin(\psi_3) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,L_f^{2}\,\frac{\sin(\psi_1)\,\sin(\psi_3)}{\sin(\psi_2)}
$$

After substituting the trigonometric relationships and simplifying, the weight per metre run of the wall becomes:

$$
W \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,\frac{\cos(\alpha - \beta)\,\cos(\theta - \alpha)}{\cos^{2}\theta\,\sin(\theta - \beta)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Every step in this derivation is *conceptually anchored* to the law of sines applied to the wedge triangle. The student is not expected to reproduce this in the **[Concept Only]** treatment but must understand that $W$ depends on $\theta$.

## 3.3 Force Polygon (Conceptual Derivation of $P_a$)

The three forces acting on the wedge are:

1. **Weight $W$** — vertical, downward.
2. **Wall reaction $P$** — inclined at angle $\delta$ below the normal to the wall back-face (because the soil slides downward relative to the wall in the active case).
3. **Failure-plane reaction $R$** — inclined at angle $\phi$ above the normal to the failure plane (because the wedge slides downward relative to the stationary soil below).

Constructing the closed force triangle, and applying Lami's theorem:

$$
\frac{P}{\sin(\theta - \phi)} \;=\; \frac{W}{\sin(90° + \alpha + \delta - \theta + \phi)}
$$

After algebraic rearrangement (intermediate trigonometric identities are suppressed here as they form the bridge to the closed-form $K_a$), we obtain:

$$
P(\theta) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,\underbrace{\left[\frac{\cos(\alpha - \beta)\,\cos(\theta - \alpha)}{\cos^{2}\theta\,\sin(\theta - \beta)}\,\cdot\,\frac{\sin(\theta - \phi)}{\sin(90° + \alpha + \delta - \theta + \phi)}\right]}_{\text{Function of } \theta \text{ only}}
$$

Define the bracketed quantity as $f(\theta)$. The active thrust is then:

$$
P_a \;=\; \max_{\theta} \, P(\theta) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,\max_{\theta}\,f(\theta)
$$

## 3.4 The Critical Wedge Condition

To locate the critical wedge, set:

$$
\frac{dP(\theta)}{d\theta} \;=\; 0
$$

This yields a transcendental equation in $\theta$ whose closed-form solution gives the well-known expression for $K_a$ (whose full derivation is deferred):

$$
K_a \;=\; \frac{\cos^{2}(\phi - \alpha)}{\cos^{2}\alpha\,\cos(\delta + \alpha)\,\left[\,1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi + \delta)\,\sin(\phi - \beta)}{\cos(\delta + \alpha)\,\cos(\alpha - \beta)}}\,\right]^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual takeaway:** The critical wedge angle $\theta_{cr}$ is the *unique* value that satisfies $\frac{dP}{d\theta}=0$. All other wedge angles correspond to *stable* (non-critical) wedges that will not actually fail.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Active vs Passive Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical matrix crystallizes the *conceptual symmetry* between the active and passive wedge problems. This is a high-yield recap point for KTU short-answer questions.

| Feature | Active Case | Passive Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wall movement direction | Wall moves **away** from soil | Wall moves **toward** soil |
| Soil state | **Loose**, in extension | **Compressed**, in compression |
| Major principal stress | Vertical ($\sigma_v = \gamma z$) | Horizontal ($\sigma_h = K_p \gamma z$) |
| Failure-plane orientation | Less steep, shallow wedge | Steeper, deeper wedge |
| Direction of $P$ relative to wall normal | Tilted **downward** by $\delta$ | Tilted **upward** by $\delta$ |
| Magnitude of thrust | **Smaller** ($P_a < P_p$) | **Larger** ($P_p \gg P_a$) |
| Search criterion | **Maximize** $P(\theta)$ | **Minimize** $P(\theta)$ |
| Typical coefficient value | $K_a \approx 0.25$–$0.35$ | $K_p \approx 3$–$5$ |

## 3.6 Conceptual Bridge to Rankine's Theory

> [!NOTE]
> Coulomb's theory is *more general* than Rankine's theory. Rankine's theory is a special case of Coulomb's theory, obtained by setting $\alpha = 0°$, $\beta = 0°$, and $\delta = 0°$ (vertical wall, level backfill, smooth wall). Under these restrictive assumptions, the Coulomb coefficient reduces to:

$$
K_{a,\text{Rankine}} \;=\; \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} \;=\; \tan^{2}\!\left(45° - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

and analogously for $K_p$. KTU examiners frequently pose comparison questions asking students to identify when Rankine's simplifications are valid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wedge Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of forces and geometry involved in Coulomb's wedge analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wall of Height H, Inclined at Angle alpha] --> B[Trial Wedge Formed]
    B --> C[Failing Soil Mass Bounded by 3 Surfaces]
    C --> D[Surface 1: Wall Back-face]
    C --> E[Surface 2: Trial Failure Plane at Angle theta]
    C --> F[Surface 3: Sloping Ground Surface at Angle beta]
    D --> G[Force P: Wall Reaction Tilted by delta]
    E --> H[Force R: Soil Reaction Tilted by phi]
    F --> I[Force W: Wedge Weight Acting Vertically]
    G --> J[Force Triangle Construction]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute P as Function of theta]
    K --> L{Search Criterion}
    L -->|Active Case| M[Maximize P over theta]
    L -->|Passive Case| N[Minimize P over theta]
    M --> O[Locate Critical Wedge: dP by dtheta = 0]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Design Thrust: Pa or Pp]
```

## 4.2 Force Polygon — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wedge Weight W, Vertical] --> B[Force Triangle Closure]
    C[Wall Reaction P, at angle delta to Wall Normal] --> B
    D[Slip-plane Reaction R, at angle phi to Plane Normal] --> B
    B --> E[Limiting Equilibrium Achieved]
    E --> F[Compute Resultant Thrust]
```

## 4.3 Active vs Passive Failure Wedge — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ACTIVE_CASE["ACTIVE CASE: Wall Yields Away from Soil"]
        A1[Shallow Wedge Forms] --> A2[Soil Loosens, Extends]
        A2 --> A3[Minor Principal Stress Vertical]
        A3 --> A4[Pa = 1/2 gamma H squared Ka]
        A4 --> A5[Search: Maximize Pa over theta]
    end
    subgraph PASSIVE_CASE["PASSIVE CASE: Wall Pushes into Soil"]
        P1[Deep Wedge Forms] --> P2[Soil Compresses]
        P2 --> P3[Major Principal Stress Horizontal]
        P3 --> P4[Pp = 1/2 gamma H squared Kp]
        P4 --> P5[Search: Minimize Pp over theta]
    end
    ACTIVE_CASE --> COMPARE[Comparison: Pa less than Pp]
    PASSIVE_CASE --> COMPARE
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Flow — From Geometry to Design Thrust

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Geometry - alpha, beta, H] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Soil Parameters - phi, delta, gamma]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Assume Trial Wedge Angle theta]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Wedge Weight W]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Construct Force Triangle]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Solve for P as Function of theta]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply Search Criterion]
    S7 --> S8{Active or Passive?}
    S8 -->|Active| S9[Find theta giving MAXIMUM P equals Pa]
    S8 -->|Passive| S10[Find theta giving MINIMUM P equals Pp]
    S9 --> S11[Final Design Thrust Established]
    S10 --> S11
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**
*State Coulomb's wedge theory and list any **four** assumptions made in deriving the active earth pressure using this theory.*

> **Model Answer:**
> Coulomb's wedge theory (1773) is a limit-equilibrium method for evaluating the lateral thrust of cohesionless soil on a retaining wall by assuming a planar failure surface and resolving the forces acting on the failing soil wedge.
>
> **Four assumptions:**
> 1. The backfill is homogeneous, dry, and cohesionless.
> 2. The failure surface is planar (straight) — passing through the heel of the wall.
> 3. The wall friction angle $\delta$ is mobilized fully along the entire wall back-face.
> 4. The wedge is in a state of limiting equilibrium (factor of safety = 1).
>
> *(Any four of the standard assumptions are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**
*Distinguish between Coulomb's theory and Rankine's theory of lateral earth pressure in **any five** aspects.*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Coulomb's Theory | Rankine's Theory |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Failure surface | Planar (assumed) | Two conjugate planes at $45° \pm \phi/2$ |
> | Wall friction | Considered ($\delta \neq 0$) | Not considered ($\delta = 0$) |
> | Backface inclination | Arbitrary angle $\alpha$ | Vertical only |
> | Backfill slope | Arbitrary angle $\beta$ | Level only |
> | Resultant thrust direction | Inclined at $\delta$ to wall normal | Horizontal (parallel to ground) |
> | Method | Trial wedge + force triangle | Stress-state analysis |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted for Module 1 Concept Coverage]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **Coulomb's wedge theory** for the determination of active earth pressure. Clearly indicate all the forces acting on the trial failure wedge and the corresponding force polygon. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Sketch Description (refer to SECTION 4 diagram 4.1):**
> - Wall of height $H$ with back-face inclined at $\alpha$ to the horizontal.
> - Backfill sloping at $\beta$ above the horizontal.
> - Trial failure plane at angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
>
> **Forces acting on the wedge:**
> 1. **$W$** — Weight of the wedge, acts vertically downward through the centroid of the wedge.
> 2. **$P_a$** — Active thrust from the wall, acts at angle $\delta$ to the normal of the wall back-face (downward tilted).
> 3. **$R$** — Reaction along the failure plane, acts at angle $\phi$ to the normal of the failure plane.
>
> **Force Polygon:**
> The three forces $W$, $P_a$, and $R$ close to form a triangle. Using Lami's theorem or sine-rule resolution, $P_a$ is expressed as a function of the trial wedge angle $\theta$.
>
> **Critical wedge:**
> The active thrust is obtained by maximizing $P(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$, leading to $\frac{dP}{d\theta} = 0$, which identifies the unique *critical* failure wedge.

> **(b)** A retaining wall with a smooth vertical back ($\delta = 0$, $\alpha = 0$) supports a level granular backfill ($\beta = 0$) of height $H = 6$ m. The soil has $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Compute the **active earth pressure coefficient** $K_a$ using the simplified Coulomb formulation that reduces to Rankine's expression. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> For a smooth vertical wall with level backfill, Coulomb's formulation reduces to Rankine's expression:
>
> $$K_a \;=\; \tan^{2}\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$
>
> Substitute $\phi = 30°$:
>
> $$K_a \;=\; \tan^{2}\!\left(45° - 15°\right) \;=\; \tan^{2}(30°) \;=\; \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3}$$
>
> Therefore $K_a = 0.333$.
>
> The active thrust is:
>
> $$P_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,K_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 6^{2} \times 0.333$$
>
> $$P_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 36 \times 0.333 \;=\; 108 \,\text{kN/m}$$
>
> **Answer:** $K_a = 0.333$ and $P_a = 108$ kN/m.

---

### Question B — Option (ii) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted for Module 1 Concept Coverage]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **assumptions and limitations** of Coulomb's wedge theory. Why is it preferred over Rankine's theory for the design of retaining walls with inclined back-faces? **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Assumptions of Coulomb's theory:**
> 1. The backfill is homogeneous, isotropic, dry, and cohesionless.
> 2. The failure surface is a straight (planar) line passing through the heel of the wall.
> 3. The wall friction angle $\delta$ is fully mobilized along the wall back-face.
> 4. The soil wedge is in a state of limiting equilibrium.
> 5. The resultant earth thrust acts at a known point (one-third the height from the base for triangular distribution).
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. The planar failure assumption is an *approximation*; in passive cases, curved failure surfaces are observed when $\phi$ is large.
> 2. The theory does not account for cohesion directly (extensions exist, but the classical form is for cohesionless soil).
> 3. The distribution of pressure along the wall height is *assumed* triangular, which may not match reality for surcharged or sloped backfills.
> 4. Wall friction is often taken as an empirical fraction of $\phi$, introducing uncertainty.
>
> **Why preferred over Rankine for inclined back-faces:**
> Rankine's theory requires a *vertical* wall back and a *horizontal* backfill surface. Coulomb's theory accommodates both wall-back inclination ($\alpha$) and backfill slope ($\beta$), making it the only classical method applicable to real-world geometries such as battered retaining walls and sloping backfills behind bridge abutments.

> **(b)** A retaining wall has an inclined back-face at $\alpha = 10°$ with the horizontal and supports a backfill sloping at $\beta = 15°$. The soil friction angle is $\phi = 35°$ and the wall friction angle is $\delta = 20°$. Estimate the **critical wedge angle** $\theta_{cr}$ that maximizes the active thrust, given that the design charts indicate $\theta_{cr} \approx 55°$ for these input parameters. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> Given:
> - $\alpha = 10°$, $\beta = 15°$, $\phi = 35°$, $\delta = 20°$.
> - Critical wedge angle (from design chart): $\theta_{cr} = 55°$.
>
> **Verification of geometric consistency:**
> The failure plane must rise *into* the backfill from the heel, so $\theta_{cr}$ must satisfy $\theta_{cr} > \beta$ and $\theta_{cr} < 90° + \phi$. Check:
> - $\theta_{cr} = 55° > \beta = 15°$ ✓
> - $\theta_{cr} = 55° < 90° + 35° = 125°$ ✓
>
> **Conceptual verification of the critical condition:**
> The angle $\theta_{cr}$ is the unique value for which $\frac{dP(\theta)}{d\theta} = 0$. For any wedge angle less than $55°$, the failure plane is too shallow to mobilize full friction, and $P$ is sub-maximal. For any angle greater than $55°$, the wedge is unrealistically deep and over-stabilized.
>
> **Design implication:**
> The retaining wall must be designed to resist the active thrust corresponding to this critical wedge:
>
> $$P_a \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,H^{2}\,K_a(\alpha, \beta, \phi, \delta)$$
>
> where $K_a$ is obtained by substituting the given angles into the closed-form Coulomb expression (covered in subsequent modules).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Coulomb's Theory Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wall friction angle $\delta$:** Many students assume a smooth wall ($\delta = 0$) when the problem explicitly states a rough wall. This *underestimates* the actual thrust when $\delta$ reduces the active pressure — but examiners penalize *incorrectly* assuming Rankine when Coulomb is specified.
> 2. **Confusing the search criterion:** In the active case, we *maximize* $P(\theta)$; in the passive case, we *minimize* $P(\theta)$. Reversing these gives a non-conservative design and loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 3. **Direction of $P$:** The wall reaction $P$ is *inclined* at $\delta$ to the wall normal, *not horizontal*. Drawing it horizontally is a frequent error that costs a mark.
> 4. **Units inconsistency:** Always express $\gamma$ in kN/m³, $H$ in metres, and $P$ in kN/m. Mixing units (e.g., $\gamma$ in N/m³ with $H$ in cm) is a frequent silly mistake.
> 5. **Assuming a vertical wall when it isn't:** Many KTU problems feature battered or inclined walls. Students who default to Rankine lose all 7 marks of part (b).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Coulomb's Theory [Concept Only]:**
>
> - **Founder & Year:** Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, **1773**.
> - **Core Method:** Trial planar wedge + limit-equilibrium force polygon.
> - **Five Key Angles:**
>   - $\alpha$ — wall-back inclination (with horizontal)
>   - $\beta$ — backfill slope (with horizontal)
>   - $\phi$ — internal friction angle of soil
>   - $\delta$ — wall–soil friction angle ($\tfrac{1}{2}\phi$ to $\tfrac{2}{3}\phi$)
>   - $\theta$ — trial failure-plane angle (**the search variable**)
> - **Three Forces on Wedge:** $W$ (weight, vertical), $P$ (wall reaction at angle $\delta$ to wall normal), $R$ (slip-plane reaction at angle $\phi$ to plane normal).
> - **Search Criterion:** **Maximize** $P(\theta)$ for active case; **minimize** $P(\theta)$ for passive case.
> - **Critical Wedge Condition:** $\frac{dP(\theta)}{d\theta} = 0$.
> - **Resultant Thrust:** $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^{2} K_a$ and $P_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^{2} K_p$.
> - **Quadratic dependence on $H$:** Doubling wall height *quadruples* the thrust.
> - **Rankine as Special Case:** Set $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 0$, $\delta = 0$ to recover Rankine's $K_a = \tan^{2}(45° - \phi/2)$.
> - **Active vs Passive:** Active state occurs when wall moves *away* from soil; passive state occurs when wall moves *toward* soil.
> - **Applicability:** Coulomb's theory is *more general* than Rankine's — use it whenever the wall back is inclined or the backfill is sloped.
> - **Limitation:** Planar failure surface is an approximation; real passive failures are curved when $\phi$ is large.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Finite Slopes - Toe Failure, Base Failure, Slip Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Stability of Finite Slopes - Toe Failure, Base Failure, Slip Failure # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Finite Slope:** A slope whose geometry has a well-defined top and toe with limited horizontal extent, bounded by specified boundaries above and below. Unlike an infinite slope, a finite slope's failure surface cannot be approximated as a straight line parallel to the slope face. **Factor of Safety (FOS) of a Slope:** The ratio of the maximum shear strength (resisting force) available along the potential failure surface to the actual shear stress (driving force) mobilized along the same surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Stability of Finite Slopes** > The stability of a finite slope is evaluated by assuming a cylindrical (or non-circular) potential failure surface and computing the Factor of Safety (FOS). The analysis is broadly classified into: > 1. **Total Stress Analysis** (φᵤ = 0 condition) — applicable to saturated clays under undrained conditions. > 2. **Effective Stress Analysis** (c′, φ′ parameters) — applicable to all soils using pore-pressure considerations. > The critical failure surface is the one that yields the **minimum** Factor of Safety. ## 1.2 The Three Modes of Slope Failure Depending on the slope geometry (angle β) and soil shear-strength parameters (c, φ), the critical failure circle may pass through different regions of the slope. The three classical failure modes are: | Failure Mode | Failure Surface Path | Geometric Condition | |---|---|---| | **Toe Failure** | Arc passes **exactly through the toe** of the slope | Slope angle β ≥ 53° (approx.) in c-φ soils | | **Base Failure** | Arc passes **below the toe** (through base stratum) | Slope angle β < 53° in c-φ soils; flat slopes | | **Slip (Face) Failure** | Arc **intersects the slope face above the toe** | Steep slopes with low cohesion; tension crack at crest | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Ski Slope" Picture** > Imagine a pile of sand on a beach (a finite slope). If you pour more sand or tilt it, eventually it will fail along a curved slip surface. Where exactly this curved surface cuts the slope depends on how *steep* the slope is and how *sticky* the sand is (cohesion): > - A **steep, sticky** pile → surface exits at the toe (**Toe Failure**). > - A **flat, sticky** pile → surface dips below the toe and exits on the base (**Base Failure**). > - A **very steep, dry** pile → the upper part crumbles and slips from a point **above** the toe, like a small avalanche (**Slip / Face Failure**). > This is why the same soil, when re-shaped, can fail in three different ways! ## 1.3 Critical Slip Surface Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Critical Slip Circle** is the trial circle that yields the **lowest FOS**. Engineers must search (manually or via software) for this minimum FOS. The center of the critical circle lies on a vertical line called the **"line of centers"** whose position depends on the slope angle β and the friction angle φ. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics - **Stability Number (Nₛ):** A dimensionless number introduced by Taylor that consolidates c, γ, H, and F into a single chart-readable parameter. **Nₛ = c / (F · γ · H)**. - **Critical Height (H_c):** The maximum height a slope can sustain at F = 1.0 (limit equilibrium). For φ = 0: **H_c = c · N_c / γ**, where N_c is Taylor's stability coefficient. - **Swedish Slip Circle Method:** Pioneered by Swedish engineers (Fellenius, 1936), it uses total weight W of soil above the arc vs. cohesive + frictional resistance along the arc. - **Limit Equilibrium Methods (LEM):** A family of methods (Fellenius, Bishop, Janbu, Spencer) all obeying three equilibrium equations: ΣFₕ = 0, ΣFᵥ = 0, ΣM = 0. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Three Failure Modes Exist — The Geometric Logic The position of the critical failure circle is dictated by the **interplay** of two physical forces: 1. **Gravity-driven driving moment** → favors a deep, sweeping arc (Base Failure). 2. **Frictional resistance along the arc** → favors a shallow, shorter arc (Toe or Face Failure). **Taylor's Geometric Classification (1948):** - When the slope angle **β > 53°** in c-φ soil → the critical circle **coincides with the toe** (Toe Failure). This is because the steep slope face means a shorter arc is sufficient to mobilize the failure. - When **β < 53°** → the critical arc is a **Base Failure** mode — the arc must dip below the toe to develop enough resistance. - When the slope is **very steep and nearly cohesionless (φ small)** → a tension crack forms near the crest and the arc fails through it (**Slip / Face Failure**). > [!NOTE] > **The 53° Magic Angle** > The angle **β ≈ 53°** is a textbook landmark. It is the boundary in Taylor's stability chart above which toe failure dominates and below which base failure dominates for purely cohesive-frictional soils. For **φ = 0** soils, toe failure is **always** the critical mode regardless of β. ## 2.2 The Swedish Circle Method — Step-by-Step Logic 1. Sketch the slope and choose a trial slip circle of radius **r** with center **O**. 2. Compute the **area A** of soil above the arc, hence weight **W = γ · A**. 3. Compute the **central angle 2θ** (in radians) subtended by the arc at the center. 4. Compute the **arc length L = r · 2θ**. 5. Apply moment equilibrium about O: - Driving moment = W · x (where x = horizontal distance from O to centroid of arc area). - Resisting moment = (c · L + N · tanφ) · r, where N = W cosα (α = angle of arc base with horizontal at the centroid). 6. FOS is then: **F = (c · L + W · cosα · tanφ) / (W · sinα)**. ## 2.3 Fellenius Method (Ordinary Method of Slices) The slope above the slip circle is divided into **n vertical slices**. For each slice i: - Weight: **Wᵢ = γ · bᵢ · hᵢ** - Base inclination: **αᵢ** - Base length: **lᵢ = bᵢ / cosαᵢ** The **Fellenius FOS** (no interslice forces considered): $$F = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ c \cdot l_i + W_i \cos\alpha_i \cdot \tan\phi \right]}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \sin\alpha_i}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Fellenius method is **conservative** (underestimates FOS) because it ignores interslice forces. KTU examiners often award marks for explicitly stating this limitation. ## 2.4 Bishop's Simplified Method (More Accurate) Bishop's method includes horizontal interslice forces but ignores vertical ones: $$F = \frac{1}{\sum W_i \sin\alpha_i} \sum \frac{\left[ c \cdot b_i + W_i \tan\phi - c \cdot b_i \cdot \tan\phi \cdot \tan\alpha_i / F \right]}{m_\alpha(i)}$$ where: - **m_α(i) = cosαᵢ + (sinαᵢ · tanφ) / F** - This equation is **implicit in F** → requires iteration (KTU: usually 3–4 iterations suffice). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Definition | Formula / Expression | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **F** | Factor of Safety (moment equilibrium) | F = M_R / M_D | dimensionless | | **F_c** | FOS w.r.t. cohesion only (φ = 0) | F_c = c · L / Σ(Wᵢ sinαᵢ) | dimensionless | | **F_φ** | FOS w.r.t. friction only (c = 0) | F_φ = Σ(Wᵢ cosαᵢ tanφ) / Σ(Wᵢ sinαᵢ) | dimensionless | | **Nₛ** | Taylor's Stability Number | Nₛ = c / (F · γ · H) | dimensionless | | **H_c** | Critical Height (limit equilibrium) | H_c = c · N_c / γ | m | | **L** | Length of slip arc | L = r · 2θ | m | | **Wᵢ** | Weight of slice i | Wᵢ = γ · bᵢ · hᵢ | kN | | **M_R** | Resisting Moment | M_R = (c · L + N · tanφ) · r | kN·m | | **M_D** | Driving Moment | M_D = W · x | kN·m | | **m_α** | Bishop's geometric factor | m_α = cosα + (sinα · tanφ)/F | dimensionless | | **N_c** | Taylor's Stability Coefficient | Read from chart (function of β, φ) | dimensionless | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Practice > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Applications:** > 1. **Highway & Railway Embankments** — Stability against circular slip must be guaranteed for service life. > 2. **Earthen Dams** — Both upstream and downstream slopes of zoned dams are designed with FOS ≥ 1.5 (steady seepage) and FOS ≥ 1.2 (end-of-construction), per IS 7894. > 3. **Open-pit Mining** — Bench and overall pit slopes must be analyzed for circular/plane failure. > 4. **Building Foundations on Sloping Ground** — Seismic + static stability checked. > 5. **Landfill Liners** — Must satisfy long-term FOS even under leachate conditions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: FOS for a Simple Cylindrical Slip Surface (Swedish Circle) ### Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry Consider a slope of height **H** with slope angle **β** above horizontal. A trial cylindrical slip surface of radius **r** has its center at **O**. The arc subtends a central angle **2θ** (measured in radians) at the center. ### Step 2 — Compute Weight of Soil Mass Above Arc Let the area of the soil mass above the arc be **A**. Then: $$W = \gamma \cdot A$$ where **γ** is the bulk unit weight of soil. (For layered soil, W is the sum of γᵢ · Aᵢ per layer.) ### Step 3 — Identify Forces Acting on the Sliding Mass The forces along the failure surface are: - **Cohesive resistance** = c · L (acting tangentially opposite to motion) - **Normal force** = N = W · cosα (perpendicular to base, at centroid) - **Frictional resistance** = N · tanφ - **Driving force** = W · sinα (tangential, along motion) where **α** is the inclination of the chord from the centroid of the sliding mass to the horizontal. ### Step 4 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about Center O Sum of resisting moments: $$M_R = c \cdot L \cdot r + N \cdot \tan\phi \cdot r = \left[ c \cdot L + W \cos\alpha \cdot \tan\phi \right] \cdot r$$ Sum of driving moments: $$M_D = W \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot r$$ ### Step 5 — Define FOS as the Ratio of Moments $$F = \frac{M_R}{M_D}$$ Substituting the expressions: $$F = \frac{c \cdot L + W \cos\alpha \cdot \tan\phi}{W \sin\alpha}$$ This is the **classical Swedish Circle FOS**. ### Step 6 — Special Case: Purely Cohesive Soil (φ = 0) When φ = 0, the frictional term vanishes: $$F = \frac{c \cdot L}{W \sin\alpha}$$ This is the basis for the **φ = 0 analysis** used for saturated clays under undrained loading. ### Step 7 — Special Case: Purely Cohesionless Soil (c = 0) When c = 0, only friction resists failure: $$F = \frac{W \cos\alpha \cdot \tan\phi}{W \sin\alpha} = \frac{\tan\phi}{\tan\alpha}$$ This is the basis of the **infinite slope analysis** where the slip surface is parallel to the slope face. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Taylor's Stability Number (for φ = 0, Toe Failure) ### Step 1 — Define the Critical Condition For a slope on a **φ = 0 soil** (saturated clay, undrained), the critical height is reached when **F = 1.0** and failure is a **toe circle** (arc passes through the toe of the slope). ### Step 2 — Establish the Slip Geometry For φ = 0, the critical toe circle subtends a central angle of **2θ = 133.6°** (≈ 2.33 rad). The center of the slip circle lies at height **H** above the toe (Taylor's geometric construction). ### Step 3 — Compute Weight W of the Sliding Mass For a slope of height H and slope angle β, the weight of the sliding soil mass is: $$W = \gamma \cdot A = \gamma \cdot H^2 \cdot f(\beta)$$ where **f(β)** is a geometric function of the slope angle. For the toe circle with 2θ = 133.6°: $$f(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}(\cot\beta \cdot (\cot\beta - \cos 46.4°) - \sin 46.4° \cdot \cot\beta)$$ (This simplifies to a polynomial/table for practical use.) ### Step 4 — Compute Arc Length L The arc length of the slip surface: $$L = r \cdot 2\theta$$ where r is the radius of the slip circle. For toe failure on φ = 0 soil, the radius r is related to H by: $$r = \frac{H}{2 \sin 46.4°} = \frac{H}{1.461}$$ So: $$L = \frac{H}{1.461} \cdot 2.33 = 1.595 \cdot H$$ ### Step 5 — Apply F = 1 Condition At limit equilibrium (F = 1), and using the centroid location at angle α = 23.2° from vertical: $$F = \frac{c \cdot L}{W \sin\alpha} = 1$$ Substituting W and L: $$c \cdot (1.595 H) = \gamma H^2 f(\beta) \cdot \sin 23.2°$$ Solving for H: $$H_c = \frac{1.595 \cdot c}{\gamma \cdot f(\beta) \cdot \sin 23.2°}$$ ### Step 6 — Express as Taylor's Stability Number Rearranging to get Taylor's stability coefficient **N_c**: $$H_c = \frac{c \cdot N_c}{\gamma}$$ Therefore: $$N_c = \frac{1.595}{f(\beta) \cdot \sin 23.2°}$$ For β = 90° (vertical cut), N_c = **3.83**. For β = 45°, N_c = **5.53**. For β = 0° (horizontal), N_c → **5.14** (limiting value for very flat slopes). > [!IMPORTANT] > **For the General Case (c-φ soil),** Taylor expressed: $$F = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H \cdot N_s}$$ where **Nₛ** is read from Taylor's stability chart as a function of slope angle β and friction angle φ. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Given:** A slope of height H = 8 m, slope angle β = 30°, soil properties c = 30 kN/m², φ = 20°, γ = 18 kN/m³. Compute FOS assuming a trial slip circle of radius r = 12 m, central angle 2θ = 100° = 1.745 rad, and centroidal chord angle α = 22°. ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute arc length:** $$L = r \cdot 2\theta = 12 \cdot 1.745 = 20.94 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute weight of sliding mass:** Assuming area A = 75 m² (computed geometrically): $$W = \gamma \cdot A = 18 \cdot 75 = 1350 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Compute driving and resisting terms:** $$\text{Resisting} = c \cdot L + W \cos\alpha \tan\phi$$ $$= 30 \cdot 20.94 + 1350 \cdot \cos 22° \cdot \tan 20°$$ $$= 628.2 + 1350 \cdot 0.927 \cdot 0.364$$ $$= 628.2 + 455.6 = 1083.8 \text{ kN}$$ $$\text{Driving} = W \sin\alpha = 1350 \cdot \sin 22° = 1350 \cdot 0.3746 = 505.7 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Compute FOS:** $$F = \frac{1083.8}{505.7} = 2.14$$ **Conclusion:** FOS = 2.14 > 1.5, so the slope is **safe** for this trial circle. However, multiple trial circles must be examined to find the critical one with minimum FOS. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Three Failure Modes of Finite Slopes ```mermaid graph TB A["FINITE SLOPE FAILURE

Swedish or Taylor]) Toe2 --> Done Base --> Done Face --> Done style Start fill:#1e40af,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Toe fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Toe2 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Base fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Face fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Done fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the following: (a) Finite slope, (b) Toe failure, (c) Critical slip circle.** **Model Answer:** (a) **Finite Slope:** A slope of limited extent, with definite crest, toe, and base boundaries, where the failure surface is curved (typically circular) and intersects both the slope face and the ground surface. (b) **Toe Failure:** A mode of slope failure in which the critical slip circle **passes through the toe** of the slope. This is the most common mode for φ = 0 soils and for steep slopes (β > 53°) in c-φ soils. (c) **Critical Slip Circle:** The trial slip circle among all possible circles that yields the **minimum Factor of Safety (FOS_min)**. This is the circle along which the slope is most likely to fail. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between toe failure and base failure in finite slopes.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Toe Failure | Base Failure | |---|---|---| | **Arc Location** | Passes exactly through the toe of the slope | Passes **below** the toe, intersecting the base stratum | | **Slope Angle** | Steep slopes (β > 53° in c-φ soil) or any β in φ = 0 soil | Flat slopes (β < 53° in c-φ soil) | | **Arc Geometry** | Smaller, shallow arc | Larger, deeper arc sweeping under the slope | | **Stability Analysis** | Taylor's chart directly applicable (Nₛ) | Must search multiple centers on the line of centers | | **Driving Forces** | Smaller (limited sliding mass) | Larger (greater sliding mass weight) | --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Modified]` **A slope of height 10 m and slope angle 30° is constructed from a soil with c = 25 kN/m², φ = 15°, and γ = 18 kN/m³. Analyze the stability using the Swedish Slip Circle Method for a trial circle of radius 15 m, central angle 110°, and centroidal angle α = 20°. The area of the sliding mass is 95 m².** **(a)** Compute the Factor of Safety for the given trial circle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the three modes of slope failure and state the conditions under which each occurs. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Compute the length of the slip arc:** $$L = r \cdot 2\theta = 15 \cdot \left(110° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180°}\right) = 15 \cdot 1.919 = 28.78 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the weight of sliding mass:** $$W = \gamma \cdot A = 18 \cdot 95 = 1710 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the resisting forces:** $$\text{Cohesive Resistance} = c \cdot L = 25 \cdot 28.78 = 719.5 \text{ kN}$$ $$\text{Normal Force} = W \cdot \cos\alpha = 1710 \cdot \cos 20° = 1710 \cdot 0.9397 = 1606.9 \text{ kN}$$ $$\text{Frictional Resistance} = N \cdot \tan\phi = 1606.9 \cdot \tan 15° = 1606.9 \cdot 0.2679 = 430.5 \text{ kN}$$ $$\text{Total Resisting Force} = 719.5 + 430.5 = 1150.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the driving force:** $$W \cdot \sin\alpha = 1710 \cdot \sin 20° = 1710 \cdot 0.342 = 585.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the FOS:** $$F = \frac{1150.0}{585.0} = 1.97$$ **Conclusion:** FOS = **1.97 > 1.5**, so the slope is safe for this trial circle. However, the critical circle (with minimum FOS) must be located by trying several circles. #### **Model Solution for (b):** The three modes of finite slope failure are: 1. **Toe Failure:** The slip surface passes exactly through the toe of the slope. The arc subtends a central angle of about **2θ = 133.6°** for φ = 0 soil, and the center lies on the slope at height H from the toe. **Conditions:** Occurs in (i) **all φ = 0 soils** regardless of slope angle, and (ii) **steep c-φ slopes** with β > 53°. 2. **Base Failure:** The slip surface passes **below the toe**, sweeping out a deep arc that intersects the base stratum at both ends. The center is much higher above the slope, and the radius is large. **Conditions:** Occurs in **flat c-φ slopes** with β < 53° where the geometry permits a deep arc to develop more resistance. 3. **Slip (Face) Failure:** The slip surface **intersects the slope face above the toe**, often with a **tension crack** near the crest. The active length of the failure surface is reduced. **Conditions:** Occurs in **very steep slopes** with low cohesion, where the steep face can fail without the arc needing to extend down to the toe. > [!NOTE] > **Taylor's Stability Number** (Nₛ) is read from a chart for any combination of slope angle β and friction angle φ, and the FOS is computed as F = c / (γ · H · Nₛ). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified]` **A cut slope is to be made in a saturated clay (φᵤ = 0) at an angle of 60° with the horizontal. The clay has cᵤ = 40 kN/m² and γ = 19 kN/m³. The slope must have a Factor of Safety of 1.5 against rotational failure.** **(a)** Using Taylor's stability chart, determine the **maximum height** to which the cut can be made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the Swedish Slip Circle Method with a neat sketch. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Identify the failure mode:** Since φᵤ = 0, the critical failure mode is **Toe Failure** (toe circle). **Step 2 — Read Taylor's Stability Coefficient N_c:** For β = 60° and φ = 0°, from Taylor's stability chart: $$N_c = 5.33 \text{ (approximate value from Taylor's chart for } \beta = 60°, \phi = 0°)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Factor of Safety Correction:** For FOS = 1.5 (design F > 1), Taylor's formula for critical height with a safety factor is: $$H_{max} = \frac{c_u \cdot N_c}{F \cdot \gamma}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Height:** $$H_{max} = \frac{40 \cdot 5.33}{1.5 \cdot 19} = \frac{213.2}{28.5} = 7.48 \text{ m}$$ **Conclusion:** The cut slope can be made up to a **maximum height of approximately 7.5 m** to maintain a Factor of Safety of 1.5. #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Swedish Slip Circle Method — Explanation:** The Swedish method, pioneered by Fellenius and colleagues, assumes the failure surface in a finite slope is a **cylinder (circular arc)** of radius r with center at O. The sliding mass is the soil above the arc. The method follows these steps: 1. **Choose a trial slip circle** of radius r with center O above the slope. 2. **Compute the weight W** of the soil mass above the arc. 3. **Compute the arc length L = r · 2θ**, where 2θ is the central angle. 4. **Identify the centroid** of the sliding mass, located at distance x from the vertical through O, with chord inclination α to horizontal. 5. **Apply moment equilibrium** about O: - Resisting moment: M_R = (c · L + W cosα tanφ) · r - Driving moment: M_D = W sinα · r 6. **Compute FOS:** F = M_R / M_D = (c · L + W cosα tanφ) / (W sinα) 7. **Repeat** for several trial circles to find the **minimum FOS**. **Advantages:** - Simple, hand-calculable. - Gives a quick, conservative estimate of FOS. - Useful as a first-pass design check. **Limitations:** - Conservative because it ignores interslice forces (Fellenius simplification). - Only valid for circular slip surfaces; not for non-circular failures. - Critical circle must be found by trial, which is tedious without software. - Does not apply to soils with complex stratification or pore pressure. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Toe/Base/Slip Failure):** > 1. **Confusing the failure modes with slope steepness only** — Remember, the soil's friction angle φ is equally important. Always state both conditions (β and φ) when classifying. > 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** for central angle in L = r·2θ — this loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Using the wrong stability number N_c** — For β = 90°, N_c = 3.83; for β = 0°, N_c = 5.14. Always read the chart carefully. > 4. **Forgetting the FOS denominator** in Taylor's formula — the corrected formula is H_c = c·N_c/(F·γ), NOT c·N_c/γ (the latter gives H at F=1). > 5. **Failing to mention tension crack depth** in slip/face failure — this is a frequent 2-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points — Stability of Finite Slopes** - **Finite slope ≠ infinite slope.** Infinite slope uses planar failure (parallel to face); finite slope uses **curved (circular)** failure. - **Three failure modes:** Toe, Base, Slip (Face) — distinguished by **where the slip surface intersects the slope geometry**. - **φ = 0 soil → ALWAYS toe failure** (regardless of slope angle). The toe circle subtends **2θ = 133.6°**. - **β > 53° in c-φ soil → Toe Failure** dominates; **β < 53° → Base Failure** dominates. - **Slip (Face) Failure** is associated with a **tension crack** at the crest, depth **Z₀ = (2c/γ) · tan(45° + φ/2)**. - **Taylor's Stability Number:** Nₛ = c / (F · γ · H). Read **N_c** from Taylor's chart for φ = 0 case. - **Critical Height (F = 1):** H_c = c · N_c / γ. **Design Height (F > 1):** H_design = c · N_c / (F · γ). - **Swedish Circle FOS:** F = (cL + W cosα tanφ) / (W sinα). Always use **radians** for 2θ. - **Fellenius Method:** No interslice forces — **conservative**. Formula uses **b/cosα** for slice base length. - **Bishop's Simplified Method:** Includes horizontal interslice forces — **more accurate** but **iterative** (F appears on both sides). - **Design FOS values (IS 7894, IS 2720, KTU/IRC):** - End-of-construction: **F ≥ 1.3** - Steady seepage: **F ≥ 1.5** - Seismic/Earthquake: **F ≥ 1.2** - **Pore water pressure** reduces effective stress (σ′ = σ − u), which **lowers frictional resistance** and **FOS**. Always check the worst-case (high water table) condition. - **Critical circle search:** Center lies on a vertical "**line of centers**" at a horizontal distance from the slope crest depending on β and φ. Use **grid search** or optimization (e.g., method of Lagrange multipliers). - **Skempton's pore-pressure ratio** rᵤ = u / (γ · h) is commonly used in effective-stress slope analysis. - **Taylor's chart entries** to memorize for KTU: For φ = 0, β = 90° → N_c = 3.83; β = 60° → N_c = 5.33; β = 30° → N_c = 5.71; β = 0° → N_c = 5.14 (limiting value). - **Fellenius is conservative by 5–20%** compared to Bishop for most slope geometries. - **Always convert the central angle to radians** before multiplying by r to get arc length. - **For the KTU 14-mark problems**, the standard template is: state failure mode → draw diagram → write FOS formula → substitute values → calculate → comment on safety.

topics.title: Swedish Circle Method (Procedure Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Swedish Circle Method (Procedure Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Swedish Circle Method (Definition)**
> The **Swedish Circle Method** (also known as the **Method of Slices** or **Slip Circle Method**) is a classical *limit equilibrium method* of analysis used in geotechnical engineering to evaluate the **stability of earth slopes** against rotational/translational failure along an assumed **circular slip surface**. Developed by Swedish engineers (notably **Fellenius, 1936**), it is one of the most widely used procedures for determining the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** of natural slopes, embankments, earth dams, and cut slopes.

The method essentially assumes that a slope tends to fail by **rotation of a mass of soil** about a center point, with the failure surface being a segment of a circle. The sliding soil mass above this arc is divided into a number of vertical slices, and equilibrium equations are applied to each slice to compute the factor of safety.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Rotating Pie Slice"**
> Imagine a large **chocolate pie sitting on a tilted table**. Gravity tries to pull the pie off the table. If the pie is too heavy or the table is too steep, the pie will **rotate and slide** off, with the contact line between the pie and table forming a **curved (circular) tear line**.
>
> Now, instead of the whole pie failing at once, suppose you cut the pie into **vertical slices** (like sectors). Each slice tries to slide down, but it is held back by friction along the curved base and by the soil cohesion.
>
> In the Swedish Circle Method, the "pie" is the sliding soil mass, the "curved tear line" is the **slip circle**, and the "vertical slices" are the **soil slices** we analyze mathematically. The **Factor of Safety** tells us how much stronger the soil is compared to the destabilizing forces trying to rotate the entire mass.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The Swedish Circle Method typically involves the following standard parameters:

- **Unit weight of soil, γ** (kN/m³) — usually ranges from **17 to 22 kN/m³** for most soils.
- **Cohesion, c** (kN/m² or kPa) — for cohesive soils, typically **10 to 100 kPa**.
- **Angle of internal friction, φ** (degrees) — for sandy soils, typically **28° to 40°**.
- **Factor of Safety, F (or FoS)** — a dimensionless number, where **F > 1** indicates stable slope.
- **Radius of the slip circle, R** (m) — geometric parameter.
- **Central angle, 2α** (radians or degrees) — subtended by the slip arc.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The Swedish Circle Method is covered under the topic *"Stability of Slopes"* in Module 1. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically asks for the **procedure** of the method — meaning students should know the *step-by-step methodology*, *assumptions*, and *general approach* rather than the detailed algebraic derivations. Numerical problems may be asked where a trial circle is given.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theory

The Swedish Circle Method is based on **limit equilibrium principles**. The fundamental idea is:

- A slope is assumed to fail along a **circular slip surface**.
- The soil mass above the slip surface is treated as a **rigid body** rotating about the center of the circle.
- The **factor of safety (F)** is defined as the ratio of the **resisting (stabilizing) moment** to the **driving (overturning) moment** about the center of the slip circle.

$$F = \frac{\text{Resisting Moment } (M_R)}{\text{Driving Moment } (M_D)}$$

For a slip circle, the general expression becomes:

$$F = \frac{c \cdot L \cdot R + \sum (W \cos\theta - u \cdot l) \tan\phi \cdot R}{\sum W \sin\theta \cdot R}$$

Where:
- $c$ = cohesion of soil
- $L$ = total length of the slip arc
- $R$ = radius of the slip circle
- $W$ = weight of each slice
- $\theta$ = angle made by the base of the slice with the horizontal
- $u$ = pore water pressure at the base of the slice
- $l$ = length of the base of each slice
- $\phi$ = angle of internal friction

### 2.2 Why and How — Step-Wise Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why' Behind the Method**
> - **Why circular?** — Most slope failures in homogeneous soils **do follow curved surfaces** close to circular arcs. The circle simplifies geometry and makes equilibrium tractable.
> - **Why slices?** — Internal stress distribution within the sliding mass is complex. Dividing into slices **averages out** these stresses and allows numerical evaluation of forces.
> - **Why multiple trial circles?** — The actual critical (most dangerous) circle is **not known in advance**. Engineers try several centers and radii to find the **minimum factor of safety**.

### 2.3 Types of Swedish Circle Analyses

There are two main variants within the Swedish Circle framework:

| **Variant** | **Description** | **Complexity** |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Ordinary Method of Slices (Fellenius, 1936)** | Ignores inter-slice forces; uses ordinary stresses at the base. Simplest and most conservative. | Low |
| **Bishop's Simplified Method (1955)** | Considers horizontal inter-slice forces; more accurate. | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 1, the **Ordinary Method of Slices (Fellenius Method)** is the primary focus under "Swedish Circle Method."

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Reference — Essential Equations for Swedish Circle Method**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Factor of Safety (F) | $F = \dfrac{M_R}{M_D}$ | Dimensionless |
| Resisting Moment | $M_R = c \cdot L \cdot R + \sum (N \tan\phi) \cdot R$ | kN·m |
| Driving Moment | $M_D = \sum W \cdot x = \sum W \cdot R \sin\theta$ | kN·m |
| Weight of slice | $W = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h$ | kN (b = width, h = mean height) |
| Arc length | $L = R \cdot 2\alpha$ | metres (2α in radians) |
| Base length of slice | $l = \dfrac{b}{\cos\theta}$ | metres |
| Normal force on base (Fellenius) | $N = W \cos\theta$ | kN |
| Tangential (driving) force | $T = W \sin\theta$ | kN |
| Pore water pressure force | $U = u \cdot l$ | kN (if submergence considered) |
| Effective normal force | $N' = N - U$ | kN |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The Swedish Circle Method is a **workhorse of geotechnical practice** and is used extensively in:

- **Earth dam design** — checking upstream/downstream slope stability.
- **Highway and railway embankments** — verifying long-term stability.
- **Open-pit mining slopes** — ensuring safe working angles.
- **Natural hillside stability** — landslide risk assessment.
- **Excavation support** — temporary cut slope analysis.

> [!TIP]
> Even with modern numerical methods (Finite Element Limit Analysis), the Swedish Circle Method remains a **first-line check** because of its simplicity, robust theoretical foundation, and ease of use in spreadsheet-based calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure — Swedish Circle Method

### 3.1 General Procedure (Fellenius / Ordinary Method of Slices)

The following is the **exhaustive step-by-step procedure** as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 1.

---

#### **STEP 1: Draw the Slope Geometry**

Sketch the cross-section of the slope to scale, showing:
- The slope angle (β)
- The height of the slope (H)
- The toe of the slope (lowest point of the slip surface is generally taken at the toe)
- Any surcharge or loading at the top
- The phreatic line (if groundwater is present)

> **Why this step?** — A clear geometric representation is essential for accurate computation of slice dimensions and forces.

---

#### **STEP 2: Assume a Trial Slip Circle**

Choose a trial slip circle defined by:
- **Center of the circle, O** (located by trial, often above and behind the slope crest)
- **Radius, R** (chosen so that the arc passes through the toe of the slope)

> **Why this step?** — The actual critical circle is unknown. We start with an assumed geometry and then optimize.

---

#### **STEP 3: Identify the Sliding Mass and Divide into Vertical Slices**

The soil mass bounded by:
- The slope surface (above)
- The trial slip circle (below)

…is divided into a series of **vertical slices** of equal width, $b$ (typically 8 to 12 slices for adequate accuracy).

> **Why this step?** — Equal width simplifies calculations. The number of slices affects accuracy — too few gives poor results, too many is time-consuming.

---

#### **STEP 4: Compute Geometric Properties of Each Slice**

For each slice $i$ (from $i = 1$ to $n$):
- **Width, $b$** (constant, usually taken as $R/10$ or $H/10$)
- **Mean height, $h_i$** (average of heights at left and right edges of slice)
- **Angle, $\theta_i$** — the angle that the base of the slice makes with the horizontal
- **Base length, $l_i = \dfrac{b}{\cos\theta_i}$**

The angle $\theta_i$ can be computed using the geometry:

$$\theta_i = \arcsin\left(\frac{x_i}{R}\right)$$

where $x_i$ is the horizontal distance from the vertical line through the center O to the midpoint of the slice base.

---

#### **STEP 5: Compute the Weight of Each Slice**

The weight of the $i^{th}$ slice is:

$$W_i = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h_i$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = unit weight of soil
- $b$ = width of slice
- $h_i$ = mean height of slice

> **Note:** If the slope is layered, use the appropriate $\gamma$ for each layer.

---

#### **STEP 6: Resolve Weights into Normal and Tangential Components**

For each slice, the weight $W_i$ is resolved into:
- **Normal (perpendicular to base):** $N_i = W_i \cos\theta_i$ — contributes to resistance
- **Tangential (parallel to base):** $T_i = W_i \sin\theta_i$ — contributes to driving moment

> **Why this step?** — This resolution is the heart of the Fellenius method. The normal force mobilizes friction ($\tan\phi$), and the tangential force drives sliding.

---

#### **STEP 7: Compute Pore Water Pressure (if applicable)**

If the slope is submerged or has a phreatic line, the **pore water pressure, $u_i$**, at the base of each slice must be determined from the phreatic line geometry. The uplift force is:

$$U_i = u_i \cdot l_i$$

The **effective normal force** then becomes:

$$N'_i = N_i - U_i = W_i \cos\theta_i - u_i \cdot l_i$$

---

#### **STEP 8: Calculate the Total Driving Moment**

The driving moment about the center O is the sum of moments due to tangential weights of all slices:

$$M_D = R \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \sin\theta_i = R \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

---

#### **STEP 9: Calculate the Total Resisting Moment**

The resisting moment has two components:
1. **Cohesive resistance along the arc:**

$$M_{R,c} = c \cdot L \cdot R = c \cdot (R \cdot 2\alpha) \cdot R = c \cdot R^2 \cdot 2\alpha$$

where $2\alpha$ is the central angle in radians and $L = R \cdot 2\alpha$ is the arc length.

2. **Frictional resistance along the base of each slice:**

$$M_{R,\phi} = R \sum_{i=1}^{n} N'_i \tan\phi$$

3. **Total Resisting Moment:**

$$M_R = c \cdot L \cdot R + R \sum_{i=1}^{n} N'_i \tan\phi$$

---

#### **STEP 10: Compute the Factor of Safety**

The factor of safety for the trial circle is:

$$F = \frac{M_R}{M_D} = \frac{c \cdot L + \sum N'_i \tan\phi}{\sum W_i \sin\theta_i}$$

> **Decision:** If $F \geq 1.5$ (typical KTU/design requirement), the trial circle is acceptable for that geometry.

---

#### **STEP 11: Repeat with Other Trial Circles**

To find the **critical slip circle** (lowest factor of safety):
- Vary the center O (try positions along a grid above the slope)
- Vary the radius R (try radii from 1.5H to 3H, where H is slope height)
- Compute F for each combination
- The **minimum F** corresponds to the critical circle

---

#### **STEP 12: Final Design Check**

The slope is considered **safe** if the minimum factor of safety exceeds the required design value:

- **Earth dams (end-of-construction):** F ≥ 1.3
- **Earth dams (steady seepage):** F ≥ 1.5
- **Highway embankments:** F ≥ 1.3 to 1.5
- **Temporary cuts:** F ≥ 1.2

> If F_min < required, the slope must be redesigned (flattened, benched, reinforced, drained, etc.).

### 3.2 Summary Equation (General Fellenius Form)

For a fully **c-φ soil** (cohesion + friction) with **effective stresses**:

$$F = \frac{\sum \left[ \dfrac{c \cdot b}{\cos\theta_i} + (W_i \cos\theta_i - u_i b \sec\theta_i) \tan\phi \right]}{\sum W_i \sin\theta_i}$$

For a **purely cohesive soil** (φ = 0, Taylor's case):

$$F = \frac{c \cdot L \cdot R}{\sum W_i \cdot x_i} = \frac{c \cdot L}{\sum W_i \sin\theta_i}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Draw Slope Geometry] --> B[Assume Trial Slip Circle Center O and Radius R]
    B --> C[Identify Sliding Soil Mass Above Arc]
    C --> D[Divide Mass into 8 to 12 Vertical Slices of Equal Width b]
    D --> E[For Each Slice: Measure h, theta, x]
    E --> F[Compute Slice Weight: W equals gamma times b times h]
    F --> G[Resolve W into Normal N and Tangential T Components]
    G --> H{Submerged Slope?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute Pore Pressure u and Uplift U]
    H -- No --> J[Set U equals 0]
    I --> K[Compute Driving Moment MD equals R times sum of T]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Resisting Moment MR equals c L R plus R times sum of N prime tan phi]
    L --> M[Compute Factor of Safety F equals MR divided by MD]
    M --> N{F greater than or equal to Required FoS?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Slope is Safe for this Trial Circle]
    N -- No --> P[Trial Circle Fails: Try Another Center or Radius]
    O --> Q[Try Other Trial Circles to Find Critical Case]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Identify Critical Circle with Minimum F]
    R --> S{F minimum greater than or equal to Design Value?}
    S -- Yes --> T[SLOPE DESIGN ACCEPTABLE: END]
    S -- No --> U[REDESIGN: Flatten, Add Berms, Install Drains, Reinforce]
    U --> A
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Components of Swedish Circle Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        A1[Slope Geometry: H, beta]
        A2[Soil Properties: c, phi, gamma]
        A3[Groundwater: Phreatic Line]
    end

    subgraph GEOMETRY[GEOMETRY MODULE]
        B1[Slip Circle: Center O, Radius R]
        B2[Slice Division: n slices of width b]
        B3[Slice Properties: h_i, theta_i, l_i]
    end

    subgraph FORCES[FORCE COMPUTATION MODULE]
        C1[Weight: W_i]
        C2[Normal: N_i equals W cos theta]
        C3[Tangential: T_i equals W sin theta]
        C4[Uplift: U_i equals u l]
    end

    subgraph EQUILIBRIUM[EQUILIBRIUM MODULE]
        D1[Driving Moment: M_D]
        D2[Resisting Moment: M_R]
        D3[Factor of Safety: F]
    end

    subgraph ITERATION[ITERATION MODULE]
        E1[Trial Center Search Grid]
        E2[Radius Variation Loop]
        E3[Critical F Identifier]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> C4
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    A2 --> D2
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
```

### 4.3 Free-Body Diagram of a Typical Slice (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SLICE[SOIL SLICE i]
        S1[Weight W acting vertically downward]
        S2[Normal reaction N equals W cos theta on base]
        S3[Tangential driving force T equals W sin theta on base]
        S4[Cohesive force c times l along base]
        S5[Frictional force N prime tan phi along base]
    end

    S1 --> S3
    S1 --> S2
```

### 4.4 Slip Circle Geometry (Schematic Representation)

> **Note:** A textual schematic representation of the slip circle is given below. The toe T, crest C, and assumed center O are key points.

```
                              * * * O (Center of slip circle)
                         *           *
                      *                 *
                   *                       *
                *    [SLICE 1] [SLICE 2] ...  *
              *  ___________________________________  *
           *     |                                |    *
         *       |                                |       *
        *        |    SOIL MASS ABOVE SLIP        |        *
        *        |         CIRCLE                 |         *
        *        |   (divided into vertical       |          *
        *        |         slices)                |           *
         *       |                                |          *
           *     |________________________________|        *
              *  T  (Toe)                          C (Crest) *
                                                          *
                                                       (Slope face)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q: Define the Swedish Circle Method of slope stability analysis. State its key assumptions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Swedish Circle Method** is a limit equilibrium method used to evaluate the stability of earth slopes against rotational failure along an assumed **circular slip surface**. The soil mass above the slip circle is divided into vertical slices, and the **factor of safety (F)** is computed as the ratio of the **resisting moment** to the **driving moment** about the center of the circle.

**Key Assumptions (1 Mark each for any 3, totalling 3):**
1. The slip surface is a **circular arc** passing through the toe.
2. The sliding soil mass behaves as a **rigid body**.
3. The factor of safety is **uniform** along the entire slip surface.
4. **Inter-slice forces are ignored** (Ordinary Method of Slices — Fellenius).
5. The soil is **homogeneous and isotropic**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q: What is meant by the "critical slip circle"? How is it located in the Swedish Circle Method?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **critical slip circle** is the slip surface that yields the **minimum factor of safety (F_min)** for a given slope. It represents the most likely failure surface.

**Location procedure (3 Marks for the following points):**
1. Assume several **trial centers** along a grid above the slope crest and a series of **trial radii** (typically 1.5H to 3H, where H is slope height).
2. For each trial circle, divide the sliding mass into **vertical slices** and compute the factor of safety.
3. The circle that gives the **lowest F** is the **critical slip circle**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Question B-A]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** State the assumptions and explain the **step-by-step procedure** of the Swedish Circle Method of slope stability analysis. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A slope of height **H = 10 m** is inclined at an angle **β = 30°**. A trial slip circle is assumed with radius **R = 15 m** and central angle **2α = 70°**. The soil has **c = 20 kN/m²**, **φ = 15°**, and **γ = 18 kN/m³**. Estimate the factor of safety assuming a purely cohesive analysis of the failure arc length only. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Swedish Circle Method** (Ordinary Method of Slices) is performed as follows:

1. **Draw the slope geometry** — Sketch the cross-section with H and β to scale. **(0.5 Mark)**
2. **Assume a trial slip circle** with center O and radius R, generally passing through the toe. **(0.5 Mark)**
3. **Divide the sliding mass** above the arc into **8 to 12 vertical slices** of equal width b. **(1 Mark)**
4. **Measure slice properties** — for each slice, determine the mean height $h_i$, the base angle $\theta_i$, and the base length $l_i = b \sec\theta_i$. **(1 Mark)**
5. **Compute slice weight** — $W_i = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h_i$. **(0.5 Mark)**
6. **Resolve W into components** — Normal $N_i = W_i \cos\theta_i$ and tangential $T_i = W_i \sin\theta_i$. **(1 Mark)**
7. **Compute driving moment** — $M_D = R \cdot \sum W_i \sin\theta_i$. **(0.5 Mark)**
8. **Compute resisting moment** — $M_R = c \cdot L \cdot R + R \cdot \sum N_i \tan\phi$ where $L = R \cdot 2\alpha$. **(1 Mark)**
9. **Compute FoS** — $F = M_R / M_D$. **(0.5 Mark)**
10. **Iterate with multiple trial circles** to find the **critical circle** with minimum F. **(0.5 Mark)**

**Key Assumptions:** (Any 2 for 1 mark total — included for completeness)
- Slip surface is a circular arc.
- Soil mass is rigid.
- Inter-slice forces are neglected.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $H = 10$ m
- $\beta = 30°$
- $R = 15$ m
- $2\alpha = 70° = 70 \times \pi/180 = 1.222$ rad
- $c = 20$ kN/m²
- $\phi = 15°$
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³

**Step 1: Compute arc length L (0.5 Mark)**

$$L = R \cdot 2\alpha = 15 \times 1.222 = 18.33 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Cohesive resisting moment $M_{R,c}$ (1 Mark)**

$$M_{R,c} = c \cdot L \cdot R = 20 \times 18.33 \times 15 = 5499 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 3: Approximate driving moment $M_D$ (3.5 Marks)**

For a circular slip, the driving moment is approximated as the disturbing moment from the soil weight. For the given geometry with $\beta = 30°$ and $H = 10$ m, taking an approximate resultant weight and lever arm:

- Approximate area of sliding wedge: $A \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{(base length)} \cdot H$
- Effective base length of sliding mass $\approx 2R \sin\alpha = 2 \times 15 \times \sin(35°) = 30 \times 0.574 = 17.21$ m
- Approximate area of slip mass: $A \approx \frac{1}{2} \times 17.21 \times 10 = 86.05$ m²
- Total weight per unit length: $W_{total} = \gamma \cdot A = 18 \times 86.05 = 1548.9$ kN/m
- Effective moment arm (approx.) for circular arc: $\bar{x} \approx R \cdot \sin\bar{\theta}$, with $\bar{\theta} \approx \alpha = 35°$ (mean angle for the arc)
- $M_D \approx W_{total} \cdot R \cdot \sin\bar{\theta} = 1548.9 \times 15 \times 0.574 = 13,335$ kN·m/m

**Step 4: Frictional resisting moment (1 Mark)**

For an approximate estimate, take $\sum N_i \approx W_{total} \cos\bar{\theta} = 1548.9 \times \cos(35°) = 1548.9 \times 0.819 = 1268.6$ kN/m

$$M_{R,\phi} = R \cdot \sum N_i \tan\phi = 15 \times 1268.6 \times \tan(15°) = 15 \times 1268.6 \times 0.268 = 5099 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 5: Total resisting moment (0.5 Mark)**

$$M_R = M_{R,c} + M_{R,\phi} = 5499 + 5099 = 10,598 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 6: Factor of Safety (0.5 Mark)**

$$F = \frac{M_R}{M_D} = \frac{10,598}{13,335} = 0.795$$

**Result: F ≈ 0.80 (less than 1)** — The trial circle **fails** (slope unstable for this trial).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (14-Mark Questions)**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** when computing arc length $L = R \cdot 2\alpha$. Always check: $2\alpha$ in **radians**, not degrees! **(–1 Mark deduction)**
> 2. **Mixing up units** — Use kN/m³ for γ, kN/m² for c, m for lengths, and kN·m/m for moments. **(–0.5 Mark)**
> 3. **Ignoring the frictional component** when $\phi \neq 0$ — students often compute only the cohesive moment, leading to an under-estimate of F. **(–1 Mark)**
> 4. **Not identifying the critical circle** — KTU expects students to mention that trial circles are iterated to find the minimum F. **(–0.5 Mark if missing)**
> 5. **Confusing Fellenius (Ordinary) with Bishop's Method** — Fellenius ignores inter-slice forces; Bishop includes them. Read the question carefully. **(–1 to –2 Marks)**
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in trig** — $\sin$, $\cos$, $\tan$ of standard angles like 30°, 35°, 15° must be accurate. **(–0.5 to –1 Mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Question B-B]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Fellenius Method of slices** with a neat sketch. List its **assumptions and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An embankment slope is to be constructed at an angle of **2H : 1V** (i.e., 2 horizontal : 1 vertical) with a height of **12 m**. The soil has the following properties: **c = 30 kN/m²**, **φ = 10°**, **γ = 19 kN/m³**. For a trial slip circle passing through the toe with radius **R = 18 m** and central angle **2α = 60°**, compute the **factor of safety** using the Swedish Circle Method. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline to Question B (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Fellenius Method is the **Ordinary Method of Slices** and forms the basis of the Swedish Circle Method.

**Procedure Sketch Description (1.5 Marks):** — Describe a slope with a circular slip surface passing through the toe, with multiple vertical slices, showing the center O, radius R, base angles θ, slice widths b, and the forces (W, N, T, cohesive and frictional forces).

**Step-by-Step Procedure (3 Marks):** — Same as detailed in Section 3 above (use it directly with proper explanation).

**Assumptions (1.5 Marks):**
1. Circular slip surface.
2. Rigid sliding mass.
3. Factor of safety same for all slices.
4. Inter-slice forces neglected.
5. Soil is homogeneous.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
1. Conservatively underestimates F (about 20% lower than more accurate methods).
2. Inaccurate for slopes with high pore pressures.
3. Cannot handle non-circular slip surfaces.

---

**Model Solution Outline to Question B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** Slope angle: 2H:1V → $\beta = \arctan(1/2) = 26.57°$; H = 12 m; c = 30 kN/m²; φ = 10°; γ = 19 kN/m³; R = 18 m; $2\alpha = 60° = \pi/3 = 1.047$ rad.

**Step 1: Arc length L (0.5 Mark)**

$$L = R \cdot 2\alpha = 18 \times 1.047 = 18.85 \text{ m}$$
