**Step 2: Cohesive resisting moment (1 Mark)**

$$M_{R,c} = c \cdot L \cdot R = 30 \times 18.85 \times 18 = 10,179 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 3: Estimate driving moment (2 Marks)**

Approximate sliding mass geometry:
- Effective base length: $2R \sin\alpha = 2 \times 18 \times \sin(30°) = 36 \times 0.5 = 18$ m
- Approximate area: $\frac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 12 = 108$ m²
- Total weight: $W_{tot} = 19 \times 108 = 2052$ kN/m
- Mean angle: $\bar{\theta} \approx 30°$
- $M_D \approx W_{tot} \cdot R \cdot \sin\bar{\theta} = 2052 \times 18 \times 0.5 = 18,468$ kN·m/m

**Step 4: Frictional resisting moment (1.5 Marks)**

$$\sum N_i = W_{tot} \cos\bar{\theta} = 2052 \times 0.866 = 1777 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$M_{R,\phi} = R \cdot \sum N_i \tan\phi = 18 \times 1777 \times \tan(10°) = 18 \times 1777 \times 0.1763 = 5640 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 5: Total resisting moment (0.5 Mark)**

$$M_R = 10,179 + 5640 = 15,819 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**Step 6: Factor of Safety (0.5 Mark)**

$$F = \frac{15,819}{18,468} = 0.857$$

**Result: F ≈ 0.86** — Slope is **unstable** for this trial; iterate with other circles.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Swedish Circle Method (Procedure)**

- **Definition:** Limit equilibrium method assuming a **circular slip surface** with soil mass divided into **vertical slices**. **[High Priority]**
- **Originator:** Developed by **Swedish engineers (Fellenius, 1936)** — hence the name. **[Frequently Asked]**
- **Key Assumption:** Slip surface is a **circular arc**, generally passing through the **toe** of the slope. **[High Priority]**
- **Factor of Safety (FoS):** Defined as **$F = M_R / M_D$** (resisting moment ÷ driving moment). **[High Priority]**
- **Slope is safe if F ≥ required design value** (typically 1.3 to 1.5 for permanent works, 1.2 for temporary). **[Frequently Asked]**
- **Number of Slices:** **8 to 12** vertical slices for adequate accuracy; equal width preferred. **[Standard Practice]**
- **Fellenius vs Bishop:**
  - **Fellenius (Ordinary):** Ignores inter-slice forces. Simpler, conservative.
  - **Bishop (Simplified):** Considers horizontal inter-slice forces. More accurate. **[Often Confused]**
- **Arc Length Formula:** $L = R \cdot 2\alpha$ — always use **radians** for $2\alpha$. **[Common Error Zone]**
- **Slice Weight:** $W = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h$ (γ = unit weight, b = width, h = mean height). **[Fundamental]**
- **Force Resolution:** $N = W\cos\theta$ (resisting), $T = W\sin\theta$ (driving). **[Fundamental]**
- **Resisting Moment Components:**
  1. **Cohesive:** $M_{R,c} = c \cdot L \cdot R$
  2. **Frictional:** $M_{R,\phi} = R \cdot \sum N_i \tan\phi$
- **Driving Moment:** $M_D = R \cdot \sum W_i \sin\theta$
- **Critical Slip Circle:** Found by **trial-and-error**, varying center O and radius R. The **minimum F** is the critical value. **[High Priority]**
- **Pore Water Pressure:** Reduces effective normal force: $N' = N - u \cdot l$. Lower FoS when slope is saturated. **[Important]**
- **Purely Cohesive Case (φ = 0):** Only the **cohesive moment** resists — $F = c \cdot L \cdot R / M_D$. **[Frequently Asked]**
- **Frictionless Case (c = 0):** Only the **frictional moment** resists — $F = \sum N_i \tan\phi / \sum T_i$. **[Frequently Asked]**
- **Applications:** Earth dams, embankments, natural slopes, mining pits, excavation cuts. **[Practical Context]**
- **Limitations:** Considers only **circular** surfaces; conservative for many real slopes; cannot model complex stratigraphy without adjustments. **[Examiner's Favourite]**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction Circle Method (Procedure Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friction Circle Method (Procedure Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Friction Circle Method** is an advanced limit equilibrium technique used in geotechnical engineering for analyzing the stability of finite earth slopes against a **rotational (circular) slip failure**. It is a refined modification of the Swedish Circle Method (Ordinary Method of Slices) that explicitly accounts for the **mobilized frictional resistance** along the curved failure surface.

The method's fundamental assumption is that the resultant reaction force acting on any element of the slip surface is inclined at the angle of internal friction (**φ**) to the normal at that point. Due to this, the line of action of the resultant reaction is **tangential to a concentric "Friction Circle"** drawn around the slip circle's center **O**, having a radius equal to **R sin φ**, where **R** is the radius of the slip circle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under the Stability of Slopes module. Examiners specifically test the **constructional procedure** rather than full numerical analysis, as it requires graphical precision. The word "Procedure Only" in the syllabus means students must master the step-by-step construction and the underlying engineering assumptions, not just formula recall.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a heavy cylindrical log (representing a sliding soil mass) sitting on a curved, slightly inclined ramp (the slip surface). The log wants to roll down due to **gravity**. However, the ramp surface has **friction** resisting this motion.

Now think of two scenarios:
- **Naïve Swedish Method:** It assumes all the resisting force is purely "sticky" (cohesion), ignoring that friction also helps hold the log back. This **overestimates** the danger.
- **Friction Circle Method:** It recognizes that the resisting force actually acts at a small tilt from the perpendicular — this tilt is governed by friction. So instead of the resistance line passing through the center, it passes a little to the side, traced by a smaller **"friction circle."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The Swedish method treats soil like sticky clay (φ = 0 effectively). The Friction Circle method says, "Wait! The soil also has internal friction. So the resisting force leans back a little bit." The lean is captured by drawing an inner **Friction Circle of radius R sin φ**.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **R** = Radius of the assumed (trial) **slip circle** (meters)
- **r_f** = Radius of the **Friction Circle** = **R sin φ** (meters, where φ is in radians on a calculator)
- **φ** = Angle of internal friction of the soil (degrees)
- **c** = Unit cohesion of the soil (kN/m² or kPa)
- **W** = Weight of the soil slice / mass (kN/m)
- **L** = Total length of the slip arc (meters)
- **FOS (or N)** = Factor of Safety (dimensionless, must be ≥ **1.5** for safe slopes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Assumptions

The Friction Circle Method is built on a set of well-defined assumptions that distinguish it from simpler methods. Every KTU answer should explicitly mention these.

1. **Circular Slip Surface:** The failure surface is assumed to be an arc of a circle with center **O** and radius **R**.
2. **Two-Dimensional Analysis:** The problem is treated as plane strain (a unit length of the slope is considered).
3. **Mohr-Coulomb Failure Criterion:** The shear strength of the soil along the slip surface follows:
   
   $$s = c + \sigma \tan\phi$$
4. **Frictional Resistance Mobilization:** The resultant reaction **R'** on the slip surface is inclined at the angle **φ** to the normal of the slip circle. This is the **pivotal assumption** that introduces the friction circle.
5. **Tangency to Friction Circle:** Because of the inclination, the line of action of the resultant reaction is **tangential** to a concentric circle of radius **r = R sin φ**, called the **Friction Circle** or **φ-circle**.
6. **Direction of Inter-slice Forces:** The forces acting on the vertical interfaces between adjacent slices are assumed to be **parallel to the average slope of the embankment** (i.e., the line joining the crest and the toe).
7. **Uniform Soil Properties:** The soil is assumed homogeneous and isotropic throughout the failure mass.

## 2.2 The Logic Behind Each Step — "Why?" and "How?"

| Step | Why is it done? | How is it implemented? |
|------|----------------|----------------------|
| Draw slip circle | Failure surface is the locus of maximum shear stress | Trial-and-error: choose center O on a grid above slope |
| Draw friction circle | Captures the φ-rotation of reaction | Draw concentric circle with radius R sin φ |
| Divide into slices | Resolves distributed weights | 5–8 vertical slices of equal width b |
| Locate line of action of W | Weight acts at the centroid of each slice | Geometric centroid of trapezoidal slice |
| Locate point of tangency | Reaction passes through intersection of slip circle and friction circle | Geometric construction on graph paper |
| Assume inter-slice force direction | Removes indeterminacy | Parallel to average slope line |
| Construct force polygon | Equilibrium of all slices | Head-to-tail vector addition |
| Compute FOS | Measures stability | Ratio of resisting moment to driving moment |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Friction Circle Radius | $r_f = R \sin\phi$ | m | Inner circle radius for reaction tangency |
| 2 | Cohesive Force along arc | $C = c \cdot L$ | kN/m | Total cohesive resistance = cohesion × arc length |
| 3 | Weight of slice | $W_i = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h_i$ | kN/m | Slice weight (γ = unit weight of soil) |
| 4 | Cohesive Moment | $M_c = c \cdot L \cdot R$ | kN·m/m | Resisting moment due to cohesion |
| 5 | Driving Moment | $M_d = \Sigma W_i \cdot x_i$ | kN·m/m | Sum of weight × horizontal lever arm |
| 6 | Factor of Safety | $FOS = \dfrac{c \cdot L \cdot R}{\Sigma W_i \cdot x_i}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of resisting to driving moment |
| 7 | Critical FOS (KTU standard) | $FOS \geq 1.5$ | — | Minimum for permanent slopes; 1.25 for temporary |
| 8 | Taylor's Stability Number | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma \cdot H}$ | dimensionless | Used in stability charts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the Friction Circle Method, the **Resultant Reaction R'** is NOT directed through center O. It is offset and tangential to the friction circle. This is what makes the method more "realistic" than the Swedish circle method, where R' passes through O.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Friction Circle Method is used in:
- **Highway Embankment Design:** Verifying the stability of road embankments on soft clays.
- **Earthen Dam Design:** Checking the upstream and downstream slopes against rotational failure.
- **Railway Cuttings:** Ensuring cut slopes in cohesive soils do not slip.
- **Landfill Slopes:** Modern geotechnical application for waste containment systems.
- **Tailings Dams:** Critical in mining engineering for slope stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Friction Circle Method is **less conservative** than the Ordinary Method of Slices because it correctly accounts for frictional resistance, giving a **higher, more accurate Factor of Safety**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Construction Procedure

The Friction Circle Method procedure is **graphical and iterative**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step construction that KTU examiners expect students to write in the answer sheet.

### **Preliminary Data Required**
Before starting construction, the following data must be given or assumed:
- Geometry of the slope (height H, slope angle β)
- Soil properties: unit weight **γ**, cohesion **c**, angle of internal friction **φ**

---

### **STEP 1: Draw the Slope to Scale**
Using a convenient scale (e.g., 1 cm = 1 m or 1 cm = 2 m), draw the cross-section of the embankment or cutting on graph paper. Mark the toe, crest, and slope line clearly.

> **Why?** The construction requires accurate geometric proportions. Any scaling error propagates to all subsequent measurements.

---

### **STEP 2: Select a Trial Slip Circle**
Choose a trial **slip circle** with:
- **Center O** located above the slope
- **Radius R** such that the arc passes through (or near) the **toe** of the slope

> For a first trial, use the empirical suggestion: O is roughly on a horizontal line at a height **H** above the toe, located at a horizontal distance of about **2H to 4H** from the toe.

---

### **STEP 3: Draw the Friction Circle**
With the **same center O** and radius **r_f = R sin φ**, draw a concentric inner circle. This is the **Friction Circle** (or φ-circle).

> **Example Calculation:** If R = 10 m and φ = 20°, then r_f = 10 × sin(20°) = 10 × 0.342 = **3.42 m**.

---

### **STEP 4: Divide the Slip Mass into Vertical Slices**
Divide the soil mass enclosed between the slip circle and the slope profile into **5 to 8 vertical slices** of equal width **b**. Number them 1, 2, 3, ... from one edge to the other.

> For accuracy, slices should be of **equal chord length** on the arc, but for simplicity, equal width is used.

---

### **STEP 5: Determine the Weight of Each Slice**
For each slice **i**:
- Measure the **average height** $h_i$ of the slice (mean of left and right ordinates at the slice edges)
- Compute the weight:
  
  $$W_i = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h_i$$

- Mark the **line of action** of $W_i$ as a vertical line passing through the **centroid** of the slice.

> **Note:** $\gamma$ is the **bulk unit weight** (not dry unit weight) because total stress analysis is typically used.

---

### **STEP 6: Locate the Point of Application of the Resultant Reaction**
For each slice:
- Find the **point of intersection P_i** of the **slip circle** and the **Friction Circle** on the lower (curved) side of the slice.
- The **line of action of the resultant reaction R'_i** on the base of the slice passes through this point **P_i**.

> **Geometric Reasoning:** Since the reaction is inclined at angle φ to the normal (which is radial), and the radial line intersects the friction circle at P_i, the reaction R'_i acts along the tangent drawn at P_i (i.e., perpendicular to the radial line at P_i).

---

### **STEP 7: Assume Direction of Inter-slice Forces**
The forces on the **vertical interfaces** between adjacent slices are assumed to be **parallel to the average slope** (i.e., parallel to the line joining the toe and the crest).

Mark these inter-slice forces as $E_1, E_2, \ldots$ on the diagram. They are **equal and opposite** on adjacent slices (Newton's third law).

---

### **STEP 8: Construct the Force Polygon**
For each slice, four forces act:
1. **Weight** $W_i$ (vertical, downward)
2. **Resultant Reaction** $R'_i$ (along the tangent at $P_i$)
3. **Left inter-slice force** $E_{i-1}$ (parallel to slope)
4. **Right inter-slice force** $E_i$ (parallel to slope, opposite to $E_{i-1}$)

For **equilibrium**, these four forces must form a **closed polygon** (head-to-tail).

**Constructional Method:**
- Start with vector $W_1$ vertically downward.
- From its head, draw a line parallel to the average slope (for $E_1$).
- From there, draw a line tangent to the friction circle through $P_1$ (for $R'_1$).
- Close back to the tail of $W_1$ — this closing vector is the reaction.
- Repeat for each slice.

---

### **STEP 9: Determine Factor of Safety (FOS)**
After graphical construction, the **FOS is computed** by taking moments about the center **O**:

$$FOS = \dfrac{\text{Resisting Moment}}{\text{Driving Moment}} = \dfrac{c \cdot L \cdot R + \Sigma N_i \tan\phi}{\Sigma W_i \cdot x_i}$$

where:
- $L$ = total arc length = $2\pi R \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{360°}$ (θ in degrees)
- $x_i$ = horizontal distance of line of action of $W_i$ from vertical through O
- $N_i$ = normal component of $R'_i$ (from polygon)

> In the simplified KTU form (when only c is considered along with friction):
> $$FOS = \dfrac{c \cdot L \cdot R}{\Sigma W_i \cdot x_i}$$

---

### **STEP 10: Repeat for Multiple Trial Circles**
Repeat Steps 2 to 9 for several trial slip circles (different centers and radii) and find the **minimum FOS**. The critical circle gives the **most probable failure surface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Most exam questions state "Procedure Only" — meaning you write Steps 1–10 as described above, supported with a clean diagram. Numerical solution is **not** required unless specifically asked in the data.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Derivation of Friction Circle Radius

**Problem:** Given a slip circle of radius **R = 8 m** and angle of internal friction **φ = 25°**, derive the radius of the friction circle.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Friction circle radius, } r_f &= R \sin\phi \\
&= 8 \times \sin(25°) \\
&= 8 \times 0.4226 \\
&= 3.381 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 3.38 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

This means the line of action of the reaction $R'$ is offset by **3.38 m** from the center **O**, rather than passing through it (as in the Ordinary Swedish Method).

## 3.3 Arc Length Derivation

For a slip circle subtending an angle **θ** (in degrees) at the center:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2\pi R \times \dfrac{\theta}{360°} \\
\text{or equivalently,} \quad L &= R \cdot \theta \quad \text{where } \theta \text{ is in radians}
\end{aligned}$$

**Example:** If R = 10 m and θ = 90°, then:

$$L = 2 \times \pi \times 10 \times \dfrac{90}{360} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 10 \times 0.25 = 15.71 \text{ m}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Friction Circle vs. Swedish Method

| Feature | Ordinary Swedish Method | Friction Circle Method |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Reaction direction | Passes through center **O** | Tangential to friction circle |
| φ considered? | No (assumed φ = 0) | Yes (full φ considered) |
| Friction circle drawn? | No | Yes, of radius R sin φ |
| Inter-slice forces | Parallel to slope (assumed) | Parallel to slope (assumed) |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (graphical precision needed) |
| Conservativeness | More conservative (lower FOS) | Less conservative (higher FOS) |
| Accuracy for c-φ soil | Underestimates FOS | More realistic FOS |
| KTU preference | Often asked as simpler question | Asked when "procedure" is required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Slope Geometry and Soil Data"] --> B["Step 1: Draw Slope to Scale"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Select Trial Slip Circle Center O and Radius R"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Draw Friction Circle radius R sin phi concentric with O"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Divide Slip Mass into 5 to 8 Vertical Slices"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Compute Slice Weights Wi and Mark Lines of Action"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Locate Intersection Points Pi on Slip Circle and Friction Circle"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Assume Inter-slice Forces Parallel to Average Slope"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Construct Force Polygon for Each Slice"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Compute FOS equals c L R divided by sum of Wi xi"]
    J --> K{"FOS less than 1.5 ?"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Repeat with Another Trial Circle"]
    K -->|No| M["Critical FOS Found - Design is Safe"]
    L --> C
    M --> N["End: Report Critical Circle and Minimum FOS"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forces on a Single Slice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Weight Wi acts vertically downward through centroid"] --> D["Slice i Equilibrium Polygon"]
    B["Reaction Ri acts along tangent at Pi on friction circle"] --> D
    C["Inter-slice force Ei on left and Ei+1 on right parallel to slope"] --> D
    D --> E["Closed Force Polygon yields Magnitude of Ri"]
    E --> F["Compute Normal Component Ni equals Ri cos phi"]
    F --> G["Compute Resisting Moment equals sum of ci Li R plus Ni tan phi"]
    G --> H["Compute Driving Moment equals sum of Wi xi"]
    H --> I["FOS equals Resisting Moment divided by Driving Moment"]
```

## 4.3 Constructional Schematic (Text Description)

Below is a labeled description of the standard Friction Circle Method diagram as it should be drawn in the KTU answer sheet. Since complex geometric drawings are difficult in Mermaid, this is a verbal schematic:

```
              O (Center of slip circle & friction circle)
              |
              |  ←——— r_f = R sin φ ———→  ●  (Friction circle)
              |                              :
              |   ...........●.............:..... ← Slip Circle (radius R)
              |  :                          :
              |  :    [Slice 3] [Slice 2]   : 
              |  :   |        |        |    :
              |  :   |   W_3  |   W_2  |    :
              |  :   |   ↓    |   ↓    |    :
              |  :___|________|________|____:  ← Toe
              |      ↑        ↑        ↑
              |     P_3      P_2      P_1
              |   (Intersection of slip & friction circle)
              
   Legend:
   - O = Center of both circles
   - R = Radius of slip circle (large arc)
   - r_f = R sin φ = Radius of friction circle (small arc)
   - W_i = Weight of slice i (vertical line at centroid)
   - P_i = Point on slip circle where reaction R'_i acts
   - The tangent to the friction circle at P_i = direction of R'_i
   - Average slope line: parallel to inter-slice forces E_i
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawing Tip for KTU Exam:** Use a sharp pencil, scale ruler, and protractor. Mark the center O clearly. Draw the friction circle slightly thinner/dashed to distinguish it from the slip circle. Use arrows on all force vectors to indicate direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Friction Circle Method and state its key assumption that distinguishes it from the Ordinary Method of Slices.**

**Model Answer:**
The Friction Circle Method is a limit equilibrium method used to analyze the stability of finite earth slopes against circular slip failure. **The key distinguishing assumption is that the resultant reaction force on the slip surface is inclined at the angle of internal friction (φ) to the normal of the slip surface, causing its line of action to be tangential to a concentric "Friction Circle" of radius R sin φ.** In the Ordinary Method of Slices, the reaction is assumed to pass through the center O, neglecting friction.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the radius of the Friction Circle? Explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The radius of the Friction Circle is given by:

$$r_f = R \sin\phi$$

where **R** is the radius of the slip circle and **φ** is the angle of internal friction. **Physical Significance:** It represents the offset of the line of action of the resultant reaction from the center O. The larger the value of φ, the larger the offset, indicating greater mobilization of frictional resistance. If φ = 0 (purely cohesive soil), the friction circle degenerates to a point at O, and the method reduces to the Ordinary Swedish Method.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the step-by-step procedure of the Friction Circle Method for analyzing the stability of an earth slope. State all assumptions clearly. [(a) 7 Marks for Procedure, (b) 7 Marks for Diagram + Assumptions]**

#### **Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure (7 Marks)**

The procedure consists of the following steps:

1. **Draw the slope** to a suitable scale (e.g., 1 cm = 1 m) on graph paper, showing the toe, crest, and slope profile.

2. **Assume a trial slip circle** with center O and radius R such that the arc passes through the toe of the slope. For the first trial, O may be taken on a horizontal line at height H above the toe, located at a horizontal distance of 2H to 4H from the toe.

3. **Draw the Friction Circle** concentric with the slip circle, having radius r_f = R sin φ.

4. **Divide the sliding mass** into 5 to 8 vertical slices of equal width b.

5. **Compute the weight of each slice** $W_i = \gamma \cdot b \cdot h_i$, where $h_i$ is the average height. Mark the line of action of $W_i$ as a vertical line through the centroid.

6. **Locate the points of application** of the resultant reaction on each slice. These are the points $P_i$ where the slip circle intersects the friction circle on the lower side of the slice.

7. **Assume the inter-slice forces** $E_i$ to be parallel to the average slope of the embankment.

8. **Construct force polygons** for each slice, considering $W_i$, $R'_i$, and the inter-slice forces. The polygon must close for equilibrium.

9. **Compute the Factor of Safety** as the ratio of resisting moment to driving moment about O:
   $$FOS = \dfrac{c \cdot L \cdot R + \Sigma N_i \tan\phi}{\Sigma W_i \cdot x_i}$$

10. **Repeat** for several trial circles to find the **minimum FOS** (critical circle).

#### **Part (b) — Diagram and Assumptions (7 Marks)**

**Key Assumptions:**
1. The failure surface is a circular arc.
2. The soil is homogeneous and isotropic.
3. Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion applies: $s = c + \sigma \tan\phi$.
4. The resultant reaction on the slip surface is inclined at angle φ to the normal.
5. The inter-slice forces are parallel to the average slope.
6. Two-dimensional (plane strain) analysis is used.

**Diagram:** (Refer to schematic in Section 4.3) — A neat diagram showing the slip circle (radius R), friction circle (radius R sin φ), vertical slices with weights, points $P_i$ of reaction application, and the inter-slice force directions parallel to the average slope.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Discuss the Friction Circle Method in detail. Compare it with the Ordinary Method of Slices, highlighting the role of the Friction Circle radius. [(a) 7 Marks for Detailed Discussion, (b) 7 Marks for Comparison]**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Discussion (7 Marks)**

The Friction Circle Method, developed as a refinement of the Swedish Circle Method, is a graphical limit equilibrium approach for analyzing slope stability. The **core idea** is that when a soil mass tends to slide along a circular arc, the reaction at the slip surface is not purely radial (as in the Swedish method) but inclined at the angle φ to the normal, due to mobilized friction.

**The Friction Circle Concept:**
- A concentric inner circle of radius $R \sin\phi$ is drawn.
- The line of action of the resultant reaction on the slip arc is **tangential** to this inner circle.
- The point of tangency is the point where the slip circle intersects the friction circle.

**Methodology:**
- The sliding mass is divided into vertical slices.
- For each slice, the weight $W_i$ and the resultant reaction $R'_i$ are determined graphically using a force polygon.
- The inter-slice forces are assumed parallel to the slope.
- FOS is computed by moment equilibrium about the center O.

**Significance of $R \sin\phi$:**
- This term represents the **geometric lever arm of friction** in the slope.
- Larger φ → larger friction circle → more frictional resistance mobilized → higher FOS.
- The method captures the **combined c-φ behavior** of soil realistically.

#### **Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Ordinary Method of Slices | Friction Circle Method |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Reaction line of action | Passes through center O | Tangential to friction circle (R sin φ) |
| Friction (φ) considered | No (φ = 0 effectively) | Yes |
| Cohesion (c) considered | Yes | Yes |
| Type of soil best suited | Purely cohesive (φ = 0) | c-φ soils (general) |
| FOS value | Lower (conservative) | Higher (realistic) |
| Graphical construction | Simpler | More complex |
| Accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Preferred use | Quick preliminary check | Detailed design analysis |

**Conclusion:** The Friction Circle Method is more accurate for c-φ soils because it correctly models the frictional resistance. However, it requires careful graphical construction and trial circles to find the critical failure surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Friction Circle:** Many students draw only the slip circle and skip drawing the friction circle. **Marks Deducted: 2-3 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong Reaction Direction:** Drawing $R'_i$ as radial (toward O) instead of tangential to the friction circle. **Marks Deducted: 2-3 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing the Formula:** Writing $r_f = R \tan\phi$ instead of $r_f = R \sin\phi$. **Marks Deducted: 1-2 marks.**
> 4. **No Diagram or Poor Diagram:** KTU examiners expect a labeled diagram. **Marks Deducted: 3-4 marks.**
> 5. **Missing Assumptions:** Not stating the assumptions explicitly. **Marks Deducted: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Not Mentioning Iterative Nature:** Forgetting that multiple trial circles are needed. **Marks Deducted: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The Friction Circle Method is a **graphical limit equilibrium** method for **circular slip surfaces**.
- ✅ The **Friction Circle** is concentric with the slip circle, with **radius $r_f = R \sin\phi$**.
- ✅ The **resultant reaction** on the slip surface is **tangential** to the friction circle, NOT passing through the center.
- ✅ The **inter-slice forces** are assumed **parallel to the average slope**.
- ✅ The **sliding mass** is divided into **5 to 8 vertical slices** of equal width.
- ✅ The **Factor of Safety** is computed by taking moments about the center **O**:
   $$FOS = \dfrac{c \cdot L \cdot R}{\Sigma W_i \cdot x_i}$$
- ✅ The method is **iterative**: multiple trial circles must be analyzed to find the **critical (minimum FOS)** circle.
- ✅ The method is **less conservative** than the Ordinary Swedish Method because it accounts for **frictional resistance**.
- ✅ For **purely cohesive soil** (φ = 0), the friction circle degenerates to a point, and the method **reduces to the Ordinary Method**.
- ✅ A **neat, labeled diagram** is **mandatory** in KTU exams — minimum **3-4 marks** are awarded for a clean diagram.
- ✅ Standard safety criterion: **FOS ≥ 1.5** for permanent slopes; **FOS ≥ 1.25** for temporary slopes.
- ✅ The Friction Circle Method is more accurate for **c-φ soils** (sandy clays, silts) than the Swedish Method.
- ✅ **Assumptions to always state:** circular slip surface, homogeneous soil, Mohr-Coulomb criterion, plane strain, inter-slice forces parallel to slope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taylor's Stability Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Taylor's Stability Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taylor's Stability Number (Ns)** is a dimensionless geotechnical coefficient introduced by **Donald W. Taylor (1937)** that characterizes the critical state of equilibrium of finite earth slopes. It is formally defined as the ratio of the **available shear strength (cohesion)** of the soil to the **gravitational driving stress** acting on a potential sliding mass of height $H$ and unit weight $\gamma$.

The mathematical representation in its canonical (KTU Board Exam) form is:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{c}{\gamma \, H}
$$

Where:
- $c$ = **Unit cohesion of the soil** [kN/m² or kPa]
- $\gamma$ = **Unit weight of the soil** [kN/m³]
- $H$ = **Vertical height of the slope** [m]

The corresponding **Factor of Safety** against sliding is:

$$
F \;=\; \frac{c}{N_s \, \gamma \, H}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When the soil is at the verge of failure ($F = 1$), the value of $N_s$ is the minimum value required to maintain equilibrium. The actual field $N_s$ is read from Taylor's stability charts for a given slope angle $\beta$ and effective friction angle $\phi$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **sandcastle on a beach**. The sand has tiny grains (friction) and a little stickiness from moisture (cohesion). The castle's slope will stand only up to a certain steepness and height. Taylor's Stability Number is the **"magic threshold"** that tells engineers how steep and how tall a soil slope can be built before it collapses.

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sticky sand pile | Cohesive soil ($c$) |
| Friction between grains | Internal friction ($\phi$) |
| Height of the pile | Slope height ($H$) |
| Density of wet sand | Unit weight ($\gamma$) |
| Slope steepness (angle of repose) | Slope angle ($\beta$) |

If we keep all soil properties constant, a **taller** slope collapses more easily — so $N_s$ must be small. If the soil is **highly cohesive**, it can stand higher — so $N_s$ is higher. **Taylor's Stability Number quantifies this balance.**

---

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights

> [!TIP]
> **What your module syllabus emphasizes:**
> 1. The **physical meaning** of $N_s$ as a *dimensionless ratio of strength to stress*.
> 2. The use of **Taylor's Stability Chart** to back-calculate either the allowable height $H$ or the factor of safety $F$.
> 3. Special case analysis for **$\phi_u = 0$ condition** (total stress method for saturated clays).
> 4. Importance in **Finite Slope Stability Analysis** alongside the Swedish Circle Method.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taylor's Stability Number (purely cohesive) | $N_s$ | $\approx 5.52$ | For $\beta \le 53^\circ$, $\phi = 0$ |
| Critical slope angle (purely cohesive) | $\beta_{crit}$ | $53^\circ$ | Limit for Taylor's circle |
| Unit weight of saturated clay | $\gamma_{sat}$ | $\mathbf{18}$ – $\mathbf{22}$ kN/m³ | Typical KTU design range |
| Unit weight of soft soil | $\gamma$ | $\mathbf{15}$ – $\mathbf{18}$ kN/m³ | Alluvial / deltaic Kuttanad soils |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Slope Analysis Methods

Taylor's framework broadly classifies slope stability into:

1. **Total Stress Analysis ($\phi_u = 0$ method)** — used for **saturated clays** where undrained shear strength governs short-term stability.
2. **Effective Stress Analysis ($\phi'$ method)** — used for **long-term stability** where pore water pressure is explicitly considered.
3. **Infinite Slope Analysis** — for long, planar slopes where failure plane is parallel to the surface.
4. **Finite Slope Analysis** — where the toe of the failure circle is at the slope face (Taylor's main contribution).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Taylor's Number

Taylor approached the slope stability problem by considering a **cylindrical slip surface** (a circular arc), with the assumption that the soil mass behaves as a **rigid-plastic material** governed by the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion. The driving moment (gravity × distance) must be balanced by the resisting moment (cohesion × arc length × radius).

Through rigorous mathematical derivation (covered exhaustively in Section 3), Taylor showed that the **minimum** factor of safety is uniquely determined by two parameters:

1. **Slope angle** $\beta$
2. **Effective angle of internal friction** $\phi$

For every $(\beta, \phi)$ pair, a single value of $N_s$ uniquely defines the failure condition. This reduction to a **two-parameter family of curves** is what makes Taylor's Stability Number so powerful for engineering practice.

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

The application of Taylor's Stability Number in a board exam problem follows this structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Identify slope geometry:** Measure (or extract from the problem) the slope height $H$ and the slope angle $\beta$.
- **Step 2 — Identify soil properties:** Extract cohesion $c$, unit weight $\gamma$, and effective friction angle $\phi$.
- **Step 3 — Read Taylor's Chart:** For the given $(\beta, \phi)$, look up the critical stability number $N_s$.
- **Step 4 — Compute Factor of Safety:**

$$
F \;=\; \frac{c}{N_s \, \gamma \, H}
$$

- **Step 5 — Interpret the result:**
  - $F > 1$ → Slope is **safe**.
  - $F = 1$ → Slope is at the **threshold of failure**.
  - $F < 1$ → Slope will **fail**; redesign required.

---

## 2.3 The Three Critical Regimes of Taylor's Chart

Taylor's Stability Number behaves differently in three distinct slope-angle regimes:

| Regime | Slope Angle Range | Governing Mechanism | Stability Number Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Mild Slope** | $\beta < 53^\circ$ | **Toe failure** — slip circle exits at slope toe | $N_s = 5.52$ (constant for $\phi = 0$) |
| **II. Critical Slope** | $\beta = 53^\circ$ | **Base failure initiation** | Transitional — $N_s = 5.52$ boundary |
| **III. Steep Slope** | $\beta > 53^\circ$ | **Base failure** — slip circle passes below the toe | $N_s$ decreases sharply with $\beta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For $\phi_u = 0$ condition (saturated clays), $N_s$ is independent of $\beta$ as long as $\beta \le 53^\circ$, making **Taylor's circle** universal in this regime.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master reference table. Memorize these equations verbatim — they appear in nearly every board exam on slope stability.**

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma H}$ | $c, \gamma, H$ | At the **verge of failure** ($F = 1$) |
| 2 | $F = \dfrac{c}{N_s \, \gamma \, H}$ | — | To compute **Factor of Safety** |
| 3 | $H_{cr} = \dfrac{c}{N_s \, \gamma}$ | $H_{cr}$ = critical height | To find **maximum stable height** |
| 4 | $N_s = f(\beta, \phi)$ | Read from Taylor's chart | For **c-$\phi$ soil** |
| 5 | $N_s = 5.52$ | $\phi_u = 0$, $\beta \le 53^\circ$ | **Saturated clay, total stress** |
| 6 | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma H F}$ | Same as Eq. 2 (rearranged) | For **stability check** |
| 7 | $F_c = N_s \cdot \gamma \cdot H$ | $F_c$ = critical cohesion | To back-calculate required $c$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Trap (frequent mark loss):** Always express $\gamma$ in **kN/m³**, $H$ in **metres**, and $c$ in **kPa** (= kN/m²). The product $\gamma H$ is therefore in **kN/m²**, matching the units of $c$. Mixing CGS/SI units is the #1 cause of arithmetric errors in KTU exams.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Taylor's Number is Used in the Real World

1. **Highway Embankment Design** — Kerala's NH-66 and hill road cuttings (Wayanad, Idukki) require slope stability checks using Taylor's chart.
2. **Railway Cutting Slopes** — Konkan Railway and proposed Kerala Semi-HSR use Taylor's analysis.
3. **Earthen Dam Design** — Idukki and Mullaperiyar earthen saddle dams.
4. **Landfill Slope Design** — Modern municipal solid waste facilities in Kochi and Thiruvananthapuram.
5. **Excavation Support** — Deep basement excavations in urban Kerala (Kochi Metro, Smart City projects).
6. **Landslide Remediation** — Kuttanad, Wayanad, and Ponmudi regions with recurring slope failures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Taylor's Stability Number from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation follows **Taylor's classical 1937 friction-circle approach** as adapted in modern KTU reference texts. It assumes:
> - 2D plane strain condition
> - Cylindrical (circular) slip surface of radius $R$
> - Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion
> - Homogeneous, isotropic soil
> - No seismic loading

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Slip Surface

Consider a finite slope of height $H$ inclined at angle $\beta$ to the horizontal. A circular slip surface of radius $R$ is assumed, with the center of rotation located at point $O$.

The slip arc subtends an angle $2\alpha$ at the center (where $\alpha$ is the half-angle of the arc). The total arc length is therefore:

$$
L \;=\; 2 \alpha R
$$

### Step 2 — Driving Moment (M_d)

The driving moment is produced by the weight $W$ of the sliding soil mass acting through its center of gravity at a horizontal distance $\ell$ from the center $O$:

$$
M_d \;=\; W \cdot \ell
$$

Expressed in terms of the slope geometry and unit weight $\gamma$:

$$
M_d \;=\; \gamma \cdot R^3 \cdot f_1(\alpha, \beta)
$$

where $f_1(\alpha, \beta)$ is a dimensionless geometric function of the arc angle and slope angle.

### Step 3 — Resisting Moment from Cohesion (M_c)

The cohesive resistance acts tangentially along the entire arc length, producing a resisting moment:

$$
M_c \;=\; c \cdot L \cdot R \;=\; c \cdot (2\alpha R) \cdot R \;=\; 2 c \alpha R^2
$$

### Step 4 — Resisting Moment from Friction (M_φ)

The frictional resistance is mobilized along the slip surface. Taylor integrated this using the **friction-circle method**, leading to:

$$
M_{\phi} \;=\; \gamma \cdot R^3 \cdot f_2(\alpha, \beta, \phi)
$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium Equation

At incipient failure, the sum of resisting moments equals the driving moment:

$$
M_d \;=\; M_c + M_{\phi}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
\gamma R^3 f_1(\alpha, \beta) \;=\; 2 c \alpha R^2 + \gamma R^3 f_2(\alpha, \beta, \phi)
$$

### Step 6 — Isolation of the Critical Condition

Taylor minimized the safety factor with respect to the slip-circle parameters (center position, radius $R$, arc angle $2\alpha$). The critical condition — when the minimum factor of safety equals unity — yields:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{c}{\gamma H} \;=\; f(\beta, \phi)
$$

The closed-form simplification gives the canonical Taylor expression:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{1}{m - n \cdot \tan\phi}
$$

where $m$ and $n$ are functions of $\beta$ derived from the geometric integration.

---

## 3.2 Special Case Derivation — Purely Cohesive Soil ($\phi = 0$)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-tested case** in KTU exams. Derivation shown in full.

### Step 1 — Set $\phi = 0$ in the General Equation

When $\phi = 0$, the friction term vanishes, and the equation reduces to:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{1}{m}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Critical Slope

Taylor showed that for $\beta \le 53^\circ$, the failure circle is a **Taylor's circle** (also called the *midpoint circle*) with the following geometric property:

$$
\frac{H}{R} \;=\; \sin\beta
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $m$

For the midpoint circle geometry, the geometric coefficient evaluates to:

$$
m \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \sin\beta \cos\beta} \cdot \left[ \frac{\pi}{2} - \beta \right] + \tan\beta
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at $\beta = 0^\circ$ (vertical cut, flat ground)

When $\beta = 0$:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{\pi + 2}{\pi - 0} \;\approx\; 5.52
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Constancy in Mild Slope Regime

For $0 \le \beta \le 53^\circ$, the value of $N_s$ remains **practically constant at 5.52**, because the geometric compensation in $m$ cancels the effect of $\beta$.

Therefore:

$$
N_s \;=\; 5.52 \quad \text{for} \quad \phi = 0, \quad \beta \le 53^\circ
$$

### Step 6 — Critical Height Expression

Substituting back into $H_{cr}$:

$$
H_{cr} \;=\; \frac{c}{N_s \, \gamma} \;=\; \frac{c}{5.52 \, \gamma}
$$

This is the **classic Taylor's formula for the critical height of an unsupported vertical cut in saturated clay**, used extensively in KTU board problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> [!TIP]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> An embankment is to be constructed at a slope angle of $\beta = 30^\circ$ and a height of $H = 8$ m. The soil has a unit cohesion $c = 30$ kPa, unit weight $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, and effective friction angle $\phi = 15^\circ$. Using Taylor's Stability Chart, determine the factor of safety against slope failure.

### Step 1 — Read Taylor's Chart

For $\beta = 30^\circ$ and $\phi = 15^\circ$, Taylor's Stability Chart gives:

$$
N_s \;=\; 0.10
$$

(Students should verify this from their KTU-supplied Taylor's chart in the exam.)

### Step 2 — Apply the Factor of Safety Equation

$$
F \;=\; \frac{c}{N_s \, \gamma \, H}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
F \;=\; \frac{30}{0.10 \times 18 \times 8}
$$

$$
F \;=\; \frac{30}{14.4}
$$

$$
F \;=\; 2.083
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Result

Since $F \approx 2.08 > 1$, the slope is **safe** with a comfortable margin. The slope can be made steeper, or the height can be increased, until $F$ drops to the design threshold (typically $F = 1.5$ for permanent slopes per IS 7894).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variation of $N_s$ with Slope Parameters

| $\beta$ (deg) | $\phi = 0^\circ$ | $\phi = 5^\circ$ | $\phi = 10^\circ$ | $\phi = 15^\circ$ | $\phi = 20^\circ$ | $\phi = 25^\circ$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 5.52 | 6.20 | 7.00 | 7.95 | 9.10 | 10.50 |
| 10 | 5.52 | 6.10 | 6.80 | 7.60 | 8.55 | 9.70 |
| 20 | 5.52 | 5.90 | 6.40 | 7.00 | 7.70 | 8.55 |
| 30 | 5.52 | 5.60 | 5.90 | 6.30 | 6.80 | 7.40 |
| 40 | 5.52 | 5.30 | 5.40 | 5.60 | 5.85 | 6.20 |
| 50 | 5.52 | 4.95 | 4.90 | 4.95 | 5.00 | 5.10 |
| 60 | 4.50 | 4.40 | 4.35 | 4.30 | 4.25 | 4.20 |
| 75 | 2.70 | 2.60 | 2.55 | 2.50 | 2.45 | 2.40 |
| 90 | 1.80 | 1.75 | 1.70 | 1.65 | 1.60 | 1.55 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the matrix:** As $\phi$ **increases**, $N_s$ **increases** (more frictional strength → slope is more stable). As $\beta$ **increases** (steeper slope), $N_s$ **decreases** (slope is less stable). For $\phi = 0$ and $\beta \le 53^\circ$, $N_s$ is locked at **5.52**.

---

## 3.5 Depth Factor — Submerged Slopes

When the failure surface extends below the slope toe (base failure regime), Taylor introduced a **depth factor**:

$$
D \;=\; \frac{H'}{H}
$$

where $H'$ is the depth of the hard stratum below the slope toe. For $D > 0$, $N_s$ is read from a modified chart with depth factor curves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Taylor's Stability Number Decision Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Slope Stability Problem] --> B{Identify Slope Geometry}
    B --> B1[Slope height H]
    B --> B2[Slope angle beta]
    A --> C{Identify Soil Properties}
    C --> C1[Cohesion c]
    C --> C2[Unit weight gamma]
    C --> C3[Friction angle phi]
    B1 --> D[Step 1: Tabulate Inputs]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E{Soil Type Decision}
    E -->|phi equals 0| F[Use Total Stress Method]
    E -->|phi greater than 0| G[Use Effective Stress Method]
    F --> F1[Check beta condition]
    F1 -->|beta less than 53 degrees| F2[Use Ns equals 5.52]
    F1 -->|beta greater than 53 degrees| F3[Use Taylor Chart with beta correction]
    G --> G1[Read Ns from Taylor Chart]
    F2 --> H[Step 2: Compute Factor of Safety]
    F3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[Apply F equals c divided by Ns times gamma times H]
    I --> J[Step 3: Evaluate F]
    J -->|F greater than 1| K1[Safe Slope]
    J -->|F equals 1| K2[Marginally Stable]
    J -->|F less than 1| K3[Unsafe: Redesign Required]
    K1 --> L[Step 4: Optional Check]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> M[Reduce H or Increase c]
    M --> D
    L --> N[End: Document Result]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Failure Mechanism Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        P1[Slope Height H]
        P2[Slope Angle beta]
        P3[Cohesion c]
        P4[Unit Weight gamma]
        P5[Friction Angle phi]
    end

    subgraph CORE[TAYLOR ANALYSIS CORE]
        Q1{Total Stress or Effective Stress}
        Q2{beta versus 53 degrees}
        Q3[Midpoint Circle Geometry]
        Q4[Base Failure Geometry]
        Q5[Chart Lookup Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[ENGINEERING OUTPUT]
        R1[Stability Number Ns]
        R2[Factor of Safety F]
        R3[Critical Height Hcr]
        R4[Required Cohesion Fc]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q1
    P5 --> Q1
    Q1 -->|phi zero| Q2
    Q1 -->|phi nonzero| Q5
    Q2 -->|yes| Q3
    Q2 -->|no| Q4
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q5 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R1 --> R3
    R1 --> R4
```

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationships Between Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Taylor Stability Number Ns]
    ROOT --> R1[Cohesion c increases Ns]
    ROOT --> R2[Height H decreases Ns at failure]
    ROOT --> R3[Unit weight gamma decreases Ns]
    ROOT --> R4[Friction angle phi increases Ns]
    ROOT --> R5[Slope angle beta decreases Ns]

    R1 --> APP1[Stronger soil permits steeper slopes]
    R2 --> APP2[Taller slopes need proportionally higher c]
    R3 --> APP3[Denser soils require more cohesion]
    R4 --> APP4[Frictional soils are inherently more stable]
    R5 --> APP5[Steeper slopes reduce margin of safety]

    APP1 --> ENG[Engineering Implication: For Kerala lateritic and alluvial soils, c must be measured precisely by UU triaxial test]
    APP2 --> ENG
    APP3 --> ENG
    APP4 --> ENG
    APP5 --> ENG
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Slip Surface Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric configuration of a Taylor-type finite slope failure (described as block layout):**

```
+-----------------------------------+
|     GROUND SURFACE (TOP)          |
|     .............................|
|     ::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::|
|     :    SOIL MASS ABOVE :::::::::|
|     :    SLIP SURFACE     ::::::::|
|     :                      :::::::|   beta (slope angle)
|     :                       ::::::|
|     :                        :::::|  ----> SLOPE FACE
|     :                         ::::|  |
|     :                          :::|  | H (slope height)
|     :                           ::|  |
|     :                            :|  |
|     ::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::|  V
|     ..............................|
|     ..............................|
|                                   |
|     === TOE OF SLOPE ===          |
+-----------------------------------+
```

The cylindrical slip arc passes **through the toe** for mild slopes ($\beta \le 53^\circ$) and **below the toe** for steep slopes ($\beta > 53^\circ$). The center $O$ lies on a vertical line drawn from the midpoint of the slope, at a height that Taylor's geometry prescribes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Taylor's Stability Number. What is its significance in the analysis of finite slopes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Taylor's Stability Number $N_s$ is defined as the ratio of the unit cohesion $c$ of the soil to the product of the unit weight $\gamma$ and the height $H$ of the slope:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{c}{\gamma \, H}
$$

It is a **dimensionless coefficient** used to evaluate the stability of finite slopes. Its significance lies in:
1. Reducing a complex slope stability problem to a chart-based lookup using two parameters ($\beta$ and $\phi$).
2. Providing a direct means to compute the Factor of Safety: $F = \dfrac{c}{N_s \, \gamma \, H}$.
3. Enabling rapid determination of the critical slope height: $H_{cr} = \dfrac{c}{N_s \, \gamma}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is Taylor's Stability Number constant ($N_s = 5.52$) for a purely cohesive soil with slope angle less than $53^\circ$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a purely cohesive soil ($\phi_u = 0$), the only resisting force along the slip surface is the cohesive strength. Taylor showed that for $\beta \le 53^\circ$, the critical slip circle is a **midpoint circle** (Taylor's circle), and the geometric compensation between the arc length and the moment arm yields a constant value of $N_s$. Specifically, evaluating Taylor's friction-circle integral for $\phi = 0$ and $\beta \le 53^\circ$ gives:

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{\pi + 2}{\pi} \;\approx\; 5.52
$$

This value is **independent of $\beta$** because as the slope becomes steeper, the slip circle geometry changes in such a way that the resisting moment per unit driving stress remains invariant. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive Taylor's Stability Number expression for a finite slope of height $H$ and slope angle $\beta$, using the friction circle method. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cutting of $H = 10$ m depth is to be made in a saturated clay ($\phi_u = 0$) with unit cohesion $c = 40$ kPa and unit weight $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. The proposed slope angle is $\beta = 30^\circ$. Determine the factor of safety against slope failure using Taylor's method. Comment on whether the proposed slope is stable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to be presented in the exam:

- Assume a cylindrical slip surface of radius $R$ and arc angle $2\alpha$. (1 mark)
- Write driving moment: $M_d = W \ell = \gamma R^3 f_1(\alpha, \beta)$. (1 mark)
- Write cohesive resisting moment: $M_c = c L R = 2 c \alpha R^2$. (1 mark)
- Write frictional resisting moment via friction circle: $M_\phi = \gamma R^3 f_2(\alpha, \beta, \phi)$. (1 mark)
- Equate $M_d = M_c + M_\phi$ and minimize with respect to $\alpha$. (1 mark)
- Derive closed form: $N_s = c / (\gamma H) = f(\beta, \phi)$. (1 mark)
- State the **assumptions**: 2D plane strain, homogeneous soil, Mohr-Coulomb criterion, no seepage, circular slip surface. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:** (1 mark)
- $H = 10$ m
- $\beta = 30^\circ$
- $c = 40$ kPa
- $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³
- $\phi_u = 0$

**Step 2 — Determine $N_s$:** (2 marks)
Since $\phi_u = 0$ and $\beta = 30^\circ \le 53^\circ$, use:

$$
N_s \;=\; 5.52
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Factor of Safety:** (2 marks)

$$
F \;=\; \frac{c}{N_s \, \gamma \, H} \;=\; \frac{40}{5.52 \times 19 \times 10}
$$

$$
F \;=\; \frac{40}{1048.8} \;\approx\; 0.0381
$$

**Step 4 — Comment:** (2 marks)
Since $F \approx 0.038 \ll 1$, the slope is **highly unstable** and will fail catastrophically. The slope must be redesigned with:
- A flatter slope angle ($\beta < 30^\circ$), or
- A reduction in height via benching, or
- Soil improvement (lime/cement stabilization) to increase $c$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain with the help of a neat sketch the three regimes of slope failure as per Taylor's classification. How does the value of $N_s$ vary in each regime for $\phi = 0$ soil? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slope is to be constructed at $\beta = 45^\circ$ and $H = 12$ m with soil having $c = 25$ kPa, $\phi = 10^\circ$, and $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³. Using Taylor's Stability Chart, compute the factor of safety. If the factor of safety is less than 1.5, suggest two engineering remediation measures. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three Regimes (7 marks):**

**Regime I — Toe Failure ($\beta < 53^\circ$):** (2 marks)
The slip circle passes through the toe of the slope. For $\phi = 0$, $N_s = 5.52$ (constant).

**Regime II — Critical Transition ($\beta = 53^\circ$):** (2 marks)
At this exact angle, the geometry of failure begins to change. The slip circle starts to dip below the toe. $N_s$ is still approximately $5.52$ at the boundary.

**Regime III — Base Failure ($\beta > 53^\circ$):** (2 marks)
The slip circle passes below the toe. $N_s$ decreases sharply with increasing $\beta$. Taylor's chart gives explicit $N_s$ values depending on the depth factor.

**Sketch:** (1 mark)
Draw the three circle positions on a slope diagram showing toe failure, transitional, and base failure configurations.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify data:** (1 mark)
- $\beta = 45^\circ$, $H = 12$ m, $c = 25$ kPa, $\phi = 10^\circ$, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³.

**Step 2 — Read $N_s$ from Taylor's Chart:** (2 marks)
For $\beta = 45^\circ$ and $\phi = 10^\circ$, Taylor's chart gives:

$$
N_s \;\approx\; 0.115
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Factor of Safety:** (2 marks)

$$
F \;=\; \frac{25}{0.115 \times 17 \times 12} \;=\; \frac{25}{23.46} \;\approx\; 1.066
$$

**Step 4 — Decision and Remediation:** (2 marks)
Since $F \approx 1.07 < 1.5$, the slope is **below the IS 7894 design threshold**. Recommended remediation:

1. **Slope flattening:** Reduce $\beta$ from $45^\circ$ to approximately $30^\circ$ to bring $F$ above 1.5.
2. **Soil reinforcement:** Install geogrid reinforcement or construct a **counter berm** at the toe to increase the resisting moment.
3. **Drainage improvement:** Install horizontal drains to reduce pore water pressure and increase effective stress.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Writing $\gamma = 1.8$ (in g/cm³) but $c$ in kPa and $H$ in m. Always convert to a consistent SI set: $c$ in kPa, $\gamma$ in kN/m³, $H$ in m.
>
> 2. **Wrong Chart Reading (–3 marks):** Reading $N_s$ from the wrong curve on Taylor's chart. Always confirm you are using the **chart for $\phi_u = 0$** OR the **chart for $\phi \neq 0$** — never mix them.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Critical $53^\circ$ Limit (–2 marks):** Forgetting to check whether $\beta \le 53^\circ$ and directly applying $N_s = 5.52$ even when $\beta > 53^\circ$ (where $N_s < 5.52$). This leads to **overestimated** $F$ values and unsafe designs.
>
> 4. **Confusing Total vs Effective Stress (–3 marks):** Using $\phi = 0$ when the problem gives $\phi' > 0$ for an effective stress analysis. Always read the problem statement carefully.
>
> 5. **No Sketch / Diagram (–1 mark):** KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks if no figure or free-body diagram is drawn for slope stability problems. Always include a labeled cross-section.
>
> 6. **Skipping Assumptions (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to list the assumptions of Taylor's analysis at the start of a derivation question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Taylor's Stability Number**

- **Definition:** $N_s = c / (\gamma H)$ — dimensionless ratio of cohesion to gravitational stress. **(Must-memorize)**
- **Factor of Safety:** $F = c / (N_s \cdot \gamma \cdot H)$. **(Must-memorize)**
- **Critical Height:** $H_{cr} = c / (N_s \cdot \gamma)$. **(Must-memorize)**
- **Special Case ($\phi = 0$, $\beta \le 53^\circ$):** $N_s = 5.52$. **(Board-favorite number)**
- **Critical Slope Angle:** $\beta_{crit} = 53^\circ$ for $\phi = 0$ soil. **(Must-memorize)**
- **Failure Regimes:** Toe failure ($\beta < 53^\circ$), Transitional ($\beta = 53^\circ$), Base failure ($\beta > 53^\circ$).
- **Chart Lookup:** $N_s = f(\beta, \phi)$ — always read from the correct Taylor's chart.
- **Unit Consistency:** $c$ in kPa, $\gamma$ in kN/m³, $H$ in m.
- **Design Threshold (IS 7894):** Minimum acceptable $F = 1.5$ for permanent slopes, $F = 1.3$ for temporary slopes.
- **Method Validity:** Taylor's method assumes **circular slip surface**, homogeneous soil, and Mohr-Coulomb failure.
- **Origin:** Developed by **Donald W. Taylor in 1937**; uses the friction-circle method for integration.
- **Engineering Application:** Embankments, cuttings, earth dams, landfills, and slope remediation projects.
- **Limitation:** Does not account for seismic loading, layered soil, or complex pore-pressure distributions (use Bishop's or Morgenstern-Price methods for advanced cases).
- **Quick Diagnostic Rule of Thumb:** If $F < 1.5$, the slope needs redesign. If $F < 1.0$, the slope will fail under the given conditions.
- **Mnemonic to remember the regimes:** **"Toe-Below-Fifty-Three; Above-Fifty-Three-Base"** — Toe failures occur for slopes **below** $53^\circ$, base failures for slopes **above** $53^\circ$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Charts (Demo Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stability Charts (Demo Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stability Charts** are pre-computed, dimensionless graphical solutions used in Geotechnical Engineering to rapidly determine the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** against rotational slope failure (slip circle failure) for finite earth slopes constructed in homogeneous cohesive and cohesive-frictional (c–φ) soils. They were originally developed by **W. Fellenius (1936)** and significantly refined by **D. W. Taylor (1937)**, whose name is permanently associated with the **Taylor's Stability Number (Nₛ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Stability charts provide a closed-form, graphical alternative to iterative methods (like the method of slices) for computing the critical height of slopes. They are a mandatory topic in Module 1 of Foundation Engineering and frequently appear as a 7-mark or 14-mark analytical problem in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

Mathematically, Taylor's Stability Number is expressed as:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H_c}$$

where:
- $c$ = **cohesion** of the soil (kN/m² or kPa)
- $\gamma$ = **unit weight** of the soil (kN/m³)
- $H_c$ = **critical height** of the slope (m) at which failure is imminent (FoS = 1)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Tower" Intuition

Imagine stacking Lego blocks to form a sloped wall (like a ramp). Each block has a sticky base (representing **cohesion, c**) and a rough surface (representating **internal friction, φ**). The wall stands perfectly as long as:

1. The sticky base (cohesion) is strong enough to glue the blocks together.
2. The slope is not too steep (low **slope angle, β**).
3. The wall is not too tall (low **height, H**).

A **Stability Chart** is essentially a pre-calculated "danger map" — a graph that tells the engineer, at a glance: *"For your given slope angle β, slope height H, soil friction angle φ, and soil cohesion c, will the Lego wall collapse?"* The chart converts a complex 3D rotational failure geometry into a single dimensionless number lookup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bold):**
> - Standard unit weight of soil $\gamma$ = **18–22 kN/m³**
> - Water unit weight $\gamma_w$ = **9.81 kN/m³**
> - Typical cohesive soil friction angle $\phi$ = **0°** (saturated clay, undrained)
> - Typical c–φ soil friction angle $\phi$ = **5°–30°**

## 1.3 Why Stability Charts Matter in Practice

Stability charts eliminate the need for repetitive trial-circle computations. During the preliminary design of:
- **Highway embankments**
- **Earth dams**
- **Railway cuttings**
- **Open-pit mine walls**
- **Canal banks**

…the engineer can quickly read off the safe height or required soil strength, saving hours of manual trial calculations. They form the **foundation** for more advanced numerical methods (Limit Equilibrium Method, FEM-based slope analysis in PLAXIS).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Failure Surfaces Considered

Stability charts categorize slope failure into three primary modes based on the **depth factor, D** (ratio of depth of soft stratum below toe to slope height):

| Mode | Description | Toe Condition |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Toe Failure** | Slip circle passes through the toe of the slope | $D < 1$ |
| **Base Failure (Deep Seated)** | Slip circle extends deep below the toe | $D \geq 1$ (soft clay) |
| **Slope Failure (Face Failure)** | Slip circle exits on the slope face above the toe | Very steep slopes ($\beta > 53°$) |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow (The "Why" and "How")

**Step 1 — Identify Soil Type.**
Determine if the soil is purely cohesive ($\phi = 0°$) or c–φ soil. This dictates which chart family to use.

**Step 2 — Compute Taylor's Stability Number.**
The chart plots $N_s$ versus the slope angle $\beta$, parameterized by the friction angle $\phi$. Each curve is a boundary of stability for a particular $\phi$ value.

**Step 3 — Determine the Critical Height.**
Rearrange the stability number formula:
$$H_c = \frac{c}{N_s \cdot \gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Factor of Safety.**
For an actual slope of height $H$:
$$F = \frac{H_c}{H} = \frac{c}{N_s \cdot \gamma \cdot H}$$

Alternatively, the Factor of Safety with respect to cohesion:
$$F_c = \frac{c}{c_m} = \frac{\text{Actual Cohesion}}{\text{Mobilized Cohesion Required for Equilibrium}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Depth Factor (if applicable).**
For soils overlying a firm stratum at depth $D \cdot H$ below the toe, use modified stability charts (Taylor's $\phi = 0°$ chart with depth factor curves).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{c}{\gamma \cdot H_c}$ | Taylor's Stability Number (for $\phi = 0°$ chart) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $H_c = \dfrac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s}$ | Critical height of slope | m |
| 3 | $F = \dfrac{H_c}{H} = \dfrac{c}{N_s \cdot \gamma \cdot H}$ | Factor of Safety (against height) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_c = \dfrac{c}{c_d}$ | Factor of Safety with respect to cohesion | dimensionless |
| 5 | $c_d = c - \sigma \cdot \tan\phi$ | Developed (mobilized) cohesion | kN/m² |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{H'}{H}$ | Depth Factor (depth of firm stratum / slope height) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_s = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot H}{c}$ (inverted form on some charts) | Inverse Taylor's Number | dimensionless |
| 8 | $S_n = \dfrac{c}{F_c \cdot \gamma \cdot H}$ | Stability Number with Factor of Safety | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Avoid Markdown Table Syntax Errors:**
> Never use the pipe character $\vert$ inside table cells. Always use $\mid$ (or $\vert$) for absolute value or "divided by" expressions in formulas to prevent breaking the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stability charts are routinely used in:
- **KTU-designated site investigations** for embankment design in Kerala's lateritic and alluvial soil belts.
- **Quick preliminary design** before detailed Bishop's or Janbu's method of slices analysis.
- **Back-analysis** of failed slopes to determine the mobilized shear strength at the time of failure (essential for forensic geotechnical engineering).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Taylor's Stability Number (Conceptual Limit Equilibrium)

The derivation follows from the **Swedish Slip Circle Method (Fellenius, 1936)**, in which the sliding mass is assumed to rotate as a rigid body about the center of the slip circle.

### Step 1 — Define the Sliding Mass Geometry

Consider a slope of height $H$ with slope angle $\beta$ with respect to horizontal. A trial slip circle is assumed with:
- Radius $R$
- Center $O$ located at a distance $x$ (horizontal) and $y$ (vertical) from the crest
- Arc subtending angle $2\alpha$ at the center

### Step 2 — Resolve Forces on the Sliding Wedge

The weight $W$ of the sliding mass acts vertically downward through the centroid. It is resolved into:
- Normal component $N = W \cos\alpha$ (resisting, along the slip plane normal)
- Tangential component $T = W \sin\alpha$ (driving, along the slip plane)

### Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium About Center O

**Driving Moment** $M_d$ due to gravity:
$$M_d = W \cdot \bar{x}$$
where $\bar{x}$ is the horizontal distance from $O$ to the line of action of $W$.

**Resisting Moment** $M_r$ due to shear strength along the arc:
$$M_r = c \cdot L_c \cdot R + N \cdot \tan\phi \cdot R$$
where:
- $L_c$ = length of the slip arc
- $N \cdot \tan\phi$ = frictional resistance on the slip plane
- $R$ = moment arm (radius)

For the critical condition (limit equilibrium, $F = 1$):
$$M_d = M_r$$

### Step 4 — Express the Critical Height

After substituting the geometric relations $L_c = 2\alpha R$ and integrating the normal forces over the arc, Taylor (1937) arrived at the compact form:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H_c} \\
\Rightarrow H_c &= \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s}
\end{aligned}$$

where $N_s$ is purely a function of the slope angle $\beta$ and the friction angle $\phi$:
$$N_s = f(\beta, \phi)$$

### Step 5 — Generalize for Factor of Safety

For a slope of actual height $H$ (not necessarily critical) with desired factor of safety $F$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } F &= \frac{H_c}{H} \\
\text{then } H_c &= F \cdot H \\
\text{and } c_{\text{required}} &= N_s \cdot \gamma \cdot F \cdot H
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently, given a soil with cohesion $c$, the available Factor of Safety is:
$$F = \frac{c}{N_s \cdot \gamma \cdot H}$$

### Step 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A slope is to be constructed at an angle $\beta = 30°$ in a soil with $\phi = 0°$, $c = 30$ kN/m², and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Determine the critical height of the slope.

**Solution:**

From Taylor's $\phi = 0°$ chart (or standard tabulated values), for $\beta = 30°$ and $\phi = 0°$:

$$N_s = 0.178 \quad \text{(standard value from Taylor's chart)}$$

Substituting into the critical height formula:

$$H_c = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s}$$

$$H_c = \frac{30 \text{ kN/m}^2}{18 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times 0.178}$$

$$H_c = \frac{30}{3.204}$$

$$H_c \approx 9.36 \text{ m}$$

**Interpretation:** The slope will fail by toe circle if the actual height exceeds **9.36 m**. Hence, a safe design height with $F = 1.5$ would be:

$$H_{\text{design}} = \frac{H_c}{F} = \frac{9.36}{1.5} \approx 6.24 \text{ m}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Stability Chart Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Slope Geometry and Soil Data] --> B[Input Slope Angle beta]
    A --> C[Input Height H]
    A --> D[Input Soil Cohesion c]
    A --> E[Input Unit Weight gamma]
    A --> F[Input Friction Angle phi]
    B --> G[Identify Soil Type]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Saturated Clay phi=0}
    G --> I[c-phi Soil phi>0}
    H --> J[Use Taylor phi=0 Chart]
    I --> K[Use Taylor c-phi Chart]
    J --> L[Read Ns from Chart]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Hc = c divided by gamma times Ns]
    M --> N[Compare Hc with H]
    N --> O{Is Hc greater than H}
    O -->|Yes| P[Slope is Stable - Compute FoS]
    O -->|No| Q[Slope Fails - Reduce H or Increase c]
    P --> R[Report Critical Height and FoS]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mode of Slope Failure Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Depth_Conditions
        A1[Depth Factor D less than 1] --> A2[Toe Circle Failure]
        A3[Depth Factor D greater than 1] --> A4[Base Circle Failure]
        A5[Slope Angle greater than 53 deg] --> A6[Face Slope Failure]
    end
    A2 --> B[Read Toe Curve from Chart]
    A4 --> C[Read Base Curve from Chart]
    A6 --> D[Read Face Curve from Chart]
    B --> E[Unified Ns Value]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply Hc Formula]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Slip Circle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ground Surface Horizontal] --> B[Slope Face at angle beta]
    B --> C[Toe of Slope]
    C --> D[Slip Arc passes through Toe]
    D --> E[Center of Slip Circle O at distance x and y]
    E --> F[Radius R]
    F --> G[Sliding Mass rotates about O]
    G --> H[Driving Moment W times x_bar]
    G --> I[Resisting Moment c times Lc times R plus N tan phi times R]
    H --> J[Limit Equilibrium when M_d equals M_r]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Taylor Stability Number Ns derived]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Since physical drawing of a slip circle is not feasible in Mermaid, the above flowcharts capture the equivalent functional architecture — every geometric input and force-resolution step is mapped explicitly to its analytical counterpart.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Taylor's Stability Number and state its significance in slope stability analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
Taylor's Stability Number, denoted as $N_s$, is a dimensionless parameter defined as the ratio of cohesion to the product of unit weight and critical height of slope:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H_c}$$

It is significant because it consolidates the three most critical soil parameters ($c$, $\gamma$, $H$) into a single number that can be directly read off pre-computed stability charts. The chart expresses $N_s$ as a function of slope angle $\beta$ and friction angle $\phi$, eliminating the need for iterative slip-circle calculations during preliminary slope design.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Distinguish between a Toe Failure and a Base Failure in slope stability.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Toe Failure:** The slip circle passes exactly through the toe of the slope. This is the most common failure mode and is critical for slopes where the soil mass is homogeneous and extends to a considerable depth below the toe.
- **Base Failure:** The slip circle does not pass through the toe but curves deep into the foundation soil and emerges on the ground surface beyond the slope. It occurs when a soft clay layer underlies the slope at a finite depth (typically when Depth Factor $D > 1$).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Swedish Slip Circle Method of analyzing the stability of finite slopes in a $\phi = 0°$ soil. Derive the expression for Taylor's Stability Number.

**Model Solution:**

The Swedish Slip Circle Method assumes:
1. The failure surface is a circular arc.
2. The soil mass above the arc behaves as a rigid body and rotates about the center of the arc.
3. The factor of safety is the ratio of resisting moment to driving moment about the center.

**Derivation:**

The driving moment about the center $O$ is:

$$M_d = W \cdot \bar{x}$$

where $W$ is the weight of the sliding wedge and $\bar{x}$ is the horizontal distance from the line of action of $W$ to $O$.

The resisting moment is:

$$M_r = c \cdot L_c \cdot R$$

where $L_c$ is the arc length and $R$ is the radius. For limit equilibrium ($F = 1$):

$$W \cdot \bar{x} = c \cdot L_c \cdot R$$

Substituting the geometric relation $\bar{x} = R \cdot \sin\alpha$ and $L_c = 2\alpha R$, and integrating the soil weight $W = \gamma \cdot (\text{area of sliding mass})$:

$$c = \frac{1}{2\alpha} \cdot \frac{W \cdot \sin\alpha}{R}$$

Taylor expressed this in the compact dimensionless form:

$$N_s = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot H_c} = f(\beta, \phi)$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cutting is to be made in a saturated clay ($\phi = 0°$) for which $c = 40$ kN/m² and $\gamma = 20$ kN/m³. The slope angle is to be $45°$. Determine the safe height of the cutting using a factor of safety of 1.5.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** From Taylor's $\phi = 0°$ chart, for $\beta = 45°$ and $\phi = 0°$:

$$N_s = 0.262 \quad \text{(standard value from Taylor's chart for toe failure)}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the critical height:

$$H_c = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s} = \frac{40}{20 \times 0.262}$$

$$H_c = \frac{40}{5.24} \approx 7.63 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Factor of Safety:

$$H_{\text{safe}} = \frac{H_c}{F} = \frac{7.63}{1.5} \approx 5.09 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The safe height of the cutting is approximately **5.09 m**. Beyond this height, the slope will fail by toe slip circle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A slope of height 8 m is constructed at an angle of 30° in a soil with $\phi = 10°$, $c = 25$ kN/m², and $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. Using Taylor's stability chart, determine the factor of safety of the slope.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** From Taylor's $c-\phi$ stability chart, for $\beta = 30°$ and $\phi = 10°$:

$$N_s = 0.116 \quad \text{(standard value from Taylor's c-phi chart)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the critical height:

$$H_c = \frac{c}{\gamma \cdot N_s} = \frac{25}{19 \times 0.116} = \frac{25}{2.204} \approx 11.34 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Factor of Safety:

$$F = \frac{H_c}{H} = \frac{11.34}{8} \approx 1.42$$

**Conclusion:** The slope has a Factor of Safety of **1.42** against rotational slip failure. Since this is greater than the minimum acceptable value of 1.5 for permanent slopes, the designer may need to either reduce the slope height, lower the slope angle, or improve the soil cohesion to meet the design criterion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are the limitations of Taylor's stability charts?

**Model Answer:**
1. **Homogeneous soil assumption:** Taylor's charts assume a uniform soil profile; layered soils require modification or numerical methods.
2. **2D analysis only:** They do not account for 3D end effects, which may be significant for short slopes.
3. **No seepage consideration:** Standard charts assume no pore water pressure; effective stress analysis requires separate treatment.
4. **No surcharge loads:** Charts do not directly handle external loads from foundations or traffic.
5. **No tension crack modeling:** Taylor's original charts do not incorporate the presence of a tension crack at the crest.
6. **Circular slip surface only:** Non-circular (e.g., log-spiral) failure surfaces in stratified soils are not covered.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Exam):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Students often forget that cohesion $c$ must be in kN/m² and unit weight $\gamma$ in kN/m³ to obtain $H_c$ in meters. Mixing kPa with kg/m³ is a frequent error.
> 2. **Wrong Chart Selection (–2 marks):** Using the $\phi = 0°$ chart for a c–φ soil (or vice versa) leads to a fundamentally incorrect $N_s$ value. Always read the chart label carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting to Apply Factor of Safety (–1 mark):** $H_c$ is the *critical* (failure) height. The *safe* height is always $H_c / F$.
> 4. **Depth Factor Ignorance (–2 marks):** When the firm stratum is within a finite depth below the toe, you MUST use the depth factor $D$ modified chart. Ignoring $D$ overestimates stability.
> 5. **Inverted Stability Number (–1 mark):** Some Indian textbooks (Punmia, Arora) define $N_s = \gamma H / c$ (inverted). Always verify the convention used in the problem statement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Taylor's Stability Number** $N_s = c / (\gamma H_c)$ is the cornerstone of slope stability charts.
- For $\phi = 0°$ soil, $N_s$ depends only on the slope angle $\beta$.
- For $c-\phi$ soil, $N_s$ depends on both $\beta$ and $\phi$.
- **Critical height** is the height at which $F = 1$ (imminent failure).
- **Safe height** $H_{\text{safe}} = H_c / F$.
- **Toe failure** is most common; **base failure** requires a soft underlying layer.
- Always verify the **chart convention** (some books plot $N_s$ versus $\beta$, others plot $\gamma H/c$).
- For depth factors $D > 1$, use the modified Taylor chart with depth parameter.
