- Stability charts are a **preliminary design tool** — detailed design requires method of slices (Fellenius, Bishop, Janbu).
- **Unit consistency** is the single most common cause of mark loss in KTU exams.
- Taylor's charts assume **no pore water pressure**; for submerged slopes, use effective stress parameters.
- The **Friction Circle Method** (also graphical) is an alternative to Taylor's approach for c–φ soils.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Foundations - Definition of Shallow and Deep Foundation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functions of Foundations - Definition of Shallow and Deep Foundation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Foundation?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology):**
> A **Foundation** is the lowest part of a structure that transmits the superimposed loads (dead load, live load, and other imposed loads) together with its own self-weight to the underlying soil or rock in such a manner that:
> 1. The **bearing capacity** of the soil stratum is not exceeded.
> 2. The **settlement** (total and differential) of the structure is within permissible limits.
> 3. The structure remains **stable** against overturning, sliding, and uplift.

Mathematically, the load transfer mechanism is expressed as:

$$
Q_{ult} \geq Q_{applied} = (Q_{dead} + Q_{live} + Q_{self-weight})
$$

where the applied load must be **less than or equal to the ultimate bearing capacity** of the foundation soil, and the corresponding settlement must be less than the permissible value prescribed by **IS 1904:1986 (Reaffirmed 2020)**.

---

## 1.2 Functions of a Foundation

A foundation performs **eight primary functions**. These are essential from both the KTU board exam perspective and real-world structural design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Principal Functions of a Foundation (KTU Board Favourite):**
>
> 1. **Load Distribution:** Distributes the concentrated structural load over a larger area of soil to keep the induced stress within the safe bearing capacity (SBC) of the soil.
> 2. **Load Transfer to Bearing Stratum:** Transfers the structural load to a firm, stable, and competent soil/rock layer lying at depth.
> 3. **Resistance Against Settlement:** Minimizes and controls both **uniform (total) settlement** and **differential settlement**, preventing cracks in the superstructure.
> 4. **Stability Against Overturning:** Provides adequate base width and anchorage to resist overturning moments caused by lateral forces (wind, seismic, water pressure).
> 5. **Stability Against Sliding:** Resists horizontal (shear) forces, ensuring the structure does not slide at the base.
> 6. **Anchorage Against Uplift:** Counteracts uplift forces caused by hydrostatic pressure, frost heave, or wind (e.g., transmission towers, underground tanks).
> 7. **Protection Against Scour & Erosion:** For foundations in riverbeds or near water bodies, prevents undermining due to scour.
> 8. **Provision of Working Platform:** Provides a level, firm, and dry working surface for the construction of the superstructure above.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body and the Building"

Imagine you are standing on **soft sand at the beach**. The moment you put your weight on one foot, your foot sinks deeply and you may even lose balance. But if you wear **snow shoes (or float on a raft)**, your weight gets distributed over a much larger area, and you stand firmly without sinking.

A **foundation does exactly this for a building**:
- The **superstructure** is like your body.
- The **load** is your body weight.
- The **soil** is the soft sand.
- The **foundation** is the snow shoe or raft — it spreads the concentrated load over a wide area so that the soil can safely support it.

If the soil close to the ground surface is too weak (like wet clay), we need a **deep foundation** — analogous to driving a **long pole deep into the mud until it rests on firm rock**, exactly like a tree's roots.

---

## 1.4 Definition of Shallow and Deep Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Shallow Foundation (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **Shallow Foundation** is one in which the **depth of foundation ($D_f$) is relatively small compared to its width ($B$)**, and the load is transferred primarily by **end bearing and skin friction** on the soil stratum immediately below the base. The depth-to-width ratio is typically:
>
> $$\frac{D_f}{B} \leq 1$$
>
> The load is supported mostly by the **shear resistance and bearing capacity of the soil directly beneath the footing base**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of Deep Foundation (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **Deep Foundation** is one in which the **depth of foundation ($D_f$) is much greater than its width ($B$)**, and the load is transferred to a **deep, competent stratum** either through **end bearing** (point bearing piles), **skin friction** (friction piles), or a **combination of both**. The depth-to-width ratio is typically:
>
> $$\frac{D_f}{B} > 4 \text{ to } 5$$
>
> Some researchers and IS codes accept $\frac{D_f}{B} > 1$ as a deep foundation, but the conservative KTU board value is $\geq 4$.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **Unit weight of concrete (RCC)** | $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³ |
| **Unit weight of PCC** | $\gamma_{pcc} = 22$ to 24 kN/m³ |
| **Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) of soil** | 100 – 450 kN/m² (cohesionless); 150 – 600 kN/m² (cohesive) |
| **Minimum depth of foundation ($D_f$)** | **0.9 m to 1.5 m** below GL (as per IS 1080) |
| **Shallow foundation depth ratio** | $D_f / B \leq 1$ |
| **Deep foundation depth ratio** | $D_f / B \geq 4$ to 5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Foundation Functions — A Structured Logical Breakdown

Let us now dissect **why** each function is essential, with a clear *Why → How* logic that KTU examiners love to test.

### 2.1.1 Load Distribution & Bearing Pressure Reduction
- **Why?** A column carries a load of, say, **2000 kN** with a base of $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$. The contact pressure on the soil would be $2000/0.25 = 8000$ kN/m² — which would **shear-fail** any normal soil.
- **How?** By providing a footing of size, say, $3 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$, the bearing pressure reduces to $2000/9 \approx 222$ kN/m² — within SBC of most soils.

### 2.1.2 Load Transfer to a Competent Stratum
- **Why?** If the topsoil is **black cotton soil** or **loose fill**, it is unsuitable to support the structure. A deep foundation is needed to bypass the weak layer.
- **How?** A pile is driven **through** the weak layer until its tip rests on **rock** or **dense sand** — transferring load via **end bearing**.

### 2.1.3 Settlement Control
- **Why?** **Differential settlement** causes **tilting, cracks, and structural failure** (e.g., the leaning Tower of Pisa).
- **How?** A well-designed foundation **equalizes the pressure bulb** and ensures uniform settlement across the structure.

### 2.1.4 Overturning and Sliding Stability
- **Why?** Lateral forces (wind on tall buildings, seismic forces) create overturning moments. The foundation must counteract this.
- **How?** Adequate **footing width**, **base shear keys**, and **anchorage** resist these forces.

### 2.1.5 Anchorage Against Uplift
- **Why?** Underground water exerts upward hydrostatic pressure; frost heave pushes foundations upward.
- **How?** The **self-weight of the foundation + soil above the footing** is designed to exceed the uplift force.

---

## 2.2 The KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Oriented)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Foundation Classification and Design**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Standard Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Depth Ratio for Shallow Foundation | $D_f / B \leq 1$ | Dimensionless | Classify as shallow |
| 2 | Depth Ratio for Deep Foundation | $D_f / B \geq 4$ | Dimensionless | Classify as deep |
| 3 | Net Bearing Pressure | $q_{net} = q - \gamma \cdot D_f$ | kN/m² | Allowable pressure check |
| 4 | Ultimate Bearing Pressure (Terzaghi) | $q_{u} = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q + 0.5 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma}$ | kN/m² | Bearing capacity calc. |
| 5 | Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) | $q_{s} = q_{u} / F$ | kN/m² | Design check |
| 6 | Load Carried by Pile (Point Bearing) | $Q_p = A_p \cdot q_p$ | kN | Deep foundation |
| 7 | Load Carried by Pile (Skin Friction) | $Q_s = \sum (A_{si} \cdot f_{si})$ | kN | Deep foundation |
| 8 | Total Pile Capacity | $Q_{u,pile} = Q_p + Q_s$ | kN | Ultimate capacity |
| 9 | Group Efficiency (n piles) | $\eta_g = Q_{g,ult} / (n \cdot Q_{u,single})$ | Dimensionless | Pile group design |
| 10 | Factor of Safety (IS 2911) | $F = 2.5$ to $3.0$ | — | For piles |

---

## 2.3 The Decision Logic — When to Use Shallow vs Deep

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board-Standard Decision Flow:**
> 1. Compute the SBC of soil at shallow depth (using Terzaghi/Meyerhof/Skempton).
> 2. If $q_{required} \leq q_{safe}$ AND $D_f \leq B$ → **Shallow Foundation**.
> 3. If the upper soil is weak and competent layer is deep → **Deep Foundation** (pile or pier).
> 4. If $D_f$ is between $1B$ and $4B$ → **Semi-deep / Well Foundation**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Knowledge

This classification is critical in:
- **Multi-storey buildings** → often on **isolated or raft (shallow)** foundations.
- **Bridges over rivers** → **well or pile (deep)** foundations.
- **Transmission towers** → need uplift-resistant **deep foundations**.
- **Offshore platforms** → driven **steel pipe piles** (deep).
- **Highways and runways** → **strip footings** (shallow).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Depth Ratio Criterion

The boundary between shallow and deep foundations is derived based on the **mechanism of load transfer** and the **stress influence zone** (pressure bulb) beneath the footing.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry
Let the foundation have:
- Width of foundation = $B$
- Length of foundation = $L$
- Depth of foundation below ground level = $D_f$

### Step 2: Define the Depth Ratio
A dimensionless parameter, the **depth ratio** $\lambda$, is defined as:

$$
\lambda = \frac{D_f}{B}
$$

### Step 3: Pressure Bulb Analysis
The **pressure bulb** (zone of significant stress influence) of a square footing extends to a depth of approximately:

$$
Z_{influence} \approx 1.5 B \text{ to } 2.0 B
$$

- For **shallow foundations** ($D_f \leq B$): The pressure bulb of the footing lies **entirely within** the bearing stratum. Load is transferred by **direct bearing** beneath the base.
- For **deep foundations** ($D_f \geq 4B$): The pressure bulb of the footing base terminates **above** the founding level. The load is transferred by **skin friction** along the shaft and **point bearing** at the tip, not by a simple bearing bulb.

### Step 4: Derive the Boundaries
The KTU classification uses the following logic:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shallow Foundation:} \quad & \lambda = \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 1 \\[6pt]
\text{Intermediate Foundation:} \quad & 1 < \frac{D_f}{B} \leq 4 \\[6pt]
\text{Deep Foundation:} \quad & \frac{D_f}{B} \geq 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation
- At $\lambda \leq 1$: The foundation depth is **less than or equal to its width**. The soil is excavated to a shallow depth, and the footing rests on soil at a level just below the frost line or the weak topsoil.
- At $\lambda \geq 4$: The foundation is **much deeper** than wide — like a slender column driven into the ground. The load travels through the shaft via shear (skin friction), reaching a competent layer.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Classification Tree (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Classification of Foundations (KTU 2024)**

**Foundations** are broadly classified into two categories:

### A. Shallow Foundations ($D_f/B \leq 1$)
Examples with their specific depth ratio range:
- **Spread Footing (Isolated):** $\lambda = 0.5$ to $1.0$
- **Combined Footing:** $\lambda = 0.5$ to $1.0$
- **Strap (Cantilever) Footing:** $\lambda = 0.5$ to $1.0$
- **Strip Footing:** $\lambda = 0.3$ to $1.0$
- **Mat / Raft Foundation:** $\lambda = 0.2$ to $0.5$
- **Pad Foundation:** $\lambda = 0.5$ to $1.0$

### B. Deep Foundations ($D_f/B \geq 4$)
Examples:
- **Pile Foundations:**
  - End-bearing pile
  - Friction pile
  - Compaction pile
  - Tension (uplift) pile
- **Pier Foundations:** $\lambda = 4$ to $10$
- **Caisson Foundations:** $\lambda = 5$ to $20$
- **Well Foundations:** $\lambda = 4$ to $8$ (used in bridges)

### C. Semi-Deep Foundations ($1 < D_f/B \leq 4$)
- **Buoyant foundations**
- **Strap footings with deep beams**
- **Short piles / piers in special cases**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Identifying Foundation Type

> **Problem:** A column footing has a width $B = 2.0$ m and is founded at a depth $D_f = 6.5$ m below ground level. Identify whether it is a shallow or deep foundation.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{D_f}{B} \\[6pt]
\lambda &= \frac{6.5}{2.0} \\[6pt]
\lambda &= 3.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1 < 3.25 \leq 4$, this lies in the **intermediate (semi-deep) zone**. The footing is **NOT a true deep foundation** in the strict KTU sense. However, in practice, depending on the soil condition, it could be treated as a **semi-deep footing** or a **short pile cap**.

If the same footing were founded at $D_f = 9.0$ m:

$$
\lambda = \frac{9.0}{2.0} = 4.5 \geq 4
$$

Then it would be classified as a **deep foundation**.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Logic Table — Choosing Foundation Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Selection Matrix for Shallow vs Deep Foundation**

| Criterion | Shallow Foundation | Deep Foundation |
|---|---|---|
| $D_f / B$ ratio | $\leq 1$ | $\geq 4$ to 5 |
| Soil type at surface | Strong, stiff | Loose / soft / compressible |
| Depth of firm stratum | Shallow (1–3 m) | Deep (> 5 m) |
| Magnitude of load | Light to medium (≤ 5000 kN/column) | Heavy (> 5000 kN/column) |
| Type of structure | Low-rise buildings, houses | High-rise, bridges, towers, offshore |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Construction time | Faster | Slower (needs heavy equipment) |
| Settlement | Controls uniform settlement | Controls both total and differential |
| Lateral load resistance | Limited | Excellent |
| Skirting of weak soil | Not possible | Possible (bypasses weak layer) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Foundations


---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Load Transfer Mechanism (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Superstructure Load P applied at column top"]
    nodeB["Column transfers P to Footing base"]
    nodeC["Shallow Path Direct Bearing on soil beneath base"]
    nodeD["Deep Path Skin Friction along shaft and End Bearing at tip"]
    nodeE["Bearing Stress q equals P over A is less than SBC of soil"]
    nodeF["Pile Capacity Q ult equals Q point plus Q skin greater than P"]
    nodeG["Structure Safe and Stable Settlement within limits"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Selecting Foundation Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1 Compute SBC of soil at shallow depth"]
    stepB["Step 2 Compare required pressure q required with SBC"]
    stepC["SBC sufficient AND Df over B less than equal to 1"]
    stepD["SBC insufficient OR competent layer is deep"]
    stepE["Choose SHALLOW FOUNDATION"]
    stepF["Choose DEEP FOUNDATION Pile or Pier"]
    stepG["Check Df over B is between 1 and 4 SEMI DEEP Foundation"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepB --> stepD
    stepC --> stepE
    stepD --> stepG
    stepG --> stepF
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Functions-of-Foundation Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    block1["INPUT Structural Load and Soil Profile"]
    block2["FUNCTION 1 Load Distribution"]
    block3["FUNCTION 2 Load Transfer to Competent Stratum"]
    block4["FUNCTION 3 Settlement Control"]
    block5["FUNCTION 4 Overturning and Sliding Resistance"]
    block6["FUNCTION 5 Anchorage and Uplift Resistance"]
    block7["OUTPUT Safe and Stable Foundation System"]

    block1 --> block2
    block2 --> block3
    block3 --> block4
    block4 --> block5
    block5 --> block6
    block6 --> block7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A: SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define a foundation. List any four functions of a foundation. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **foundation** is the part of a structure that transmits the loads from the superstructure to the underlying soil or rock, ensuring that the bearing capacity is not exceeded and settlements are within permissible limits.

**Four functions of a foundation:**
1. **Distributes** the concentrated structural load over a larger area of soil.
2. **Transfers** the load to a deep, competent bearing stratum.
3. **Controls** total and differential settlement.
4. Provides **stability against overturning and sliding** due to lateral forces.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between a shallow foundation and a deep foundation. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Shallow Foundation | Deep Foundation |
|---|---|---|
| Depth-to-width ratio ($D_f / B$) | $\leq 1$ | $\geq 4$ to 5 |
| Founding depth | Shallow (1–3 m typically) | Deep (> 5 m typically) |
| Load transfer mechanism | Direct bearing beneath base | Skin friction + end bearing |
| Soil condition | Competent soil near surface | Weak soil near surface, firm layer at depth |
| Examples | Spread footing, raft, strip footing | Pile, pier, well, caisson |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

## PART B: ESSAY-TYPE QUESTIONS (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **eight principal functions of a foundation** in detail with neat sketches where necessary. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The eight principal functions of a foundation are:

1. **Load Distribution:** A foundation spreads the concentrated load from columns or walls over a larger contact area, reducing the bearing pressure $q = P/A$ to within the safe bearing capacity of the soil. For example, a $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$ column on a $3 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$ footing reduces pressure 36 times.

2. **Load Transfer to Bearing Stratum:** When the topsoil is weak (e.g., loose fill, expansive clay), the foundation transfers the load through piles or piers to a deeper, competent layer such as rock or dense sand.

3. **Settlement Control:** By providing a rigid base, the foundation equalizes settlement across the structure, preventing differential settlement that leads to cracking.

4. **Overturning Stability:** Lateral forces (wind, earthquake) cause overturning moments. A wider footing base increases the resisting moment arm, ensuring stability.

5. **Sliding Resistance:** The friction between the base of the foundation and the soil, along with passive earth pressure on the embedded depth, resists horizontal sliding.

6. **Uplift Anchorage:** For structures like transmission towers and underground tanks, the weight of the foundation and the soil above it must exceed the uplift force.

7. **Scour Protection:** For bridge piers in rivers, deep foundations (well or pile) extend below the maximum scour depth to prevent undermining.

8. **Provision of Working Platform:** The foundation provides a level, firm surface for the superstructure to be constructed upon.

---

**(b)** Define a **shallow foundation**. With the help of a sketch, explain the behaviour of a **combined footing** and state the conditions under which it is preferred over an isolated footing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **shallow foundation** is one in which the depth of foundation $D_f$ is **less than or equal to the width $B$** of the footing, i.e., $D_f / B \leq 1$. The load is transferred to the soil by direct bearing beneath the base.

A **combined footing** is a single footing supporting **two or more columns** in a row. It is typically rectangular or trapezoidal in plan.

**Sketch (Schematic):**

```
|     Column C1     |        |     Column C2     |
|       |           |        |       |           |
|       |           |        |       |           |
|___________________|________|___________________|
                   Combined Footing
        (Rests on soil with Df less than B)
```

**Conditions when combined footing is preferred:**
1. **Columns are very close** to each other such that their isolated footings would overlap.
2. **Property line / building line restriction** prevents the isolated footing of an exterior column from extending beyond the boundary.
3. The **soil bearing capacity is low**, requiring a large contact area, which is more economical as a single combined footing.
4. Two columns carry **unequal loads** — a trapezoidal combined footing is designed to keep the centroid of the footing aligned with the centroid of the combined column loads (uniform pressure distribution).

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define a **deep foundation**. Discuss in detail the classification of **pile foundations** based on **load transfer mechanism**, with neat sketches. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **deep foundation** is one in which the depth of the foundation $D_f$ is **much greater than its width $B$** (typically $D_f / B \geq 4$), and the load is transferred to a deep, competent stratum via end bearing, skin friction, or both.

**Classification of Piles Based on Load Transfer:**

1. **End-Bearing Piles:** The pile tip rests on a hard stratum (rock or dense sand). The entire load is transferred through the **point** of the pile.

```
     Surface
     ~~~~~~~ Soft soil ~~~~~~~
       |\
       | \
       |  \  Pile shaft
       |   \
       |    \___________
       |                |   Point
       |________________|   ==== ROCK STRATUM ====
                                 (End bearing)
```

$$
Q_{u,pile} \approx Q_{point} = A_p \cdot q_p
$$

2. **Friction Piles:** The pile does not rest on a hard stratum. The load is transferred to the surrounding soil by **skin friction** along the pile shaft.

```
     Surface
     ~~~~~~~ Soft soil ~~~~~~~
       |======================|
       || Friction (Skin)   ||
       ||  f_s along shaft  ||
       |======================|
       |        ||           |
       |        || Pile tip  |
       |        ||           |
       ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ (No rock; soft all the way)
```

$$
Q_{u,pile} \approx Q_{skin} = \sum A_{si} \cdot f_{si}
$$

3. **Compaction Piles:** Driven into loose granular soil to **densify** it. They do not carry structural load directly; instead, they improve the bearing capacity of the surrounding soil.

4. **Tension (Uplift) Piles:** Designed to resist **upward** pull-out forces. Used under transmission towers, tall chimneys, and anchorages.

5. **Combined End-Bearing and Friction Piles:** Most common in practice. The pile transfers load by **both** point resistance and skin friction.

---

**(b)** A column carries a load of **1500 kN**. The soil has a Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) of **150 kN/m²**. The depth of foundation is restricted to **1.5 m**. Design a suitable **square footing** and verify if it is a shallow or deep foundation. Take $\gamma_{soil} = 18$ kN/m³ and $\gamma_{concrete} = 24$ kN/m³. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Net Safe Bearing Pressure**
The net SBC available at the base level is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= q_{safe} - \gamma_{soil} \cdot D_f \\
q_{net} &= 150 - 18 \times 1.5 \\
q_{net} &= 150 - 27 \\
q_{net} &= 123 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Approximate Area of Footing**
Assuming the weight of footing is 10% of column load, total load on soil = $1.10 \times 1500 = 1650$ kN.

$$
A_{req} = \frac{Q_{total}}{q_{net}} = \frac{1650}{123} = 13.41 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Side of Square Footing**

$$
B = \sqrt{13.41} = 3.66 \text{ m}
$$

Provide $B = 3.7 \text{ m}$.

**Step 4: Verify Depth Ratio**
Given $D_f = 1.5$ m and $B = 3.7$ m:

$$
\lambda = \frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{1.5}{3.7} = 0.405
$$

Since $\lambda = 0.405 \leq 1$, this is a **SHALLOW FOUNDATION**. ✅

**Step 5: Final Design Check**
Actual bearing pressure with $B = 3.7$ m:

$$
q_{actual} = \frac{1500}{3.7 \times 3.7} = 109.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \leq 150 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Hence the design is SAFE.** Provide a square footing of size **3.7 m × 3.7 m × 0.5 m thick** (assume thickness 0.5 m for self-weight check).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the depth ratio:** Some students write "$D_f / B > 1$ is deep." This is **wrong by KTU standard**. The accepted KTU value is $D_f / B \geq 4$. Always quote the conservative textbook limit.
> 2. **Forgetting $\gamma \cdot D_f$ in net SBC calculation:** Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if you forget to subtract the overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$ when computing $q_{net}$.
> 3. **Not including self-weight of footing in the design:** Always add the weight of the footing (assume 10–15% of column load as a thumb rule) when computing the total load on soil.
> 4. **Listing only 3–4 functions of foundation instead of 8:** The question is worth 7 marks and demands **at least 6–8 functions** with one-line explanations.
> 5. **Not sketching for combined footing or pile questions:** A neat labelled sketch carries **2–3 easy marks**. Skipping the sketch is a common mistake.
> 6. **Mixing up end-bearing and friction piles:** Be clear — end bearing transfers load at the **tip**; friction transfers along the **shaft**. Marks are lost for the wrong description.
> 7. **No units in the final answer:** Always write kN, kN/m², m, m² with every numerical result.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Foundation Functions & Classification**
>
> ✅ **Foundation** = Structural element transmitting load from superstructure to soil safely.
>
> ✅ **Eight Functions of Foundation (memorize in order):**
> 1. Load distribution
> 2. Load transfer to bearing stratum
> 3. Settlement control
> 4. Overturning stability
> 5. Sliding resistance
> 6. Uplift anchorage
> 7. Scour protection
> 8. Working platform
>
> ✅ **Shallow Foundation:** $D_f / B \leq 1$; e.g., spread, combined, strap, strip, raft.
>
> ✅ **Deep Foundation:** $D_f / B \geq 4$ to 5; e.g., pile, pier, well, caisson.
>
> ✅ **Semi-Deep (Intermediate):** $1 < D_f / B \leq 4$.
>
> ✅ **Pile classification (load transfer):**
> - End-bearing (point resistance)
> - Friction (skin friction)
> - Compaction (soil densification)
> - Tension (uplift resistance)
> - Combined end-bearing + friction
>
> ✅ **Key Formula:** $q_{net} = q_{safe} - \gamma \cdot D_f$
>
> ✅ **Minimum depth of foundation:** 0.9 m to 1.5 m below GL (IS 1080).
>
> ✅ **Factor of safety for pile (IS 2911):** 2.5 to 3.0.
>
> ✅ **Combined footing preferred when:** columns are close, property line exists, SBC is low, or columns have unequal loads.
>
> ✅ **Pressure bulb depth** ≈ 1.5B to 2B — the basis for the depth ratio criterion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Site Investigation and Soil Exploration: Objectives - Planning - Reconnaissance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Site Investigation and Soil Exploration: Objectives - Planning - Reconnaissance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — Site Investigation and Soil Exploration: Objectives, Planning & Reconnaissance

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Site Investigation (SI)** is the comprehensive engineering process of determining the surface and subsurface conditions of a proposed construction site, including its geological, hydrological, geotechnical, and environmental characteristics, to enable the safe, economical, and serviceable design and construction of foundations and earthworks.

In the context of **KTU PCCVT503 (Foundation Engineering)**, Site Investigation — often called **Subsurface Exploration** or **Soil Exploration** — is treated as the *forensic and diagnostic phase* of any civil engineering project. It is formally defined in the KTU syllabus as a **multi-stage, phased inquiry** that links the **geological history** of a site to its **engineering performance** under proposed loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soil Exploration** is the specific *technical subset* of Site Investigation that deals with determining the **stratigraphy, index properties, and engineering parameters** of the soil and rock strata beneath the proposed foundation level.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly recognises Site Investigation as a **prerequisite design input** — it sits *upstream* of all bearing capacity, settlement, slope stability, and lateral earth pressure calculations in Modules 3 through 5.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Check-up" Paradigm

Imagine a senior cardiologist (geotechnical consultant) examining a patient (the proposed structure) before prescribing medication (the foundation type). A doctor would never prescribe a dosage without:

- **Asking preliminary questions** (age, symptoms, history) → **Reconnaissance**
- **Conducting a physical examination** (blood pressure, heartbeat) → **Preliminary Investigation**
- **Ordering blood tests and X-rays** (detailed diagnostics) → **Detailed Investigation**
- **Monitoring under stress** (treadmill test) → **Specialist Tests & In-situ Testing**

Likewise, a foundation engineer must **never design a footing, pile, or raft** without first interrogating the site through these same four stages. Skipping reconnaissance is like a doctor prescribing a drug after only hearing the patient's chief complaint — the consequence may be a **catastrophic differential settlement, bearing failure, or even structural collapse**.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Rule-of-Thumb from KTU Valuators:** Roughly **0.1% to 0.5%** of the total project cost should be earmarked for site investigation. For a 50-crore project, this is **₹50 lakh — a small insurance premium against a multi-crore foundation failure**.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Engineering Constants Used in SI Planning

The following standard values are used throughout KTU-style problems on planning of site investigation:

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum depth of exploration | **1.5 × B** (where B = footing width) | IS 1080 / IS 1892 guideline |
| For piles | **10 m to 30 m below pile tip** | Function of pile group geometry |
| For rafts | **1.5 × D_f** minimum | D_f = depth of foundation |
| Borehole spacing (buildings) | **15 m to 30 m** | IS 1892 |
| Borehole spacing (dams, long alignments) | **50 m to 150 m** | IS 6955 |
| Standard Penetration Test (SPT) | **N-value at every 1.5 m** | IS 2131 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **1.5 × B** rule is the most heavily tested formula in KTU 2024 Module 2 derivations. Memorise it with its boundary conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Objectives, Planning & Reconnaissance

## 2.1 The Fourfold Objectives of Site Investigation

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following **four canonical objectives**. Every 14-mark question in this module typically asks the student to *enumerate, explain, and justify* these objectives with a project-specific context.

### Objective 1 — Site Suitability Assessment

To determine whether the proposed site is **geologically and geotechnically suitable** for the intended structure. This includes:
- Identification of **flood plains, landslides, karst features, and filled ground**.
- Detection of **active faults, seismic zones, and high-water-table regions**.
- Assessment of **bearing strata continuity** across the footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A site may be geologically *stable* but engineeringly *unsuitable* (e.g., a deep soft clay layer requiring expensive ground improvement). KTU examiners reward this distinction.

### Objective 2 — Selection of Foundation Type and Depth

To acquire the data necessary to select between:
- **Shallow foundations** (isolated, combined, strip, raft)
- **Deep foundations** (piles, piers, caissons, wells)

And to determine the **depth of the foundation (D_f)** so that it rests on a stratum of adequate **bearing capacity** and **compressibility**.

### Objective 3 — Estimation of Engineering Parameters

To obtain the **in-situ and laboratory** values of:
- **Shear strength parameters** (c, φ)
- **Compressibility parameters** (C_c, e_0, OCR)
- **Hydraulic conductivity** (k)
- **Unit weight** (γ)
- **Dynamic properties** (for seismic design)

### Objective 4 — Anticipation of Construction Problems

To foresee and design for:
- **Groundwater inflow** and dewatering requirements
- **Excavation stability** (shoring, underpinning)
- **Adverse chemical environment** (sulphate attack, aggressive groundwater)
- **Neighbouring structure interaction** (underpinning of adjacent buildings)

---

## 2.2 The KTU-Approved Planning Hierarchy

Planning of a site investigation is **not arbitrary**. The **KTU 2024 Scheme** adopts the **three-tier planning hierarchy** prescribed in **IS 1892 (Code of Practice for Site Investigations for Foundations)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this hierarchy verbatim** — KTU Part A questions routinely ask: *"List the stages in the planning of a site investigation."*

### Tier 1 — Desk Study (Office Planning)

Performed *without visiting the site*. It involves:
- **Topographic maps** (Survey of India, scale 1:50,000 or 1:25,000)
- **Geological maps** (Geological Survey of India — GSI)
- **Aerial photographs and satellite imagery** (Google Earth Pro, ISRO Bhuvan)
- **Historical land-use records** (to identify filled ground, old water bodies)
- **Seismic zone maps** (IS 1893 Part 1)
- **Hydrological and flood records**

**Output:** A *preliminary geological model* and an *initial hypothesis* of subsurface conditions.

### Tier 2 — Reconnaissance Visit (Field Walk-Over)

This is the **physical, on-ground inspection** by an experienced geotechnical engineer. It is the *first-hand* phase and is the focus of the present topic. (Detailed in §2.3 below.)

### Tier 3 — Detailed Investigation Planning

Based on the findings of Tiers 1 and 2, the engineer plans:
- **Number, depth, and spacing of boreholes**
- **Type and frequency of sampling** (disturbed vs undisturbed)
- **In-situ tests** (SPT, SCPT, DCPT, Plate Load, Pressuremeter)
- **Geophysical tests** (if applicable)
- **Laboratory testing programme** on recovered samples

---

## 2.3 Reconnaissance — The First Field Encounter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconnaissance** is the *systematic, on-foot, visual and tactile inspection* of the site and its surroundings, conducted to gather first-hand, qualitative information that complements the desk study and guides the detailed investigation.

### 2.3.1 The Eight Aims of Reconnaissance (KTU High-Yield)

| Sl. No. | Aim of Reconnaissance | Practical Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Topography & Slope** — Identify steep slopes, depressions, drainage patterns | Visual inspection, hand-held GPS, clinometer |
| 2 | **Geology & Rock Outcrops** — Locate exposed rock, identify weathering grade | Hand lens, geological hammer, acid test (HCl) for carbonate |
| 3 | **Soil Type & Profile** — Observe cuts, nalas, road embankments | Visual + manual identification (feel, dilatancy, plasticity) |
| 4 | **Groundwater** — Identify seepage, springs, water-table depth | Probing wells, talking to local residents |
| 5 | **Existing Structures** — Note nearby buildings, their type, distress, foundations | Visual survey, photographic record |
| 6 | **Vegetation & Land Use** — Type of vegetation often indicates soil moisture and depth | Botanical indicators (e.g., date palms → high water table) |
| 7 | **Accessibility** — For drilling rigs, equipment movement, approach roads | Visual + vehicle inspection |
| 8 | **Sources of Disturbance** — Vibrations, blasting, heavy traffic, flooding | Local enquiry + site visit |

### 2.3.2 Reconnaissance Tools & Equipment

The following **field kit** is typically carried by a reconnaissance engineer:

- **Hand-held GPS** (for recording coordinates of key features)
- **Geological hammer & chisel** (for rock identification)
- **Dilatant test apparatus** (simple field tools for soil)
- **Hand lens (10×)**
- **Dilute hydrochloric acid bottle** (for carbonate rock identification)
- **Measuring tape, ranging rods**
- **Camera** (for photographic documentation)
- **Notebook & pre-printed Reconnaissance Proforma** (per IS 1892)
- **Hand-auger (post-hole type)** for shallow probing (≤ 3 m)

### 2.3.3 Reconnaissance Proforma (IS 1892)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award full marks when a student **lists the columns of the standard proforma** rather than just describing it. The proforma typically includes:
> 
> 1. Project name & reference
> 2. Date and time of visit
> 3. Weather conditions
> 4. Topography (flat, undulating, hilly, marshy)
> 5. Surface soil type
> 6. Drainage pattern
> 7. Water-table observations
> 8. Rock outcrops and depth to rock
> 9. Existing structures and their condition
> 10. Evidence of distress, cracking, settlement
> 11. Access roads
> 12. Sketch map (hand-drawn) of the site

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Site Investigation Planning

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **forbidden** inside table cells. Use the LaTeX macros `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Equation | LaTeX Form | Application / KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum depth of exploration (isolated footing) | $D_{min} = 1.5 \, B$ | Where B = width of footing |
| Minimum depth of exploration (raft / mat) | $D_{min} = 1.5 \, D_f$ | D_f = depth of foundation |
| Depth of exploration for piles | $D_{pile} = L + 10 \text{ to } 30 \text{ m}$ | L = pile length, below pile tip |
| Safe bearing capacity approximation | $q_s = 2.5 \, N \, \text{(kPa)}$ | From SPT N-value (IS 6403) — quick estimate |
| Number of boreholes (small buildings) | $n = \dfrac{L}{15} + 1$ | L = length in metres; spacing 15 m |
| Number of boreholes (large buildings) | $n = \dfrac{L}{30} + 1$ | Spacing 30 m per IS 1892 |
| Corrected SPT N-value | $N_{corr} = N \cdot C_N$ | $C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2000}{\sigma'}\right)$ (Liao & Whitman) |
| Vertical effective stress | $\sigma' = \gamma' \cdot z$ | Used in N-correction |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SI planning described above is **not academic** — it directly influences:

1. **Cost optimisation** — Over-investigation wastes money; under-investigation risks failure (e.g., the **Lotus Riverside Building, Shanghai (2009)** collapsed due to an unrecognised buried river channel; investigation cost was a fraction of the loss).
2. **Schedule adherence** — Late discovery of a weak stratum can delay a project by **6 to 18 months**.
3. **Insurance and liability** — Most international design codes (Eurocode 7, AS 2159) make site investigation a *legally mandatory* input.
4. **Sustainability** — Knowing the in-situ soil allows use of *ground improvement* rather than deep foundations, reducing the **carbon footprint** by up to 40%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the "1.5 B" Rule for Minimum Depth of Exploration

> [!NOTE]
> The *1.5 B* rule is **empirically derived** from the theory of stress distribution under a shallow footing and IS 1892 / IS 1080 recommendations. The step-by-step logic is shown below — this is a high-frequency KTU derivation question.

### Statement
For an isolated square footing of width **B** resting at depth **D_f**, the minimum depth of exploration below ground level should be:

$$
D_{e, \, min} = 1.5 \, B
$$

**subject to the lower bound:**

$$
D_{e, \, min} \geq D_f + 2B
$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the zone of significant stress influence.**

According to Boussinesq's theory (extended by Newmark), the vertical stress increment $\Delta \sigma_z$ beneath a flexible loaded area of width **B** decays with depth. The empirical rule of thumb states that a stress influence of **≈ 20% of the applied pressure (q)** extends to a depth of approximately **1.5 B** below the footing base.

**Step 2 — Define the boundary of practical influence.**

The "1.5 B" boundary represents the depth beyond which the additional stress from the footing is **negligible** for settlement and bearing capacity calculations. Therefore, the borehole must penetrate *at least* this far to capture the full stress-influenced profile.

**Step 3 — Apply the depth correction for embedment.**

Since the footing is founded at depth $D_f$ (not at ground level), the exploration must start from the **base of the footing**, not from the ground surface. Therefore:

$$
D_{e,\,total} = D_f + 1.5 \, B
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety lower bound.**

To ensure that a competent bearing stratum (if any) below the influence zone is also characterised, the **IS code lower bound** is:

$$
D_{e,\,total} = \max \!\left\{D_f + 1.5B, \quad D_f + 2B\right\}
$$

**Step 5 — Compact final form.**

$$
\boxed{D_{e,\,total} = D_f + 1.5 \, B \quad \text{(with a minimum of } D_f + 2B \text{ for soft soils)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Borehole Spacing for Building Footprints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is *exam-favourite* in KTU 2024 Part B questions.

### Step 1 — Establish the spacing criteria from IS 1892.

For *light buildings and small structures*:
$$
S_{bh} = 15 \text{ m}
$$

For *heavy and important structures*:
$$
S_{bh} = 30 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Relate spacing to total length of building.

For a rectangular building of length **L** and width **W**, the number of boreholes along the length is:
$$
n_L = \frac{L}{S_{bh}} + 1
$$

(The "+1" ensures boreholes at both ends of the building.)

### Step 3 — Analogously, along the width:
$$
n_W = \frac{W}{S_{bh}} + 1
$$

### Step 4 — Total number of boreholes:
$$
\boxed{n_{total} = n_L \times n_W = \left( \frac{L}{S_{bh}} + 1 \right) \left( \frac{W}{S_{bh}} + 1 \right)}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 60 m × 24 m building is to be constructed. As per IS 1892, determine the number of boreholes required for a light building.

**Given:**
- L = 60 m
- W = 24 m
- $S_{bh}$ = 15 m (light building)

**Step 1:** Boreholes along length:
$$
n_L = \frac{60}{15} + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

**Step 2:** Boreholes along width:
$$
n_W = \frac{24}{15} + 1 = 1.6 + 1 \approx 2.6 \rightarrow 3 \, \text{(rounded up to integer)}
$$

**Step 3:** Total boreholes:
$$
n_{total} = 5 \times 3 = 15
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Always **round up** fractional borehole counts — the partial borehole still needs to be drilled.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reconnaissance Indicators vs. Subsurface Conditions

The following matrix is a **high-density study tool** linking visible surface evidence (reconnaissance observation) to probable subsurface conditions. KTU examiners often ask: *"What does the presence of [X] indicate about the subsurface?"*

| Surface Observation (Reconnaissance) | Probable Subsurface Inference | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Stagnant water / marshy ground** | High water table, possibly soft clay / organic soil | Reject site or use deep foundations |
| **Ponded water disappearance overnight** | Highly permeable sandy / gravelly soil | Good drainage; check for piping |
| **Cracked nearby buildings (diagonal cracks)** | Heave or settlement of foundation soil | Expansive clay or consolidation issue |
| **Tilted trees / tilted compound walls** | Lateral soil movement / slope instability | Retaining wall or ground anchors needed |
| **Outcrop of soft rock (e.g., laterite, shale)** | Weathered rock profile, may be highly variable | Allow for differential bearing capacity |
| **Outcrop of hard rock (granite, basalt)** | Shallow rock, good bearing | Possible shallow foundation, but watch for cavities |
| **Sand boils / boils after rainfall** | Liquefaction-prone saturated sand | Seismic design (IS 1893) |
| **Sinkholes / closed depressions** | Karstic limestone, cavities | Unsuitable without grouting |
| **Filled ground (construction debris visible)** | Variable, uncompacted fill | Soil replacement or deep foundations |
| **Springs / seepage on slopes** | Perched water table, possible artesian conditions | Dewatering and slope drainage |
| **Bermuda grass / phreatophytes** | High water table, possibly saline | Reconsider site or provide capillary break |
| **Cracks in pavement (alligator pattern)** | Soft subgrade, possible CBR < 2% | Deep pavement or ground improvement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Phased Site Investigation Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram presents the **functional flow** of a KTU-standard site investigation programme from desk study through reporting. Each block represents a *deliverable stage*, not a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Inception and TOR] --> A1[Tier 1 Desk Study]
    A1 --> A1a[Topographic Maps GSI]
    A1 --> A1b[Geological Maps GSI]
    A1 --> A1c[Satellite and Aerial Imagery]
    A1 --> A1d[Historical Land Use Records]
    A1 --> A1e[Seismic Zone IS 1893]
    A1 --> A2{Preliminary Geological Model}
    A2 --> A3[Tier 2 Reconnaissance Visit]
    A3 --> A3a[Visual Topography and Slope]
    A3 --> A3b[Rock Outcrop Mapping]
    A3 --> A3c[Soil Profile Observation in Cuts]
    A3 --> A3d[Groundwater Indicators]
    A3 --> A3e[Existing Structure Distress]
    A3 --> A3f[Accessibility and Logistical Survey]
    A3 --> A4[Reconnaissance Proforma IS 1892]
    A4 --> A5[Updated Geological Model]
    A5 --> A6[Tier 3 Detailed Investigation Planning]
    A6 --> A6a[Borehole Number Depth Spacing]
    A6 --> A6b[Sampling Programme Disturbed and Undisturbed]
    A6 --> A6c[In Situ Tests SPT SCPT PLT]
    A6 --> A6d[Geophysical Tests Resistivity Seismic]
    A6 --> A6e[Laboratory Testing Programme]
    A6 --> A7[Execution of Field Investigation]
    A7 --> A8[Interpretation and Reporting]
    A8 --> A9[Foundation Design Recommendation]
    A9 --> A10[KTU Valued Output: Safe Economical Foundation]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The flow is **strictly sequential** with **feedback loops** implicit at every stage.
- Tier 1 (Desk Study) and Tier 2 (Reconnaissance) are **non-intrusive** (no drilling) and constitute **≈ 20%** of the total SI cost but provide **≈ 60%** of the actionable intelligence.
- Tier 3 (Detailed Investigation) is **intrusive** and is the *most expensive* component.

## 4.2 Schematic — Decision Logic for Selecting Foundation Type from SI Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start: SI Data Available] --> B2{Is competent bearing stratum within 3 m?}
    B2 -- Yes --> B3[Choose Shallow Foundation]
    B2 -- No --> B4{Bearing stratum within 10 m?}
    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Consider Deep Strip or Mat]
    B4 -- No --> B6[Choose Deep Foundation: Piles or Piers]
    B3 --> B7{Settlement less than 65 mm?}
    B7 -- Yes --> B8[Proceed: Isolated Footing]
    B7 -- No --> B9[Upgrade to Raft or Combined Footing]
    B5 --> B10{Soft layer thickness more than 4 m?}
    B10 -- Yes --> B11[Soil Improvement or Piles]
    B10 -- No --> B12[Proceed: Raft Foundation]
    B6 --> B13{N value at pile tip more than 30?}
    B13 -- Yes --> B14[Proceed: Bored Cast In Situ Pile]
    B13 -- No --> B15[Proceed: Driven Precast Pile with Pile Cap]
    B8 --> B16[End: Foundation Selected]
    B9 --> B16
    B11 --> B16
    B12 --> B16
    B14 --> B16
    B15 --> B16
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Reconnaissance Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Site Engineer On Foot] --> R2[Visual Observation]
    R1 --> R3[Photographic Documentation]
    R1 --> R4[Hand Auger Probe up to 3 m]
    R1 --> R5[Local Enquiry with Residents]
    R2 --> R6[Reconnaissance Proforma]
    R3 --> R6
    R4 --> R6
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> R7[Sketch Map of Site]
    R6 --> R8[Photographic Log]
    R6 --> R9[Distress Inventory of Adjacent Structures]
    R7 --> R10[Tier 3 Planning Input]
    R8 --> R10
    R9 --> R10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern** for the B.Tech (Civil Engineering) programme. The question-paper pattern mandates **Part A (5 × 3 = 15 marks)** consisting of short-answer questions and **Part B (5 × 14 = 70 marks)** with internal choice. Questions are tagged with simulated past-year references to familiarise students with the pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define site investigation. List its four main objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Site Investigation** is the systematic process of collecting surface and subsurface information about a proposed construction site to enable safe and economical foundation design.

**The four main objectives are:**
1. To assess the **suitability of the site** for the proposed project.
2. To determine the **type, depth, and dimensions** of the foundation.
3. To estimate the **engineering properties** of soil and rock (strength, compressibility, permeability).
4. To anticipate and design for **construction difficulties** (groundwater, instability, contamination).

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark per objective)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**What is reconnaissance in the context of site investigation? Mention any four observations made during reconnaissance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reconnaissance** is the *first field visit* to the proposed site, conducted to make a visual, qualitative assessment of surface conditions and to plan the detailed subsurface investigation.

**Four key reconnaissance observations:**
1. **Topography and slope** of the site and surroundings.
2. **Type of soil** visible in exposed cuts, nalas, or embankments.
3. **Groundwater conditions** indicated by seepage, springs, or well-water levels.
4. **Condition of adjacent structures** (signs of cracking, settlement, tilting).

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark per observation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Module 2 — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Question A:**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **objectives of site investigation** for a major civil engineering project such as a multi-storey building. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **planning stages of site investigation** as per IS 1892. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Site Suitability Assessment (2 marks):**
The first objective of site investigation is to determine whether the site is *geologically and geotechnically suitable* for the proposed structure. This includes the study of:
- Topography and slope stability
- Flooding history and drainage
- Seismic zone classification (IS 1893)
- Presence of geological hazards (faults, landslides, sinkholes)

**2. Foundation Selection and Depth (2 marks):**
The investigation must provide data to choose between:
- Shallow foundations (isolated, strip, raft)
- Deep foundations (piles, piers)

The minimum depth of exploration is **$D_e = 1.5 \, B$** below the footing base, ensuring that the *full stress-influence zone* is characterised.

**3. Estimation of Engineering Parameters (2 marks):**
Soil samples are tested in the laboratory to determine:
- **Index properties:** Specific gravity, Atterberg limits, grain size distribution
- **Strength parameters:** Cohesion (c), angle of internal friction (φ)
- **Compressibility parameters:** Compression index (C_c), preconsolidation pressure (p_c)
- **Hydraulic conductivity (k)** for seepage and consolidation analysis

**4. Anticipation of Construction Problems (1 mark):**
The investigation identifies:
- Groundwater inflow and need for dewatering
- Excavation stability (shoring, underpinning)
- Chemical attack on concrete (sulphates, chlorides)
- Effect on neighbouring structures

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The **three-stage planning hierarchy** of IS 1892 is as follows:

**Stage 1 — Desk Study (2 marks):**
- Compilation of all existing information *without* visiting the site.
- Topographic and geological maps from Survey of India and GSI.
- Aerial photographs and satellite imagery (ISRO Bhuvan, Google Earth).
- Historical land-use records to detect filled ground or buried water bodies.
- Seismic zonation (IS 1893).
- *Output:* Preliminary geological model.

**Stage 2 — Reconnaissance (3 marks):**
- *On-foot inspection* of the site by an experienced geotechnical engineer.
- Visual, photographic, and tactile observations of topography, soil, rock, water, vegetation, and existing structures.
- Use of a pre-printed **Reconnaissance Proforma** (IS 1892) for documentation.
- Output: Updated geological model + decision on whether detailed investigation is needed.

**Stage 3 — Detailed Investigation Planning (2 marks):**
- *Number of boreholes:* $n = \left( \frac{L}{S_{bh}} + 1 \right) \left( \frac{W}{S_{bh}} + 1 \right)$ where $S_{bh} = 15$ to 30 m.
- *Depth of boreholes:* $D_e = D_f + 1.5B$ (minimum).
- *Sampling programme:* Disturbed (for classification) and undisturbed (for shear and consolidation tests).
- *In-situ tests:* SPT at every 1.5 m, Plate Load Test for shallow foundations, Pile Load Test for deep foundations.
- *Geophysical tests:* Seismic refraction, electrical resistivity (for large sites).
- *Laboratory testing programme:* As per IS 2720.

---

### Module 2 — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Question B:**

**(a)** What is **reconnaissance**? Describe the **methodology and observations** made during a reconnaissance visit for a proposed bridge site across a river valley. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 45 m × 18 m building is planned on a soft clay deposit. The footings are 2.5 m square at 1.5 m depth. As per IS 1892, determine the **number of boreholes** and the **minimum depth of each borehole**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Definition of Reconnaissance (1 mark):**
Reconnaissance is the *qualitative, on-foot field inspection* of a site to record surface features, soil and rock exposures, groundwater indicators, and existing structural conditions, used to plan the detailed subsurface investigation.

**2. Reconnaissance Methodology for a River Valley Bridge Site (3 marks):**
- **Walk the entire alignment** on both banks, recording the surface geology, slope, and vegetation.
- **Inspect the river banks** for soil type, scour marks, and rock outcrops using a hand lens and geological hammer.
- **Probe for shallow groundwater** using a hand-auger at the proposed pier and abutment locations.
- **Talk to local residents and panchayat officials** about historical flooding, river-course changes, and past foundation problems.
- **Photograph all key features** (river bank, abutment areas, existing bridge if any).
- **Complete the IS 1892 reconnaissance proforma** for each pier/abutment location.

**3. Key Observations to Record (3 marks):**
- **Topography:** Channel cross-section, bank slopes, meander patterns.
- **Geology:** Rock outcrops on banks indicating the depth to rock and its weathering grade.
- **Soil profile:** Alluvial silt, sand, gravel, and clay lenses visible in the cut-banks.
- **Groundwater:** Water level in the river, seepage on banks, presence of springs.
- **Scour and erosion:** Maximum scour depth in the river (critical for pile design).
- **Existing structures:** Condition of any nearby old bridge, culvert, or building (cracks, tilting).
- **Accessibility:** Road access for drilling rigs, availability of water for drilling.
- **Hazards:** Flood levels, seismic zone (e.g., Zone III or IV in Kerala).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Building plan: L = 45 m, W = 18 m
- Footing size: B = 2.5 m (square)
- Footing depth: $D_f = 1.5$ m
- Building type: *Light building* (use spacing $S_{bh} = 15$ m as per IS 1892)

**Step 1 — Number of boreholes along length (1 mark):**
$$
n_L = \frac{L}{S_{bh}} + 1 = \frac{45}{15} + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Number of boreholes along width (1 mark):**
$$
n_W = \frac{W}{S_{bh}} + 1 = \frac{18}{15} + 1 = 1.2 + 1 = 2.2 \rightarrow 3 \, \text{(round up)}
$$

**Step 3 — Total number of boreholes (1 mark):**
$$
\boxed{n_{total} = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ boreholes}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum depth of exploration per IS 1892 (2 marks):**
$$
D_e = D_f + 1.5 \, B = 1.5 + 1.5 \times 2.5 = 1.5 + 3.75 = 5.25 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the lower bound for soft clay (2 marks):**
Since the deposit is *soft clay*, the **lower bound** $D_f + 2B$ is also checked:
$$
D_f + 2B = 1.5 + 2 \times 2.5 = 1.5 + 5.0 = 6.5 \text{ m}
$$

The governing depth is the **maximum** of the two:
$$
\boxed{D_{e,\,min} = 6.5 \text{ m per borehole}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student **must explicitly state** that the larger of the two depths governs, and *justify* the choice using the soft-clay lower-bound rule. This is the most common place where KTU students lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Site Investigation Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in borehole count.** If a building is 30 m long, the student writes $n = 30/15 = 2$. The correct answer is $30/15 + 1 = 3$. Lose 1 mark instantly.
> 
> 2. **Conflating footing width (B) and footing depth (D_f).** Many students use $1.5 \times D_f$ instead of $1.5 \times B$. This is the *most common* error in KTU 2023 and 2024 papers. Always: **$1.5 \times B$** for isolated footings.
> 
> 3. **Not specifying units.** $D_e = 6.5$ is wrong; $D_e = 6.5 \text{ m}$ is correct. Lose 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 
> 4. **Conflating "reconnaissance" with "detailed investigation".** Reconnaissance is *visual and qualitative*; detailed investigation involves *drilling, sampling, and testing*. Mixing up the definitions costs 2–3 marks.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the sketch map / proforma reference.** A site investigation answer without a reference to IS 1892 or the reconnaissance proforma is considered *incomplete* by KTU examiners. Lose 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Not stating the design code.** Every answer in PCCVT503 should reference **IS 1080, IS 1892, IS 2131, IS 6403, or IS 2720**. The code reference is worth 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Site Investigation:**

- [x] **Definition:** Site investigation = comprehensive study of surface and subsurface conditions.
- [x] **Four Objectives:** Suitability, Foundation selection, Parameter estimation, Construction-problem anticipation.
- [x] **Three Planning Stages:** Desk Study → Reconnaissance → Detailed Investigation.
- [x] **IS 1892** is the *primary design code* for site investigation planning.
- [x] **1.5 B rule:** $D_e = D_f + 1.5B$ is the *default* minimum depth.
- [x] **Lower bound:** For soft soils, use $\max(D_f + 1.5B, \; D_f + 2B)$.
- [x] **Borehole spacing:** 15 m (light buildings) or 30 m (heavy buildings) per IS 1892.
- [x] **Borehole count formula:** $n = (L/S + 1)(W/S + 1)$, *always* round *up*.
- [x] **Reconnaissance is qualitative, not quantitative** — no drilling, no SPT, no lab tests.
- [x] **Reconnaissance deliverables:** Reconnaissance Proforma + Sketch Map + Photographic Log.
- [x] **Eight reconnaissance aims:** Topography, geology, soil, groundwater, structures, vegetation, access, disturbances.
- [x] **For piles:** Explore to 10–30 m below the pile tip.
- [x] **Corrected SPT N:** Use $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(2000/\sigma')$ for cohesionless soils.
- [x] **Quick SBC estimate:** $q_s = 2.5N$ kPa (for preliminary design only).
- [x] **Cost rule:** Budget **0.1%–0.5%** of project cost for site investigation.
- [x] **Always cite IS codes** (IS 1892, IS 1080, IS 2131, IS 6403) in every answer.
- [x] **Units matter:** Never quote a numerical answer without units.
- [x] **Reconnaissance precedes detailed investigation** — never reverse this order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When in doubt about the *depth of exploration*, always default to $1.5 B$ and add the embedment depth $D_f$. When in doubt about *borehole spacing*, default to **15 m** for residential/commercial and **30 m** for industrial/heavy structures. The KTU valuation key rewards correct IS-code citations generously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guidelines for Choosing Spacing and Depth of Borings [I.S. Guidelines Only]
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Guidelines for Choosing Spacing and Depth of Borings [I.S. Guidelines Only]

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Terminology)

**Spacing of Borings:**
The horizontal distance between two successive vertical boreholes in a subsurface investigation programme, governed primarily by the type, size, and uniformity of the proposed structure, as well as the geological variability of the site. The relevant Indian Standard is **IS 1892:1979** (Reaffirmed 2002) — *"Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation for Foundations."*

**Depth of Borings:**
The vertical distance that a borehole must be advanced below the existing ground surface (or below the proposed formation level) such that the soil profile explored is sufficient to (a) locate the bearing stratum, (b) determine its thickness, and (c) evaluate compressibility and shear strength parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary IS Code Reference for Examinations:**
> The two IS codes that KTU examiners expect you to quote verbatim are:
> 1. **IS 1892:1979** — Spacing and Depth of Borings
> 2. **IS 2131:1981** — Standard Penetration Test (often tested alongside borings)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient before prescribing treatment. A doctor does not perform a single blood test and conclude the diagnosis — they take **multiple samples** at strategic intervals, and from a **sufficient depth**, to capture a true picture of the patient's health.

A geotechnical engineer is the *doctor of the ground*. A single borehole is like one blood test — it gives a point reading. The **spacing** is analogous to how many samples to draw (resolution of the diagnosis), and the **depth** is analogous to how deep the needle must penetrate to find the real problem (the load-bearing stratum).

**Why does this matter in KTU exams?**
The 2024 Scheme emphasises *Outcome-Based Education*. A student who memorises tables without understanding the *logic* of why spacing tightens for heterogeneous soils will fail application-based Part B questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two key engineering questions solved by this topic:**
> 1. *How many boreholes are required?* (Spacing — depends on the plan area of the structure and geological uniformity.)
> 2. *How deep must each borehole go?* (Depth — depends on the type of foundation, the stress influence zone, and the depth of the competent bearing stratum.)

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The governing metrics in this topic are:
- **S** = Spacing between boreholes (in **metres**)
- **D** or **d** = Depth of boring (in **metres**)
- **B** = Width of foundation (in **metres**)
- **B_c** = Critical depth of investigation (in **metres**)
- **q_0** = Gross overburden pressure (in **kN/m²**)
- **Δσ** = Increase in vertical stress due to the structure (in **kN/m²**)
- **N** = Minimum number of boreholes (dimensionless)
- **L** = Length of the structure along the alignment (in **metres**)
- **A** = Plan area of the building (in **m²**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy

Choosing spacing and depth is **not an arbitrary decision** — it is governed by three engineering principles:

1. **The Uniformity Principle:** The more homogeneous the soil profile, the wider the spacing. A site with erratic, highly variable soil demands a denser borehole grid.
2. **The Pressure-Bulb Principle (Boussinesq's Stress Influence Zone):** The boring must extend to a depth where the *net stress increase* due to the proposed structure becomes insignificant. A classical benchmark: extend boring until **Δσ ≤ 0.1 q₀** (10% rule).
3. **The Foundation-Size Principle:** The depth is fundamentally tied to the *width (B)* of the foundation. Wider foundations need deeper borings because the stress influence zone is larger.

### 2.2 IS 1892:1979 — Spacing Guidelines (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Always Quote in Exams):**
> The minimum number of boreholes for any major project is **at least four (4)**, even for a small isolated building. This is to delineate the soil profile from all four corners and to detect any localised anomalies.

**Step-by-step logical framework (Spacing):**

**Step 1 — Identify the structure type:**
- Building, bridge, dam, highway, airport runway, retaining wall, industrial plant, etc.

**Step 2 — Classify the investigation stage:**
- *Preliminary Investigation* (reconnaissance-level, before final design)
- *Detailed Investigation* (final design stage)

**Step 3 — Select the spacing range from IS 1892 table.**

**Step 4 — Refine based on geological complexity.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Spacing of Borings (IS 1892)

| Type of Structure | Stage | Spacing of Borings (m) |
|---|---|---|
| **Multi-storey Buildings** | Preliminary | 30 – 60 |
| **Multi-storey Buildings** | Detailed | 15 – 30 |
| **Industrial Layouts / Large Plants** | Preliminary | 60 – 100 |
| **Industrial Layouts / Large Plants** | Detailed | 15 – 30 |
| **Highways / Runways (along centreline)** | Preliminary | 300 – 600 |
| **Highways / Runways (along centreline)** | Detailed | 30 – 60 |
| **Earthen Dams (along dam axis)** | — | 30 – 60 |
| **Concrete / Masonry Dams (along axis)** | — | 15 – 30 |
| **Bridges (over rivers)** | Preliminary | 50 – 150 |
| **Bridges (over rivers)** | Detailed | 15 – 30 |
| **Retaining Walls (per 30 m length)** | — | 1 boring |
| **Canals / Embankments** | — | 60 – 300 |
| **Transmission Towers (per 4 bases)** | — | 1 boring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When a building has plan dimensions exceeding **30 m × 30 m**, KTU examiners expect you to mention that boreholes must be **both at the corners AND at the centre** to capture the soil profile in the central zone. This is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in Part B.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Depth of Borings (IS 1892)

| Type of Foundation / Structure | Recommended Depth (D) |
|---|---|
| **Isolated Footing (Square/Circular)** | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2\,B$ below base of footing |
| **Strip / Continuous Footing** | $D = 3\,B$ below base of footing |
| **Raft / Mat Foundation** | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2\,B$ below base of raft (where B = width of raft) |
| **Pile Foundation (Bored Cast In-Situ)** | $D = 10$ to $30$ m, or 2 to 3 pile diameters below pile tip |
| **Pile Foundation (Driven)** | $D = $ depth of pile + 5 to 10 m |
| **Multi-storey Buildings (general)** | $D = 1.5 \times$ (width of building) |
| **Highways & Airfields** | $D = 1.5$ to $3$ m below formation level |
| **Dams** | $D = $ depth to bedrock (or through all pervious strata) |
| **Bridges (over rivers)** | $D = 1.5\,B$ below scour level at deepest point |
| **Retaining Walls** | $D = 1.5 \times$ (height of wall) below base |
| **Tower / Chimney Foundations** | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2\,B$ below base |

### 2.5 The Three Master Rules for Depth (Often Quoted in KTU Exams)

| Rule | Statement | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Terzaghi's Rule** | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2\,B$ below foundation | Isolated footings, rafts |
| **Boussinesq's 10% Rule** | Boring continues until $\Delta\sigma \le 0.1\,q_0$ | All foundations |
| **Settlement-Based Rule** | $D = $ depth to incompressible stratum | All foundations |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost Optimisation:** Closer borings = more accurate = more expensive. Choosing optimum spacing balances *technical accuracy* against *project budget*. The IS 1892 ranges are derived from decades of empirical data to hit this sweet spot.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Inadequate borings can lead to *forensic failures* — leaning buildings, cracked rafts, differential settlement. The 2013 Mumbai building collapses are textbook examples of insufficient subsurface investigation.
- **Legal Compliance:** Building bylaws in Kerala (KMBR) and across India **mandate** adherence to IS 1892 for issuing completion certificates.
- **Foundation Type Selection:** The boring depth dictates whether a *shallow* or *deep* foundation is viable, directly influencing the *BOQ* (Bill of Quantities) and project cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 10% Rule (Boussinesq Stress Influence)

This is the most important analytical derivation in this module, frequently asked in KTU Part B questions.

**Statement:** The depth of boring should extend to a level at which the net increase in vertical stress ($\Delta\sigma$) due to the structural load is less than or equal to **10% of the effective overburden pressure** ($q_0$ or $\sigma'_0$).

**Step 1 — Define the gross overburden pressure at depth z:**

$$q_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \cdot z_i$$

where $\gamma_i$ is the unit weight and $z_i$ is the thickness of the $i^{th}$ soil layer above the point of interest.

**Step 2 — Define the increase in vertical stress at depth z using Boussinesq's solution for a uniformly loaded flexible circular footing of radius R:**

$$\Delta\sigma = q \cdot I_z$$

where $q$ is the net contact pressure and $I_z$ is the influence factor.

**Step 3 — The 10% criterion (boundary condition for critical depth):**

$$\frac{\Delta\sigma}{q_0} = 0.10$$

**Step 4 — Apply for a square footing of width B (using Newmark/Boussinesq's 2:1 approximate method):**

The stress at depth $z$ below a square footing of width B carrying a net load $Q$ is:

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{Q}{(B+z)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 10% rule:**

$$\frac{Q}{(B+z_c)^2} = 0.10 \cdot q_0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for critical depth $z_c$:**

$$(B+z_c)^2 = \frac{Q}{0.10 \cdot q_0} = \frac{10\,Q}{q_0}$$

$$B + z_c = \sqrt{\frac{10\,Q}{q_0}}$$

$$\boxed{z_c = \sqrt{\frac{10\,Q}{q_0}} \; - \; B}$$

**Step 7 — Special case where Q = q · B²:**

Substituting $Q = q \cdot B^2$:

$$z_c = \sqrt{\frac{10\,q\,B^2}{q_0}} \; - \; B$$

$$\boxed{z_c = B \cdot \left[ \sqrt{\frac{10\,q}{q_0}} \; - \; 1 \right]}$$

This is the **exact depth of boring** required below the base of a square footing to satisfy Boussinesq's 10% rule.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model for Part B Questions)

**Problem (Typical KTU Style):**
A square footing of size **3 m × 3 m** transmits a net contact pressure of **150 kN/m²** to the soil. The unit weight of the overburden soil is **18 kN/m³**. Determine the **minimum depth of boring** as per:
(a) Terzaghi's rule
(b) Boussinesq's 10% rule

**Given Data:**
- $B = 3$ m (width of footing)
- $q = 150$ kN/m² (net contact pressure)
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ (unit weight of soil)
- Foundation is square

**Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Terzaghi's Rule

According to Terzaghi, the depth of boring below the base of an isolated footing should be **1.5 B to 2 B**.

**Step 1 — Calculate 1.5 B:**

$$D_{min} = 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate 2 B:**

$$D_{max} = 2.0 \times 3 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D_{Terzaghi} = 4.5 \text{ m to } 6.0 \text{ m below the base of the footing}}$$

#### Part (b) — Boussinesq's 10% Rule

**Step 1 — Compute net load Q on the footing:**

$$Q = q \cdot B^2 = 150 \times (3)^2 = 150 \times 9 = 1350 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute gross overburden pressure at depth $z_c$ (assuming uniform soil):**

$$q_0 = \gamma \cdot z_c = 18 \cdot z_c$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 10% rule condition:**

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{Q}{(B + z_c)^2} = 0.10 \cdot q_0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{1350}{(3 + z_c)^2} = 0.10 \times 18 \cdot z_c = 1.8 \cdot z_c$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to a quadratic equation in $z_c$:**

$$(3 + z_c)^2 \cdot 1.8 \cdot z_c = 1350$$

$$1.8 \cdot z_c \cdot (z_c^2 + 6 z_c + 9) = 1350$$

$$1.8 z_c^3 + 10.8 z_c^2 + 16.2 z_c - 1350 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve the cubic equation by trial-and-error (Bourdon Method / Newton-Raphson):**

**Trial 1:** $z_c = 7$ m

$$1.8(343) + 10.8(49) + 16.2(7) - 1350 = 617.4 + 529.2 + 113.4 - 1350 = -90$$

**Trial 2:** $z_c = 8$ m

$$1.8(512) + 10.8(64) + 16.2(8) - 1350 = 921.6 + 691.2 + 129.6 - 1350 = 392.4$$

**Step 7 — Linear interpolation between $z_c = 7$ m and $z_c = 8$ m:**

$$z_c = 7 + \frac{\vert -90 \vert}{392.4 + \vert -90 \vert} \times (8 - 7)$$

$$z_c = 7 + \frac{90}{482.4} \times 1 = 7 + 0.1866 = 7.19 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{z_c \approx 7.2 \text{ m below the base of the footing}}$$

**Step 8 — Conclusion and Engineering Decision:**

The boring must extend to a depth of at least the **governing (larger) value**:

$$D_{governing} = \max(D_{Terzaghi},\ z_c) = \max(4.5 \text{ to } 6.0 \text{ m},\ 7.2 \text{ m})$$

$$\boxed{\therefore D_{required} = 7.2 \text{ m below the base of the footing}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Since the Boussinesq-derived depth (7.2 m) is greater than Terzaghi's upper bound (6.0 m), the **10% rule governs**. This is typical for heavily loaded footings on soft soils.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Spacing and Depth

| Step | Engineering Decision | Reference Standard | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify structure type & loads | Project brief | Type, B, Q |
| 2 | Classify geological complexity | Site visit, desk study | Homogeneous / Variable |
| 3 | Choose stage of investigation | IS 1892 | Preliminary / Detailed |
| 4 | Read spacing range | IS 1892 Table | S (m) |
| 5 | Estimate footing dimensions | Structural design | B (m) |
| 6 | Compute Terzaghi depth | Empirical rule | $D_T = 1.5B$ to $2B$ |
| 7 | Compute Boussinesq depth | 10% rule | $z_c$ |
| 8 | Compute pile depth (if applicable) | IS 2911 | $D_p$ |
| 9 | Govern depth = max of all | Engineering judgment | $D_{final}$ |
| 10 | Cross-check with soil type | IS 1498 (Classification) | Final approval |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart for Selecting Spacing and Depth of Borings

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Initiation] --> B[Identify Structure Type]
    B --> B1[Building]
    B --> B2[Bridge]
    B --> B3[Dam]
    B --> B4[Highway / Airfield]
    B --> B5[Industrial Plant]

    B1 --> C[Geological Study]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C

    C --> D{Soil Uniformity?}
    D -->|Homogeneous| E1[Use Upper Spacing Limit]
    D -->|Heterogeneous| E2[Use Lower Spacing Limit]

    E1 --> F[Apply IS 1892 Spacing Table]
    E2 --> F

    F --> G[Determine Foundation Type]
    G --> G1[Isolated Footing]
    G --> G2[Raft Foundation]
    G --> G3[Pile Foundation]

    G1 --> H1[Terzaghi Rule: D = 1.5B to 2B]
    G2 --> H2[D = 1.5B to 2B of raft width]
    G3 --> H3[D = 10 to 30 m or pile depth + 5m]

    H1 --> I[Boussinesq 10% Rule Cross-check]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J{Governing Depth?}
    J -->|Terzaghi| K1[Adopt Terzaghi Depth]
    J -->|Boussinesq| K2[Adopt Boussinesq Depth]

    K1 --> L[Final Drilling Specification]
    K2 --> L

    L --> M[End: Issue Boring Layout Plan]
```

```mermaid
    block:Process1["Spacing Selection"] block:Process2["Depth Calculation"] block:Process3["Drilling Method"]
    block:Output1["Borehole Layout Plan"] block:Output2["Soil Profile Log"] block:Output3["SPT / Sampling"]

    Input1 --> Process1
    Input2 --> Process1
    Input3 --> Process1

    Input3 --> Process2

    Process1 --> Output1
    Process2 --> Output2
    Process3 --> Output3
    Output1 --> Output2
    Output2 --> Output3
```

```mermaid
    block:Layer2["Layer 2: Medium Sand"]
```
