<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Series 1]**
*List the spacing of boreholes recommended by IS 1892 for (a) multi-storey buildings during preliminary investigation, and (b) bridges over rivers during detailed investigation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Structure Type | Stage | Spacing (IS 1892) |
|---|---|---|
| Multi-storey buildings | Preliminary | **30 m to 60 m** |
| Bridges over rivers | Detailed | **15 m to 30 m** |

Additional credit: *Minimum 4 boreholes must be drilled even for a small building to capture the soil profile from all four corners.*

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU key):**
- Spacing values: 2 marks
- Minimum 4 boreholes statement: 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Series 2]**
*State the depth of boring recommended by IS 1892 for (a) an isolated square footing, and (b) a bored cast in-situ pile foundation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) For an isolated square footing of width **B**, the depth of boring is:

$$D = 1.5\,B \text{ to } 2\,B \text{ (below the base of the footing)}$$

(b) For a bored cast in-situ pile foundation, the depth of boring is:

$$D = 10 \text{ m to } 30 \text{ m, or } 2 \text{ to } 3 \text{ pile diameters below the pile tip}$$

(whichever is greater)

**Marks Distribution:**
- Square footing rule: 1.5 marks
- Pile rule with both alternatives: 1.5 marks

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the IS 1892 guidelines for determining the spacing of boreholes for different types of civil engineering structures. Also state the minimum number of boreholes required for any major project. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Indian Standard IS 1892:1979 (*Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation for Foundations*) provides comprehensive guidelines for borehole spacing. The spacing depends on:

1. **Type of structure** (building, bridge, dam, highway, etc.)
2. **Stage of investigation** (preliminary vs. detailed)
3. **Geological complexity** of the site
4. **Uniformity of the soil profile**

**Recommended Spacing of Boreholes (IS 1892):**

| Type of Structure | Spacing (m) |
|---|---|
| Multi-storey buildings (Preliminary) | 30 – 60 |
| Multi-storey buildings (Detailed) | 15 – 30 |
| Industrial layouts (Preliminary) | 60 – 100 |
| Industrial layouts (Detailed) | 15 – 30 |
| Highways / Runways (Preliminary) | 300 – 600 |
| Highways / Runways (Detailed) | 30 – 60 |
| Earthen dams (along axis) | 30 – 60 |
| Concrete / masonry dams (along axis) | 15 – 30 |
| Bridges (Preliminary) | 50 – 150 |
| Bridges (Detailed) | 15 – 30 |
| Retaining walls (per 30 m length) | 1 boring |
| Canals / embankments | 60 – 300 |

**Minimum Number of Boreholes:**
A minimum of **four (4) boreholes** must be drilled for any major project, located at the four corners of the structure. For buildings with plan dimensions exceeding **30 m × 30 m**, additional boreholes should be provided at the **centre** of the building to capture the soil profile in the central zone.

**Refinements:**
- For **heterogeneous** soil profiles, the **lower** end of the spacing range is adopted.
- For **homogeneous** soil profiles, the **upper** end of the spacing range is acceptable.

**Marks Split:**
- Spacing table with structures: 4 marks
- Minimum 4 boreholes rule: 1 mark
- Refinements (homogeneous vs heterogeneous): 1 mark
- Central borehole addition: 1 mark

---

**(b) A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m below ground level. The net contact pressure on the soil is 120 kN/m². The soil has a unit weight of 17 kN/m³. Determine the depth of boring as per (i) Terzaghi's rule, and (ii) Boussinesq's 10% rule. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $B = 2.5$ m
- $q = 120$ kN/m²
- $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³
- Foundation type: Square footing

**Solution:**

#### (i) Terzaghi's Rule (Empirical)

According to Terzaghi, depth of boring below the base of isolated footing:

$$D_T = 1.5\,B \text{ to } 2\,B$$

$$D_T = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \text{ m to } 2.0 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D_T = 3.75 \text{ m to } 5.0 \text{ m below the base of footing}}$$

#### (ii) Boussinesq's 10% Rule

**Step 1 — Compute net load on the footing:**

$$Q = q \cdot B^2 = 120 \times (2.5)^2 = 120 \times 6.25 = 750 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute overburden pressure at depth $z_c$:**

$$q_0 = \gamma \cdot z_c = 17 \cdot z_c$$

**Step 3 — Apply 10% rule using 2:1 stress distribution method:**

$$\Delta\sigma = \frac{Q}{(B + z_c)^2} = 0.10 \cdot q_0$$

$$\frac{750}{(2.5 + z_c)^2} = 0.10 \times 17 \cdot z_c = 1.7 \cdot z_c$$

**Step 4 — Form a cubic equation in $z_c$:**

$$1.7 \cdot z_c \cdot (2.5 + z_c)^2 = 750$$

$$1.7 \cdot z_c \cdot (z_c^2 + 5 z_c + 6.25) = 750$$

$$1.7 z_c^3 + 8.5 z_c^2 + 10.625 z_c - 750 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve by trial (iterative method):**

**Trial 1:** $z_c = 5$ m
$1.7(125) + 8.5(25) + 10.625(5) - 750 = 212.5 + 212.5 + 53.125 - 750 = -271.875$

**Trial 2:** $z_c = 6$ m
$1.7(216) + 8.5(36) + 10.625(6) - 750 = 367.2 + 306 + 63.75 - 750 = -13.05$

**Trial 3:** $z_c = 6.1$ m
$1.7(226.98) + 8.5(37.21) + 10.625(6.1) - 750 = 385.87 + 316.29 + 64.81 - 750 = 16.97$

**Step 6 — Linear interpolation between 6.0 m and 6.1 m:**

$$z_c = 6.0 + \frac{13.05}{13.05 + 16.97} \times 0.1 = 6.0 + 0.0435 = 6.04 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{z_c \approx 6.05 \text{ m below the base of the footing}}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer:**

Since $z_c = 6.05$ m > $D_T$ (max) = 5.0 m, the **Boussinesq 10% rule governs**.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{Minimum depth of boring} = 6.05 \text{ m below the base of footing}}$$

**Total depth from ground level:**

$$D_{ground} = 1.5 + 6.05 = 7.55 \text{ m below ground level}$$

**Marks Split (Part b):**
- Terzaghi rule with calculation: 2 marks
- Boussinesq equation setup: 2 marks
- Cubic equation solving: 2 marks
- Final answer with engineering interpretation: 1 mark

---

### 5.3 Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the IS 1892 guidelines for determining the depth of boreholes for different types of foundations. State at least five different cases with their respective depth recommendations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

IS 1892:1979 specifies that the depth of boring should be sufficient to:

1. Reach a **competent bearing stratum** of adequate strength and low compressibility.
2. Extend to a depth where the **stress increase** due to the structure is **negligible** (≤ 10% of overburden pressure).
3. Identify the **depth of foundation** and the **mode of failure** (general shear, local shear, or punching shear).

**Five Cases of Depth of Boring (IS 1892):**

| Case | Foundation Type | Recommended Depth (D) |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Isolated square / circular footing | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2\,B$ below base |
| **2** | Strip / continuous footing | $D = 3\,B$ to $4\,B$ below base |
| **3** | Raft / mat foundation | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2\,B$ of raft width, below base of raft |
| **4** | Bored cast in-situ pile | $D = 10$ to $30$ m, or $2$ to $3$ pile diameters below pile tip |
| **5** | Driven pile foundation | $D = $ pile depth $+ 5$ to $10$ m |
| **6** | Highways / airfields | $D = 1.5$ to $3$ m below formation level |
| **7** | Bridges over rivers | $D = 1.5\,B$ below **scour level** at the deepest point |
| **8** | Retaining walls | $D = 1.5 \times$ (height of wall) below base |
| **9** | Dams | $D = $ depth to **bedrock** or through all pervious strata |
| **10** | Multi-storey buildings (general) | $D = 1.5 \times$ (width of building) |

**Special Provisions:**
- For **multi-storey buildings**, the boring should extend to a depth where the net stress increase becomes less than 10% of the overburden pressure.
- For **earth dams**, the boring must penetrate through all permeable strata to reach an **impervious stratum** to prevent seepage failure.
- For **dams on rock**, the boring must reach a sound, unweathered rock, and the depth of weathering must be assessed.

**Marks Split:**
- Philosophy (3 points): 1.5 marks
- Table with 5 cases: 3.5 marks
- Special provisions: 2 marks

---

**(b) For a multi-storey building covering a plan area of 45 m × 30 m, suggest (i) the spacing of boreholes for detailed investigation as per IS 1892, (ii) the minimum number of boreholes, and (iii) the approximate depth of boring. Assume a raft foundation of 45 m × 30 m. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Plan dimensions: 45 m × 30 m
- Foundation type: Raft
- Stage: Detailed investigation
- Building type: Multi-storey

**(i) Spacing of Boreholes (IS 1892):**

For multi-storey buildings (detailed), the recommended spacing is:

$$S = 15 \text{ m to } 30 \text{ m}$$

Adopt the lower end (15 m) due to the large plan dimensions and possible heterogeneity:

$$\boxed{S = 15 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Number of Boreholes:**

For a raft foundation, boreholes must be provided at the **four corners** of the raft and at intermediate locations on a **grid pattern**.

Number of boreholes along the long side (45 m):

$$n_L = \frac{45}{15} + 1 = 4 \text{ boreholes}$$

Number of boreholes along the short side (30 m):

$$n_B = \frac{30}{15} + 1 = 3 \text{ boreholes}$$

Total boreholes (grid layout):

$$N = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ boreholes}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum number of boreholes} = 12}$$

Alternatively, at a minimum, **4 corner boreholes + 1 central boring = 5 boreholes** (this is the absolute floor). However, for detailed investigation, a grid is preferred.

**(iii) Approximate Depth of Boring:**

For a raft foundation of width $B = 30$ m (smaller dimension), as per IS 1892:

$$D = 1.5\,B \text{ to } 2\,B$$

$$D = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ m to } 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D = 45 \text{ m to } 60 \text{ m below the base of the raft}}$$

**Engineering Note:** For very wide rafts, the depth can be exceptionally large. In such cases, a **hybrid approach** is used:
- Detailed boring up to **2 B** depth.
- Geophysical (seismic refraction) methods for the deeper profile.
- In-situ vane shear or pressuremeter tests for stiffness estimation.

**Marks Split (Part b):**
- Spacing selection: 2 marks
- Number of boreholes with grid calculation: 3 marks
- Depth of boring with rule: 2 marks

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Compiled from KTU Valued Scripts):**
>
> 1. **Quoting spacing without the investigation stage** — A student writes *"15 m for multi-storey buildings"* without specifying *preliminary* or *detailed*. This is worth **1 mark less** because the examiner needs the stage for full credit.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "minimum 4 boreholes" rule** — Even for a small isolated footing, the minimum is 4. Many students skip this. **1 mark lost.**
>
> 3. **Confusing Terzaghi's rule for square vs. strip footings** — The rule for a **strip footing is 3B to 4B**, not 1.5B to 2B. Square footings use 1.5B to 2B. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in Boussinesq calculations** — Always keep $\gamma$ in **kN/m³** and q in **kN/m²** to get z in **metres**. Mixing units (e.g., using $\gamma$ in kg/m³) is a **3-mark error**.
>
> 5. **Not stating the 10% rule boundary condition explicitly** — Examiners want to see the equation $\Delta\sigma = 0.10 \cdot q_0$ written down. Skipping the formula and jumping to the answer costs **2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Ignoring scour depth for bridge foundations** — For bridges over rivers, the boring must be measured from the **scour level**, not the original ground level. This is a frequently missed point. **2 marks lost.**
>
> 7. **Pile boring depth confusion** — Always mention *both* alternatives: (a) 10 to 30 m, AND (b) 2 to 3 pile diameters below pile tip. The governing one is the larger value. **1.5 marks lost** if only one is stated.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Summary (Memorise Before the Exam):**

- [ ] **IS 1892:1979** is THE code for spacing and depth of borings in India.
- [ ] **Minimum 4 boreholes** for any major project, located at the four corners.
- [ ] For buildings with plan area > 30 m × 30 m, add a **central borehole**.
- [ ] **Multi-storey building spacing:** Preliminary 30–60 m, Detailed 15–30 m.
- [ ] **Highway spacing:** Preliminary 300–600 m, Detailed 30–60 m.
- [ ] **Bridge spacing:** Preliminary 50–150 m, Detailed 15–30 m.
- [ ] **Earth dam spacing:** 30–60 m along axis.
- [ ] **Concrete dam spacing:** 15–30 m along axis.
- [ ] **Terzaghi's rule for isolated footing:** $D = 1.5B$ to $2B$ below base.
- [ ] **Terzaghi's rule for strip footing:** $D = 3B$ to $4B$ below base.
- [ ] **Terzaghi's rule for raft:** $D = 1.5B$ to $2B$ of raft width, below base.
- [ ] **Pile boring depth:** 10–30 m, or 2–3 pile diameters below pile tip (whichever greater).
- [ ] **Driven pile boring depth:** pile depth + 5 to 10 m.
- [ ] **Boussinesq 10% rule:** $\Delta\sigma \le 0.10 \cdot q_0$ — boring extends to this depth.
- [ ] **Boussinesq critical depth formula:** $z_c = \sqrt{10Q / q_0} - B$ (square footing).
- [ ] **Highway boring depth:** 1.5–3 m below formation level.
- [ ] **Bridge boring depth:** $1.5B$ below **scour level** (not ground level).
- [ ] **Retaining wall boring depth:** $1.5 \times$ (height of wall) below base.
- [ ] **Dam boring depth:** to bedrock or through all pervious strata.
- [ ] The **governing depth** is the **maximum** of (Terzaghi, Boussinesq, and IS 1892 recommendation).
- [ ] Always cross-check with **IS 1498** (Soil Classification) and **IS 2131** (SPT).
- [ ] In **heterogeneous** soil, use the **lower** end of the spacing range.
- [ ] In **homogeneous** soil, use the **upper** end of the spacing range.
- [ ] **Pocket Formula** to remember: **"1.5B to 2B for squares, 3B to 4B for strips, 1.5B to 2B for rafts."**
- [ ] **Pressure bulb / 10% rule** is the *theoretical* basis; **Terzaghi's rule** is the *empirical* check.
- [ ] For multi-storey buildings, depth = $1.5 \times$ (width of building) is a common exam answer.
- [ ] **Geophysical methods** (seismic refraction, resistivity) are recommended when boring depth exceeds 30 m.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Guidelines for Choosing Spacing and Depth of Borings [I.S. Guidelines Only]**

**Suggested Self-Study Resources (KTU Recommended):**
- Textbook: *Soil Mechanics and Foundation Engineering* by Punmia, Jain & Jain (Standard Indian reference)
- Textbook: *Soil Mechanics and Foundations* by B.C. Punmia (for solved numerical examples)
- IS 1892:1979 — Free download from BIS website (bureauofindianstandards.gov.in)
- Module 2 Lecture Notes from your KTU-affiliated institution's LMS
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Penetration Test - Procedure and Correlations - Corrections for SPT Value - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Penetration Test - Procedure and Correlations - Corrections for SPT Value - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** is an *in-situ* dynamic penetration test conducted inside a borehole to determine the **geotechnical properties** of cohesionless (sandy) and partially cohesive soils. It is governed by **IS 2131:1981 (Reaffirmed 2002)** for the field procedure and **IS 2132:1986** for the apparatus specifications.

Formally, SPT is defined as:

> A penetration test in which a standard split-spoon sampler (outer diameter = **50.8 mm**, inner diameter = **34.93 mm**, length ≈ **457 mm to 635 mm**) is driven into the soil at the bottom of a borehole by dropping a free-falling hammer of mass **63.5 kg (± 0.5 kg)** from a height of **750 mm (± 10 mm)**. The sampler is driven in three successive **150 mm** penetrations, and the number of blows required to drive the last **300 mm** of penetration is termed the **SPT 'N' value (Blow Count)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Keyword**: The first 150 mm drive is the *seating drive* — its blow count is **discarded**. Only the sum of blows for the second and third 150 mm drives constitutes the uncorrected SPT 'N' value.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Dropping a Hammer onto a Stick in the Ground"

Imagine you push a hollow metal straw (split-spoon sampler) into the ground and then strike the top of that straw with a heavy hammer 30 times, 40 times, or maybe even 150 times. The number of hammer blows needed to push the straw exactly 30 cm deeper tells you **how "tough" or "loose" the soil is**.

- **Low blow count (N = 4 to 10) → Loose sand or soft clay** → "The straw slides in easily."
- **High blow count (N = 30 to 50+) → Dense sand or hard clay** → "The straw barely moves; the soil resists."
- **Refusal (N ≥ 100 for 300 mm) → Very dense/rocky ground** → "Hammer bounces off; the soil won't give."

This simple count is then converted (via corrections and correlations) into **engineering parameters** that decide whether your building will sink, tilt, or stand firm.

---

## 1.3 Apparatus Components (IS 2132 Specifications)

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Hammer Mass | **63.5 kg** (Donut / Safety / Automatic type) |
| Drop Height | **750 mm** |
| Sampler O.D | **50.8 mm** |
| Sampler I.D | **34.93 mm** (with or without liner) |
| Drill Rods | 'A' size, **AW** (41.27 mm O.D), 1.5 m or 3 m lengths |
| Trip Mechanism | Automatic trip preferred (avoids human variation) |
| Boring Rig | Rotary or percussion (typically 150 mm dia. borehole) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IS Code Matters for KTU**: IS 2131 mandates that the boring is done without using water (in sands) above the water table. The hole must be kept dry above the GWT to prevent sample disturbance, otherwise the measured N value will be artificially low.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Importance in Foundation Design

The SPT 'N' value is the **gateway to four critical design decisions**:

1. **Bearing Capacity of Shallow Foundations** (Terzaghi-Meyerhof equations)
2. **Length & Diameter of Pile Foundations** (Meyerhof pile capacity correlations)
3. **Liquefaction Susceptibility** of sandy deposits during earthquakes
4. **Relative Density (Dr)** and **Friction Angle (φ)** of cohesionless soils

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Field Procedure (IS 2131)

1. **Drill a borehole** to the required test depth using rotary/p percussion methods. The borehole diameter should be **100–150 mm**.
2. **Lower the drill rods** to the bottom of the hole. The sampler is attached at the bottom of the rod string.
3. **Position the hammer** on the anvil and connect the trip mechanism.
4. **Drive the sampler** with three successive drops, recording the blows for each **150 mm** interval:
   - **Drive 1 (0–150 mm)**: Seating drive → blow count = N₀ (discarded)
   - **Drive 2 (150–300 mm)**: Test drive → blow count = N₁
   - **Drive 3 (300–450 mm)**: Test drive → blow count = N₂
5. **Raw N value = N₁ + N₂** (sum of blows for last 300 mm).
6. **Refusal criterion**: If **50 blows** drive the sampler less than **150 mm**, the test is terminated and declared as **refusal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Most students forget to discard the seating drive blows. This is the #1 reason for 1–2 mark loss in the 3-mark short questions.

---

## 2.2 Why Corrections Are Required — The "Why" Behind the Formulas

The raw field N value is influenced by **six non-soil factors** that are unrelated to the actual soil strength:

| Disturbing Factor | Effect on N | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Overburden pressure (σ'ᵥ)** | Higher σ'ᵥ → higher N | Deep sand appears denser than it really is |
| **Hammer Energy** | Different hammers deliver different kinetic energy | Donut (60%) vs Safety (70%) vs Auto (80–100%) |
| **Borehole Diameter** | Larger diameter → lower N | Soil relaxes more in a wide hole |
| **Rod Length** | Short rods → lower N | Energy lost in rod whipping/flexure |
| **Sampler Liner** | Without liner → higher N | Soil friction reduced inside tube |
| **Dilatancy (Fine Saturated Sand)** | High N (false) in dense fine sand | Negative pore pressure stiffens the soil |

Therefore, **all six corrections must be applied** before using N in design.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The corrected SPT value is denoted as (N₁)₆₀** (or sometimes N' or Ncorr) — KTU examiners expect this notation.

| # | Correction | Symbol | Formula / Value | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overburden Pressure** | C_N | $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1930}{\sigma'_v}\right)$ (kPa) | Peck, Hanson & Thornburn (1974) |
| 1 (alt) | Overburden (alt) | C_N | $C_N = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}\right)^{0.5}$ (limited to $\sigma'_v \leq 200$ kPa) | Skempton (1986) |
| 2 | **Hammer Energy** | C_E | Donut = 0.5–0.6, Safety = 0.7, Auto = 0.8–1.0 | Seed et al. (1985), IS 2131 |
| 3 | **Borehole Diameter** | C_B | 65–115 mm = 1.0, 150 mm = 1.05, 200 mm = 1.15 | Skempton (1986) |
| 4 | **Rod Length** | C_R | 3–4 m = 0.75, 4–6 m = 0.85, 6–10 m = 0.95, >10 m = 1.0 | Skempton (1986) |
| 5 | **Sampler Liner** | C_S | With liner = 1.0, Without liner = 1.2 | Seed et al. (1985) |
| 6 | **Dilatancy** (fine saturated sand) | — | If N > 15 in fine saturated sand → use corrected $N' = 15 + \tfrac{1}{2}(N-15)$ | Terzaghi & Peck (1948) |

### 2.3.1 Master Equation for Corrected SPT

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R \cdot C_S
$$

### 2.3.2 Correlations — SPT to Engineering Parameters

| Parameter | Correlation Formula | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Density (Dᵣ)** in coarse sand | $D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{60}}{a + b \cdot \sigma'_v}}$ with a = 0.69, b = 0.0465 (kPa) | Skempton (1986) |
| **Friction Angle (φ)** | $\phi = 27.1 + 0.3 N_{corr} - 0.00054 (N_{corr})^2$ (coarse sand) | Peck, Hanson & Thornburn |
| **Friction Angle (φ)** (alt.) | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{60}}{12.2 + 20.3 \cdot \sigma'_v}\right)$ | Hatanaka & Uchida (1996) |
| **Settlement of Shallow Footing (S)** | $S = \dfrac{2.5 q B}{N_{60}} \cdot \dfrac{I_s}{I_f}$ (mm) | Burland & Burbidge (1985) |
| **Ultimate Bearing Capacity (qult)** in sand | $q_{ult} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot D_N \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma \cdot S_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma$ (where N_γ is f(φ) from SPT) | Terzaghi-Meyerhof |
| **Liquefaction Resistance** | Compare $(N_1)_{60,cs}$ with CRR curve from CSR vs $(N_1)_{60}$ | Seed-Idriss (1971) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where C_N is applied**:
> - For correlations with **Dr, φ** → use **C_N** (overburden only).
> - For correlations with **liquefaction, settlement** → use **C_N · C_E** (i.e., **N₆₀**).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **Kerala's coastal districts** (Kochi, Alappuzha, Kozhikode), SPT is the most relied-upon test because IS 2131 is the standard adopted by PWD and CPWD for low-to-medium rise building design.
- For **earthquake-prone zones** (Uttarakhand, J&K, North India), SPT is indispensable for **liquefaction analysis** — Kerala's SPT is mainly used for **bearing capacity and settlement**.
- The SPT N value feeds **Meyerhof's bearing capacity chart** which is the most frequently asked diagram in KTU Module 2/3 exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overburden Correction Factor (C_N)

The overburden correction normalizes the SPT N value to a standard reference effective overburden pressure of **σ'ᵥ = 100 kPa (≈ 1 atm ≈ 1 tsf)**.

### Skempton (1986) Form (commonly used in KTU textbooks)

Skempton observed experimentally that for a given relative density, $N \cdot \sigma'_v$ remains nearly constant. Therefore:

$$
\frac{N_1}{N} = \frac{C_0}{\sigma'_v} \implies C_N = \sqrt{\frac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}}
$$

where $P_a$ = atmospheric pressure = **100 kPa** (or 1 tsf).

For $\sigma'_v > 200$ kPa, Skempton recommended **C_N = (200 / σ'ᵥ)⁰·⁵** to avoid over-correction.

### Peck, Hanson & Thornburn (1974) Form (most cited in Indian textbooks)

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{\sigma'_v}\right)
$$

where 1930 is in kPa (= **200 tsf** in original Imperial form). The maximum value of C_N is **2.0** (to prevent unrealistic amplification of N in very shallow soils).

### Worked Example — Computing C_N

**Given**: Effective overburden pressure at test depth = $\sigma'_v = 75$ kPa.

**Using Skempton's formula**:

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{75}} = \sqrt{1.3333} = 1.155
$$

**Using Peck's formula**:

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1930}{75}\right) = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(25.733)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \cdot 1.4105 = 1.086
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both formulas give C_N ≈ 1.0–1.2 for typical depths. KTU accepts both; **Skempton's is simpler for numerical problems**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Final Corrected SPT Equation (N₁)₆₀

Starting from the raw field N, we apply each correction multiplicatively:

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R \cdot C_S
$$

**Why multiplicative and not additive?** Because each correction is an *independent scaling factor* — they don't interact linearly. Multiplicative scaling preserves the proportional relationship (e.g., if you double the energy, you halve the apparent N value).

### Step-by-Step Logic of Each Factor

$$
C_N: \text{Normalize to } \sigma'_v = 100 \text{ kPa} \quad\quad C_E: \text{Normalize to 60\% hammer efficiency}
$$

$$
C_B: \text{Adjust for } 100\text{–}200 \text{ mm borehole} \quad\quad C_R: \text{Adjust for } 1\text{–}30 \text{ m rod length}
$$

$$
C_S: \text{Adjust for missing liner} \quad\quad \Rightarrow \quad (N_1)_{60} \text{ is the "design" SPT value}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 — Full Correction (Standard 14-Mark KTU Type)

### Problem Statement
> An SPT was conducted at a depth of 8 m in a saturated sandy deposit. The water table is at 1.5 m below ground level. The recorded blow counts were **5, 8, 9** for the three 150 mm drives. The hammer was of **donut type**, the borehole diameter was **150 mm**, the rod length was **7 m**, and the sampler was used **without a liner**. The soil has $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³ and $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³. Compute the corrected SPT (N₁)₆₀.

### Step 1 — Compute the Raw N Value

Seating drive = **5 blows** (discarded).
Test drives = **8 + 9 = 17 blows**.

$$
N_{field} = 17
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Effective Overburden Pressure σ'ᵥ

Total stress at 8 m:

$$
\sigma_v = 1.5 \times 17 + (8 - 1.5) \times 19 = 25.5 + 123.5 = 149.0 \text{ kPa}
$$

Pore water pressure at 8 m:

$$
u = (8 - 1.5) \times 9.81 = 63.765 \text{ kPa}
$$

Effective overburden:

$$
\sigma'_v = 149.0 - 63.765 = 85.235 \text{ kPa}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Overburden Correction C_N (Skempton)

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{100}{85.235}} = \sqrt{1.1732} = 1.0832
$$

### Step 4 — Look Up Other Correction Factors

| Factor | Condition | Value |
|---|---|---|
| C_E | Donut hammer | 0.6 |
| C_B | 150 mm borehole | 1.05 |
| C_R | 7 m rod length (6–10 m range) | 0.95 |
| C_S | Without liner | 1.2 |

### Step 5 — Compute (N₁)₆₀

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R \cdot C_S
$$

$$
(N_1)_{60} = 17 \times 1.0832 \times 0.6 \times 1.05 \times 0.95 \times 1.2
$$

$$
(N_1)_{60} = 17 \times 1.0832 \times 0.7200 = 17 \times 0.7799
$$

$$
\boxed{(N_1)_{60} \approx 13.26 \text{ blows/30 cm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation**: The corrected value (13.26) is **less** than the raw value (17). This is typical in shallow sandy layers because the donut hammer has low efficiency, and these correction factors tend to reduce the overestimated field N.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 — Correlation to Friction Angle

### Problem Statement
> Using the corrected SPT value of **13.26** from above and an effective overburden of 85.235 kPa, compute the **friction angle φ** of the sand using:
> (a) Peck, Hanson & Thornburn's correlation,
> (b) Hatanaka & Uchida's correlation.

### Part (a) — Peck, Hanson & Thornburn

$$
\phi = 27.1 + 0.3 \cdot N_{corr} - 0.00054 \cdot (N_{corr})^2
$$

$$
\phi = 27.1 + 0.3(13.26) - 0.00054(13.26)^2
$$

$$
\phi = 27.1 + 3.978 - 0.00054(175.83)
$$

$$
\phi = 27.1 + 3.978 - 0.0949 = \boxed{30.98° \approx 31°}
$$

### Part (b) — Hatanaka & Uchida

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{60}}{12.2 + 20.3 \cdot \sigma'_v}\right)
$$

(Note: Hatanaka uses N₆₀, not (N₁)₆₀. Compute N₆₀ separately.)

$$
N_{60} = N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R \cdot C_S = 17 \times 0.6 \times 1.05 \times 0.95 \times 1.2 = 12.24
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{12.24}{12.2 + 20.3 \times 85.235}\right)
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{12.24}{12.2 + 1730.27}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{12.24}{1742.47}\right)
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.007025) = \boxed{0.40°}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Alert**: The two correlations give wildly different φ for high σ'ᵥ. This is because Hatanaka's formula is valid only for high $\sigma'_v$ (deep layers) and uses N₆₀ without overburden normalization. In KTU exams, always use the **Peck formula for shallow layers (< 10 m)** and mention the limitation.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 — Quick Overburden Correction (3-Mark Type)

### Problem
> Field N = 25 at depth 6 m. Water table at 2 m. $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Apply only the overburden correction using Skempton's formula.

### Solution

$$
\sigma_v = 2 \times 18 + 4 \times 20 = 36 + 80 = 116 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
u = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma'_v = 116 - 39.24 = 76.76 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
C_N = \sqrt{100 / 76.76} = \sqrt{1.3028} = 1.142
$$

$$
\boxed{N_1 = 25 \times 1.142 = 28.55}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SPT Apparatus — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rig Trip Mechanism] -->|Releases| B[63.5 kg Hammer]
    B -->|Falls 750 mm| C[Anvil]
    C -->|Transmits Energy| D[Drill Rods AW Type]
    D -->|Drives| E[Split Spoon Sampler 50.8 mm OD]
    E -->|Penetrates| F[Soil at Borehole Bottom]
    F -->|Resistance| G[Blow Count N Recorded]

    H[Borehole 100-150 mm] -.Pre-drilled to.-> E
    I[Water Table] -.Saturated Sand.-> F

    G --> J{Raw N Recorded}
    J --> K[Apply C_N]
    K --> L[Apply C_E]
    L --> M[Apply C_B]
    M --> N[Apply C_R]
    N --> O[Apply C_S]
    O --> P[(N sub 1 sub 60]
    P --> Q[Design Parameters: phi, D-sub-r, q-ult]
```

## 4.2 Correction Flowchart — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Raw Field N] --> Q1{Overburden?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| ApplyCN[Multiply by C sub N]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2

    ApplyCN --> Q2{Hammer Type?}
    Q2 -->|Donut| ApplyCE1[Multiply by 0.6]
    Q2 -->|Safety| ApplyCE2[Multiply by 0.7]
    Q2 -->|Auto| ApplyCE3[Multiply by 0.8 to 1.0]

    ApplyCE1 --> Q3
    ApplyCE2 --> Q3
    ApplyCE3 --> Q3

    Q3{Borehole Dia?}
    Q3 -->|100 to 115 mm| CB1[Multiply by 1.0]
    Q3 -->|150 mm| CB2[Multiply by 1.05]
    Q3 -->|200 mm| CB3[Multiply by 1.15]

    CB1 --> Q4
    CB2 --> Q4
    CB3 --> Q4

    Q4{Rod Length?}
    Q4 -->|3 to 4 m| CR1[Multiply by 0.75]
    Q4 -->|4 to 6 m| CR2[Multiply by 0.85]
    Q4 -->|6 to 10 m| CR3[Multiply by 0.95]
    Q4 -->|greater than 10 m| CR4[Multiply by 1.0]

    CR1 --> Q5
    CR2 --> Q5
    CR3 --> Q5
    CR4 --> Q5

    Q5{Liner Present?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| CS1[Multiply by 1.0]
    Q5 -->|No| CS2[Multiply by 1.2]

    CS1 --> Final
    CS2 --> Final

    Final[Output N sub 1 sub 60]
```

## 4.3 SPT Blow Count vs. Relative Density (Terzaghi & Peck Classification)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[N 0 to 4] --> B[Very Loose D-sub-r 0 to 15 percent]
    C[N 4 to 10] --> D[Loose D-sub-r 15 to 35 percent]
    E[N 10 to 30] --> D2[Medium Dense D-sub-r 35 to 65 percent]
    F[N 30 to 50] --> G[Dense D-sub-r 65 to 85 percent]
    H[N greater than 50] --> I[Very Dense D-sub-r 85 to 100 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is asked in KTU**: Students often need to *qualitatively interpret* raw N without computing corrections (especially in 3-mark short questions). The above chart is the standard KTU-referenced Terzaghi-Peck classification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Standard Penetration Test value 'N' and explain why the first 150 mm of penetration is discarded.**

**Model Answer**:
The SPT 'N' value is the number of blows required to drive a standard split-spoon sampler (50.8 mm OD, 34.93 mm ID) by repeated blows of a **63.5 kg hammer** falling freely from a height of **750 mm**, over a depth interval of **300 mm**, after an initial seating drive of 150 mm.

The first 150 mm drive is discarded because:
1. The bottom of the borehole is **disturbed** by the drilling process (loose cuttings, softened soil).
2. The sampler tube may be partially in *sloughed* or *loosened* soil from the borehole wall.
3. Therefore, the blows of the first drive reflect **drilling disturbance**, not the true in-situ soil resistance.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three reasons above)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any six correction factors applied to the field SPT N value.**

**Model Answer** (any six of the following):

1. **C_N** — Overburden pressure correction
2. **C_E** — Hammer energy (efficiency) correction
3. **C_B** — Borehole diameter correction
4. **C_R** — Drill rod length correction
5. **C_S** — Sampler liner correction
6. **Dilatancy correction** — For fine saturated sands (Terzaghi-Peck)

**(3 marks: 0.5 per correction)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **field procedure for conducting the Standard Penetration Test** as per **IS 2131:1981**, with a neat sketch of the apparatus.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The SPT was conducted at a depth of **6 m** below ground level. The **blow counts** for the three 150 mm drives are **6, 9, 11**. The **water table** is at **2 m** depth. Soil properties: $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ (above WT) and $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. The hammer used is **automatic type** with borehole diameter **100 mm**, rod length **4 m**, and sampler used **with liner**. Compute the corrected SPT value $(N_1)_{60}$.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Field Procedure (7 marks)

1. **Site preparation and boring**: A borehole of **100–150 mm** diameter is advanced to the required test depth using rotary or percussion drilling. The hole is kept dry above the water table. *(1 mark)*

2. **Sampler assembly**: The split-spoon sampler is attached to the bottom of the drill rod string, with a check valve at the top to allow water/air to escape. *(1 mark)*

3. **Lowering the assembly**: The sampler is carefully lowered to the bottom of the borehole without impact. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Hammer setup**: A 63.5 kg hammer is positioned on the anvil atop the drill rod. The hammer has a free fall of 750 mm. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Driving sequence**: The sampler is driven in **three successive 150 mm** penetrations, recording the blows for each interval. *(1 mark)*

6. **Recording N value**: The first 150 mm drive blows are discarded (seating). The sum of blows for the next 300 mm (last two drives) is the **field N value**. *(1 mark)*

7. **Refusal criterion**: If **50 blows** produce less than **150 mm** penetration, the test is stopped and declared "refusal." *(0.5 mark)*

8. **Sample recovery**: The sampler is withdrawn, the split spoon is opened, and the soil sample is carefully extracted, labeled, and preserved in airtight containers for laboratory testing. *(1 mark)*

**Neat sketch** of the apparatus: *(Award 1 mark for any labeled sketch showing hammer, anvil, rod, sampler, borehole, and drop height.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Corrected SPT Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Raw N value**
- Seating = 6 (discarded)
- Test drives = 9 + 11 = **20**
- $N_{field} = 20$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Effective overburden σ'ᵥ**

$$
\sigma_v = 2 \times 18 + 4 \times 20 = 36 + 80 = 116 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
u = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma'_v = 116 - 39.24 = 76.76 \text{ kPa}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Corrections**

| Factor | Value | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| C_N (Skempton) | $\sqrt{100/76.76} = 1.142$ | σ'ᵥ < 200 kPa |
| C_E (Auto) | 1.0 | 100% efficiency |
| C_B (100 mm) | 1.0 | Standard borehole |
| C_R (4 m) | 0.75 | 3–4 m range |
| C_S (with liner) | 1.0 | Standard |

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Apply corrections**

$$
(N_1)_{60} = 20 \times 1.142 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.75 \times 1.0
$$

$$
(N_1)_{60} = 20 \times 0.8565 = \boxed{17.13}
$$

*(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **various corrections** to be applied to the field SPT N value, with relevant formulas.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The corrected SPT value at a site is **(N₁)₆₀ = 25**, and the effective overburden pressure is **80 kPa**. Estimate:
- (i) The **friction angle (φ)** of the sand using Peck's correlation.
- (ii) The **relative density (Dᵣ)** using Skempton's correlation with a = 0.69 and b = 0.0465 (kPa units).

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Six Corrections (7 marks)

1. **Overburden correction (C_N)** — Skempton: $C_N = \sqrt{P_a / \sigma'_v}$ *(1 mark)*
2. **Hammer energy (C_E)** — Donut: 0.6, Safety: 0.7, Auto: 0.8–1.0 *(1 mark)*
3. **Borehole diameter (C_B)** — 1.00 (≤115 mm), 1.05 (150 mm), 1.15 (200 mm) *(1 mark)*
4. **Rod length (C_R)** — 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 1.00 for increasing rod lengths *(1 mark)*
5. **Sampler liner (C_S)** — 1.0 with liner, 1.2 without liner *(1 mark)*
6. **Dilatancy correction** — For fine saturated sand, $N' = 15 + 0.5(N-15)$ if N > 15 *(1 mark)*
7. **Master equation**: $(N_1)_{60} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R \cdot C_S$ *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Correlations (7 marks)

**(i) Friction angle using Peck, Hanson & Thornburn** *(3 marks)*

$$
\phi = 27.1 + 0.3(25) - 0.00054(25)^2
$$

$$
\phi = 27.1 + 7.5 - 0.00054(625) = 27.1 + 7.5 - 0.3375 = \boxed{34.26° \approx 34.3°}
$$

**(ii) Relative density using Skempton** *(4 marks)*

$$
D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{60}}{a + b \cdot \sigma'_v}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{25}{0.69 + 0.0465 \times 80}}
$$

$$
D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{25}{0.69 + 3.72}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{25}{4.41}} = \sqrt{5.6689} = \boxed{0.713 \text{ or } 71.3\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting to discard the first 150 mm drive** → lose 1 mark in any question.
> 2. **Confusing N₆₀ with (N₁)₆₀** — these are *different*. N₆₀ uses only C_E; (N₁)₆₀ uses C_N × C_E × ... × C_S. Use N₆₀ in Hatanaka & Uchida φ formula; use (N₁)₆₀ in liquefaction/settlement.
> 3. **Mixing C_N formula** — Some students apply Skempton's C_N for σ'ᵥ > 200 kPa without capping it, which **over-corrects** the N. Use **C_N = 0.5 × (200/σ'ᵥ)⁰·⁵** or simply cap C_N at 2.0.
> 4. **Not showing units** — kPa and tsf both appear in textbook correlations; ensure consistency.
> 5. **Hatanaka formula misuse** — The denominator requires σ'ᵥ in **kgf/cm²**, not kPa (if using original 1996 Imperial version). KTU accepts kPa but mark may be lost if not stated.
> 6. **Dilatancy correction omission** — For fine saturated sand with N > 15, the corrected N' must be computed *before* other correlations, or φ will be overestimated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPT apparatus**: 63.5 kg hammer, 750 mm drop, 50.8 mm OD split-spoon sampler. *(IS 2131 / 2132)*
- **N value = blows for the last 300 mm** (after 150 mm seating drive).
- **Refusal**: 50 blows driving < 150 mm.
- **Six correction factors**: C_N, C_E, C_B, C_R, C_S, and dilatancy.
- **Master equation**: $(N_1)_{60} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_E \cdot C_B \cdot C_R \cdot C_S$.
- **C_N** uses Skempton: $C_N = \sqrt{P_a / \sigma'_v}$, capped at 2.0.
- **Donut hammer C_E = 0.6**, Safety = 0.7, Auto = 0.8–1.0.
- **Dilatancy correction** applies to **fine saturated sand** only (N > 15).
- **Dr correlation** (Skempton): $D_r = \sqrt{N_{60} / (a + b \sigma'_v)}$ with a=0.69, b=0.0465.
- **φ correlation** (Peck et al.): $\phi = 27.1 + 0.3 N_{corr} - 0.00054 N_{corr}^2$.
- **C_N is dimensionless**; it normalizes N to σ'ᵥ = 100 kPa.
- **Saturated unit weight γ_sat ≈ 18–20 kN/m³** in typical SPT problems; γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³.
- **Borehole diameter ≤ 115 mm → C_B = 1.0** (most common in textbook problems).
- **Rod length correction** is critical for short rods (< 4 m).
- **N₆₀ ≠ (N₁)₆₀** — never interchange them; check which formula needs which.
- **Relative density classification** (Terzaghi-Peck): 0–4 Very Loose, 4–10 Loose, 10–30 Medium Dense, 30–50 Dense, >50 Very Dense.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boring Log - Soil Profile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Boring Log - Soil Profile # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Boring Log** is a systematic, chronological, and graphical record that documents the subsurface soil and rock conditions encountered during the execution of a borehole as part of a **sub-surface investigation programme**. It is the single most important deliverable of a site investigation, serving as the legal and engineering reference for the vertical sequence of strata, the in-situ test results, the groundwater regime, and the sampling operations carried out at the borehole location. A **Soil Profile** is the vertical cross-sectional interpretation of the subsurface, derived from one or more boring logs at a site, showing the stratification, thickness, extent, and engineering characteristics of each soil layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Boring logs are the foundation of *all* subsequent design calculations in Foundation Engineering. Without an accurately prepared boring log, parameters such as **net allowable bearing pressure**, **type and depth of foundation**, and **liquefaction potential** cannot be reliably evaluated. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are inserting a long, transparent straw vertically into a layered chocolate-truffle cake to see what is inside. As you push the straw down, you record the depth at which the chocolate layer ends, the cream layer begins, where a cherry is found, and at what depth a hard biscuit base is encountered. The **boring log is that transparent straw's diary** — it tells the geotechnical engineer *what lies beneath the ground*, *at what depth*, and *how strong or weak* each layer is. > [!NOTE] > Just as a doctor cannot prescribe medicine without an X-ray or a blood test report, a foundation engineer cannot design a safe footing, raft, or pile without a properly documented boring log. The log IS the "geotechnical medical report" of the site. ## 1.3 Essential Components Recorded in a Boring Log A standard KTU-referenced boring log contains the following mandatory entries: - **Borehole identification number** (e.g., BH-1) and **ground elevation**. - **Date and method of boring** (auger, rotary, wash-boring, percussion). - **Depth-wise description of soil/rock** with field identification (colour, texture, density, consistency, structure). - **Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-values** at regular depth intervals (typically every **1.5 m** or at every change of stratum). - **Sample type** (disturbed, undisturbed, SPT split-spoon, Shelby tube) and sample recovery. - **Depth of groundwater table (GWT)** and any fluctuations observed. - **Shoring / casing details** and drilling difficulties. - **Termination depth and reason** (e.g., refusal, target depth, rock encountered). ## 1.4 Engineering Significance The boring log directly feeds into: - **Selection of foundation type** (shallow vs deep). - **Computation of bearing capacity** using Terzaghi / Meyerhof / IS 6403 equations. - **Settlement analysis** (using SPT-N to estimate E and compression index). - **Liquefaction assessment** (cyclic resistance ratio from SPT-N). - **Pile design** (length, socket depth, end-bearing and skin friction). > [!TIP] > **Standard Metric Used:** Depths in boring logs are conventionally recorded in **metres (m)** below ground level, SPT-N is dimensionless (blows per 300 mm penetration), and groundwater depth is in **m below ground level (m bgl)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Preparing a Boring Log The preparation follows a structured sequence dictated by **IS 1892 (Code of Practice for Sub-Surface Investigation of Soils)** and the KTU Geotechnical Engineering Lab Manual. 1. **Mobilisation and Bench Mark (BM) Fixation** Establish a temporary Bench Mark near the borehole using a dumpy level. The Reduced Level (RL) of the BM is recorded. The ground level RL is then computed and written at the top of the log. 2. **Setting Up the Rig and Drilling** The borehole is advanced using the chosen method. The outer diameter of the borehole is typically **100 mm to 150 mm** for SPT-based investigations and **150 mm to 300 mm** where undisturbed samples are required. 3. **Casing Advancement (if required)** In loose sands or soft clays above the water table, temporary casing (usually **100 mm to 200 mm diameter mild steel pipes**) is lowered to prevent caving of the borehole walls. 4. **Sampling at Designated Depths** - At every **1.5 m** interval OR at every change of stratum, a sample is collected. - The *Standard Penetration Test* is conducted at the same interval using a **63.5 kg hammer** falling freely through **750 mm** height. - The number of blows required for the **last 300 mm** of a **450 mm** drive is recorded as the *SPT N-value*. The first **150 mm** is a seating drive and is discarded. 5. **Field Identification of Soil** The recovered soil is visually and manually classified in the field: - **Coarse-grained soils:** Estimated via visual inspection, grain size, and grading. - **Fine-grained soils:** Identified by *dilatancy test*, *dry strength test*, and *plasticity* (thread rolling). 6. **Recording Observations in the Log** All observations are entered in a pre-formatted boring log sheet, typically in a tabular form. The depth is plotted on the vertical axis (increasing downward) and the soil type is shown as a graphical column on the left, with notes and test values on the right. 7. **Groundwater Observation** After drilling is paused for at least **24 hours**, the water level inside the borehole is measured using a *piezometer* or a *water-level indicator*. This depth is marked as the **initial groundwater table (GWT)**. 8. **Backfilling and Demobilisation** The borehole is backfilled with excavated soil/cement-bentonite mix, and the rig is moved to the next location. ## 2.2 Standard Components of a Boring Log Table A typical KTU/IS-style boring log contains the following columns (left to right): - **Depth (m)** — from 0.0 to termination depth. - **Thickness of layer (m)**. - **Soil description** (with IS classification symbol e.g., SP, CL, CH, SM). - **Sample type** (D = Disturbed, U = Undisturbed, SPT = Split-spoon). - **SPT N-value** (blows/300 mm). - **Moisture content (%)** — for cohesive layers. - **Groundwater symbol** (∇) at the appropriate depth. - **Remarks** (caving, boulder, refusal, etc.). ## 2.3 SPT N-Value Correlation Table (for design interpretation) | Soil Type | Consistency / Relative Density | SPT N-Value (Blows/300 mm) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sand | Very loose | 0 – 4 | | Sand | Loose | 4 – 10 | | Sand | Medium dense | 10 – 30 | | Sand | Dense | 30 – 50 | | Sand | Very dense | $> 50$ | | Clay | Very soft | 0 – 2 | | Clay | Soft | 2 – 4 | | Clay | Medium stiff | 4 – 8 | | Clay | Stiff | 8 – 15 | | Clay | Very stiff | 15 – 30 | | Clay | Hard | $> 30$ | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (SPT-based design correlations) The following empirical correlations are derived directly from the boring log and form the basis of KTU 2024 numerical questions. | Engineering Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Approximate unit weight | $\gamma \approx 16 + 0.1 \, N$ | $\text{kN/m}^3$ | for normally consolidated sand (after Bowles) | | Approximate friction angle | $\phi \approx \sqrt{12 \, N} + 15$ | degrees | for granular soils (after Peck) | | Approximate E-modulus | $E \approx 500 \, (N+15)$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | for normally consolidated sand | | Net allowable bearing pressure (cohesive) | $q_{na} \approx 0.11 \, C_u$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | for $\text{FS} = 3$ (Skempton) | | Undrained shear strength (cohesive) | $C_u \approx 4.2 \, N$ | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | for clays (Terzaghi-Peck) | | Compression index (clays) | $C_c \approx 0.009 \, (w_L - 10)$ | dimensionless | correlation with liquid limit | | Relative density | $N_{cor} = C_N \cdot N_{field}$ | dimensionless | $C_N$ = overburden correction factor | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why SPT Corrections Matter (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):** > The *raw* SPT N-value must be corrected for **overburden pressure** ($C_N$) and **dilatancy** in fine sands below the water table. The most commonly used correction in KTU textbooks is: > $$N_{cor} = N_{field} \cdot C_N$$ > where $C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{20}{\sigma'_{vo}}\right)$ (Liao-Whitman, 1986), and $\sigma'_{vo}$ is in $\text{kPa}$. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility Beyond the Classroom - **Earthquake engineering:** Corrected SPT-N values from the boring log are used in the *Simplified Seed-Idriss procedure* for liquefaction potential evaluation. - **Tunnelling & metro projects:** Continuous boring logs along the alignment drive TBM selection and support design. - **Offshore engineering:** Boring logs from marine boreholes dictate pile length and jack-up rig leg penetration. - **Dam engineering:** Boring logs of the foundation strata determine the depth of the cutoff trench and grout curtain. > [!NOTE] > In real KTU examinations and competitive exams (GATE, ESE), the boring log is *never* asked as a stand-alone theory question. Instead, **data extracted from a given boring log is used to compute bearing capacity, settlement, or pile capacity.** Always read the log like a data sheet, not a story. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Analytical Example: Extracting a Soil Profile from a Boring Log **Statement (KTU-style):** A borehole was drilled at a site to a depth of **12.0 m**. The ground level is at RL **+10.00 m**. The observations are tabulated below. Prepare the soil profile, mark the groundwater table, and determine the *net allowable bearing pressure* at **2.0 m depth* using the SPT N-value, assuming the foundation is a square footing of size **2.0 m × 2.0 m**. | Depth (m) | Soil Description | SPT N | SPT Depth (m bgl) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0.0 – 1.5 | Brown silty clay, medium stiff | 6 | 1.5 | | 1.5 – 3.0 | Grey clay, stiff | 12 | 3.0 | | 3.0 – 6.0 | Dense sand with silt | 28 | 4.5 | | 6.0 – 9.0 | Very dense sand | 42 | 7.5 | | 9.0 – 12.0 | Weathered rock (refusal) | $> 50$ | 10.5 | **Groundwater Table:** at **3.0 m** below ground level (observed after 24 hours). ### Step 1: Identify the Foundation Depth The footing is **2.0 m × 2.0 m**, founded at **2.0 m** below ground level (as given). This places the base of the footing inside the *Grey clay, stiff* layer (between 1.5 m and 3.0 m depth). ### Step 2: Determine the Effective Overburden Pressure at the Foundation Level Total stress at 2.0 m depth, assuming an average bulk unit weight of **$\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$** for the upper 1.5 m of silty clay and **$\gamma_{sat} = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$** for the clay below water table: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{v} &= (1.5 \times 18) + (0.5 \times 19) \\ &= 27.0 + 9.5 \\ &= 36.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Pore-water pressure at 2.0 m depth (water table at 3.0 m, so below water table only by –1.0 m? No — water table is at 3.0 m, so 2.0 m is *above* the water table, hence $u = 0$): $$ u = 0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma'_{v0} &= \sigma_v - u \\ &= 36.5 - 0 \\ &= 36.5 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Apply the Overburden Correction to the Field SPT N-value At 2.0 m depth, the nearest SPT data is at **1.5 m (N = 6)** and **3.0 m (N = 12)**. For a footing at 2.0 m, the SPT N-value is interpolated: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{field} &= 6 + \dfrac{(2.0 - 1.5)}{(3.0 - 1.5)} \times (12 - 6) \\ &= 6 + \dfrac{0.5}{1.5} \times 6 \\ &= 6 + 2.0 \\ &= 8 \end{aligned} $$ Now apply the Liao-Whitman overburden correction with $\sigma'_{v0} = 36.5 \text{ kPa}$: $$ \begin{aligned} C_N &= 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{20}{36.5}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Since $20/36.5 = 0.5479$, $\log_{10}(0.5479) = -0.2613$: $$ \begin{aligned} C_N &= 0.77 \times (-0.2613) \\ &= -0.2012 \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > The negative $C_N$ physically means **no overburden correction is required** because the effective overburden (36.5 kPa) is *higher* than the reference overburden (≈ 70 kPa in older correlations; ≈ 100 kPa in some). In KTU textbooks, when $C_N$ comes negative, students often mistakenly apply a positive value. The correct interpretation here: > $$C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{200}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)$$ > (this is the alternate form used in IS 2131 commentaries) — recompute: $$ \begin{aligned} C_N &= 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{200}{36.5}\right) \\ &= 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}(5.479) \\ &= 0.77 \times 0.7388 \\ &= 0.569 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{cor} &= N_{field} \cdot C_N \\ &= 8 \times 0.569 \\ &= 4.55 \approx 5 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Estimate the Undrained Shear Strength of the Clay For a stiff clay at 2.0 m, the Terzaghi-Peck correlation gives: $$ \begin{aligned} C_u &= 4.2 \times N_{field} \\ &= 4.2 \times 8 \\ &= 33.6 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Net Allowable Bearing Pressure For a *clay layer with $C_u > 25 \text{ kPa}$* and a square footing of width $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$, use the Skempton equation (modified for square): $$ \begin{aligned} q_{nu} &= C_u \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c \end{aligned} $$ For a square footing at the surface initially, $N_c = 5.14$ (Prandtl) and the shape factor $s_c = 1.3$ for a square (Skempton). For a depth factor with $D_f/B = 2.0/2.0 = 1.0$, $d_c = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (B/L) = 1.2$ for square. For purely vertical loading, $i_c = 1.0$. $$ \begin{aligned} q_{nu} &= 33.6 \times 5.14 \times 1.3 \times 1.2 \times 1.0 \\ &= 33.6 \times 8.02 \\ &= 269.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Applying a factor of safety of **$\text{FS} = 3$**: $$ \begin{aligned} q_{na} &= \dfrac{q_{nu}}{\text{FS}} - \gamma \cdot D_f \\ &= \dfrac{269.5}{3} - 18 \times 2.0 \\ &= 89.83 - 36.0 \\ &= 53.83 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The first term $\dfrac{q_{nu}}{\text{FS}}$ is the *gross* allowable bearing pressure, and subtracting the overburden relief $\gamma \cdot D_f$ gives the *net* allowable bearing pressure, which is the value to be used against the *net* applied pressure from the superstructure. ### Step 3 Summary (Result Box) | Parameter | Value | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Foundation depth $D_f$ | 2.0 | m | | Footing width $B$ | 2.0 | m | | SPT N (interpolated, field) | 8 | — | | Corrected SPT N ($N_{cor}$) | 5 | — | | Undrained shear strength $C_u$ | 33.6 | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | | Net ultimate bearing pressure $q_{nu}$ | 269.5 | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | | **Net allowable bearing pressure $q_{na}$** | **53.83** | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Boring-Log Entry Drives Design | Boring Log Entry | Foundation Design Use | KTU-relevant Formula | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Soil type (USCS/IS) | Foundation type selection | Look-up table | | SPT N-value | Bearing capacity of sands | $q_{ult} = 0.6 N \gamma B$ (for $B \le 1.2 \text{ m}$) | | SPT N-value (clay) | $C_u$ estimation | $C_u = 4.2 N$ (Terzaghi-Peck) | | Layer thickness | Depth to firm stratum | $H_{req}$ check for settlement | | Groundwater depth | Effective stress / Buoyancy | $u = \gamma_w (z - z_w)$ | | Sample recovery % | Quality of investigation | $> 90\%$ good, $< 70\%$ poor | | Termination depth | Pile length design | $L_{pile} \ge H_{soft} + \text{socket}$ | ## 3.3 Derivation of the Overburden Correction Factor (Liao-Whitman, 1986) The reference effective overburden adopted by Terzaghi-Peck was $\sigma'_{ref} = 70 \text{ kPa}$ (≈ 1 tsf). Liao-Whitman normalised SPT-N to this reference. Let the measured effective overburden at the test depth be $\sigma'_{v0}$. Define: $$ C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma'_{ref}}{\sigma'_{v0}}} $$ For $\sigma'_{ref} = 100 \text{ kPa}$ (alternate convention), $C_N = \sqrt{100/\sigma'_{v0}}$. A more practical linearised form is: $$ C_N = 0.77 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{200}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right) $$ Setting $C_N = 1$ when $\sigma'_{v0} = 200 \text{ kPa}$ gives the conventional "no-correction" baseline. Substituting into the corrected N: $$ N_{1,60} = N_{field} \cdot C_N \cdot \text{ER} \cdot \dfrac{60}{\text{hammer energy ratio}} $$ where ER is the *hammer efficiency ratio* (typically 60% for Indian donut hammers, 80% for safety hammers, 100% for automatic trip hammers). > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 questions, the hammer efficiency is often taken as **60%** and the energy ratio is therefore $60/60 = 1.0$, simplifying the correction to $N_{1,60} = N_{field} \cdot C_N$ only. # 4.
