KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a boring log. List any four essential entries recorded in it.** **Model Answer:** A boring log is a detailed written and graphical record of the subsurface conditions encountered at a specific borehole location during a sub-surface investigation. Four essential entries are: 1. Depth-wise description of the soil/rock strata. 2. SPT N-values at regular depth intervals. 3. Depth of the groundwater table. 4. Type of sample collected (disturbed, undisturbed, or SPT split-spoon). *(Note: 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for listing four entries. Use a table format if asked for a "list with categories.")* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a soil profile? How is it prepared from a set of boring logs?** **Model Answer:** A soil profile is a vertical cross-section of the sub-surface showing the type, sequence, thickness, and lateral extent of various soil strata at a site. It is prepared by: 1. Drilling at least **3 to 4 boreholes** in a grid pattern across the site. 2. Recording the soil layers, their depths, and engineering properties in each boring log. 3. Drawing a *section line* passing through the borehole locations. 4. Correlating the layers between adjacent boreholes using stratigraphic markers (e.g., a hard clay layer or a particular SPT value). 5. Plotting the section with depth on the vertical axis and horizontal distance on the horizontal axis, and shading/different textures for each layer. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for procedure.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** The following observations were recorded in a boring log for a site investigation: - 0.0 – 1.5 m: Top soil, loose silty sand, SPT N = 4. - 1.5 – 4.5 m: Medium dense sand, SPT N = 16 at 3.0 m. - 4.5 – 7.5 m: Stiff clay, SPT N = 14 at 6.0 m. - 7.5 – 10.5 m: Dense sand, SPT N = 35 at 9.0 m. - Groundwater table at 2.0 m depth. Plot the soil profile and comment on the suitability of a shallow foundation at **1.5 m depth** for a **1.5 m × 1.5 m** square footing carrying a load of **350 kN**. Use Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation for a square footing with a factor of safety of **3**. Take $\gamma_{sand} = 17 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\phi = \sqrt{12 N} + 15$ for the medium dense sand layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the procedure to be followed in preparing a site soil profile from multiple borehole data. State the minimum number of boreholes required for a 500 m² building plot as per IS 1892. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A (a) **Step 1: Compute the angle of shearing resistance at the foundation level** At 1.5 m depth, the soil is *medium dense sand*. SPT N = 16: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi &= \sqrt{12 \times N} + 15 \\ &= \sqrt{12 \times 16} + 15 \\ &= \sqrt{192} + 15 \\ &= 13.86 + 15 \\ &= 28.86^{\circ} \approx 29^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Use Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 29^{\circ}$** $$ \begin{aligned} N_q &= e^{\pi \tan \phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \\ N_c &= (N_q - 1) \cdot \cot \phi \\ N_{\gamma} &= (N_q - 1) \cdot \tan(1.4 \phi) \end{aligned} $$ For $\phi = 29^{\circ}$, standard tables give: $$ N_c = 27.86, \quad N_q = 16.44, \quad N_{\gamma} = 13.10 $$ **Step 3: Terzaghi's equation for a square footing** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{ult} &= 1.3 \cdot c \cdot N_c + \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q + 0.4 \cdot \gamma \cdot B \cdot N_{\gamma} \end{aligned} $$ For sand, $c = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} q_{ult} &= 0 + 17 \times 1.5 \times 16.44 + 0.4 \times 17 \times 1.5 \times 13.10 \\ &= 419.22 + 133.62 \\ &= 552.84 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Net ultimate bearing pressure** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{nu} &= q_{ult} - \gamma D_f \\ &= 552.84 - 17 \times 1.5 \\ &= 527.34 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Net allowable bearing pressure (FS = 3)** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{na} &= \dfrac{q_{nu}}{3} = \dfrac{527.34}{3} = 175.78 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Compare with applied pressure** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{applied} &= \dfrac{350}{1.5 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{350}{2.25} = 155.56 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $q_{applied} \; (155.56) \; < \; q_{na} \; (175.78)$. The shallow foundation is **safe in bearing** at 1.5 m depth. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > Students often use $N = 4$ (from the top 0 – 1.5 m layer) instead of $N = 16$ (from the *medium dense sand* layer at 1.5 m). The boring log clearly shows the foundation base at **1.5 m** lies **in the medium dense sand**, so the correct SPT-N is **16**, not 4. Choosing the wrong N-value is the **#1 reason for full-mark loss** in this problem. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A (b) **Procedure for preparing a soil profile:** 1. Plan a grid of boreholes as per IS 1892 spacing. 2. Drill to the desired depth at each location. 3. Record depth, soil type, SPT-N, and GWT in each boring log. 4. Draw a section line through the boreholes. 5. Correlate similar strata between adjacent boreholes (using lithology, SPT-N, and lab data). 6. Plot the cross-section to scale (e.g., 1 cm = 1 m vertical, 1 cm = 5 m horizontal). 7. Mark the GWT as a continuous line. 8. Annotate each layer with its IS classification symbol, SPT-N, and other properties. **Minimum boreholes (IS 1892):** For a 500 m² plot, IS 1892 recommends **1 borehole per 250 m² to 500 m²**, with a **minimum of 3 boreholes** for any building. Hence, for this 500 m² plot, **at least 3 boreholes** are required. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** During a soil investigation, a borehole was drilled and the following strata were encountered: - 0.0 – 2.0 m: Loose sand, SPT N = 6. - 2.0 – 5.0 m: Medium stiff clay, SPT N = 8, liquid limit = 45%. - 5.0 – 8.0 m: Dense sand, SPT N = 32. - Groundwater table at 3.0 m. A square footing of size **2.0 m × 2.0 m** is to be placed at **2.0 m depth** in the clay layer. Calculate the **net ultimate bearing capacity** using Skempton's equation and the **net allowable bearing pressure** with FS = 3. The footing is subjected to a column load of **500 kN**. Check its adequacy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the limitations of a boring log? Discuss the various field tests whose results are recorded in a boring log. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B (a) **Step 1: Identify the soil at foundation level** At 2.0 m depth, the soil is *medium stiff clay*. SPT N = 8. **Step 2: Estimate undrained shear strength** Using Terzaghi-Peck correlation: $$ \begin{aligned} C_u &= 4.2 \times N \\ &= 4.2 \times 8 \\ &= 33.6 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Skempton's bearing capacity equation for clay** For a *square* footing with $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$: $$ N_c = 6.0 \cdot \left(1 + 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L}\right) = 6.0 \cdot (1 + 0.2) = 6.0 \times 1.2 = 7.2 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} q_{nu} &= C_u \cdot N_c \\ &= 33.6 \times 7.2 \\ &= 241.92 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Net allowable bearing pressure** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{na} &= \dfrac{241.92}{3} = 80.64 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Applied pressure check** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{applied} &= \dfrac{500}{2.0 \times 2.0} = \dfrac{500}{4.0} = 125 \text{ kN/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $q_{applied} = 125 \text{ kN/m}^2 \; > \; q_{na} = 80.64 \text{ kN/m}^2$. The foundation is **NOT adequate**; either increase the footing size, reduce the load, or use a raft/pile foundation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > A common mistake is to use the *gross* ultimate bearing capacity directly (i.e., $q_{ult} = C_u N_c$ without subtracting overburden) and then apply the safety factor. The KTU textbook notation explicitly requires **net** ultimate bearing capacity in the formula $q_{na} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{\text{FS}}$, where $q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$. For undrained clay with $\phi = 0$, however, the *net* and *gross* values differ only by the overburden term; in Skempton's form, $q_{nu} = C_u N_c$ already gives a *net* value if the surcharge is properly accounted for. Read the textbook convention carefully. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B (b) **Limitations of a Boring Log:** 1. Provides information **only at a point** (the borehole location); soil conditions between boreholes are *interpolated*, not directly observed. 2. Disturbed sampling destroys the *in-situ* fabric of sensitive clays. 3. Cannot detect *boulders, cavities, or soft pockets* between sampling intervals. 4. SPT-N is highly operator-dependent and rig-dependent (use of donut vs safety hammer, rope vs automatic trip). 5. Disturbed soil samples cannot give reliable *shear strength* or *consolidation* parameters. 6. Drilling mud or water used during boring may alter the natural moisture content of the surrounding soil. **Field tests recorded in a boring log:** 1. **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** — for granular and stiff cohesive soils. 2. **Dynamic Cone Penetration Test (DCPT)** — for shallow granular strata. 3. **Static Cone Penetration Test (SCPT / CPT)** — for continuous stratigraphic profiling. 4. **Vane Shear Test (VST)** — for in-situ undrained shear strength of soft clays. 5. **Pressuremeter Test (PMT)** — for lateral stress and deformability of soil. 6. **Water level observation** — for groundwater table depth and permeability estimation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - A **boring log** is the *primary deliverable* of a sub-surface investigation; it records **depth, soil type, SPT N, sample type, and GWT**. - The **standard SPT hammer** weighs **63.5 kg**, free-fall **750 mm**, and N is blows for the **last 300 mm** of a 450 mm drive. - **SPT N is interpolated** linearly between adjacent test depths if a design parameter is needed at an intermediate depth. - Always apply the **overburden correction** $C_N$ to the field N-value before using it in any bearing capacity formula in KTU questions. - The **Terzaghi-Peck correlation** $C_u = 4.2 N$ is the most-tested formula for cohesive soils in KTU exams. - For a **square footing**, the shape factor $s_c = 1.3$ in Terzaghi and $N_c = 7.2$ in Skempton. - The **net allowable bearing pressure** is **gross allowable minus $\gamma D_f$** — this distinction is *frequently* tested. - The **minimum number of boreholes** for a small building (up to 500 m²) is **3** as per IS 1892. - The **groundwater table** affects both effective stress and bearing capacity; always subtract the buoyancy effect for foundations below the GWT. - A **soil profile** is derived from **multiple boring logs** correlated by stratigraphic markers; never assume uniformity between boreholes. - For the KTU 2024 scheme, *internal choice* in Part B usually pairs a *computational* question (using boring log data) with a *theoretical* question (about preparation/interpretation). Practice both formats.

topics.title: Plate Load Test - Procedure, Uses and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plate Load Test - Procedure, Uses and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Load Test (PLT):** A *semi-direct*, in-situ field loading test conducted at the proposed foundation level to evaluate the **ultimate bearing capacity**, **safe bearing pressure**, **settlement characteristics**, and the **modulus of subgrade reaction (k)** of the underlying soil by applying a known, incremental load through a rigid steel plate of standard size and measuring the corresponding settlement response.

The test essentially simulates, at a reduced scale, the pressure–settlement behaviour that a prototype shallow foundation would experience under service loads. It is one of the **most reliable field tests** for cohesionless soils (sands and gravels), where undisturbed sampling is extremely difficult.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Plate Load Test falls under *Module 2 – Bearing Capacity and Shallow Foundations*. Students must clearly distinguish its use for **granular (cohesionless) soils** (where it is preferred) versus **cohesive (clay) soils** (where its reliability is questionable due to the "size effect").

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a beach and want to know how firm the sand is before building a sandcastle. You could:

1. **Poke the sand with your finger** → small area, small depth → small sink.
2. **Press down with the flat of your palm** → larger area, larger depth → bigger sink.
3. **Stand on the sand with both feet** → largest area, deepest influence → biggest sink.

The **Plate Load Test is the engineering version of "pressing your palm" on the soil**. By using a known plate (usually **300 mm × 300 mm** or **600 mm × 600 mm** square) and measuring exactly how much it sinks under each load increment, engineers can:

- **Extrapolate** the behaviour to the *actual* foundation size.
- **Avoid** the need to send huge soil samples to the lab.
- **Verify** whether the assumed bearing capacity from empirical/theoretical methods is realistic at the site.

## 1.3 Standard Plate Sizes (IS 1888:1982 Reaffirmed 2020)

| Standard Plate Shape | Common Dimensions |
|---|---|
| Square Plate | **300 mm × 300 mm** (most common) |
| Square Plate | **450 mm × 450 mm** |
| Square Plate | **600 mm × 600 mm** |
| Circular Plate | **300 mm, 450 mm, 600 mm** diameter |

> [!NOTE]
> The plate is usually made of **mild steel** with a thickness of **25 mm to 40 mm** to ensure rigidity. The plate must be **truly rigid** so that the load is transmitted uniformly.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard plate area (A):** typically **0.09 m²** (for 300 mm × 300 mm).
- **Load increment:** usually **one-fifth** of the estimated safe bearing capacity or **1/10 to 1/15** of ultimate load.
- **Hydraulic jack capacity:** **500 kN to 3000 kN** is common.
- **Loading rate:** Each load increment is held until **settlement stabilises** (rate < 0.02 mm/min, as per IS 1888).
- **Reaction source:** Either **kentledge** (dead weights, e.g., loaded platform, sand bags) or **anchor piles** with reaction truss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis — Why the Plate Load Test Works

The test works on the principle of **stress bulb propagation**. When a load is applied on a small plate:

- A **bulb of pressure** develops beneath the plate.
- This bulb has a depth of influence roughly equal to **1.5 B to 2.0 B**, where **B** is the plate width.
- Soil within this bulb contributes to the observed settlement.
- The **pressure–settlement curve** recorded in the field is a *true* representation of the soil's response, *not* an inferred value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** The fundamental purpose of a Plate Load Test is to *replace uncertain empirical correlations* with a *direct, site-specific measurement* of the load–deformation response of the soil at the exact depth where the foundation will rest.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Excavation of Test Pit:** A pit is excavated up to the proposed **foundation depth**. The pit width should be at least **5 times the plate width** to avoid the pit walls influencing the failure zone.
2. **Plate Placement:** The rigid steel plate is placed on a thin layer of **fine sand or plaster of Paris** (leveling layer, max 5 mm) at the centre of the pit to ensure uniform contact.
3. **Loading Column Assembly:** A **hydraulic jack** is centred over the plate. A reaction load is provided by **kentledge** (e.g., a loaded truck, sand bags, concrete blocks) or by **anchor piles** connected through a reaction truss.
4. **Dial Gauge Setup:** **Two or three dial gauges** (least count **0.01 mm**) are mounted on independent supports to measure the **average settlement** of the plate. Gauges are placed diametrically opposite for uniform reading.
5. **Incremental Loading:** Load is applied in **equal increments** (e.g., one-fifth of the estimated safe load). Each increment is maintained until the rate of settlement falls below **0.02 mm per minute** or for a minimum of **1 hour**, whichever is later.
6. **Settlement Reading:** Settlement is recorded at **0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 15, 30, 60, 120 minutes** and at the next loading.
7. **Plotting Load-Settlement Curve:** A graph of **load (P) vs. settlement (S)** is plotted. From this, the **bearing capacity** and **subgrade modulus** are derived.
8. **Unloading Phase:** The test is usually followed by **unloading in decrements** to study **rebound behaviour** (useful for identifying elastic vs. plastic settlement components).

## 2.3 Interpretation of the Load–Settlement Curve

The shape of the load–settlement curve determines the interpretation method:

### Type 1: Clear Failure Point (Dense Sand / Stiff Clay)
- The curve rises steeply, then **bends sharply and plunges** vertically at a clear failure load.
- **Ultimate load (P_u)** is read directly from the curve.
- The curve is essentially **bimodal**: linear portion + failure portion.

### Type 2: No Clear Failure (Loose Sand / Soft Clay)
- The curve **gradually flattens** without a sharp break.
- Failure is **defined by a convention**:
  - **0.1 B criterion:** Failure load is the load causing settlement = **10% of plate width** (Terzaghi).
  - **20% rule:** Ultimate load is the load at which settlement = **20% of plate width** (some codes).
  - **Double-tangent method:** Two tangents are drawn (one to the initial straight portion, one to the final curved portion); their intersection gives **P_u**.
  - **log P – log S method:** Plot on log-log graph; the point where the curve deviates from linearity gives the failure load.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** When the load-settlement curve has no clear failure point, *do not* state "no failure" — always mention the **convention used** (Terzaghi's 0.1B, double-tangent, or log-log method) and explain that the ultimate load is **interpreted**, not directly observed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure under plate | $q = \dfrac{P}{A_p}$ | kN/m² (kPa) |
| 2 | Settlement of plate | $S_p$ | mm (measured) |
| 3 | Modulus of subgrade reaction | $k_s = \dfrac{q}{S_p}$ | kN/m³ |
| 4 | Settlement of *actual* foundation in **sand** (Terzaghi & Peck) | $S_f = S_p \left( \dfrac{2 B_p}{B_f + B_p} \right)^2$ | mm; $B_p$ = plate width, $B_f$ = foundation width |
| 5 | Settlement of *actual* foundation in **clay** (consistency check) | $S_f = S_p \cdot \dfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ | mm (sized proportionally) |
| 6 | Ultimate bearing capacity from test | $q_u = \dfrac{P_u}{A_p}$ | kN/m² |
| 7 | Safe bearing pressure (with FS) | $q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_u}{F.S.}$ | F.S. = 2 to 3 typical |
| 8 | Settlement-based safe bearing pressure | $q_{safe} = \dfrac{S_{allow} \cdot k_s}{F.S.}$ | kN/m² |
| 9 | Failure criterion (0.1B rule) | $S_{fail} = 0.1 \, B_p$ | mm |
| 10 | Pressure bulb depth | $z_{inf} \approx 1.5 B_p \text{ to } 2.0 B_p$ | Below plate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** The settlement in **sand** is governed by the **square** of the ratio $\tfrac{2B_p}{B_f + B_p}$, while the settlement in **clay** scales **linearly** with $\tfrac{B_f}{B_p}$. Always identify the soil type *first* before picking the correct extrapolation formula.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why PLT is Used |
|---|---|
| **Raft / Mat Foundations** on granular soils | Direct measurement of $k_s$ for design of the raft |
| **Pre-construction site verification** | Confirms assumed bearing capacity from SPT/CPT |
| **Large industrial buildings** | Provides confidence for non-standard soil profiles |
| **Highway / runway subgrade** | $k_s$ value needed for rigid pavement design |
| **Bearing capacity of weathered rock** | Useful when core recovery is poor |
| **Back-analysis of failed foundations** | Replicates failure conditions for forensic engineering |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Under the Plate (Trivial but Critical)

The **average pressure transmitted to the soil** by the plate is:

$$
q = \frac{P}{A_p}
$$

where $P$ is the total applied load and $A_p$ is the **plan area of the plate**.

For a **square plate** of side $B_p$:

$$
A_p = B_p^2
$$

$$
q = \frac{P}{B_p^2}
$$

For a **circular plate** of diameter $D_p$:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4}
$$

$$
q = \frac{4P}{\pi D_p^2}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Load in kN is divided by area in m² to get kN/m² (kPa). The plate area is a *pure geometry* term — there is no soil property here.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulus of Subgrade Reaction ($k_s$)

By definition, the modulus of subgrade reaction is the **pressure required to produce a unit settlement**:

$$
k_s = \frac{q}{S_p}
$$

The corresponding SI unit is:

$$
[k_s] = \frac{\text{kN/m}^2}{\text{m}} = \frac{\text{kN}}{\text{m}^3}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a 300 mm × 300 mm plate settles **2.5 mm** under a load of **30 kN**.

Step 1: Plate area.

$$
A_p = (0.300)^2 = 0.09 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 2: Pressure.

$$
q = \frac{30}{0.09} = 333.33 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Step 3: Convert settlement to metres.

$$
S_p = 2.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0025 \text{ m}
$$

Step 4: Modulus.

$$
k_s = \frac{333.33}{0.0025} = 1.333 \times 10^5 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because $k_s$ is highly sensitive to the unit of settlement, always express settlement in **metres** (not mm) when computing $k_s$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Settlement Extrapolation for Sand (Terzaghi & Peck)

The classical formula relates the **settlement of the actual foundation** ($S_f$) to the **settlement observed in the plate load test** ($S_p$):

$$
S_f = S_p \cdot \left( \frac{2 B_p}{B_f + B_p} \right)^2
$$

**Derivation Rationale (Why the squared term?):**

- The pressure bulb depth is proportional to the foundation width.
- In sand, the modulus $E_s$ *increases* with depth (due to overburden).
- The combined effect of **bulb depth scaling** and **stiffness scaling** yields a **quadratic** correction.
- For $B_f = B_p$ (no extrapolation), $S_f = S_p$ ✓
- For $B_f \gg B_p$ (large foundation), the ratio $\tfrac{2B_p}{B_f + B_p} \to 0$, so $S_f \to 0$. This is **physically incorrect** for sand in reality, but matches the conservative Terzaghi-Peck empirical trend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation Note:** The Terzaghi-Peck formula is *empirical*, valid for *medium-dense to dense sands* and for $B_f \leq 4 B_p$. It **over-estimates** settlement reduction for very large foundations.

**Worked Example:**

Given: $B_p = 0.30$ m, $S_p = 5$ mm, $B_f = 1.5$ m.

Step 1: Compute the ratio term.

$$
\frac{2 B_p}{B_f + B_p} = \frac{2 \times 0.30}{1.5 + 0.30} = \frac{0.60}{1.80} = 0.3333
$$

Step 2: Square the term.

$$
(0.3333)^2 = 0.1111
$$

Step 3: Compute $S_f$.

$$
S_f = 5 \times 0.1111 = 0.5556 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The 0.30 m plate settlement is 5 mm; the 1.5 m foundation is predicted to settle only ~0.56 mm under the same average pressure, because the larger foundation mobilises a much deeper and stiffer soil zone.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Settlement Extrapolation for Clay

In clay, the settlement is governed primarily by **consolidation** (volume change), not shear failure. Since the *stress increment* beneath both plate and foundation is similar in shape but the *consolidation thickness* scales with $B$:

$$
S_f = S_p \cdot \frac{B_f}{B_p}
$$

This is a *linear* scaling because the relevant soil layer (compressible clay) is essentially constant thickness across the loaded area, and strain integrates over the depth.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Safe Bearing Pressure from Settlement Criterion

If the structure can tolerate a maximum settlement $S_{allow}$, then the allowable pressure on the foundation is:

$$
q_{allow} = \frac{S_{allow} \cdot k_s}{F.S.}
$$

For a **raft foundation** of width $B_f$ on the same soil (after applying Terzaghi-Peck correction for $k_s$ of a wider plate, $k_{s,f} = k_{s,p} \cdot \tfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ for clays, etc.), the final design pressure is cross-checked against both:

- **Shear failure criterion:** $q_{allow} \leq \dfrac{q_u}{F.S.}$ (with $F.S. = 3$)
- **Settlement criterion:** $q_{allow} \leq \dfrac{S_{allow} \cdot k_{s,f}}{F.S.}$ (with $F.S. = 2$)

The **smaller of the two** governs the design.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Soil Type | Settlement Formula | Failure Criterion | Reliability of PLT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dense sand / Gravel** | $S_f = S_p \left(\tfrac{2B_p}{B_f+B_p}\right)^2$ | 0.1 B / Double tangent | **High** ✓ |
| **Loose sand** | Same as above (with caution) | Double tangent / log-log | Moderate |
| **Stiff clay** | $S_f = S_p \cdot \tfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ | 20% rule / direct | Moderate |
| **Soft clay** | Same as stiff clay | Often not reached in test | **Low** ✗ |
| **Weathered rock** | Empirical correlations | Plate behaves as footing | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plate Load Test — Functional Architecture & Field Setup

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **field setup, instrumentation, and procedural flow** of the Plate Load Test. It captures all physical components and their interactions (which is the most useful schematic representation for a field test of this nature).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Site Investigation Complete] --> A1[Excavate Test Pit to Foundation Depth]
    A1 --> A2[Excavation Width at least 5 times Plate Width]
    A2 --> B1[Place Leveling Layer of Fine Sand]
    B1 --> B2[Position Rigid Steel Plate on Leveled Bed]
    B2 --> C1[Mount Hydraulic Jack Centrally above Plate]
    C1 --> C2[Connect Jack to Reaction Source]
    C2 --> R1[Kentledge Method]
    C2 --> R2[Anchor Pile Method]
    R1 --> D1[Apply Load in Equal Increments]
    R2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Hold Each Load until Settlement Rate below 0.02 mm per minute]
    D2 --> E1[Record Settlement from Dial Gauges at Scheduled Intervals]
    E1 --> E2[Increment Loaded until Failure or Planned Maximum]
    E2 --> F1[Plot Load versus Settlement Curve]
    F1 --> F2[Identify Ultimate Load using 0.1B or Double Tangent Method]
    F2 --> G1[Compute Modulus of Subgrade Reaction ks]
    G1 --> G2[Extrapolate Settlement to Actual Foundation using Sand or Clay Formula]
    G2 --> H1[Report Safe Bearing Pressure and Allowable Settlement]
    H1 --> H2[Unload in Decrements and Record Rebound]
    H2 --> Z[Test Complete and Pit Backfilled]

    style A0 fill:#2E86AB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#1B4965
    style Z fill:#5CB85C,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#3D8B3D
    style F2 fill:#F0AD4E,color:#000000,stroke:#D58512
    style D2 fill:#FF9966,color:#000000,stroke:#CC6600
```

## 4.2 Plate Load Test — Instrumentation & Load Path Block Diagram

This diagram focuses on the **mechanical load path** and **measurement chain** of the test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Reaction Source\nKentledge or Anchor Pile] --> col[Reaction Column or Truss]
    col --> jack[Hydraulic Jack\n500 to 3000 kN]
    jack --> sp1[Spherical Platen]
    sp1 --> plate[Rigid Steel Plate\n300 mm x 300 mm]
    plate --> sand[Leveling Sand Layer]
    sand --> soil[Soil Mass in Pit]
    soil -.-> settlement[Vertical Settlement]
    settlement --> dg1[Dial Gauge 1]
    settlement --> dg2[Dial Gauge 2]
    settlement --> dg3[Dial Gauge 3]
    dg1 --> ref[Independent Reference Beam]
    dg2 --> ref
    dg3 --> ref
    ref --> support[Supports outside Stress Bulb]
    dg1 --> logger[Manual / Digital Logger]
    dg2 --> logger
    dg3 --> logger

    style src1 fill:#A23B72,color:#FFFFFF
    style jack fill:#F18F01,color:#000000
    style plate fill:#C73E1D,color:#FFFFFF
    style soil fill:#6A994E,color:#FFFFFF
    style logger fill:#386641,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 Interpretation of Load–Settlement Curve — Decision Flow

This block diagram shows the **decision logic** for interpreting the load-settlement curve, a common KTU exam question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Load Settlement Curve Plotted] --> Q1{Sharp Failure Point Visible?}
    Q1 -- YES --> P2[Read Ultimate Load Directly from Curve]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Use Convention for Failure Load}
    Q2 --> P3[Terzaghi 0.1B Rule]
    Q2 --> P4[Double Tangent Method]
    Q2 --> P5[Log P versus Log S Method]
    P2 --> R1[Compute qu = Pu divided by Ap]
    P3 --> R1
    P4 --> R1
    P5 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Compute Safe Bearing Pressure\nqsafe = qu divided by F.S.]
    R1 --> R3[Compute Subgrade Modulus\nks = q divided by Sp]
    R2 --> S1[Cross Check with Settlement Criterion]
    R3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Extrapolate to Actual Foundation Width]
    S2 --> S3{Final Design Pressure\nSmaller of Shear and Settlement Criteria}

    style P1 fill:#3D5A80,color:#FFFFFF
    style Q1 fill:#EE6C4D,color:#FFFFFF
    style S3 fill:#293241,color:#FFFFFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Part A questions are typically **2 marks** or **3 marks** and require concise, definition-oriented or justification-oriented answers. Below are two 3-mark questions.

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Q: List the various field situations in which the Plate Load Test is preferred over other in-situ tests. Briefly justify.**

**Model Answer:**

The Plate Load Test (PLT) is preferred in the following field situations:

1. **Granular / cohesionless soils (sands and gravels):** Undisturbed sampling is practically impossible for such soils, so the lab triaxial or unconfined compression tests cannot be performed reliably. The PLT directly measures the bearing and settlement behaviour of the in-situ soil mass. **(1 mark)**

2. **Site-specific design of large raft or mat foundations on non-uniform soil:** Empirical correlations from SPT/CPT become unreliable for layered or heterogeneous deposits. The PLT gives a direct, location-specific pressure–settlement response, increasing design confidence. **(1 mark)**

3. **Verification of assumed bearing capacity from preliminary geotechnical investigation:** Before finalising the design, the PLT is often used to validate the bearing capacity and settlement predicted by SPT/CPT correlations, especially for important or sensitive structures. **(1 mark)**

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per correct situation with brief justification.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 (Model)]

**Q: Explain why the Plate Load Test is generally not reliable for soft clays. Mention any two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

The Plate Load Test (PLT) is **not reliable for soft clays** because:

1. **Size effect / Scale effect:** Soft clays are highly compressible, and the consolidation settlement depends on the *thickness of the compressible layer* relative to the loaded area. Since the test plate is small, the depth of influence is limited, and consolidation in deeper layers is not captured. The extrapolation to the actual foundation size using $S_f = S_p \cdot \tfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ is empirical and can grossly under- or over-estimate settlement. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Time effect / Consolidation delay:** The plate load test is typically a **short-duration** test (a few hours), whereas soft clays exhibit **time-dependent consolidation settlement** that develops over months or years. The PLT captures only the **immediate (undrained) settlement**, missing the long-term consolidation settlement that often dominates in soft clays. **(1.5 marks)**

> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for the size effect explanation, 1.5 marks for the time effect explanation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Convention: Part B Module questions carry **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students are given an **internal choice** — i.e., they can attempt either OR-style Option A or Option B for the same question. We provide two **completely independent** 14-mark options.

---

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]

**Q: Describe in detail the procedure for conducting a Plate Load Test as per IS 1888. With the help of neat sketches, explain the determination of ultimate bearing capacity and modulus of subgrade reaction from the test results. Use a suitable soil type and justify the extrapolation formula used to predict settlement of the actual foundation.**

#### Part (a) — Procedure for Plate Load Test (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Site Preparation (1 mark):**
A test pit is excavated to the **proposed depth of the foundation**. The minimum plan dimensions of the pit should be **at least five times** the width of the test plate to eliminate boundary effects of the pit walls. The bottom of the pit is carefully levelled and cleared of loose material.

**Step 2 — Plate and Leveling Layer (1 mark):**
A thin layer of **fine, dry sand** (or plaster of Paris, ≤ 5 mm thick) is spread at the centre of the pit to provide a uniform, level contact surface. The **rigid steel plate** (typically 300 mm × 300 mm) is gently placed on this layer. A **spherical platen or ball-and-socket joint** is placed on top of the plate to ensure the hydraulic load is transmitted concentrically (eliminating eccentric loading).

**Step 3 — Loading Assembly (1.5 marks):**
A **hydraulic jack** is positioned on top of the plate, connected through the reaction frame. The reaction is provided by either:
- **Kentledge method:** Dead weights (concrete blocks, sand bags, loaded truck) placed on a platform above the pit.
- **Anchor pile method:** Two or more anchor piles driven outside the pit, connected by a reaction truss to the jack.

**Step 4 — Instrumentation (1 mark):**
**Two or three dial gauges** (least count **0.01 mm**, travel ≥ 25 mm) are mounted on **independent reference beams** supported on firm ground *outside* the pressure bulb. Gauges are placed **diametrically opposite** on the plate to capture any tilting.

**Step 5 — Loading (1.5 marks):**
Load is applied in **equal increments** of about **one-fifth of the estimated safe load** (or as per IS 1888, in convenient increments). Each load increment is held constant until the rate of settlement drops below **0.02 mm per minute** or for a minimum holding time of **1 hour**, whichever is later. Settlements are recorded at **0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 15, 30, 60, 120 minutes** after each load application. The test continues until **failure** (sharp break in curve) or until the settlement reaches a defined limit (0.1 B plate).

**Step 6 — Unloading (1 mark):**
After reaching the maximum test load, the load is released in **decrements** (typically the same increments as loading), and rebound readings are taken. This provides the **elastic (recoverable) settlement component** and helps in identifying plastic deformation.

---

#### Part (b) — Determination of Ultimate Bearing Capacity and Subgrade Modulus (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Plotting the Load-Settlement Curve (1.5 marks):**
A graph of **load (P in kN) on the y-axis** vs. **settlement (S in mm) on the x-axis** is plotted for each incremental load. Both the loading and unloading (rebound) curves are shown for clarity. A typical curve shows an initial **linear (elastic) portion** followed by a **curved (plastic) portion**, ending in a **steeply rising (failure) portion** for dense/stiff soils.

**Step 2 — Ultimate Bearing Capacity (2.5 marks):**
For **soils showing a clear failure point** (e.g., dense sand, stiff clay), the ultimate load $P_u$ is read **directly** as the load at which the curve becomes nearly vertical. The ultimate bearing capacity is:

$$
q_u = \frac{P_u}{A_p}
$$

For **soils with no clear failure** (e.g., loose sand, soft clay), $P_u$ is **interpreted** using the **Terzaghi 0.1 B criterion** — the load corresponding to a settlement of **10% of the plate width** ($S = 0.1 B_p$). The **double-tangent method** is also widely used: two tangents are drawn — one along the initial linear portion, one along the final curved portion — and their intersection gives $P_u$.

**Step 3 — Safe Bearing Capacity (1 mark):**

$$
q_{safe} = \frac{q_u}{F.S.}
$$

where $F.S. = 2$ to $3$ is the factor of safety.

**Step 4 — Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (1.5 marks):**
From the initial **linear (elastic) portion** of the load-settlement curve, the slope is determined:

$$
k_s = \frac{q}{S_p} = \frac{P / A_p}{S_p}
$$

The units are **kN/m³**. The value of $k_s$ is used directly in the **Winkler model** for raft foundation design, where the soil is modelled as a series of independent linear springs of stiffness $k_s$.

**Step 5 — Settlement Extrapolation to Actual Foundation (0.5 marks):**

For a **sandy soil**, the Terzaghi & Peck formula is used:

$$
S_f = S_p \cdot \left( \frac{2 B_p}{B_f + B_p} \right)^2
$$

This is justified because in sand, both the depth of stress influence and the soil modulus scale with the foundation width, leading to a **quadratic** reduction of settlement with increasing foundation size.

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 (Adapted)]

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the various uses and limitations of the Plate Load Test. How does the test result help in the design of a raft foundation on a sandy soil? (7 marks)**

**(b) A Plate Load Test was conducted using a 30 cm × 30 cm square plate on a sandy soil. The failure load was observed to be 90 kN, and the corresponding settlement was 7.5 mm. Compute:**
- **(i) The ultimate bearing capacity of the soil.** (2 marks)
- **(ii) The safe bearing capacity assuming a factor of safety of 2.5.** (2 marks)
- **(iii) The modulus of subgrade reaction.** (2 marks)
- **(iv) The expected settlement of a 1.5 m wide square footing resting on the same soil under the same average pressure.** (1 mark)

#### Part (a) — Uses and Limitations of the Plate Load Test (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Uses of the Plate Load Test (3.5 marks):**

1. **Determination of ultimate and safe bearing capacity** of the soil at the foundation level — a direct, field-measured value. **(1 mark)**
2. **Determination of settlement** of the actual foundation using extrapolation formulas (Terzaghi & Peck for sand, linear scaling for clay). **(1 mark)**
3. **Determination of modulus of subgrade reaction ($k_s$)** for use in **Winkler model** of soil–structure interaction (especially for **raft / mat foundations**). **(1 mark)**
4. **Validation of empirical correlations** from SPT, CPT, and other in-situ tests, particularly important for **granular soils** where undisturbed sampling is impossible. **(0.5 mark)**

**Limitations of the Plate Load Test (3.5 marks):**

1. **Size effect:** The plate is much smaller than the actual foundation. The depth of stress influence scales with plate size, so a small plate cannot capture the response of deeper strata that may dominate for a large foundation. **(1 mark)**
2. **Depth effect:** The test is conducted at the proposed foundation depth, but it cannot replicate **deep seated failures** or **stratified profiles** that influence the actual foundation. **(1 mark)**
3. **Time effect:** The test is essentially **short-term (a few hours)**, so it captures **immediate (undrained) settlement** but not the **long-term consolidation settlement** in clays. This makes the test unreliable for soft clays. **(1 mark)**
4. **Capillary tension effects:** In dry or partially saturated sands, capillary stresses artificially increase the measured bearing capacity, giving an **over-optimistic** result. **(0.5 mark)**

**Raft Foundation on Sandy Soil — How PLT Helps (0 marks; part (b) covers numerical):**
The PLT directly yields the **subgrade modulus $k_s$**, which is the Winkler spring constant. For a raft on sand, the design pressure is the **smaller of**:

- Shear criterion: $q_{safe} = q_u / F.S.$
- Settlement criterion: $q_{allow} = S_{allow} \cdot k_{s,\text{eff}} / F.S.$

where $k_{s,\text{eff}}$ is obtained by extrapolating the plate $k_s$ to the raft width.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Plate: square, $B_p = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- Failure load: $P_u = 90 \text{ kN}$
- Settlement at failure: $S_p = 7.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0075 \text{ m}$
- Factor of safety: $F.S. = 2.5$
- Foundation: square, $B_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Soil: **sand** → use Terzaghi & Peck formula.

---

**(i) Ultimate Bearing Capacity (2 marks):**

Step 1: Plate area.

$$
A_p = B_p^2 = (0.30)^2 = 0.09 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 2: Ultimate bearing capacity.

$$
q_u = \frac{P_u}{A_p} = \frac{90}{0.09} = 1000 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

---

**(ii) Safe Bearing Capacity (2 marks):**

$$
q_{safe} = \frac{q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{1000}{2.5} = 400 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

---

**(iii) Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (2 marks):**

The pressure corresponding to the failure load:

$$
q_u = \frac{1000 \text{ kN/m}^2}{1} = 1000 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
k_s = \frac{q_u}{S_p} = \frac{1000}{0.0075} = 1.333 \times 10^5 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

---

**(iv) Expected Settlement of 1.5 m Footing (1 mark):**

Using the **Terzaghi & Peck** formula for sand:

$$
S_f = S_p \cdot \left( \frac{2 B_p}{B_f + B_p} \right)^2
$$

Step 1: Ratio term.

$$
\frac{2 B_p}{B_f + B_p} = \frac{2 \times 0.30}{1.5 + 0.30} = \frac{0.60}{1.80} = 0.3333
$$

Step 2: Square.

$$
(0.3333)^2 = 0.1111
$$

Step 3: Settlement.

$$
S_f = 7.5 \times 0.1111 = 0.833 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $S_f \approx 0.83$ mm

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Plate Load Test Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units of $k_s$** — Examiners will deduct 0.5 marks if the unit of $k_s$ is missing or written as kN/m² (it must be kN/m³). This is the **#1 KTU error** in this module.
>
> 2. **Applying the wrong extrapolation formula** — Using the sand formula ($S_f = S_p \cdot (\tfrac{2B_p}{B_f+B_p})^2$) for clay (where $S_f = S_p \cdot \tfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ is correct) **loses 2 to 3 marks** instantly. Always **state the soil type** *before* writing the formula.
>
> 3. **Confusing diameter with side for circular vs. square plates** — For a circular plate of diameter $D_p$, area is $\tfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4}$, **not** $D_p^2$.
>
> 4. **Not specifying the failure criterion** — When the curve has no clear failure point, you **must** state the convention (0.1 B / double tangent / log-log) and **justify** it.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the level of water table** — If water table is within the pressure bulb (1.5 B to 2.0 B), the effective stress reduces and the bearing capacity drops. Examiners expect a mention of this effect for full marks in design questions.
>
> 6. **Mixing up settlement criterion and shear criterion** — Always compute **both** safe bearing pressures (shear: $q_u/F.S.$; settlement: $S_{allow} \cdot k_s / F.S.$) and use the **smaller** of the two. Skipping this cross-check loses 1 to 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Plate Load Test**

- **Definition:** In-situ field test to determine bearing capacity, settlement, and $k_s$ of soil by loading a rigid plate incrementally.

- **Standard Plate Sizes (IS 1888):** 300 mm × 300 mm (most common), 450 mm × 450 mm, 600 mm × 600 mm. Thickness 25–40 mm. Circular plates of 300/450/600 mm diameter also permitted.

- **Pit dimensions:** Depth = foundation depth; Width ≥ 5 × plate width.

- **Reaction system:** Kentledge (dead weights) or Anchor piles with reaction truss.

- **Loading:** Increments of ~$P_{safe}/5$; hold each increment until settlement rate ≤ 0.02 mm/min or ≥ 1 hour.

- **Dial gauges:** ≥ 2, least count 0.01 mm, mounted on independent reference beams outside the stress bulb.

- **Unloading:** Done in decrements; rebound curve gives elastic settlement.

- **Failure criterion when curve is gradual:**
  - 0.1 B rule (Terzaghi)
  - Double tangent method
  - Log P – log S method

- **Ultimate bearing capacity:** $q_u = P_u / A_p$.

- **Safe bearing capacity:** $q_{safe} = q_u / F.S.$ (F.S. = 2 to 3).

- **Modulus of subgrade reaction:** $k_s = q / S_p$ (units: **kN/m³**).

- **Settlement extrapolation (sand):** $S_f = S_p \cdot \left( \tfrac{2B_p}{B_f+B_p} \right)^2$ — Terzaghi & Peck.

- **Settlement extrapolation (clay):** $S_f = S_p \cdot \tfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ — linear scaling.

- **Influence depth:** Pressure bulb = 1.5 B to 2.0 B below plate.

- **Most reliable for:** Granular soils (sands, gravels) where undisturbed sampling is impossible.

- **Least reliable for:** Soft clays (size effect + time effect = unreliable).

- **Limitations to memorise:** Size effect, depth effect, time effect, capillary tension, water table effect, difficulty in deep foundations, scale effect, short-duration loading.

- **Final design pressure:** Smaller of shear criterion and settlement criterion — always cross-check both.

- **Killer formula to remember:** $S_f = S_p \cdot \left( \tfrac{2B_p}{B_f+B_p} \right)^2$ (sand) — *the squared term* is the giveaway that this is the sand formula; the clay formula has *no square*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mechanism (General, Local and Punching Shear Failure) - Situations in Which Each of Them Can Be Expected
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Failure Mechanism (General, Local and Punching Shear Failure) - Situations in Which Each of Them Can Be Expected

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In geotechnical engineering, when a shallow foundation is loaded progressively to its ultimate capacity, the underlying soil fails along characteristic surfaces. **Vesic (1963)** classified this failure into three distinct mechanisms, based on the relative density of the soil, the depth of the foundation, and the stiffness of the load–settlement response. The three mechanisms are **General Shear Failure (GSF)**, **Local Shear Failure (LSF)**, and **Punching Shear Failure (PSF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All three failure modes belong to the **Terzaghi Bearing Capacity Theory** framework (1943), which forms the foundation of Module-2. Every KTU question on this topic is essentially asking: *"Which mode dominates and how do we modify the bearing capacity equation?"*

### 1.2 The Three Failure Mechanisms — Quick Overview

| Mode | Soil Type | Failure Surface | Failure Pattern |
|------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| General Shear Failure | Dense sand / Stiff clay | Reaches ground surface | Sudden, catastrophic, well-defined |
| Local Shear Failure | Medium sand / Medium clay | Partially developed, hidden | Significant settlement before failure |
| Punching Shear Failure | Loose sand / Soft clay | Vertical, hidden | Continuous settlement, no heave |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Pressing a Stick into Different Soils:**
> - Imagine pushing a wooden stick vertically into **dry, compacted beach sand** (dense). The sand around it suddenly cracks, bulges up, and the stick tilts to one side. The failure is **sudden and noisy** — that is **General Shear Failure**.
> - Now push the same stick into **moderately compacted garden soil**. The soil bulges slightly, the stick settles gradually, and there is no loud snap. The failure is **progressive and quiet** — that is **Local Shear Failure**.
> - Finally, push the stick into **loose, wet, fluffy soil** (like a marshy area). The stick simply sinks straight down without any surface bulging. The soil is being squeezed downward in a narrow column. This is **Punching Shear Failure**.

The same physics governs how real footings behave under a building load — the type of soil underneath decides which "analogy" applies.

### 1.4 Key Physical Indicators

> [!TIP]
> **Look for these signatures during a plate-load test:**
> - A **clear peak** in the load–settlement curve → General Shear
> - A **rounded, gradual** rise with no peak → Local Shear
> - A **continuously rising** curve with no defined failure → Punching Shear
> - **Heaving of ground surface** around the plate → General Shear
> - **No heave, only vertical penetration** → Punching Shear
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Shear Failure (GSF) — Detailed Mechanism

**GSF** is the classical, well-defined failure mode originally assumed by Terzaghi. It occurs in **dense/stiff soils with low compressibility**.

**Step-by-step behaviour under increasing load:**

- The load–settlement curve shows a **linear elastic region**, followed by a **clear peak (qᵤ)**, and then a **sudden drop** with continued settlement.
- A **distinct failure surface** propagates from the base of the footing **all the way up to the ground surface**.
- The footing **tilts visibly** just before failure.
- **Heaving (bulging) of soil** is observed adjacent to the footing on both sides.
- The **ultimate bearing pressure (qᵤ)** is well-defined and easily read from the curve.

**The Three Failure Zones (Terzaghi's Model):**

- **Zone I — Active Wedge (Elastic Equilibrium):** A triangular wedge directly beneath the footing, bounded by the base of the footing and lines inclined at **α = 45° + φ/2** with the horizontal. This zone moves *downward* with the footing.
- **Zone II — Radial Shear Zone (Log-Spiral):** A transition zone bounded by a log-spiral curve. It accommodates the rotation of principal stress axes.
- **Zone III — Passive Wedge (Passive Equilibrium):** A triangular zone on each side, emerging at the ground surface at angle **β = 45° − φ/2** with the horizontal. This zone is pushed *upward and outward*, causing the visible heave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "sketch" the failure mechanism, always label the three zones, indicate the angles (45° + φ/2) and (45° − φ/2), and mark the radial shear zone distinctly. Half the marks come from a *neat, fully-annotated* diagram.

### 2.2 Local Shear Failure (LSF) — Detailed Mechanism

**LSF** is an **intermediate mode** occurring in **medium-dense sands or medium-stiff clays**.

**Step-by-step behaviour under increasing load:**

- The load–settlement curve has **no clear peak** — failure is **progressive**.
- The **failure surface** initiates at the base of the footing but **does not reach the ground surface**; it terminates within the soil mass.
- The footing undergoes **significant settlement** before the ultimate load is reached.
- **Slight bulging** of soil may be observed near the footing, but no distinct heave.
- **No tilting** of the foundation.
- The **ultimate bearing pressure (qᵤ)** is defined conventionally as the pressure causing a settlement equal to **10% of the footing width (B/10 or 0.1B)**.

**Why does this happen?**
In medium-dense soil, the soil particles can still rearrange (compress), so the failure surface "stops" inside the soil. The failure is governed by both shear and volumetric compression.

**Terzaghi's Modification for LSF:**
Terzaghi proposed a simple reduction:
- Modified cohesion: $c_m = \dfrac{2}{3} c$
- Modified friction angle: $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3} \tan\phi\right)$

The bearing capacity factors $N_c'$, $N_q'$, $N_\gamma'$ are then calculated using $\phi_m$ as if it were the actual angle of internal friction.

### 2.3 Punching Shear Failure (PSF) — Detailed Mechanism

**PSF** is the most severe mode, occurring in **loose sands or soft clays** with high compressibility.

**Step-by-step behaviour under increasing load:**

- The load–settlement curve **rises continuously** with no peak — no defined failure point.
- The failure surface is **vertical** (not inclined), extending downward from the edges of the footing.
- The footing **punches straight into the soil**, like a piston.
- **No heaving** of soil at the surface.
- **No tilting** of the foundation.
- The footing undergoes **large, continuous settlements** even at moderate loads.
- The ultimate bearing capacity is again defined at a settlement of **0.1B** (or sometimes 0.2B in deep foundations).

**Why does this happen?**
In loose/soft soil, the soil below the footing is compressed and squeezed downward. The shear strength is mobilised only along a narrow vertical column, not a wide inclined surface.

### 2.4 Vesic's Classification Criteria (Vesic, 1963)

Vesic proposed the following conditions based on **relative density (I_D)** and **depth ratio (D_f/B)**:

| Failure Mode | Relative Density (I_D) | Depth Ratio (D_f / B) |
|--------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| General Shear | I_D > 0.67 (Dense) | D_f / B < 1 (Shallow) |
| Local Shear | 0.33 < I_D < 0.67 (Medium) | Any |
| Punching Shear | I_D < 0.33 (Loose) | D_f / B > 4 (Deep) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For Clays**, use the undrained shear strength ratio $c_u / (\gamma \cdot D_f)$ or the stiffness index $I_r$ as a guide instead of relative density.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in almost every Part-B question.**

| Equation / Term | Formula | Application |
|-----------------|---------|-------------|
| **Terzaghi's GSF — Strip Footing** | $q_u \;=\; c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma$ | General Shear Failure |
| **Terzaghi's GSF — Square Footing** | $q_u \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.4\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma$ | General Shear Failure |
| **Terzaghi's GSF — Circular Footing** | $q_u \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.3\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma$ | General Shear Failure |
| **Local Shear — Modified Cohesion** | $c_m \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3}\,c$ | LSF |
| **Local Shear — Modified Friction Angle** | $\phi_m \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\tan\phi\right)$ | LSF |
| **Terzaghi's LSF — Strip Footing** | $q_u' \;=\; c_m\,N_c' \;+\; q\,N_q' \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma'$ | Local Shear Failure |
| **Bearing Capacity Factor N_q** | $N_q \;=\; e^{\pi\tan\phi}\,\tan^{2}\!\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Both GSF & LSF |
| **Bearing Capacity Factor N_c** | $N_c \;=\; (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | Both GSF & LSF |
| **Bearing Capacity Factor N_γ (Terzaghi)** | $N_\gamma \;=\; (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ | Both GSF & LSF |
| **Bearing Capacity Factor N_γ (IS Code / Vesic)** | $N_\gamma \;=\; 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | Both GSF & LSF |
| **Surcharge at base level** | $q \;=\; \gamma\,D_f$ | All modes |
| **Ultimate Settlement (definition)** | $s_u \;=\; 0.1\,B$ to $0.2\,B$ | LSF & PSF only |

### 2.6 Typical Bearing Capacity Factor Values (GSF, for Quick Reference)

| φ (degrees) | N_c | N_q | N_γ |
|-------------|-----|-----|-----|
| 0 | 5.7 | 1.0 | 0.0 |
| 5 | 7.3 | 1.6 | 0.5 |
| 10 | 9.6 | 2.7 | 1.2 |
| 15 | 12.9 | 4.4 | 2.5 |
| 20 | 17.7 | 7.4 | 5.0 |
| 25 | 25.1 | 12.7 | 9.7 |
| 30 | 37.2 | 22.5 | 19.7 |
| 35 | 57.8 | 41.4 | 42.4 |
| 40 | 95.7 | 81.3 | 100.4 |
| 45 | 172.3 | 173.3 | 297.5 |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design Selection:** Knowing the failure mode helps engineers choose between a **shallow footing, mat, or deep pile foundation**. Loose soils with PSF tendency often warrant deep foundations.
- **Soil Improvement Decisions:** If the natural soil would fail by PSF, engineers may opt for **compaction, vibro-flotation, or stone columns** to upgrade it to LSF or GSF.
- **Plate Load Test Interpretation:** The three modes give a *direct reading* on the in-situ soil behaviour, validating the laboratory parameters.
- **Limit State Design:** Modern codes (IS 6403, EC-7) explicitly use the failure mode to set partial safety factors.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation for General Shear Failure (Strip Footing)

Terzaghi (1943) derived the ultimate bearing capacity for a strip footing by considering the **three-zone failure mechanism** described earlier. The complete step-by-step derivation is given below.

#### Step 1: Define the Problem Geometry

A strip footing of width **B** rests at depth **D_f** below the ground surface. The soil has cohesion **c**, angle of internal friction **φ**, and unit weight **γ**. The surcharge at the base of the footing is $q = \gamma D_f$.

#### Step 2: Identify the Failure Wedges

Using the principle of superposition, Terzaghi considered three separate effects acting on the footing base:

- The **cohesion c** of the soil (active case)
- The **surcharge q** on either side of the footing
- The **weight of the soil wedge** (the zone that the footing tends to push down)

#### Step 3: Cohesion Term — Assume Smooth Base, φ = 0 Condition

For the cohesion-only component, Terzaghi used the **Meyerhof & Reissner / Prandtl-type wedge analysis**.

The failure surface consists of:
- A **log-spiral** in the radial shear zone
- The **cohesion c** acts uniformly along the entire failure surface

For a weightless soil (γ = 0) with surcharge q = 0, the ultimate load per unit length is:

$$
P_c \;=\; c\,B\,N_c
$$

Dividing by the area B (per unit length) to get the bearing pressure:

$$
q_{u,c} \;=\; \dfrac{P_c}{B} \;=\; c\,N_c
$$

where $N_c$ is determined by the geometry of the failure surface.

#### Step 4: Surcharge Term — Treat as Passive Earth Pressure Problem

The surcharge $q$ on the sides of the footing (over the length equal to B) acts as a surcharge on a virtual wall inclined at angle (45° − φ/2). The equivalent passive earth pressure coefficient gives the surcharge component:

$$
q_{u,q} \;=\; q\,N_q
$$

where:

$$
N_q \;=\; \dfrac{e^{\pi\tan\phi}\,\cos^{2}(45 + \phi/2)}{\cos^{2}(45 - \phi/2)} \cdot \tan\!\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

This simplifies to the well-known KTerzaghi form:

$$
N_q \;=\; e^{\pi\tan\phi}\,\tan^{2}\!\left(45 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

#### Step 5: Self-Weight Term — Heuristic Integration

The weight of the soil in the failure wedge below the footing is the most difficult to derive analytically. Terzaghi used an **empirical approach** by integrating the soil weight within the three zones. The result is:

$$
q_{u,\gamma} \;=\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

where $N_\gamma$ was given empirically by Terzaghi as:

$$
N_\gamma \;=\; (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **IS Code (IS 6403:1981)** and **Vesic (1973)** give an alternative form: $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$. The KTU board accepts either; however, state the source clearly to avoid deduction.

#### Step 6: Superposition (Terzaghi's Final Equation)

Adding the three components, the ultimate bearing capacity for a **strip footing under General Shear Failure** is:

$$
q_u \;=\; c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

This is the **Terzaghi Bearing Capacity Equation**.

#### Step 7: Modification for Local Shear Failure

For LSF, Terzaghi substituted the modified soil parameters:

$$
c_m \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3}\,c
$$

$$
\phi_m \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\,\tan\phi\right)
$$

The modified bearing capacity factors are then **recalculated** using $\phi_m$ (i.e., $N_c' = N_c(\phi_m)$, etc.), giving:

$$
q_u' \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3}\,c\,N_c' \;+\; q\,N_q' \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma'
$$

#### Step 8: Final Form with Shape Factors (for KTU reference)

For a **square footing**:

$$
q_u \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.4\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

For a **circular footing**:

$$
q_u \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.3\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Local Shear Failure — KTU Style)

**Problem:** A strip footing of width **2.0 m** is placed at a depth of **1.0 m** in a sandy soil with $c = 10$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$, and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Determine the ultimate bearing capacity assuming **local shear failure**.

**Solution:**

**Step A: Compute modified soil parameters**

$$
c_m \;=\; \dfrac{2}{3} \times 10 \;=\; 6.67\;\text{kN/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\phi_m \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\tan 30°\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.5774\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.3849) \;\approx\; 21.05°
$$

**Step B: Find bearing capacity factors at φ_m ≈ 21°**

Using the table for $\phi \approx 21°$ (interpolating between 20° and 25°):
- $N_c' \approx 19.3$ (interpolated)
- $N_q' \approx 8.7$ (interpolated)
- $N_\gamma' \approx 6.5$ (interpolated)

**Step C: Compute surcharge at base level**

$$
q \;=\; \gamma \cdot D_f \;=\; 18 \times 1.0 \;=\; 18\;\text{kN/m}^{2}
$$

**Step D: Apply Terzaghi's LSF equation**

$$
q_u' \;=\; c_m\,N_c' \;+\; q\,N_q' \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma'
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
q_u' \;=\; (6.67)(19.3) \;+\; (18)(8.7) \;+\; 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.0 \times 6.5
$$

$$
q_u' \;=\; 128.7 \;+\; 156.6 \;+\; 117.0
$$

$$
q_u' \;\approx\; 402.3\;\text{kN/m}^{2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show the **interpolation step** clearly. Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating the interpolated values, even if you approximate the final number.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (GSF vs LSF vs PSF)

| Parameter | General Shear Failure | Local Shear Failure | Punching Shear Failure |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Soil Density** | Dense (I_D > 0.67) | Medium (0.33–0.67) | Loose (I_D < 0.33) |
| **Soil Type** | Stiff clay, dense sand | Medium clay, medium sand | Soft clay, loose sand |
| **Compressibility** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Failure Surface** | Reaches ground | Stops below ground | Vertical, hidden |
| **Surface Heave** | Significant | Slight | None |
| **Tilting of Footing** | Yes | No | No |
| **Load–Settlement Curve** | Clear peak | Rounded, no peak | Continuous rise |
| **Ultimate Pressure** | Well-defined | Defined at s = 0.1B | Defined at s = 0.1B |
| **Depth Ratio D_f/B** | < 1 (shallow) | 1 to 4 | > 4 (deep) |
| **Bearing Capacity Eqn** | $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma$ | Same with $\phi_m, c_m$ | Same with $\phi_m, c_m$ (sometimes $\phi=0$ analysis) |
| **Failure Mode in Plate Load Test** | Sudden, brittle | Progressive | Gradual, ductile |
| **Engineering Remedy** | None needed; ideal case | Soil improvement may help | Compaction or deep foundation required |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow — Failure Mechanism Decision Tree

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **decision logic** an engineer follows to identify the dominant failure mode in the field, based on the soil, foundation geometry, and load test observations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["Start: Identify Soil and Foundation Parameters"]
    nodeSoil["Determine Soil Type and Relative Density or Consistency Index"]
    nodeDepth["Compute Depth Ratio D_f divided by B"]
    nodeCheckDense{"Is Soil Dense or Stiff and D_f/B less than 1?"}
    nodeCheckMedium{"Is Soil Medium Dense or Medium Stiff?"}
    nodeCheckLoose{"Is Soil Loose or Soft and D_f/B greater than 4?"}
    nodeGSF["GENERAL SHEAR FAILURE"]
    nodeLSF["LOCAL SHEAR FAILURE"]
    nodePSF["PUNCHING SHEAR FAILURE"]
    nodeGSFFeatures["Clear failure surface to ground surface, tilting, heaving, well defined ultimate load"]
    nodeLSFFeatures["Failure surface does not reach surface, no tilting, significant settlement before failure"]
    nodePSFFeatures["Vertical failure surface, no heave, no tilt, continuous settlement, no defined peak"]
    nodeCheckPlate{"Plate Load Test Observation"}
    nodeCurvePeak["Load Settlement curve has a sharp PEAK"]
    nodeCurveRound["Load Settlement curve is ROUNDED, no peak"]
    nodeCurveRise["Load Settlement curve RISES continuously"]
    nodeEnd["End: Apply Terzaghi equation with appropriate mode"]

    nodeStart --> nodeSoil
    nodeSoil --> nodeDepth
    nodeDepth --> nodeCheckDense
    nodeCheckDense -- YES --> nodeGSF
    nodeCheckDense -- NO --> nodeCheckMedium
    nodeCheckMedium -- YES --> nodeLSF
    nodeCheckMedium -- NO --> nodeCheckLoose
    nodeCheckLoose -- YES --> nodePSF
    nodeGSF --> nodeGSFFeatures
    nodeLSF --> nodeLSFFeatures
    nodePSF --> nodePSFFeatures
    nodeGSF --> nodeCheckPlate
    nodeLSF --> nodeCheckPlate
    nodePSF --> nodeCheckPlate
    nodeCheckPlate -- SHARP PEAK --> nodeCurvePeak
    nodeCheckPlate -- ROUNDED --> nodeCurveRound
    nodeCheckPlate -- CONTINUOUS RISE --> nodeCurveRise
    nodeCurvePeak --> nodeEnd
    nodeCurveRound --> nodeEnd
    nodeCurveRise --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Failure Zones in General Shear Failure (Side-by-Side Schematic Logic)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **geometric logic** of the three failure zones (Active Wedge, Radial Shear Zone, Passive Wedge) for the GSF case, as originally conceptualised by Terzaghi.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFoot["STRIP FOOTING OF WIDTH B"]
    nodeActive["ZONE I: ACTIVE WEDGE - Triangular, angle equals 45 plus phi divided by 2 with horizontal, moves DOWN with footing"]
    nodeRadial["ZONE II: RADIAL SHEAR ZONE - Log spiral curve from edge of footing to ground, principal stress rotation"]
    nodePassiveL["ZONE IIIA: PASSIVE WEDGE LEFT - angle 45 minus phi divided by 2 with horizontal, pushed UP and OUT"]
    nodePassiveR["ZONE IIIB: PASSIVE WEDGE RIGHT - symmetric to left wedge"]
    nodeSurface["GROUND SURFACE - Heaving visible here"]
    nodeHeave["SURFACE HEAVE: Yes for GSF, Slight for LSF, No for PSF"]

    nodeFoot --> nodeActive
    nodeActive --> nodeRadial
    nodeRadial --> nodePassiveL
    nodeRadial --> nodePassiveR
    nodePassiveL --> nodeSurface
    nodePassiveR --> nodeSurface
    nodeSurface --> nodeHeave
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Load vs Settlement Behaviour for the Three Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["Load Q applied progressively to footing"]
    nodeGSFPath["GSF Path: Elastic, then PLASTIC YIELD at qu, then DROP, with HEAVE"]
    nodeLSFPath["LSF Path: Elastic, then PROGRESSIVE YIELD, with SLIGHT BULGE"]
    nodePSFPath["PSF Path: ELASTIC, then CONTINUOUS COMPRESSION, no heave, no peak"]
    nodeGSFMarker["Peak qu at settlement su around 0.05B"]
    nodeLSFMarker["qu at settlement su equals 0.1B"]
    nodePSFMarker["qu at settlement su equals 0.1B to 0.2B"]
    nodeEnd["Interpreted Failure Mode"]

    nodeStart --> nodeGSFPath
    nodeStart --> nodeLSFPath
    nodeStart --> nodePSFPath
    nodeGSFPath --> nodeGSFMarker
    nodeLSFPath --> nodeLSFMarker
    nodePSFPath --> nodePSFMarker
    nodeGSFMarker --> nodeEnd
    nodeLSFMarker --> nodeEnd
    nodePSFMarker --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.4 Description of the Physical Failure Sketches (for Drawing in the Exam)

Since Mermaid cannot draw physical soil wedges with inclined angles, here is a **text-based schematic description** that you should reproduce in your answer script for a 7-mark question:

**General Shear Failure — Skeleton Sketch:**

```
        ___________           GROUND SURFACE (with HEAVE on both sides)
       \          \         /
        \  Zone    \       /  Zone III (Passive Wedge)
         \  III     \     /   angle = 45° − φ/2
          \  (Left)  \   /
           \         \ /
   _________\_________X_______________________
   | FOOTING  \       / Zone II (Radial Shear)
   |  Width B  \     /   (Log-spiral curve)
   |___________ \   /
                 \ /
                  \  Zone I (Active Wedge)
                   \   angle = 45° + φ/2
                    \
                     \________________________
```

**Local Shear Failure — Skeleton Sketch:**

```
        ___________           GROUND SURFACE (slight or NO heave)
       \          \         /
        \ Failure  \       /  Zone III (partial / hidden)
         \ Surface  \     /   ends BELOW ground
          \  Hidden  \   /
           \         \ /
   _________\_________X_______________________
   | FOOTING  \       / Zone II
   |  Width B  \     /   (curved, partial)
   |___________ \   /
                 \ /
                  \  Zone I
                   \   angle = 45° + φ_m/2
                    \
                     \________________________
```

**Punching Shear Failure — Skeleton Sketch:**

```

   _________\_________X_______________________
   | FOOTING  |       |
   |  Width B |       |   Vertical failure surface

   |__________|       |
                     |

                     |  compressed
                     |  downward
                     |
                     |________________________
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always **label the angles, the failure zones, the surcharge q, the footing width B, and the heave/no-heave indicator** on the right side. Marks are awarded for **labelled** sketches, not artistic quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] Define general shear failure. List its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
General shear failure is a sudden, well-defined failure mechanism that occurs in dense/stiff soils with low compressibility. **Key characteristics:**
- A distinct failure surface develops from the base of the footing **all the way up to the ground surface**.
- The foundation **tilts** noticeably just before failure.
- **Heaving (bulging) of soil** is visible on both sides of the footing.
- The load–settlement curve shows a **clear peak (ultimate load)**, after which the load drops.
- It is associated with **dense sands and stiff over-consolidated clays**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] List any three situations in which punching shear failure can be expected in the field.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Punching shear failure can be expected in the following situations:
1. **Loose sandy soils** (relative density $I_D < 0.33$) where soil grains rearrange easily under load.
2. **Soft, compressible clays** with high water content and low undrained shear strength.
3. **Deep foundations** where the depth ratio $D_f / B > 4$, causing the failure surface to remain vertical and hidden below the footing.
4. *(Bonus point)* Soil with very high in-situ void ratio, recent uncompacted fills, or marshy deposits.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, modified]*
>
> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the three modes of shear failure in shallow foundations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the various factors affecting the type of shear failure and the situations in which each type can be expected. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The three modes of shear failure, originally classified by **Vesic (1963)** based on Terzaghi's framework, are:

**(i) General Shear Failure (GSF):** A sudden, well-defined failure with a continuous failure surface from footing base to ground surface, accompanied by heaving and tilting. Failure zones: Active wedge (45° + φ/2), radial shear (log-spiral), passive wedge (45° − φ/2). The load–settlement curve shows a sharp peak.

**(ii) Local Shear Failure (LSF):** A progressive failure where the failure surface does not reach the ground. There is significant settlement before failure, slight bulging of soil, but no tilting. The load–settlement curve has no clear peak; ultimate load is defined at $s = 0.1B$.

**(iii) Punching Shear Failure (PSF):** A gradual failure where the footing "punches" vertically into the soil, with no heave and no tilting. The failure surface is vertical and remains hidden. The load–settlement curve rises continuously.

*Full-mark sketch must include:* the three zones for GSF with all angles labelled, the partial/hidden failure surface for LSF, and the vertical failure surface for PSF. The heave indicator on the right side of each sketch is essential.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The type of shear failure is governed by the following factors and situations:

| Factor | GSF | LSF | PSF |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Soil Density (I_D)** | I_D > 0.67 (dense) | 0.33 ≤ I_D ≤ 0.67 (medium) | I_D < 0.33 (loose) |
| **Depth Ratio (D_f/B)** | < 1 (shallow) | 1 to 4 | > 4 (deep) |
| **Compressibility** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Typical Soil** | Dense sand, stiff clay | Medium sand, medium clay | Loose sand, soft clay |

**Situations expected in field practice:**
- **GSF** is expected for **shallow footings on dense sand or stiff clay**; examples include footings on glacial till, dense alluvial sand, or weathered rock.
- **LSF** is expected for **medium-dense sands with moderate SPT N-values (10–30)** or for footings on **medium-stiff clay** with $c_u$ between 50–100 kN/m².
- **PSF** is expected in **loose silty sands, soft marine clays, recent fills, or below the water table** where pore pressure dissipation is slow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Forgetting to mention that for LSF, the **cohesion and friction angle are both modified** (not just one). Marks are deducted if $c_m = (2/3)c$ is used but $\phi$ is left unchanged, or vice-versa.
> - Failing to **label the three zones** in the GSF sketch. A sketch without labels scores at most 1–2 marks out of 7.
> - Confusing **D_f/B** with **B/D_f** in the depth ratio. Depth ratio is **D_f divided by B**, not the other way around.
> - Stating that the failure surface is **vertical for GSF** — this is incorrect. GSF has **inclined** failure surfaces (45° + φ/2 and 45° − φ/2). Only PSF has a vertical failure surface.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks):** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, modified]*
>
> **(a)** Define general shear failure. With a neat sketch, show the three failure zones and the angle made by each wedge with the horizontal. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A strip footing of width 2.5 m is founded at a depth of 1.5 m in a sandy soil with $c = 0$, $\phi = 30°$, and $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³. Determine the ultimate bearing capacity using Terzaghi's equation. Use $N_q = 22.5$ and $N_\gamma = 19.7$ for $\phi = 30°$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):** General shear failure is a sudden and catastrophic failure mechanism that occurs in dense/stiff soils, in which a well-defined failure surface propagates from the base of the footing to the ground surface, accompanied by heaving of soil and tilting of the foundation.

**Sketch (5 marks):** The sketch should clearly show:
- The strip footing of width B at depth D_f.
- **Zone I — Active Wedge** beneath the footing, bounded by lines inclined at **α = 45° + φ/2** with the horizontal.
- **Zone II — Radial Shear Zone** as a log-spiral curve from the edge of the footing.
- **Zone III — Passive Wedge** on each side, inclined at **β = 45° − φ/2** with the horizontal, emerging at the ground surface.
- Heave indicators on both sides of the footing.
- Surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$ shown on either side of the footing.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: Strip footing, $B = 2.5$ m, $D_f = 1.5$ m, $c = 0$, $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³, $N_q = 22.5$, $N_\gamma = 19.7$.

**Step 1: Compute the surcharge at the base of the footing**

$$
q \;=\; \gamma \cdot D_f \;=\; 17.5 \times 1.5 \;=\; 26.25\;\text{kN/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation (General Shear Failure)**

For a strip footing:

$$
q_u \;=\; c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

Since $c = 0$, the first term vanishes:

$$
q_u \;=\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values**

$$
q_u \;=\; (26.25)(22.5) \;+\; 0.5 \times 17.5 \times 2.5 \times 19.7
$$

$$
q_u \;=\; 590.625 \;+\; 430.9375
$$

**Step 4: Final answer**

$$
q_u \;\approx\; 1021.56\;\text{kN/m}^{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Calculation Errors:**
> - Students frequently **forget to multiply $N_c$ by c** (i.e., they drop the first term silently even when $c \ne 0$). Always show the term explicitly; if $c = 0$, state "$cN_c = 0$" and then proceed.
> - The **shape factor** (1.3 for square, 1.0 for strip) is **NOT** applicable to a strip footing. Applying it is a common error that costs 1 mark.
> - The depth of the footing $D_f$ is **not** the same as the depth of the failure zone. Do not include $D_f$ in the third term of the equation — only $\gamma$ and $B$ appear there.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — The 15-Point Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- [x] **Three modes** of failure: **GSF, LSF, PSF** — classified by **Vesic (1963)** within **Terzaghi's framework (1943)**.
- [x] **GSF** → Dense/stiff soil, low compressibility, $D_f/B < 1$, clear peak in load–settlement curve, heaving and tilting present.
- [x] **LSF** → Medium-dense/medium-stiff soil, $0.33 \le I_D \le 0.67$, failure surface hidden, $s_u = 0.1B$ defines $q_u$, no tilting.
- [x] **PSF** → Loose/soft soil, high compressibility, $D_f/B > 4$, vertical failure surface, no heave, no tilting, $s_u = 0.1B$ to $0.2B$.
- [x] **Three failure zones in GSF**: Active wedge (45° + φ/2), radial shear (log-spiral), passive wedge (45° − φ/2).
- [x] **Terzaghi's GSF equation** (strip): $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma$.
- [x] **Terzaghi's LSF equation** (strip): $q_u' = (2/3)c N_c' + q N_q' + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma'$.
- [x] **Modified cohesion for LSF**: $c_m = (2/3)c$.
- [x] **Modified friction angle for LSF**: $\phi_m = \tan^{-1}((2/3)\tan\phi)$.
- [x] **Bearing capacity factor formulas**:
  - $N_q = e^{\pi\tan\phi} \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$
  - $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$
  - $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ *(Terzaghi)* or $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ *(IS/Vesic)*
- [x] **Shape factors**: 1.0 (strip), 1.3 (square/circular) for the first and third terms; 1.0 for the second term.
- [x] **Surcharge**: $q = \gamma D_f$ is the effective vertical stress at the base of the footing.
- [x] **Vesic's depth ratio criteria**: $D_f/B < 1$ → GSF, $1 < D_f/B < 4$ → LSF, $D_f/B > 4$ → PSF.
- [x] **PSF has vertical failure surface** — this is the most distinctive feature. Don't confuse it with GSF.
- [x] **In exams**, always draw the **sketch first**, label **all zones and angles**, then write the equation. Sketches carry 40–50% of the marks for a 7-mark sub-question.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Golden Rule:** If a problem gives a dense sand and asks for the bearing capacity → use **GSF directly**. If it gives a medium sand or hints at compressibility → use **LSF with modified parameters**. If it gives loose sand or a very deep footing → use **PSF** (treat as $\phi = 0$ analysis for clays or use reduced factors for sands). The KTU board examiner will mark step-by-step; show all the substitutions to secure full marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory for Strip Footing - Assumptions - Gross and Net Bearing Pressure - Ultimate and Safe Bearing Capacity - Allowable Soil Pressure - Bearing Capacity Factors - Numerical Problems
