> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory for Strip Footing - Assumptions - Gross and Net Bearing Pressure - Ultimate and Safe Bearing Capacity - Allowable Soil Pressure - Bearing Capacity Factors - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory — The Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> Terzaghi (1943) developed the first rational, comprehensive theory to evaluate the **ultimate bearing capacity of shallow foundations** by treating the soil beneath a footing as a generalized plastic failure medium. The theory identifies three distinct failure zones and combines their resistance to obtain the maximum load per unit area that a soil can support before shear failure occurs.

**For a Strip Footing** (where the footing length $L$ is much greater than its width $B$, typically $L/B \geq 5$, giving plane-strain conditions), the classical Terzaghi equation for **gross ultimate bearing capacity** is:

$$q_u \;=\; c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma$$

where:
- $c$ = **cohesion of the soil (kN/m²)**
- $q = \gamma D_f$ = **overburden pressure (kN/m²)** acting at the level of the footing base
- $\gamma$ = **unit weight of soil (kN/m³)** beneath the footing
- $B$ = **width of the strip footing (m)**
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = **Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors** — dimensionless functions of the angle of internal friction $\phi$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Iceberg Sinking"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Iceberg in Sand" Analogy**
> Imagine trying to push a wide, flat-bottomed plank (the **footing**) downward into dry sand. The sand directly underneath gets compressed and tries to escape sideways. It bulges out in a curved, rotating mass, pushing against the undisturbed sand further out, which in turn pushes against the soil at the surface.
>
> The total resistance you feel at the plank is the sum of three efforts:
> 1. **The sticky grip** of the soil particles (cohesion, $c$)
> 2. **The weight of sand already lying on top** of the future footing level (surcharge, $q$)
> 3. **The lateral shoulder** of soil resisting the wedge being pushed sideways (self-weight term, $\gamma B$)
>
> Terzaghi's genius was to *mathematically add* these three resistances to predict exactly how much load the soil can take before it fails in shear.

## 1.3 Strip Footing — What Makes it "Strip"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plane-Strain Condition**
> A footing is classified as a **strip footing** when its length-to-width ratio $L/B \geq 5$. Under such geometry, the failure pattern is essentially two-dimensional, identical at every cross-section. The analysis assumes **plane strain**, which simplifies the mathematics considerably and avoids 3D corner effects.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Critical Pressure Definitions Every KTU Student Must Memorize**
>
> | Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
> |---|---|---|
> | Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity | $q_u$ | Total failure load per unit area of footing (includes soil weight above base) |
> | Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity | $q_{nu}$ | Failure load **excluding** the overburden already in place — $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ |
> | Safe Bearing Capacity (Gross) | $q_s$ | Failure load divided by factor of safety $F$ — $q_s = q_u / F$ |
> | Net Safe Bearing Capacity / Allowable Soil Pressure | $q_{ns}$ | Net ultimate divided by $F$ — $q_{ns} = q_{nu}/F = (q_u - \gamma D_f)/F$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Failure Zones — Terzaghi's Physical Model

Terzaghi visualized the soil below a **rough-base strip footing** breaking into three contiguous zones at the point of failure:

1. **Zone I — Active Wedge (Triangular)**
   - Located *immediately* beneath the footing base.
   - Bounded by the footing base and two failure planes inclined at $\phi$ to the horizontal (Rankine active failure).
   - The soil here acts as part of the footing and moves downward with it.

2. **Zone II — Radial Shear Zone (Log-Spiral)**
   - A curved transition zone where shear stress rotates from vertical (at the active wedge boundary) to roughly horizontal (at the passive wedge boundary).
   - Bounded by a **logarithmic spiral** curve, since the radial stress is the major principal stress in this region.

3. **Zone III — Passive Rankine Wedge (Triangular)**
   - A triangular wedge pushed upward and outward at the surface.
   - Its failure plane is inclined at $45^\circ - \phi/2$ to the horizontal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the base assumed rough?**
> A rough footing base prevents the underlying soil wedge from sliding horizontally. This means the wedge moves *with* the footing as a rigid block, generating a friction angle $\phi$ at the base. This is the standard Terzaghi assumption and is what gives the equations their characteristic form.

## 2.2 Assumptions of Terzaghi's Theory (Strip Footing) — KTU Mandatory List

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Assumption List (Board Examiners Expect This Exact Set)**
>
> 1. The soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite**, with the water table located far below the base.
> 2. The footing is **long (strip)** with $L/B \to \infty$, so plane-strain conditions prevail.
> 3. The **base of the footing is rough**, so the soil beneath does not slip laterally.
> 4. The **depth of the footing $D_f$ is less than its width $B$** ($D_f < B$).
> 5. The soil above the base of the footing is **replaced by a uniform surcharge** of intensity $q = \gamma D_f$, and its shear strength is neglected.
> 6. The footing is subjected to a **vertical, centric load**.
> 7. **General shear failure** (not local or punching) is assumed to occur.
> 8. The bearing capacity equation is derived assuming soil is **incompressible** for the self-weight term.

## 2.3 Bearing Capacity Factors — Mathematical Form

The three factors depend **only on the soil friction angle $\phi$**:

$$N_q \;=\; e^{\,\pi \tan\phi}\;\tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$N_c \;=\; (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$

$$N_\gamma \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi) \quad \text{(Terzaghi's empirical fit)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Verification Identity**
> For $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive soil): $N_q = 1$ and $N_c = 5.7$, $N_\gamma = 0$. The equation reduces to $q_u = 5.7\,c$, recovering the classical Skempton/Terzaghi result for $\phi = 0$ soils.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Complete Cheat Sheet for Numerical Problems**
>
> | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Gross ultimate bearing capacity | $q_u$ | $c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5\,\gamma B N_\gamma$ | kN/m² |
> | 2 | Net ultimate bearing capacity | $q_{nu}$ | $q_u - \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² |
> | 3 | Gross safe bearing capacity | $q_s$ | $q_u / F$ | kN/m² |
> | 4 | Net safe / Allowable soil pressure | $q_{ns}$ | $q_{nu} / F$ | kN/m² |
> | 5 | Surcharge (overburden) at base | $q$ | $\gamma D_f$ | kN/m² |
> | 6 | $N_q$ factor | $N_q$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | dimensionless |
> | 7 | $N_c$ factor | $N_c$ | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | dimensionless |
> | 8 | $N_\gamma$ factor | $N_\gamma$ | $0.5(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ | dimensionless |
> | 9 | Ultimate load per m length | $Q_u$ | $q_u \cdot B$ (per m run) | kN/m |
> | 10 | Safe load per m length | $Q_s$ | $Q_u / F$ | kN/m |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where This Theory is Applied in Practice**
> - **Design of building footings** (wall footings, combined footings) where $L/B \geq 5$.
> - **Bridge abutments and retaining wall bases** — typically behave as strip elements.
> - **Earth dam design** for checking crest stability.
> - **Preliminary sizing of shallow foundations** before refined analysis (Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic corrections).
> - **Estimating factor of safety** against bearing failure of existing footings before loading (e.g., heavy equipment placement).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation Outline — How Terzaghi Built the Equation

Terzaghi's full analytical derivation proceeds by **superposing** the resistance from each of the three zones. The mathematical steps are:

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium on the footing** (per unit length, plane strain):
- Vertical load $Q_u$ per unit length acts on the footing of width $B$.
- This load must be resisted by stresses along the three boundaries of Zone I and the surcharge reaction on its top.

**Step 2 — Identify stress state on failure surfaces:**
- The failure surface consists of: an inclined plane (Zone I → Zone II interface) and a log-spiral (Zone II), terminating in another inclined plane (Zone II → Zone III interface).
- Along each failure plane, the Mohr-Coulomb relation $\tau = c + \sigma \tan\phi$ applies.

**Step 3 — Compute three contributions to $q_u$:**

**(a) Cohesion contribution** — Resistance from the inherent stickiness of the soil.
Since for $\phi = 0$ soil, $N_c = 5.7$, and the general expression is:
$$q_{u,\text{cohesion}} = c\,N_c$$

**(b) Surcharge contribution** — Resistance from the weight of soil already lying on the failure planes.
The active wedge's inclined failure surface has a length that, after trigonometry, gives:
$$q_{u,\text{surcharge}} = \gamma D_f\,N_q$$

**(c) Self-weight contribution** — Resistance from the soil's own weight in the failure wedge.
The lateral spread of the wedge is governed by $\phi$ and the depth of the failure surface, giving:
$$q_{u,\text{self-weight}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma B N_\gamma$$

**Step 4 — Superpose the three contributions:**
Since each term is independent, the total ultimate bearing capacity is the sum:
$$q_u = c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The equation is a **superposition of three classical solutions** — (1) Prandtl's punch problem (c, $\phi$, surcharge), (2) Caquot-Kerisel's self-weight correction, and (3) Terzaghi's geometric correction. This additive structure is why Terzaghi's formula remains the pedagogical standard worldwide.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Purely Cohesive Soil

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern)**
> A strip footing of width **2 m** is founded at a depth of **1.2 m** in a saturated clay soil having $\phi_u = 0^\circ$ and $c_u = 60$ kN/m². The unit weight of the clay is $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Determine the **gross ultimate**, **net ultimate**, **gross safe** (with $F = 3$), and **net safe** bearing capacities.

**Solution:**

For $\phi = 0^\circ$:
- $N_q = 1.0$
- $N_c = 5.7$
- $N_\gamma = 0$

Step (a) — Surcharge:
$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.2 = 21.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (b) — Gross ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

Substituting:
$$q_u = (60 \times 5.7) + (21.6 \times 1.0) + (0.5 \times 18 \times 2 \times 0)$$

$$q_u = 342 + 21.6 + 0 = 363.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (c) — Net ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 363.6 - 21.6 = 342 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (d) — Gross safe bearing capacity (with $F = 3$):
$$q_s = \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{363.6}{3} = 121.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (e) — Net safe / Allowable soil pressure (with $F = 3$):
$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} = \frac{342}{3} = 114 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $q_u$ | 363.6 | kN/m² |
| $q_{nu}$ | 342.0 | kN/m² |
| $q_s$ ($F=3$) | 121.2 | kN/m² |
| $q_{ns}$ ($F=3$) | 114.0 | kN/m² |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Cohesionless Soil (C-$\phi$)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern)**
> A strip footing of width **3 m** is placed at a depth of **1.5 m** in a sandy soil. Given: $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. Find the **net safe bearing capacity** using $F = 3$ and the **ultimate load per metre run** of the footing.

**Solution:**

For $\phi = 35^\circ$:

Step (a) — Compute $N_q$:
$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 35^\circ} \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \tfrac{35^\circ}{2}\right)$$

$$\tan 35^\circ = 0.7002$$

$$\pi \tan 35^\circ = \pi \times 0.7002 = 2.1995$$

$$e^{2.1995} = 9.024$$

$$45^\circ + 17.5^\circ = 62.5^\circ$$

$$\tan 62.5^\circ = 1.9205$$

$$\tan^2 62.5^\circ = 3.6883$$

$$N_q = 9.024 \times 3.6883 = 33.30$$

Step (b) — Compute $N_c$:
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = (33.30 - 1)\cot 35^\circ$$

$$\cot 35^\circ = \frac{1}{0.7002} = 1.4281$$

$$N_c = 32.30 \times 1.4281 = 46.13$$

Step (c) — Compute $N_\gamma$ (Terzaghi's form):
$$N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi) = 0.5 \times 32.30 \times \tan(49^\circ)$$

$$\tan 49^\circ = 1.1504$$

$$N_\gamma = 0.5 \times 32.30 \times 1.1504 = 18.58$$

Step (d) — Surcharge:
$$q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 1.5 = 28.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (e) — Gross ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = (0 \times 46.13) + (28.5 \times 33.30) + (0.5 \times 19 \times 3 \times 18.58)$$

$$q_u = 0 + 949.05 + 529.53 = 1478.58 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (f) — Net ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1478.58 - 28.5 = 1450.08 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (g) — Net safe bearing capacity:
$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} = \frac{1450.08}{3} = 483.36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (h) — Ultimate load per metre run of the footing:
$$Q_u = q_u \cdot B = 1478.58 \times 3 = 4435.74 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $N_q$ | 33.30 | — |
| $N_c$ | 46.13 | — |
| $N_\gamma$ | 18.58 | — |
| $q_u$ | 1478.58 | kN/m² |
| $q_{nu}$ | 1450.08 | kN/m² |
| $q_{ns}$ ($F=3$) | 483.36 | kN/m² |
| $Q_u$ (per m run) | 4435.74 | kN/m |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — General C-$\phi$ Soil with Both Components

> **Problem Statement**
> A strip footing of width **2.5 m** is founded at 1.0 m depth in a soil with $c = 25$ kN/m², $\phi = 25^\circ$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. Calculate the **safe load per metre run** with $F = 2.5$.

**Solution:**

For $\phi = 25^\circ$:

Step (a) — Compute $N_q$:
$$\tan 25^\circ = 0.4663$$

$$\pi \tan 25^\circ = 1.4649$$

$$e^{1.4649} = 4.327$$

$$\tan^2(45 + 12.5)^\circ = \tan^2(57.5^\circ) = (1.5697)^2 = 2.4640$$

$$N_q = 4.327 \times 2.464 = 10.66$$

Step (b) — Compute $N_c$:
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot 25^\circ = (10.66 - 1) \times \frac{1}{0.4663} = 9.66 \times 2.1445 = 20.72$$

Step (c) — Compute $N_\gamma$:
$$N_\gamma = 0.5(10.66 - 1)\tan(1.4 \times 25^\circ) = 0.5 \times 9.66 \times \tan(35^\circ)$$

$$\tan 35^\circ = 0.7002$$

$$N_\gamma = 0.5 \times 9.66 \times 0.7002 = 3.38$$

Step (d) — Surcharge:
$$q = 18 \times 1.0 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (e) — Gross ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = (25 \times 20.72) + (18 \times 10.66) + (0.5 \times 18 \times 2.5 \times 3.38)$$

$$q_u = 518.0 + 191.88 + 76.05 = 785.93 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step (f) — Ultimate load per m run:
$$Q_u = q_u \cdot B = 785.93 \times 2.5 = 1964.83 \text{ kN/m}$$

Step (g) — Safe load per m run:
$$Q_s = \frac{Q_u}{F} = \frac{1964.83}{2.5} = 785.93 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $N_q$ | 10.66 | — |
| $N_c$ | 20.72 | — |
| $N_\gamma$ | 3.38 | — |
| $q_u$ | 785.93 | kN/m² |
| $Q_u$ (per m) | 1964.83 | kN/m |
| $Q_s$ ($F=2.5$) | 785.93 | kN/m |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Failure Zone Architecture Below a Strip Footing

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the three failure zones identified by Terzaghi beneath a rough-base strip footing at the point of general shear failure.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["FOOTING BASE - Width B - Rough Interface"] --> nodeB["ZONE I - Active Wedge - Triangular Block - Angle phi to Horizontal"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ZONE II - Radial Shear Zone - Logarithmic Spiral - Curved Transition"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ZONE III - Passive Rankine Wedge - Triangular - Surface Uplift Visible"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SURFACE - Ground Level - Surcharge q = gamma Df Above Footing"]

    nodeF["Cohesion Force - C x Nc"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeG["Surcharge Force - q x Nq"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeH["Self Weight Force - 0.5 gamma B x N gamma"] -.-> nodeA

    nodeA --> nodeI["ULTIMATE BEARING CAPACITY qu - Summation of Three Terms"]
```

## 4.2 Pressure Nomenclature and Derivation Pathway

This second Mermaid diagram is a **block-level functional flow** showing how the four pressure definitions are derived from one another, which is a frequently-tested concept in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["GROSS ULTIMATE BEARING CAPACITY qu = c Nc + q Nq + 0.5 gamma B N gamma"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["NET ULTIMATE BEARING CAPACITY q nu = qu minus gamma Df"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["GROSS SAFE BEARING CAPACITY qs = qu divided by F"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["NET SAFE BEARING CAPACITY q ns = q nu divided by F"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["ALLOWABLE SOIL PRESSURE q ns is used in design"]

    nodeF["Bearing Capacity Factors"] --> nodeA
    nodeF1["Nc = Nq minus 1 times cot phi"] -.-> nodeF
    nodeF2["Nq = e to power pi tan phi times tan squared 45 plus phi by 2"] -.-> nodeF
    nodeF3["N gamma = 0.5 times Nq minus 1 times tan 1.4 phi"] -.-> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Three-Zone Spatial Geometry (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOIL_MASS["SOIL MASS - SEMI INFINITE HOMOGENEOUS MEDIUM"]
        nodeA1["SURFACE LINE - GROUND LEVEL - Surcharge q present above footing base"]
        nodeA2["FOOTING - WIDTH B - DEPTH Df - ROUGH BASE"]
        nodeA3["ZONE I - ACTIVE WEDGE - Triangular - Inclined at phi - Moves downward with footing"]
        nodeA4["ZONE II - RADIAL SHEAR - Log spiral curve - Shear stress rotates - Curved surface"]
        nodeA5["ZONE III - PASSIVE WEDGE - Triangular - Emerges at surface - Angle 45 minus phi by 2"]
    end

    nodeA2 -.embeds.-> nodeA3
    nodeA3 -.transitions.-> nodeA4
    nodeA4 -.terminates.-> nodeA5
```

## 4.4 Reading the Diagrams

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Hints for KTU Exam Drawings**
> - The **active wedge** is the soil that moves *with* the footing (downward), behaving as a rigid extension of the foundation.
> - The **radial shear zone** is the region of maximum plastic deformation, bounded by a logarithmic spiral because the radial stress is the major principal stress there.
> - The **passive wedge** is the soil that is *pushed up and out*, eventually causing ground heave at the surface — a key visible warning sign of impending bearing failure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
>
> **Q1.** Define *Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity* of soil. How is it different from *Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity*? **[3 Marks]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The **net ultimate bearing capacity** $q_{nu}$ is the maximum net pressure (i.e., additional pressure over and above the existing overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$) that the soil can support before shear failure occurs.
>
> $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$
>
> The **gross ultimate bearing capacity** $q_u$ is the total pressure (including the overburden already acting at the footing base) at which the soil fails in shear. Hence, $q_u = q_{nu} + \gamma D_f$. The net value is more meaningful in design because it represents only the *additional* stress the foundation applies to the soil.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **Q2.** List any **six** assumptions of Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory for a strip footing. **[3 Marks]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. The soil is homogeneous, isotropic, and semi-infinite.
> 2. The footing is a long strip with $L/B \to \infty$ (plane-strain condition).
> 3. The base of the footing is rough, so the soil beneath moves with the footing.
> 4. The depth of footing $D_f$ is less than its width $B$.
> 5. The soil above the base is replaced by a surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$ whose shear strength is neglected.
> 6. The footing is subjected to a vertical, centric load, and general shear failure is assumed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting**
> 1. **Do not forget to subtract $\gamma D_f$** when computing the *net* ultimate bearing capacity — a classic 1-mark deduction point.
> 2. **Round off the friction angle** consistently — use the angle in degrees within $\tan(\cdot)$ functions, but report the final bearing capacity to at least **two decimal places**.
> 3. **Mention the units** of every quantity (kN/m², kN/m) — failure to do so costs 0.5–1 mark per answer.
> 4. **Always state the assumed mode of failure** (general shear for Terzaghi's theory) in your solution's preamble.
> 5. For $N_\gamma$, **state the formula explicitly** (Terzaghi's empirical form $0.5(N_q-1)\tan(1.4\phi)$) — examiners look for this.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
>
> **Q(A).** A strip footing **2.5 m wide** is to be placed at a depth of **1.5 m** in a soil with the following properties: $c = 30$ kN/m², $\phi = 30^\circ$, $\gamma = 18.5$ kN/m³. Using Terzaghi's theory:
>
> **(a)** Compute the gross and net ultimate bearing capacities. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Determine the safe load per metre run of the footing using a factor of safety of **3**. Also report the allowable soil pressure. **[7 Marks]**

### Solution to Q(A)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$:

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30^\circ} \tan^2(45^\circ + 15^\circ)$$

$$\tan 30^\circ = 0.5774,\ \pi \tan 30^\circ = 1.8138,\ e^{1.8138} = 6.132$$

$$\tan 60^\circ = 1.7321,\ \tan^2 60^\circ = 3.000$$

$$N_q = 6.132 \times 3.000 = 18.40$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot 30^\circ = (18.40 - 1) \times 1.7321 = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$

$$N_\gamma = 0.5(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4 \times 30^\circ) = 0.5 \times 17.40 \times \tan 42^\circ$$

$$\tan 42^\circ = 0.9004,\ N_\gamma = 0.5 \times 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 7.83$$

Step 2 — Surcharge:
$$q = \gamma D_f = 18.5 \times 1.5 = 27.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 3 — Gross ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = (30 \times 30.14) + (27.75 \times 18.40) + (0.5 \times 18.5 \times 2.5 \times 7.83)$$

$$q_u = 904.20 + 510.60 + 181.09 = 1595.89 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 4 — Net ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1595.89 - 27.75 = 1568.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Ultimate load per metre run:
$$Q_u = q_u \times B = 1595.89 \times 2.5 = 3989.73 \text{ kN/m}$$

Step 2 — Safe load per metre run ($F = 3$):
$$Q_s = \frac{Q_u}{F} = \frac{3989.73}{3} = 1329.91 \text{ kN/m}$$

Step 3 — Net safe / Allowable soil pressure:
$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} = \frac{1568.14}{3} = 522.71 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 4 — Final results table:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross ultimate bearing capacity | $q_u$ | 1595.89 | kN/m² |
| Net ultimate bearing capacity | $q_{nu}$ | 1568.14 | kN/m² |
| Safe load per metre run | $Q_s$ | 1329.91 | kN/m |
| Allowable soil pressure | $q_{ns}$ | 522.71 | kN/m² |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q(B).** A strip footing **3 m wide** rests on a purely cohesive soil at a depth of **2 m**. Given: $c = 50$ kN/m², $\phi = 0^\circ$, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³. Use Terzaghi's theory.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **gross ultimate** and **net ultimate** bearing capacities. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** If the applied column load on the wall is **300 kN per metre run**, determine the **factor of safety** against bearing capacity failure. **[7 Marks]**

### Solution to Q(B)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 0^\circ$:
- $N_c = 5.7$
- $N_q = 1.0$
- $N_\gamma = 0$

Step 2 — Surcharge:
$$q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 2 = 38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 3 — Gross ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = (50 \times 5.7) + (38 \times 1.0) + 0 = 285 + 38 = 323 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 4 — Net ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 323 - 38 = 285 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Step 1 — Applied pressure (gross) from the column load:
$$q_{\text{applied}} = \frac{Q}{B} = \frac{300}{3} = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Step 2 — Factor of safety against bearing failure (using gross values):
$$F = \frac{q_u}{q_{\text{applied}}} = \frac{323}{100} = 3.23$$

Step 3 — Equivalent verification using net values:
$$q_{\text{net applied}} = q_{\text{applied}} - \gamma D_f = 100 - 38 = 62 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$F = \frac{q_{nu}}{q_{\text{net applied}}} = \frac{285}{62} = 4.60$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Inconsistency:** The two calculations of $F$ differ because $F$ based on gross values is **not** the same as $F$ based on net values when surcharge is significant. KTU examiners expect the **gross-based** factor of safety for bearing failure. Always state which convention you are using.

Step 4 — Final results:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross ultimate bearing capacity | $q_u$ | 323 | kN/m² |
| Net ultimate bearing capacity | $q_{nu}$ | 285 | kN/m² |
| Applied gross pressure | $q_{\text{applied}}$ | 100 | kN/m² |
| Factor of safety (gross basis) | $F$ | **3.23** | — |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ☐ **Terzaghi's strip footing equation**: $q_u = c N_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$ — the *single most important formula* of Module 2.
>
> ☐ **Three failure zones**: Active wedge (triangular) + Radial shear (log-spiral) + Passive wedge (triangular) — always reproduce in the order asked.
>
> ☐ **Three bearing capacity factors** depend *only* on $\phi$ — they are *not* properties of the soil itself, but parameters derived from Mohr-Coulomb failure theory.
>
> ☐ **Assumptions to memorize** (eight total) — particularly: rough base, plane-strain, general shear, surcharge replaces soil above base.
>
> ☐ **Net vs Gross**: subtract $\gamma D_f$ to go from gross to net for *all* four quantities (ultimate, safe, etc.).
>
> ☐ **Factor of safety** in KTU problems is typically 2.5 to 3.5 — always state which value is used.
>
> ☐ **For purely cohesive soil** ($\phi = 0$): $N_c = 5.7$, $N_q = 1$, $N_\gamma = 0$ — this gives the elegant form $q_u = 5.7c + \gamma D_f$.
>
> ☐ **For purely cohesionless soil** ($c = 0$): $N_c$ term vanishes, leaving $q_u = \gamma D_f N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$.
>
> ☐ **The factor $N_\gamma$** uses Terzaghi's empirical form $\frac{1}{2}(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ — different from Meyerhof/Hansen's forms, *do not mix*.
>
> ☐ **Load per metre run** for a strip footing = $q_u \times B$ (since $L = 1$ m run is implicit in plane-strain analysis).
>
> ☐ **KTU marks split** (typical 14-mark question): 2 marks for stating assumptions / sketching failure zones, 4 marks for bearing capacity factors, 5 marks for $q_u$ computation, 3 marks for $q_{ns}$ / $Q_s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terzaghi's Formulae for Circular and Square Footings - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Terzaghi's Formulae for Circular and Square Footings - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory** is the classical analytical framework used to compute the **ultimate bearing capacity (q_u)** of shallow foundations resting on a cohesionless, c-φ soil mass. The general form of Terzaghi's equation is:

$$q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma$$

where **B** is the width of the footing, **q = γ·D_f** is the surcharge at the base level, and **N_c, N_q, N_γ** are Terzaghi's **dimensionless bearing capacity factors** that depend solely on the soil's angle of internal friction **φ**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The original equation above is **strictly valid only for a long strip footing (L/B → ∞, plane strain case)**. For **square (B × B)** and **circular (B = diameter)** footings, the geometry of the failure zone changes, and **shape correction factors** must be applied. This is one of the **highest-weightage topics** in KTU End Semester Examinations for Module 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plunger in Honey"

Imagine pressing a flat, round plate (a **circular footing**) versus a long, thin ruler (a **strip footing**) into a tub of thick honey.

- The **strip footing** is like sliding a wall of cardboard sideways — honey escapes in only **two directions** (along the length of the wall), so resistance is high.
- The **circular footing** is like pushing a round plunger — honey escapes in **all radial directions**, so resistance is comparatively lower.
- The **square footing** lies in between.

This physical intuition explains why the **N_γ term is reduced** for square (factor **0.8**) and circular (factor **0.6**) footings compared to a strip (factor **1.0**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** As the shape becomes more compact (square → circular), the soil mass has more "escape routes" and offers slightly lower bearing capacity per unit area than an equivalent strip.

### 1.3 Standard Symbols and Units (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| q_u | Ultimate bearing capacity | kN/m² |
| q_safe | Safe/Net safe bearing capacity | kN/m² |
| c | Cohesion of soil | kN/m² |
| φ | Angle of internal friction | degrees (°) |
| γ | Unit weight of soil | kN/m³ |
| B | Width / Diameter of footing | m |
| D_f | Depth of foundation | m |
| FS | Factor of Safety | dimensionless |
| N_c, N_q, N_γ | Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Terzaghi's Modification

Terzaghi assumed three distinct failure-zone geometries beneath the footing:

1. **Triangular wedge** immediately below the base (elastic wedge).
2. **Radial shear zone** that curves outward.
3. **Passive Rankine zone** that pushes upward at the surface.

For a **strip footing (B × L, L → ∞)**, the failure surface is a **2D plane-strain wedge**. For **square** and **circular** footings, the failure becomes **3D axisymmetric / symmetric**, and Terzaghi introduced **empirical shape factors** to scale down the strip equation. The logic is summarized below:

- The **cohesion term** (cN_c) is enhanced by a factor **1.3** for both square and circular footings (3D confinement increases cohesion-driven resistance).
- The **surcharge term** (qN_q) is left **unchanged** (factor = 1.0) because surcharge acts vertically and is not significantly affected by shape.
- The **self-weight term** (0.5γBN_γ) is **reduced** — by a factor of **0.8** for square and **0.6** for circular, since the mobilized soil volume shrinks with compactness.

### 2.2 The Three Governing Equations

| Footing Type | Terzaghi's Ultimate Bearing Capacity Equation | Shape Factors Used |
|--------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| **Strip** (L/B ≥ 5) | q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5γBN_γ | s_c = 1.0, s_q = 1.0, s_γ = 1.0 |
| **Square** (B × B) | q_u = **1.3**cN_c + qN_q + **0.4**γBN_γ | s_c = 1.3, s_q = 1.0, s_γ = 0.8 |
| **Circular** (B = D) | q_u = **1.3**cN_c + qN_q + **0.3**γBN_γ | s_c = 1.3, s_q = 1.0, s_γ = 0.6 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|-------------|------------|------------------|
| (1) | q = γ · D_f | Effective surcharge at footing base |
| (2) | q_u (Square) = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4γBN_γ | Ultimate BC of square footing |
| (3) | q_u (Circular) = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3γBN_γ | Ultimate BC of circular footing |
| (4) | q_safe = q_u / FS | Safe bearing capacity (Gross) |
| (5) | q_nsafe = (q_u - γD_f) / FS | Net safe bearing capacity |
| (6) | Q_allow = q_safe × A | Allowable load on footing (kN) |
| (7) | A = B² (Square) or A = (π/4)B² (Circular) | Footing area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Terzaghi's N-factors are NOT the same as Meyerhof's, Vesic's, or Hansen's.** KTU 2024 Module 2 strictly mandates Terzaghi's original values, so always use the table below and never substitute factors from other theories.

### 2.4 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors (Reference Table)

| φ (°) | N_c | N_q | N_γ |
|-------|-----|-----|------|
| 0 | 5.7 | 1.0 | 0.0 |
| 5 | 7.3 | 1.6 | 0.5 |
| 10 | 9.6 | 2.7 | 1.2 |
| 15 | 12.9 | 4.4 | 2.5 |
| 20 | 17.7 | 7.4 | 5.0 |
| 25 | 25.1 | 12.7 | 9.7 |
| 30 | 37.2 | 22.5 | 19.7 |
| 35 | 57.8 | 41.4 | 42.4 |
| 40 | 95.7 | 81.3 | 100.4 |
| 45 | 172.3 | 173.3 | 297.5 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Square footings** are extensively used for isolated columns of framed buildings, bridge piers, and tower bases.
- **Circular footings** are the standard choice for **circular silos, water tanks, transmission towers, chimneys, and wind turbine bases**.
- A rigorous design engineer must compute **q_u** for the specific shape, apply **FS = 3** (standard per IS 6403), and check against the **net loading** from the superstructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Numerical Problems)

### 3.1 Derivation: From Strip to Square / Circular Equations

The general 3-term equation is:

$$q_u = c \cdot s_c \cdot N_c + q \cdot s_q \cdot N_q + 0.5\gamma B \cdot s_\gamma \cdot N_\gamma$$

Substituting Terzaghi's empirical shape factors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Square Footing: } q_u &= (c)(1.3)N_c + (q)(1.0)N_q + (0.5\gamma B)(0.8)N_\gamma \\
&= 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma BN_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For Circular Footing: } q_u &= (c)(1.3)N_c + (q)(1.0)N_q + (0.5\gamma B)(0.6)N_\gamma \\
&= 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\gamma BN_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the **two master equations** you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE.

---

### 3.2 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 1 — Square Footing (KTU Standard)

**Given:** A square footing of size **B = 2.0 m** is placed at depth **D_f = 1.5 m** in a soil with **c = 20 kN/m²**, **φ = 25°**, and **γ = 18 kN/m³**. Find the **ultimate and safe bearing capacity**. Take **FS = 3**.

**Step 1 — Identify the equation:**

$$q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma BN_\gamma \quad \text{(Square Footing)}$$

**Step 2 — Look up Terzaghi's factors for φ = 25°:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 25.1 \\
N_q &= 12.7 \\
N_\gamma &= 9.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the surcharge (q):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \gamma \cdot D_f \\
&= 18 \times 1.5 \\
&= 27 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term separately:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1: Cohesion term} &= 1.3 \times 20 \times 25.1 \\
&= 1.3 \times 502 \\
&= 652.60 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 2: Surcharge term} &= 27 \times 12.7 \\
&= 342.90 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 3: Self-weight term} &= 0.4 \times 18 \times 2.0 \times 9.7 \\
&= 0.4 \times 349.2 \\
&= 139.68 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the three terms to get q_u:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 652.60 + 342.90 + 139.68 \\
&= 1135.18 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply factor of safety to obtain q_safe:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{safe} &= \frac{q_u}{FS} \\
&= \frac{1135.18}{3} \\
&= 378.39 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the allowable column load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= B^2 = 2.0^2 = 4.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{allow} &= q_{safe} \times A \\
&= 378.39 \times 4.0 \\
&= 1513.56 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** q_u = **1135.18 kN/m²**, q_safe = **378.39 kN/m²**, Q_allow ≈ **1513.56 kN**.

---

### 3.3 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 2 — Circular Footing (KTU Standard)

**Given:** A circular footing of diameter **B = 3.0 m** is founded at **D_f = 2.0 m** in a soil with **c = 30 kN/m²**, **φ = 30°**, **γ = 19 kN/m³**. Determine the **ultimate bearing capacity and safe load**. Take **FS = 3**.

**Step 1 — Identify the equation:**

$$q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\gamma BN_\gamma \quad \text{(Circular Footing)}$$

**Step 2 — Look up Terzaghi's factors for φ = 30°:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 37.2 \\
N_q &= 22.5 \\
N_\gamma &= 19.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the surcharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \gamma \cdot D_f = 19 \times 2.0 = 38 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1: Cohesion term} &= 1.3 \times 30 \times 37.2 \\
&= 1.3 \times 1116 \\
&= 1450.80 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 2: Surcharge term} &= 38 \times 22.5 \\
&= 855.00 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 3: Self-weight term} &= 0.3 \times 19 \times 3.0 \times 19.7 \\
&= 0.3 \times 1122.9 \\
&= 336.87 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute q_u:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1450.80 + 855.00 + 336.87 \\
&= 2642.67 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute q_safe and Q_allow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{safe} &= \frac{2642.67}{3} = 880.89 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\[4pt]
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(3.0)^2 = 7.069 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
Q_{allow} &= 880.89 \times 7.069 \\
&= 6227.0 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** q_u = **2642.67 kN/m²**, q_safe = **880.89 kN/m²**, Q_allow ≈ **6227 kN**.

---

### 3.4 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 3 — Comparison Problem (Advanced KTU Pattern)

**Given:** Two footings are placed at the same depth **D_f = 1.0 m** on the same soil (**c = 10 kN/m², φ = 20°, γ = 17 kN/m³**). One is a **strip footing of width B = 2.0 m**, the other is a **square footing B = 2.0 m**. Compare the ultimate bearing capacities. Comment on the result.

**Step 1 — Look up N-factors for φ = 20°:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 17.7, \quad N_q = 7.4, \quad N_\gamma = 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Surcharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q = 17 \times 1.0 = 17 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Strip Footing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{u,\text{strip}} &= cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma \\
&= (10)(17.7) + (17)(7.4) + 0.5(17)(2.0)(5.0) \\
&= 177.0 + 125.8 + 85.0 \\
&= 387.80 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Square Footing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{u,\text{square}} &= 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma BN_\gamma \\
&= 1.3(10)(17.7) + (17)(7.4) + 0.4(17)(2.0)(5.0) \\
&= 230.10 + 125.80 + 68.00 \\
&= 423.90 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{q_{u,\text{square}}}{q_{u,\text{strip}}} &= \frac{423.90}{387.80} = 1.093 \quad (\approx 9.3\% \text{ higher})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** The square footing exhibits a **higher ultimate bearing capacity** than an equivalent strip footing due to the **3D confinement effect**, captured by the cohesion shape factor **s_c = 1.3**. This is a classic conceptual KTU question that tests both calculation skill and physical reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input B, Df, c, phi, gamma, FS] --> B[Compute Surcharge q = gamma x Df]
    B --> C[Identify Footing Shape]
    C --> C1[Strip Footing]
    C --> C2[Square Footing]
    C --> C3[Circular Footing]
    C1 --> D[Use Terzaghi Nc, Nq, Ngamma from Table for given phi]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Apply Shape Factors]
    E --> E1[Strip: sc=1.0, sq=1.0, sgamma=1.0]
    E --> E2[Square: sc=1.3, sq=1.0, sgamma=0.8]
    E --> E3[Circular: sc=1.3, sq=1.0, sgamma=0.6]
    E1 --> F[Compute qu via 3-term equation]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Compute qsafe = qu / FS]
    G --> H[Compute Qallow = qsafe x Area]
    H --> I[End: Report qu, qsafe, Qallow]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Failure Zone Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Strip Footing 2D Failure]
        A1[Elastic Wedge] --> A2[Radial Shear Zone] --> A3[Passive Rankine Zone]
    end
    subgraph B[Square Footing 3D Failure]
        B1[Central Wedge] --> B2[Curved Radial Shear] --> B3[Corner Passive Zones]
    end
    subgraph C[Circular Footing Axisymmetric Failure]
        C1[Central Cone Wedge] --> C2[Log Spiral Shear] --> C3[Annular Passive Lift]
    end
    A --- B --- C
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Variation of N_γ with φ

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[phi = 0 deg: Ngamma = 0.0] --> P2[phi = 15 deg: Ngamma = 2.5]
    P2 --> P3[phi = 25 deg: Ngamma = 9.7]
    P3 --> P4[phi = 30 deg: Ngamma = 19.7]
    P4 --> P5[phi = 40 deg: Ngamma = 100.4]
    P5 --> P6[phi = 45 deg: Ngamma = 297.5]
    P6 --> P7[phi = 50 deg: Ngamma = 1153.2]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** N_γ grows **exponentially** with φ. A small increase in friction angle causes a massive increase in bearing capacity — this is why sandy soils (high φ) are excellent foundation materials.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

State Terzaghi's ultimate bearing capacity equations for:
- (a) a square footing
- (b) a circular footing

Explain the physical significance of the shape factors used.

**Model Answer:**

For a square footing:

$$q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma BN_\gamma$$

For a circular footing:

$$q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\gamma BN_\gamma$$

**Physical significance of shape factors:**

- **s_c = 1.3** (cohesion term): In a 3D failure (square/circular), the soil is laterally confined on all sides, increasing the cohesion-driven resistance compared to the 2D strip case.
- **s_q = 1.0** (surcharge term): The surcharge q = γD_f acts vertically; its contribution is not affected by footing shape.
- **s_γ = 0.8 (square) or 0.6 (circular)** (self-weight term): The volume of soil participating in shear failure is smaller for compact footings, reducing the self-weight contribution to bearing capacity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A circular footing of 2.5 m diameter is founded at 1.5 m depth in a soil with c = 15 kN/m², φ = 20°, and γ = 18 kN/m³. Using Terzaghi's theory, calculate the ultimate bearing capacity.

**Model Answer:**

For φ = 20°: N_c = 17.7, N_q = 7.4, N_γ = 5.0

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
q_u &= 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3\gamma BN_\gamma \\
&= 1.3(15)(17.7) + (27)(7.4) + 0.3(18)(2.5)(5.0) \\
&= 345.15 + 199.80 + 67.50 \\
&= 612.45 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ q_u = 612.45 kN/m²**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

(a) **7 Marks:** A square footing of size **3 m × 3 m** rests on a saturated clay (φ_u = 0, c_u = 40 kN/m²) at a depth of **2.0 m**. The water table is at the ground surface. Compute the ultimate and safe bearing capacity. Use FS = 3. Submerged unit weight **γ' = 9.81 kN/m³**.

(b) **7 Marks:** A circular footing of diameter **2.0 m** is placed on a sandy soil with **c = 0, φ = 35°**, **γ = 19 kN/m³**, and **D_f = 1.5 m**. Find the safe load the footing can carry. Use FS = 3.

---

### 5.3 MODEL SOLUTION — Question 3(A)

**Solution to (a) — Square Footing on Clay:**

For φ_u = 0: N_c = 5.7, N_q = 1.0, N_γ = 0.0 (water table at ground surface)

Since water table is at GL, use **submerged unit weight** for the surcharge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \gamma' \cdot D_f = 9.81 \times 2.0 = 19.62 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1.3 c_u N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma' B N_\gamma \\
&= 1.3(40)(5.7) + (19.62)(1.0) + 0.4(9.81)(3.0)(0.0) \\
&= 296.40 + 19.62 + 0 \\
&= 316.02 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{safe} &= \frac{316.02}{3} = 105.34 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Solution to (b) — Circular Footing on Sand:**

For φ = 35°: N_c = 57.8, N_q = 41.4, N_γ = 42.4. Since c = 0, cohesion term vanishes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= 19 \times 1.5 = 28.5 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
q_u &= 1.3(0)(57.8) + (28.5)(41.4) + 0.3(19)(2.0)(42.4) \\
&= 0 + 1179.90 + 483.36 \\
&= 1663.26 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{safe} &= \frac{1663.26}{3} = 554.42 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
A &= \frac{\pi}{4}(2.0)^2 = 3.142 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{allow} &= 554.42 \times 3.142 = 1742.0 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ The footing can safely carry ≈ 1742 kN.**

---

### 5.4 MODEL SOLUTION — Question 3(B) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q3 (B). (a) 7 Marks:** Derive the expressions for ultimate bearing capacity of a **square footing** from Terzaghi's general equation using shape factors.

**Model Answer:**

Terzaghi's general (3D-corrected) equation is:

$$q_u = c \cdot s_c N_c + q \cdot s_q N_q + 0.5\gamma B \cdot s_\gamma N_\gamma$$

For a square footing, Terzaghi's empirical shape factors are:
- s_c = 1.3
- s_q = 1.0
- s_γ = 0.8

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= (c)(1.3)(N_c) + (q)(1.0)(N_q) + (0.5\gamma B)(0.8)(N_\gamma) \\
q_u &= 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4\gamma BN_\gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q3 (B). (b) 7 Marks:** A square footing of **2.5 m × 2.5 m** is founded at **1.2 m** depth in soil with **c = 25 kN/m², φ = 30°, γ = 18 kN/m³**. Determine the **net safe bearing pressure** and the **allowable column load**. Take FS = 3.

**Model Solution:**

For φ = 30°: N_c = 37.2, N_q = 22.5, N_γ = 19.7

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= 18 \times 1.2 = 21.6 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
q_u &= 1.3(25)(37.2) + (21.6)(22.5) + 0.4(18)(2.5)(19.7) \\
&= 1209.00 + 486.00 + 354.60 \\
&= 2049.60 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{nsafe} &= \frac{q_u - \gamma D_f}{FS} = \frac{2049.60 - 21.6}{3} = \frac{2028.0}{3} = 676.0 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\[4pt]
A &= (2.5)^2 = 6.25 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{allow} &= 676.0 \times 6.25 = 4225 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Net safe bearing pressure = 676 kN/m², Allowable load = 4225 kN.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Mixing up Meyerhof / Vesic factors with Terzaghi's:** Always use the Terzaghi-specific table provided in SECTION 2.4. KTU strictly expects Terzaghi's values, NOT the general (Meyerhof) values which give higher N_c and N_q.
> 2. **Forgetting the shape factor on N_γ:** Students often write 0.5γBN_γ for square/circular footings. The correct value is **0.4** (square) and **0.3** (circular). A 1-mark penalty applies.
> 3. **Using γ instead of γ' below the water table:** If the water table is at or above the footing base, **submerged unit weight γ' = γ_sat − γ_w** must be used in the N_γ term (and surcharge term if water table is above base).
> 4. **Confusing gross vs. net safe bearing capacity:** q_safe is **gross**, q_nsafe is **net** (subtract γD_f). Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Not showing the three-term breakdown:** Always write the three terms (cohesion, surcharge, self-weight) separately before summing. Examiners reward a clear structure.

### 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Master equation (Square):** q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.4γBN_γ
- **Master equation (Circular):** q_u = 1.3cN_c + qN_q + 0.3γBN_γ
- **Surcharge:** q = γ · D_f (use γ' if water table at/above base)
- **Shape factors (Terzaghi):** s_c = 1.3, s_q = 1.0, s_γ = 0.8 (square); s_γ = 0.6 (circular)
- **N-factor table** is the most frequently tested reference; memorize values at φ = 0°, 20°, 25°, 30°, 35°, 40°.
- **q_safe = q_u / 3** (FS = 3 for shallow foundations per IS 6403)
- **Q_allow = q_safe × A**, where A = B² (square) or A = (π/4)B² (circular)
- For **clay (φ = 0)**: N_γ = 0 → the entire self-weight term vanishes.
- For **sand (c = 0)**: the entire cohesion term vanishes; only N_q and N_γ terms contribute.
- **Always show the three-term breakdown** in numerical solutions.
- **Net safe BC** = (q_u − γD_f) / FS, used when **structural load = soil weight** of excavated soil.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity - Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity - Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Factors Affecting Bearing Capacity

The **bearing capacity of soil** is the maximum load per unit area that the soil can support without undergoing shear failure or excessive settlement. Several factors influence this capacity, which can be broadly classified into soil-related factors and foundation-related factors.

### 1.1.1 Soil-Related Factors

| Factor | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Cohesion (c)** | Shear strength from inter-particle bonding in cohesive soils |
| **Angle of Internal Friction (φ)** | Frictional resistance in cohesionless soils |
| **Unit Weight (γ)** | Influences overburden pressure and failure zone geometry |
| **Density Index (ID)** | Relative density affects φ and stiffness |
| **Water Table Position** | Reduces effective stress and shear strength |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Water table position is one of the most frequently tested factors in numerical problems, as it directly modifies the unit weight, surcharge, and shear strength parameters.

### 1.1.2 Foundation-Related Factors

| Factor | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Width of Foundation (B)** | Larger B → higher bearing capacity |
| **Depth of Foundation (Df)** | Deeper foundations gain surcharge benefit |
| **Shape of Foundation** | Strip, square, circular → shape factors |
| **Type of Soil (Homogeneous/Stratified)** | Failure mechanism changes with stratification |
| **Load Inclination & Eccentricity** | Inclined/eccentric loads reduce capacity |

---

## 1.2 Effect of Water Table on Bearing Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** The water table influences bearing capacity by reducing the **effective overburden pressure**, modifying the **unit weight of soil** (from bulk to submerged), and potentially introducing **seepage forces** that destabilize the foundation.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine placing a heavy book on a **wet sponge** versus a **dry sponge**:

- **Dry sponge** resists compression strongly → high bearing capacity.
- **Wet sponge** yields easily because water reduces internal friction and effective stress.

Similarly, when the water table rises near a foundation:
- Soil grains become "buoyant" (submerged condition).
- Effective stress drops → soil loses shear strength.
- Bearing capacity **decreases**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Water Table Positions

The water table can occupy **three critical positions** relative to the foundation:

| Position | Symbol | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Above Base of Foundation** | Case I | Water table between ground surface and base of footing |
| **At Base of Foundation** | Case II | Water table coincides with footing base (B/2 zone relevant) |
| **Below Base of Foundation** | Case III | Water table lies between B and (Df + B) below ground |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Depth Rule:** The water table influences the Nγ term only if it lies within a depth of **B** below the foundation base. The Nq term is affected if the water table lies within a depth of **Df** above the base.

---

## 1.4 Water Table Correction Factors

Three correction factors are introduced to modify Terzaghi's/Meyerhof's equation:

| Factor | Applied To | Purpose |
|--------|------------|---------|
| **W'** | Nq term (surcharge) | Accounts for reduced effective surcharge |
| **W"** | Nc term (cohesion) | Minor correction (usually neglected) |
| **Wq** | Nγ term (self-weight) | Accounts for submerged unit weight in failure zone |

> [!NOTE]
> The W' factor is applied to the Nq term, and Wq is applied to the Nγ term. The Nc term is generally **not affected** by water table position (IS Code recommendation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation (Foundation Reference)

Terzaghi (1943) proposed the following general bearing capacity equation for a strip footing:

$$q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$$

Where:
- $q_u$ = Ultimate bearing capacity (kN/m²)
- $c$ = Cohesion of soil (kN/m²)
- $q$ = Overburden pressure = $\gamma D_f$ (kN/m²)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil (kN/m³)
- $B$ = Width of foundation (m)
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (functions of φ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** While Terzaghi's equation is the conceptual base, the **IS Code (IS 6403:1981, reaffirmed 2002)** is the **mandatory method** for KTU numerical problems. Always check the question for the specified method.

---

## 2.2 IS Code Bearing Capacity Equation

As per **IS 6403:1981**, the ultimate bearing capacity is:

$$q_u = cN_c S_c d_c + q(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma$$

The **net safe bearing capacity** is given by:

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_u - \gamma D_f}{F} + \gamma D_f$$

Where $F$ = Factor of Safety (typically 3.0 for IS Code).

### 2.2.1 Water Table Modifications (IS Code)

When the water table is present, the IS Code modifies the equation as:

$$q_u = cN_c S_c d_c + q'(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5\gamma' B N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma$$

Where:
- $q' = \overline{\sigma}$ = Effective surcharge considering water table
- $\gamma'$ = Submerged unit weight (γsub) in failure zone
- $W_q$ and $W_\gamma$ may be applied as alternatives

---

## 2.3 Detailed Water Table Correction Factors

### 2.3.1 Case I: Water Table Above Base of Foundation (Between GS and Df)

Let $Z_{w1}$ = depth of water table below ground surface (m).

**Modified surcharge (q'):**

$$q' = \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w)$$

**Submerged unit weight in failure zone:**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

> If water table is at ground surface: $q' = \gamma' D_f$

### 2.3.2 Case II: Water Table At Base of Foundation

**Surcharge term:**

$$q' = \gamma D_f - (D_f)(\gamma - \gamma_w) = \gamma_w D_f + \gamma' D_f$$

If soil above WT is saturated: $q' = \gamma' D_f$

**Failure zone unit weight:**

$$\gamma' = \gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$$

### 2.3.3 Case III: Water Table Below Base of Foundation

Let $Z_{w2}$ = depth of water table below base of foundation (m).

**Surcharge term is unaffected:**

$$q' = \gamma D_f$$

**Failure zone unit weight (using interpolation):**

If $Z_{w2} \leq B$:

$$\gamma_{avg} = \gamma' + \left(\frac{Z_{w2}}{B}\right)(\gamma - \gamma')$$

If $Z_{w2} > B$:

$$\gamma_{avg} = \gamma \text{ (full unit weight applies)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Convention:** When $Z_{w2} \geq B$, water table has **no effect** on Nγ term. When $Z_{w2} = 0$, use **fully submerged** unit weight.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma B N_\gamma$ | Terzaghi's UBC (Strip) | kN/m² |
| 2 | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ | Nq factor | — |
| 3 | $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | Nc factor | — |
| 4 | $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | Terzaghi's Nγ | — |
| 5 | $q_{ns} = (q_u - \gamma D_f)/F + \gamma D_f$ | Net Safe BC | kN/m² |
| 6 | $S_c = 1 + 0.2(B/L)$ | Shape factor (IS) | — |
| 7 | $S_q = 1 + 0.2(B/L)$ | Shape factor (IS) | — |
| 8 | $S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(B/L)$ | Shape factor (IS) | — |
| 9 | $d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B)$ | Depth factor (IS, for Df/B ≤ 1) | — |
| 10 | $d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B)$ | Depth factor (IS) | — |
| 11 | $d_\gamma = 1.0$ | Depth factor (IS) | — |
| 12 | $\gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Submerged unit weight | kN/m³ |
| 13 | $\gamma_w = 9.81 \approx 10$ kN/m³ | Unit weight of water | kN/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** For KTU 2024, if the question mentions "strip footing" without specifying IS Code, use Terzaghi's factors. If "IS Code method" is mentioned, use Skempton's $N_c$ and IS Code shape/depth factors.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The water table correction is **essential** in:
- **Coastal regions** (Kerala backwaters, Mumbai coastal line)
- **Floodplain foundations** (Kuttanad, Kole lands of Kerala)
- **High-rise basement construction** (Dewatering design)
- **Tank farm / reservoir adjacent structures**
- **Bridge piers in rivers** (monsoon water table rise)
- **Underground metro projects** (seepage and uplift)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Modified Surcharge (q') for Water Table Correction

### Setup

Consider a strip footing of width $B$ at depth $D_f$. The water table is at depth $Z_{w1}$ below the ground surface, where $0 \leq Z_{w1} \leq D_f$.

The soil above the water table has unit weight $\gamma$ (moist/dry), and below the water table has saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat}$.

### Derivation

The total overburden pressure at the base of the foundation is:

$$q = \gamma Z_{w1} + \gamma_{sat}(D_f - Z_{w1})$$

The pore water pressure at the base is:

$$u = \gamma_w(D_f - Z_{w1})$$

The **effective overburden pressure** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q' &= q - u \\
q' &= \gamma Z_{w1} + \gamma_{sat}(D_f - Z_{w1}) - \gamma_w(D_f - Z_{w1}) \\
q' &= \gamma Z_{w1} + (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w)(D_f - Z_{w1}) \\
q' &= \gamma Z_{w1} + \gamma_{sub}(D_f - Z_{w1})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Alternative Compact Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
q' &= \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) - (D_f - Z_{w1})\gamma_w + (D_f - Z_{w1})\gamma_{sat} - \gamma Z_{w1} + \gamma Z_{w1} \\
q' &= \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{q' = \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w)}$$

> This is the **standard IS Code formula** when the soil above the water table has unit weight $\gamma$ and below has $\gamma_{sat}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #1 (KTU Standard Pattern)

### Problem Statement
A strip footing of width 2 m is founded at a depth of 1.5 m in a soil with cohesion $c = 15$ kN/m², angle of internal friction $\phi = 30°$, unit weight $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, and saturated unit weight $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. The water table is at a depth of 1 m below the ground surface. Compute the **net ultimate bearing capacity** using **IS Code method** (Strip footing, FOS = 3).

### Step 1: Identify Bearing Capacity Factors (Skempton / IS Code)

For $\phi = 30°$:
- $N_c = 30.14$ (Skempton's value for $\phi \geq 29°$, constant at 30.14)
- $N_q = 18.40$
- $N_\gamma = 22.40$ (IS Code: $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$)

Verify: $N_\gamma = 2(18.40 + 1)\tan 30° = 2(19.40)(0.5774) = 22.40$ ✓

### Step 2: Determine Water Table Position

- $D_f = 1.5$ m
- $Z_{w1} = 1$ m (water table above base of footing)
- Since $Z_{w1} < D_f$, we are in **Case I**

### Step 3: Compute Effective Surcharge (q')

$$
\begin{aligned}
q' &= \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w) \\
q' &= (18)(1.5) - (1.5 - 1)(18 - 10) \\
q' &= 27 - (0.5)(8) \\
q' &= 27 - 4 = 23 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Submerged Unit Weight

$$\gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

### Step 5: Apply Shape and Depth Factors (Strip Footing: B/L = 0)

For strip footing: $S_c = S_q = S_\gamma = 1.0$

Depth factors (IS Code):
- $d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B) = 1 + 0.2(1.5/2) = 1 + 0.15 = 1.15$
- $d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B) = 1 + 0.1(1.5/2) = 1 + 0.075 = 1.075$
- $d_\gamma = 1.0$

### Step 6: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= cN_c S_c d_c + q'(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5\gamma_{sub} B N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma \\
q_u &= (15)(30.14)(1)(1.15) + (23)(18.40 - 1)(1)(1.075) + (0.5)(10)(2)(22.40)(1)(1) \\
q_u &= (15)(30.14)(1.15) + (23)(17.40)(1.075) + (10)(22.40) \\
q_u &= 519.92 + 430.15 + 224.0 \\
q_u &= 1174.07 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Compute Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1174.07 - (18)(1.5) = 1174.07 - 27 = 1147.07 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 8: Compute Net Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f = \frac{1147.07}{3} + 27 = 382.36 + 27 = 409.36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Answer:** $q_{ns} \approx 409.36$ kN/m²

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #2 (Water Table at Base)

### Problem Statement
A square footing of size 2 m × 2 m is placed at depth 1.5 m in sandy soil with $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 20.5$ kN/m³, and $c = 0$. The water table is at the **base of the footing**. Calculate the **net safe bearing capacity** using IS Code method. Take FOS = 3.

### Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors (IS Code)

For $\phi = 35°$:
- $N_c = 46.12$ (Skempton's value, constant ≥ 29°)
- $N_q = 33.30$
- $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi = 2(34.30)\tan 35° = 2(34.30)(0.7002) = 48.03$

### Step 2: Water Table Position

- $Z_{w1} = D_f = 1.5$ m → Water table exactly at base
- $\gamma' = \gamma_{sub} = 20.5 - 10 = 10.5$ kN/m³

### Step 3: Effective Surcharge (q')

$$
\begin{aligned}
q' &= \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w) \\
q' &= (19)(1.5) - 0 = 28.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Shape Factors (Square: B/L = 1)

- $S_c = 1 + 0.2(1) = 1.2$
- $S_q = 1 + 0.2(1) = 1.2$
- $S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(1) = 0.6$

### Step 5: Depth Factors

- $d_c = 1 + 0.2(1.5/2) = 1.15$
- $d_q = 1 + 0.1(1.5/2) = 1.075$
- $d_\gamma = 1.0$

### Step 6: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= cN_c S_c d_c + q'(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5\gamma' B N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma \\
q_u &= 0 + (28.5)(33.30 - 1)(1.2)(1.075) + (0.5)(10.5)(2)(48.03)(0.6)(1) \\
q_u &= (28.5)(32.30)(1.290) + (10.5)(48.03)(0.6) \\
q_u &= 1187.6 + 302.6 \\
q_u &= 1490.2 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Net Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_{ns} = \frac{1490.2 - 28.5}{3} + 28.5 = \frac{1461.7}{3} + 28.5 = 487.23 + 28.5 = 515.73 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Answer:** $q_{ns} \approx 515.73$ kN/m²

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem #3 (Water Table Below Base)

### Problem Statement
A strip footing of width 3 m is at depth 1.5 m in soil with $\phi = 30°$, $c = 10$ kN/m², $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³. The water table is at **1 m below the base of the footing**. Find the **net ultimate bearing capacity** using IS Code (FOS not required for this problem).

### Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors

For $\phi = 30°$: $N_c = 30.14$, $N_q = 18.40$, $N_\gamma = 22.40$

### Step 2: Water Table Position Analysis

- $Z_{w1} > D_f$ (water table is below base of footing)
- $Z_{w2} = 1$ m (depth below base)
- $B = 3$ m
- Since $Z_{w2} = 1 < B = 3$ m, **interpolation required**

### Step 3: Effective Surcharge (q')

Since water table is below base, $q' = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27$ kN/m²

### Step 4: Average Unit Weight in Failure Zone

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{avg} &= \gamma' + \frac{Z_{w2}}{B}(\gamma - \gamma') \\
\gamma_{avg} &= 10 + \frac{1}{3}(18 - 10) \\
\gamma_{avg} &= 10 + 2.667 = 12.667 \text{ kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: For Strip Footing, S = 1, d_factors as before

$d_c = 1 + 0.2(1.5/3) = 1.10$, $d_q = 1 + 0.1(1.5/3) = 1.05$, $d_\gamma = 1.0$

### Step 6: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= cN_c d_c + q'(N_q - 1) d_q + 0.5\gamma_{avg} B N_\gamma d_\gamma \\
q_u &= (10)(30.14)(1.10) + (27)(17.40)(1.05) + (0.5)(12.667)(3)(22.40) \\
q_u &= 331.54 + 493.29 + 425.61 \\
q_u &= 1250.44 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = 1250.44 - 27 = 1223.44 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Answer:** $q_{nu} \approx 1223.44$ kN/m²

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List any three factors affecting the bearing capacity of soil.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cohesion (c) and Angle of Internal Friction (φ):** These are the primary shear strength parameters that directly appear in bearing capacity factors (Nc, Nq, Nγ). Higher c and φ result in higher bearing capacity.

2. **Unit Weight of Soil (γ):** Affects the overburden pressure (q = γDf) and the self-weight term (0.5γBNγ). Submerged unit weight (γ') is used when the water table is above the failure zone.

3. **Width and Depth of Foundation (B, Df):** Larger B increases capacity (Nγ term), while greater Df provides surcharge benefit (Nq term). Both are also limited by the water table influence zone.

> Other valid factors: Shape of foundation, type of soil (cohesive/cohesionless), load eccentricity, and ground water table position.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: How does the water table above the base of the foundation affect the bearing capacity?**

**Model Answer:**

When the water table lies between the ground surface and the base of the foundation:

1. The **effective overburden pressure (q') decreases** because the saturated soil above the water table contributes with reduced effective stress.

2. The **unit weight in the failure zone** becomes the submerged unit weight (γ' = γsat − γw), which is roughly half of the bulk unit weight.

3. The bearing capacity is **reduced** primarily through the Nq term (surcharge) and the Nγ term (self-weight).

4. The modified surcharge is given by: $q' = \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w)$

5. The Nγ term becomes: $0.5 \gamma' B N_\gamma$ instead of $0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A rectangular footing of size 2 m × 3 m (B × L) is founded at a depth of 1.5 m in a soil mass with the following properties:
- Cohesion, $c = 20$ kN/m²
- Angle of internal friction, $\phi = 25°$
- Unit weight above water table, $\gamma = 17.5$ kN/m³
- Saturated unit weight, $\gamma_{sat} = 19.5$ kN/m³

The water table is located at a depth of 1.0 m below the ground surface. Determine the **net safe bearing capacity** using the **IS Code method**. Adopt a factor of safety of 3.0.

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity

**Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors (φ = 25°)**

Using IS Code tables:
- $N_c = 25.13$
- $N_q = 12.72$
- $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi = 2(13.72)\tan 25° = 2(13.72)(0.4663) = 12.79$

**Step 2: Water Table Position Analysis**

$Z_{w1} = 1.0$ m, $D_f = 1.5$ m → Water table is **above base** (Case I)

**Step 3: Effective Surcharge (q')**

$$
q' = (17.5)(1.5) - (1.5 - 1.0)(17.5 - 10) = 26.25 - 3.75 = 22.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Submerged Unit Weight**

$$\gamma' = 19.5 - 10 = 9.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Shape Factors (B/L = 2/3 = 0.667)**

- $S_c = 1 + 0.2(0.667) = 1.133$
- $S_q = 1 + 0.2(0.667) = 1.133$
- $S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(0.667) = 0.733$

**Step 6: Depth Factors (Df/B = 1.5/2 = 0.75)**

- $d_c = 1 + 0.2(0.75) = 1.15$
- $d_q = 1 + 0.1(0.75) = 1.075$
- $d_\gamma = 1.0$

**Step 7: Compute qu**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= cN_c S_c d_c + q'(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5\gamma' B N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma \\
q_u &= (20)(25.13)(1.133)(1.15) + (22.5)(11.72)(1.133)(1.075) + (0.5)(9.5)(2)(12.79)(0.733)(1) \\
q_u &= 654.85 + 320.95 + 88.99 \\
q_u &= 1064.79 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Compute Net Safe Bearing Capacity

**Step 1: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1064.79 - (17.5)(1.5) = 1064.79 - 26.25 = 1038.54 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Net Safe Bearing Capacity (FOS = 3)**

$$
q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f = \frac{1038.54}{3} + 26.25 = 346.18 + 26.25 = 372.43 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $q_{ns} \approx 372.43$ kN/m²

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is placed at a depth of 2.0 m in a sandy soil stratum. The soil properties are:
- $\phi = 35°$, $c = 0$
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³

The water table is located at the **base of the foundation** (i.e., 2.0 m below ground surface). Determine the **net safe bearing capacity** using the IS Code method. Take FOS = 3.0.

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity

**Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors (φ = 35°)**

- $N_c = 46.12$ (Skempton, for $\phi \geq 29°$)
- $N_q = 33.30$
- $N_\gamma = 2(33.30 + 1)\tan 35° = 2(34.30)(0.7002) = 48.03$

**Step 2: Water Table Position**

$Z_{w1} = D_f = 2.0$ m → Water table **at base of footing**

**Step 3: Effective Surcharge**

$$q' = \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w) = (18)(2.0) - 0 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Submerged Unit Weight**

$$\gamma' = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Shape Factors (Square, B/L = 1)**

- $S_c = 1.2$, $S_q = 1.2$, $S_\gamma = 0.6$

**Step 6: Depth Factors (Df/B = 2.0/2.5 = 0.8)**

- $d_c = 1 + 0.2(0.8) = 1.16$
- $d_q = 1 + 0.1(0.8) = 1.08$
- $d_\gamma = 1.0$

**Step 7: Compute qu**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 0 + q'(N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5\gamma' B N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma \\
q_u &= (36)(32.30)(1.2)(1.08) + (0.5)(10)(2.5)(48.03)(0.6)(1) \\
q_u &= 1506.16 + 360.23 \\
q_u &= 1866.39 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Compute Net Safe Bearing Capacity

**Step 1: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity**

$$q_{nu} = 1866.39 - (18)(2.0) = 1866.39 - 36 = 1830.39 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Net Safe Bearing Capacity (FOS = 3)**

$$
q_{ns} = \frac{1830.39}{3} + 36 = 610.13 + 36 = 646.13 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $q_{ns} \approx 646.13$ kN/m²

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Most common error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to subtract $\gamma D_f$ in the net bearing capacity formula. Some students directly use $q_u/3$ which is **incorrect** for net safe BC.
>
> 2. **Second common error (–2 marks):** Using $\gamma_{sat}$ instead of $\gamma_{sub}$ in the failure zone. Always subtract $\gamma_w$ (10 kN/m³) when the failure zone is submerged.
>
> 3. **Third common error (–1 mark):** Applying the surcharge correction formula when the water table is below the base. **Correction is only needed if $Z_{w1} \leq D_f$**.
>
> 4. **Critical error (–3 marks):** Mixing Terzaghi factors with IS Code shape/depth factors. **Stay consistent with one method** throughout the problem.
>
> 5. **Significant digit error:** Always report answers to 2 decimal places for full credit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Three Water Table Cases:**
  - Case I: WT above base → Modified q' and γ' = γsub
  - Case II: WT at base → γ' = γsub, q' uses total weight if WT just at base
  - Case III: WT below base (Zw2 < B) → Interpolated unit weight

- ⭐ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $q' = \gamma D_f - (D_f - Z_{w1})(\gamma - \gamma_w)$ (surcharge correction)
  - $\gamma_{avg} = \gamma' + (Z_{w2}/B)(\gamma - \gamma')$ (interpolation for Nγ)
  - $\gamma_{sub} = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ (submerged unit weight)

- ⭐ **IS Code Bearing Capacity Factors (For φ = 30°, 35°, 40°):**
  - φ = 30°: Nc = 30.14, Nq = 18.40, Nγ = 22.40
  - φ = 35°: Nc = 46.12, Nq = 33.30, Nγ = 48.03
  - φ = 40°: Nc = 75.31, Nq = 64.20, Nγ = 109.41

- ⭐ **Shape & Depth Factors (IS Code):**
  - Strip: S = 1.0; Square: Sc = Sq = 1.2, Sγ = 0.6
  - Rectangular: $S_c = S_q = 1 + 0.2(B/L)$, $S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(B/L)$
  - Depth: $d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B)$, $d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B)$, $d_\gamma = 1.0$

- ⭐ **Net vs Gross:**
  - $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$
  - $q_{ns} = (q_{nu}/F) + \gamma D_f = (q_u/F) - \gamma D_f(1 - 1/F)$
  - Gross safe BC: $q_s = q_u/F$ (rarely used in KTU)

- ⭐ **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
  - Forgetting to apply shape factors for square/rectangular footings
  - Using γ instead of γ' in failure zone term
  - Not identifying water table case correctly
  - Misapplying Skempton's Nc (use 30.14 for φ ≥ 29°)

- ⭐ **Critical Rule:** Water table has **no effect** on Nγ term if it lies at depth ≥ B below the footing base. It has **no effect** on Nq term if it lies at depth ≥ Df below the ground surface (i.e., below the footing base).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction - Causes of Settlement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction - Causes of Settlement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Settlement in Geotechnical Engineering** is defined as the *vertical downward displacement* of a structure or soil mass caused by the application of structural loads, self-weight, or environmental changes that alter the stress state within the underlying soil strata. It represents the cumulative response of the soil skeleton to changes in effective stress.

In the context of **KTU Foundation Engineering (PCCVT503)**, settlement is a serviceability criterion that must be controlled within permissible limits (typically **65 mm for total settlement** and **40 mm for differential settlement** for isolated footings on clays, as per IS 1904 : 2021).

Mathematically, the total settlement $S_T$ of a foundation is expressed as the sum of three independent components:

$$S_T = S_i + S_c + S_s$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = **Immediate (Elastic) Settlement** — occurs almost instantly upon load application
- $S_c$ = **Consolidation (Primary) Settlement** — time-dependent in cohesive soils
- $S_s$ = **Secondary (Creep) Settlement** — long-term, viscous response of soil skeleton

## 1.2 Real-World Intuition & Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Loaded Sponge in a Bathtub"**
> Imagine placing a heavy book on a soft, water-soaked sponge sitting in a bathtub. The moment you place the book, the sponge compresses slightly (this is **immediate settlement**). Then, over the next few hours, water slowly squeezes out of the sponge pores, allowing it to compress even further (this is **consolidation settlement**). Days later, even after all water has drained, the sponge continues to slowly deform under the constant weight (this is **secondary creep settlement**). Foundations on real soil behave identically!

A second analogy: think of a multi-storey building as a **giant pressing its weight onto a layered mattress (the soil)**. The mattress compresses non-uniformly depending on the stiffness of each layer — stiffer sand layers resist quickly, while soft clay layers deform slowly over years.

## 1.3 Why is Settlement a Critical Design Concern?

| Phenomenon | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| Excessive **Total Settlement** | Tilting, loss of gradient in floors, plumbing failures |
| **Differential Settlement** | Cracking of walls, distortion of frames, jammed doors/windows |
| **Non-uniform Settlement** | Structural distress even when total settlement is acceptable |
| **Long-term Creep** | Progressive damage in clays, organic soils, fills |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Settlement is governed by IS 1904, IS 8009 (Part 1), and IS 1904 : 2021** — the primary KTU-referenced codes for permissible settlement limits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Settlement

Settlement is broadly classified based on **mechanism**, **soil type**, and **time-dependence**.

### A. Based on Mechanism
1. **Immediate Settlement ($S_i$)** — distortion without volume change
2. **Consolidation Settlement ($S_c$)** — volume change due to expulsion of pore water
3. **Secondary Settlement ($S_s$)** — plastic readjustment of soil grains

### B. Based on Soil Type Predominance
- **Sandy soils** → dominated by $S_i$ (immediate)
- **Clayey soils** → dominated by $S_c$ (consolidation)
- **Organic/Peat soils** → dominated by $S_s$ (secondary)
- **Mixed soils** → combined response

## 2.2 Detailed Causes of Settlement

### Cause 1: **Compression of Soil Mass Under Load (Primary Cause)**

When a foundation transfers load $Q$ to the soil, the stress increase propagates through the soil skeleton. The soil grains rearrange, voids reduce, and the mass compresses vertically.

**Why it happens:** Soil is a two-phase (or three-phase) system of solids + water + air. The applied stress is carried by the soil skeleton (effective stress) and pore water (pore pressure). As pore pressure dissipates, effective stress rises and grains pack closer.

**How it manifests:** Vertical strain $\varepsilon_z$ within the compressible layer.

### Cause 2: **Consolidation of Cohesive Soils**

In saturated clays, low permeability (typically $k = 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-9}$ cm/s) prevents rapid drainage. The applied load is initially carried by excess pore water pressure ($u$), which gradually transfers to the soil skeleton.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terzaghi's 1-D Consolidation Theory** (1925) is the foundation for predicting time-rate of consolidation settlement.

### Cause 3: **Elastic Compression of Coarse-Grained Soils**

Sands and gravels have high permeability, so excess pore pressure dissipates almost instantly. Settlement occurs due to **elastic distortion** of the grain skeleton, governed by elasticity theory (Schleicher, 1926; Steinbrenner, 1934; Fox, 1948).

### Cause 4: **Secondary Compression (Creep)**

Under sustained load, clay particles undergo viscous reorientation, plastic flow around grain contacts, and adjustment of inter-particle bonds. This is most significant in **organic soils, peats, and sensitive clays**.

### Cause 5: **Lowering of Ground Water Table**

A drop in water table increases the effective unit weight of soil above the new water table, causing consolidation of underlying compressible layers.

$$\Delta\sigma' = \Delta \gamma_{sub} \cdot \Delta h$$

Where:
- $\Delta\sigma'$ = increase in effective stress (kN/m²)
- $\Delta \gamma_{sub}$ = submerged unit weight ≈ **9.81 kN/m³**
- $\Delta h$ = drop in water table (m)

### Cause 6: **Vibrations and Dynamic Loads**

Machinery vibrations, traffic loads, blasting, and seismic events cause **rearrangement of grains** in loose sands (potentially leading to liquefaction) and **fatigue creep** in clays.

### Cause 7: **Adjacent Excavation / Tunnelling**

Excavation removes lateral confinement, causing **heave** in the excavation pit but **settlement** in the surrounding ground due to lateral soil movement and stress relief.

### Cause 8: **Mining Subsidence**

Underground mining creates voids that propagate upward, causing large-scale surface subsidence (often > 1 m in magnitude).

### Cause 9: **Self-Weight Consolidation of Fills**

Recent hydraulic fills, mine tailings, and reclaimed lands undergo self-weight consolidation over decades.

### Cause 10: **Desiccation and Shrinkage**

Seasonal moisture variations cause shrink-swell cycles in **expansive clays (black cotton soils)**, producing cyclic settlement-heave patterns.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_T = S_i + S_c + S_s$ | Total settlement | mm |
| 2 | $S_i = q \cdot B \cdot \dfrac{1-\mu^2}{E_s} \cdot I_w \cdot I_s$ | Immediate settlement (Schleicher-Fox) | mm |
| 3 | $S_i = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_1 \cdot \dfrac{q \cdot B}{E_s}$ | Immediate settlement (simplified) | mm |
| 4 | $S_c = \dfrac{C_c \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log\dfrac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}$ | Consolidation settlement (NC clay) | mm |
| 5 | $S_c = \dfrac{C_r \cdot H}{1+e_0} \cdot \log\dfrac{\sigma'_0 + \Delta\sigma'}{\sigma'_0}$ | Consolidation settlement (OC clay, recompression) | mm |
| 6 | $S_s = C_\alpha \cdot H \cdot \log\dfrac{t_2}{t_1}$ | Secondary settlement | mm |
