# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Differentiate between an isolated spread footing and a raft foundation. List any three situations where a raft foundation is preferred over isolated footings.**

**Model Answer:**

An **isolated spread footing** is a single, separate footing supporting one column, while a **raft foundation** is a single large slab supporting a group of columns or the entire structure.

| Aspect | Isolated Footing | Raft Foundation |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| Supports | Single column | Multiple columns/walls |
| Plan area | Small (≤ 4 m²) | Very large (≥ 50 m²) |
| Stiffness | Low | Very high (rigid mat) |
| Settlement behavior | Individual, may be uneven | Uniform, differential minimized |

**Three situations preferring a raft:**
1. When the **safe bearing capacity of soil is very low** (e.g., < 100 kN/m²) and individual footings would be uneconomically large.
2. When the **sum of individual footing areas exceeds 50%** of the building footprint.
3. When **closely spaced heavily loaded columns** would cause overlapping footings or excessive differential settlement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget the **basement scenario** as a reason for raft selection. If the building has a basement, the raft doubles as the basement floor—always mention this when asked.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**State and explain Skempton's formula for the ultimate bearing capacity of a raft foundation on clay. Define each term used.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
q_u = 5.0 \, c_u \left(1 + 0.2 \frac{B}{L}\right)\left(1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}\right) \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Definitions:**
- $q_u$ = Ultimate bearing capacity of the raft (gross, kN/m²)
- $c_u$ = Undrained cohesion of the saturated clay (kN/m²)
- $B$ = Smaller plan dimension of the raft (m)
- $L$ = Larger plan dimension of the raft (m)
- $D_f$ = Depth of the raft below ground surface (m)

The first factor $(1 + 0.2B/L)$ is the **shape factor** (1.0 for a strip, 1.2 for a square/circle). The second factor $(1 + 0.2D_f/B)$ is the **depth factor** (valid for $D_f/B \le 2.5$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write $N_c = 5.14$ for the base, then apply Skempton's shape and depth factors on top, **double-counting** the corrections. Skempton's formula is a **single integrated equation**—do not multiply by an additional $N_c$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-level internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Each typically has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solve **either** OR. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sand / Teng's Equation**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

A raft foundation of size **18 m × 24 m** is to be constructed at a depth of **3 m** in a sandy deposit. The SPT N-value (uncorrected) at the foundation level is **22**. The unit weight of sand is **18.5 kN/m³**, and the water table is well below the foundation.

**(a)** Compute the **net ultimate bearing capacity** and the **gross ultimate bearing capacity** using **Teng's equation**.

**(b)** Determine the **net allowable bearing pressure** and **total load** the raft can support, taking a **factor of safety of 3**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Given Data:**
- $B = 18$ m (smaller dimension)
- $L = 24$ m
- $D_f = 3$ m
- $N = 22$ (uncorrected — assume already corrected, or apply $C_N \approx 1$)
- $\gamma = 18.5$ kN/m³
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³
- $F.S. = 3$

#### Part (a) — Net & Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute the geometric ratio.

$$
\frac{B}{D_f} = \frac{18}{3} = 6
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the unit-weight ratio.

$$
\frac{\gamma}{\gamma_w} = \frac{18.5}{9.81} = 1.886
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Teng's equation for net ultimate bearing capacity.

$$
q_{nu} = 0.05 \times N \times \left(3 + \frac{B}{D_f}\right) \times \frac{\gamma}{\gamma_w}
$$

$$
q_{nu} = 0.05 \times 22 \times (3 + 6) \times 1.886
$$

$$
q_{nu} = 0.05 \times 22 \times 9 \times 1.886
$$

$$
q_{nu} = 1.1 \times 9 \times 1.886
$$

$$
q_{nu} = 9.9 \times 1.886
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{nu} = 18.67 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2} \quad \text{(Net Ultimate)}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the gross ultimate bearing capacity.

$$
q_{u(gross)} = q_{nu} + \gamma D_f
$$

$$
q_{u(gross)} = 18.67 + 18.5 \times 3
$$

$$
q_{u(gross)} = 18.67 + 55.5
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{u(gross)} = 74.17 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2} \quad \text{(Gross Ultimate)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Net Allowable Bearing Pressure and Total Load [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute net allowable pressure.

$$
q_{a(net)} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F.S.} = \frac{18.67}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{a(net)} = 6.22 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute gross allowable pressure.

$$
q_{a(gross)} = \frac{q_{u(gross)}}{F.S.} = \frac{74.17}{3} = 24.72 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute plan area.

$$
A = B \times L = 18 \times 24 = 432 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute total allowable net load.

$$
Q_{allowable,net} = q_{a(net)} \times A = 6.22 \times 432
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{allowable,net} = 2687.04 \;\; \text{kN}}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute total allowable gross load (including self-weight).

$$
Q_{allowable,gross} = q_{a(gross)} \times A = 24.72 \times 432
$$

$$
Q_{allowable,gross} = 10679.04 \;\; \text{kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *net* with *gross* allowable pressure. Remember: **settlement is governed by net**; **structural design of raft thickness** uses **gross**. Read the question carefully to choose the right one.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Clay / Skempton's Formula**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

A raft foundation of size **10 m × 15 m** is to be placed at a depth of **2.0 m** in a soft saturated clay deposit. The undrained cohesion of the clay is **80 kN/m²**.

**(a)** Determine the **ultimate bearing capacity** of the raft using **Skempton's formula**. Also compute the **allowable bearing capacity** with F.S. = 3.

**(b)** If the depth were increased to **5.0 m** (basement construction), what would be the **percentage increase in ultimate bearing capacity**? Comment on the practical implications.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Given Data:**
- $B = 10$ m
- $L = 15$ m
- $D_f = 2.0$ m
- $c_u = 80$ kN/m²
- $F.S. = 3$

#### Part (a) — Ultimate & Allowable Bearing Capacity at D_f = 2 m [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Compute shape factor.

$$
s_c = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{B}{L} = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{10}{15} = 1 + 0.133 = 1.133
$$

**Step 2:** Compute depth factor.

$$
d_c = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{D_f}{B} = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{2}{10} = 1 + 0.04 = 1.04
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Skempton's formula.

$$
q_u = 5.0 \times c_u \times s_c \times d_c
$$

$$
q_u = 5.0 \times 80 \times 1.133 \times 1.04
$$

$$
q_u = 400 \times 1.133 \times 1.04
$$

$$
q_u = 400 \times 1.178
$$

$$
\boxed{q_u = 471.36 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2} \quad \text{(Ultimate)}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute allowable bearing capacity.

$$
q_a = \frac{q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{471.36}{3} = 157.12 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{q_a = 157.12 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2} \quad \text{(Allowable)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Effect of Increased Depth to 5 m [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Recompute the depth factor at $D_f = 5$ m.

$$
d_c' = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{5}{10} = 1 + 0.10 = 1.10
$$

**Step 2:** Recompute ultimate bearing capacity at $D_f = 5$ m.

$$
q_u' = 5.0 \times 80 \times 1.133 \times 1.10
$$

$$
q_u' = 400 \times 1.133 \times 1.10
$$

$$
q_u' = 400 \times 1.246
$$

$$
\boxed{q_u' = 498.56 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute percentage increase.

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \frac{q_u' - q_u}{q_u} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \frac{498.56 - 471.36}{471.36} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \frac{27.20}{471.36} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ increase} = 5.77\%}
$$

**Practical Implications:**
- For **shallow rafts** ($D_f/B \leq 0.5$), the depth factor gives a modest 4–6% gain.
- For **deep basements** ($D_f/B \geq 1.0$), the increase can be 20% or more, but the excavation cost rises sharply.
- A **cost–benefit analysis** is required beyond $D_f/B = 1.0$.
- Skempton's depth correction is **valid only up to $D_f/B = 2.5$**; for deeper, use a deep-foundation analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **multiply shape and depth factors**, writing them as additive terms like $1 + 0.2B/L + 0.2D_f/B$. This is **wrong**—Skempton's factors are **multiplicative**, not additive.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Deduction Triggers for This Topic (from Past KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting SPT correction** — KTU increasingly tests overburden correction $C_N$. If N is uncorrected, deduct 1 mark in Part A and 2 marks in Part B.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — Mixing $\gamma$ (kN/m³) with $q$ (kN/m²). Always cross-check dimensions.
> 3. **Net vs. Gross mix-up** — Adding $\gamma D_f$ when the question asks for *net* (or vice versa).
> 4. **Wrong F.S.** — Using 4 instead of 3 (or the reverse). KTU default for rafts is **3**.
> 5. **Applying Teng on clay or Skempton on sand** — These are mutually exclusive. Check soil type first.
> 6. **Skipping shape and depth factors in Skempton's** — Both must be applied; partial marking gives only 4/7.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Raft = mat** covering a large plan area, used when individual footings are uneconomical or overlap.
- [x] **Net pressure** $q_{net} = (Q - W_s)/A$ governs **settlement**; **gross** governs **structural design**.
- [x] **Teng's equation** applies to **sand (cohesionless)** and uses **SPT N-value**:
  $$q_{nu} = 0.05 N (3 + B/D_f)(\gamma/\gamma_w)$$
- [x] **Skempton's formula** applies to **clay (saturated, Φ=0)** and uses **undrained cohesion $c_u$**:
  $$q_u = 5.0 c_u (1 + 0.2B/L)(1 + 0.2D_f/B)$$
- [x] **Shape factor** $(1 + 0.2B/L)$: 1.0 for strip, 1.2 for square/circle.
- [x] **Depth factor** $(1 + 0.2D_f/B)$: valid only for $D_f/B \le 2.5$.
- [x] **Default F.S. for rafts** = **3** (lower than 4 for footings, because empirical equations are reliable).
- [x] **Overburden correction $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_v)$** should be applied to N for accuracy.
- [x] **Always check soil type** before selecting the equation—**Teng ≠ Skempton**.
- [x] **Shape and depth factors are multiplicative** in Skempton, not additive.
- [x] **Teng's equation gives net**; **Skempton's gives gross** — convert as needed.
- [x] **Loose sand with N < 10** → Teng may over-predict; apply cautious F.S.
- [x] **Soft clay with $c_u$ < 25 kN/m²** → Skempton's depth factor can mask consolidation settlement — always run a separate **settlement analysis**.
- [x] **Maximum Teng applicability:** $B/D_f \leq 10$ (extrapolation beyond is risky).
- [x] **Water table correction in Teng:** Use **submerged unit weight** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ if water table is at or above foundation level.
- [x] **Conversion unit reminders:** 1 t/m² = 10 kN/m²; 1 kg/cm² = 100 kN/m² (approx).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating Foundations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Floating Foundations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Floating Foundation** (also referred to as a **Compensated Foundation** or **Balanced Foundation**) is a type of deep foundation system in which the weight of the superstructure carried by the foundation is approximately balanced by the weight of the soil excavated from the site, such that the **net increase in vertical effective stress** at foundation level is **zero or negligible**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Terzaghi's Apparent Pressure Concept):** > *"A floating foundation is one in which the weight of the excavated soil equals the weight of the proposed structure (including the foundation), so that the net loading on the subsoil is theoretically zero and no settlement occurs."* In mathematical form, the design condition is: $$q_{gross} \approx \gamma \cdot D_f$$ where: - $q_{gross}$ = Gross bearing pressure transmitted to soil ($\text{kN/m}^2$) - $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil ($\text{kN/m}^3$) - $D_f$ = Depth of foundation below ground level (m) The structure is said to "float" because the removal of soil creates a relief of stress that supports the structural load — analogous to a ship floating in water where displaced water equals the ship's weight. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Floating Ship Principle** > Imagine a massive cargo ship resting in a harbor. The ship does not sink because the weight of water it displaces is exactly equal to its own weight. The ship is *partially submerged* and is in **equilibrium**. > > Similarly, when a building basement is excavated deeply into soft soil, the soil removed *would have* exerted a downward pressure on the deeper strata. If the building's weight is made equal to this removed soil weight, the deeper strata "feel" no change. The building essentially **floats** on a "cushion" of stress relief. **Why is it called "floating"?** Because the structure is not supported by the soil's bearing strength in the traditional sense — instead, the **buoyancy effect of the relieved overburden** carries the load. The deeper you dig, the more soil weight you remove, and the more "carrying capacity" you generate from the stress relief itself. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Range | Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | **Unit weight of soil ($\gamma$)** | **15 – 22 kN/m³** | For clay, sand, or submerged soil ($\gamma'$) | | **Submerged unit weight ($\gamma'$)** | **8 – 12 kN/m³** | Used when water table is high | | **Typical depth ($D_f$)** | **3 m – 25 m** | For basements and compensated rafts | | **Net pressure ($q_{net}$)** | **0 – 50 kN/m²** | Should be small to limit settlement | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often quote submerged unit weight ($\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$) when the water table is above the foundation level. Always check whether the problem specifies water table conditions. ## 1.4 When are Floating Foundations Used? Floating foundations are preferred when: 1. The **soil near the surface is weak** (soft clay, loose sand, organic soil). 2. **Loose deposits extend to a great depth**, making deep piles uneconomical. 3. A **basement is required** for the building (so excavation is anyway needed). 4. The **bearing capacity at shallow depth is insufficient**, but deep excavation naturally creates a compensated condition. 5. The structure carries **heavy column loads** on a relatively small footprint. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Terzaghi's Apparent Pressure Concept The classical theory of floating foundations is based on **Terzaghi's concept of apparent pressure**. The logic is structured below: - **Step 1: Original Stress State (Before Excavation)** The vertical effective stress at depth $D_f$ in the undisturbed ground is: $$\sigma'_0 = \gamma \cdot D_f$$ - **Step 2: After Excavation to Depth $D_f$** The soil at foundation level has its overburden removed. The vertical stress is now theoretically **zero**, and the soil tends to heave (rebound). - **Step 3: After Construction of Structure** The gross bearing pressure $q_{gross}$ is applied. The new effective stress becomes: $$\sigma'_1 = q_{gross}$$ - **Step 4: Net Change in Effective Stress** $$\Delta\sigma' = \sigma'_1 - \sigma'_0 = q_{gross} - \gamma \cdot D_f$$ - **Step 5: Design Criterion for Floating Foundation** If we want the **net change in stress to be zero**: $$q_{gross} = \gamma \cdot D_f$$ Hence, the depth of the foundation is determined as: $$D_f = \frac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$$ ## 2.2 Types of Floating Foundations > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Classification** — There are three main categories: ### Type 1: Fully Compensated Floating Foundation - The net increase in effective stress is **zero**. - $q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f = 0$ - Settlement is theoretically **zero**. - Practically difficult to achieve because of soil rebound and re-compression. ### Type 2: Partially Compensated Floating Foundation - The net pressure is **small but positive** ($q_{net} > 0$). - Settlement occurs but is **within permissible limits**. - Most commonly adopted in practice. ### Type 3: True Floating Foundation - The structure is buoyant due to water pressure (e.g., structures below water table, dry docks, swimming pools). - Subjected to **uplift forces**; must check against flotation failure. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $q_{gross} = \dfrac{Q + W_f}{A}$ | Gross bearing pressure (Q = column load, W_f = foundation weight) | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | | 2 | $q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f$ | Net pressure increase on subsoil | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | | 3 | $D_f = \dfrac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$ | Depth required for full compensation | m | | 4 | $\Delta\sigma' = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f$ | Net effective stress increment | $\text{kN/m}^2$ | | 5 | $W_{excavated} = \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot A$ | Total weight of soil removed | kN | | 6 | $S_e = \sum \dfrac{\Delta\sigma'_i \cdot H_i}{E_{si}}$ (using $\Delta\sigma'$) | Settlement using elastic theory | mm | | 7 | $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$ | Submerged unit weight (when WT is high) | $\text{kN/m}^3$ | | 8 | $FS_{flotation} = \dfrac{W_{structure}}{U_{uplift}}$ | Factor of safety against flotation (must be $\geq 1.2$) | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget that when the **water table** is above foundation level, the **buoyant weight of the submerged soil** should be used. That is, replace $\gamma$ with $\gamma'$ in the formulas. Examiner penalizes 1–2 marks for this. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Floating foundations are extensively used in: - **Tall buildings** in coastal cities (Mumbai, Dubai, Singapore) with deep basements. - **Underground metro stations** and parking structures. - **Pump houses and sump pits** in water treatment plants. - **Swimming pools and dry docks** subjected to uplift. - **Mat/raft foundations** on soft clays (e.g., Mexico City clay, Bangkok clay). The economic advantage: it **eliminates the need for deep piling** in many soft soil conditions, making construction faster and cheaper. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Depth of Foundation for Full Compensation Consider a building of total weight $Q$ (including its foundation) supported on a raft of area $A$ at depth $D_f$ below ground level. **Step 1:** Define the gross bearing pressure acting on the soil at foundation level. $$q_{gross} = \frac{\text{Total Load on Foundation}}{\text{Area of Foundation}} = \frac{Q}{A}$$ **Step 2:** Recognize that before construction, the soil at depth $D_f$ was carrying an overburden pressure. $$q_{overburden} = \gamma \cdot D_f$$ **Step 3:** Compute the net increase in effective stress at the foundation level after construction. $$ \begin{aligned} q_{net} &= q_{gross} - q_{overburden} \\ q_{net} &= \frac{Q}{A} - \gamma \cdot D_f \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the **design condition for a floating foundation** — net increase in stress should be zero (fully compensated case). $$ \begin{aligned} q_{net} &= 0 \\ \frac{Q}{A} - \gamma \cdot D_f &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Rearrange the equation to solve for the required depth $D_f$. $$ \begin{aligned} \gamma \cdot D_f &= \frac{Q}{A} \\ D_f &= \frac{Q}{A \cdot \gamma} = \frac{q_{gross}}{\gamma} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The depth $D_f$ is inversely proportional to the unit weight $\gamma$. For a **light soil** (low $\gamma$), the required depth is **greater** — which is precisely why floating foundations are most useful in soft, light deposits. ## 3.2 Derivation — Weight of Excavated Soil vs. Structure **Step 1:** Calculate the volume of soil excavated for a raft of area $A$ and depth $D_f$. $$V_{exc} = A \cdot D_f$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the total weight of soil removed (which "buoys up" the structure). $$W_{soil} = \gamma \cdot V_{exc} = \gamma \cdot A \cdot D_f$$ **Step 3:** Equate to the total weight of the structure (Q) for full compensation. $$Q = W_{soil} = \gamma \cdot A \cdot D_f$$ **Step 4:** Solve for depth (consistency check — matches Section 3.1). $$D_f = \frac{Q}{\gamma \cdot A}$$ ## 3.3 Settlement Calculation for Partially Compensated Foundation For a partially compensated raft, the settlement is calculated using the **elastic settlement equation** with the **net pressure** (not the gross pressure): $$ S = \mu_s \cdot \mu_g \cdot \frac{q_{net} \cdot B}{E_s} $$ where: - $\mu_s$ = shape factor - $\mu_g$ = depth factor - $B$ = width of foundation - $E_s$ = modulus of elasticity of soil - $q_{net}$ = net bearing pressure $= q_{gross} - \gamma D_f$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A building has a total weight (including foundation) of 60,000 kN. The raft area is 600 m². The soil has a unit weight of 18 kN/m³. Determine: > 1. The depth required for a fully compensated floating foundation. > 2. If the actual excavation depth is 5 m, calculate the net pressure on the soil. > 3. Calculate the settlement (assume $E_s = 12,000$ kN/m², $\mu_s \mu_g = 1.2$). **Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate gross pressure** $$q_{gross} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{60{,}000}{600} = 100 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 2: Depth for full compensation** $$ D_f = \frac{q_{gross}}{\gamma} = \frac{100}{18} = 5.56 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: Net pressure at actual depth 5 m** $$ q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f = 100 - (18 \times 5) = 100 - 90 = 10 \text{ kN/m}^2 $$ **Step 4: Settlement calculation** $$ S = \mu_s \mu_g \cdot \frac{q_{net} \cdot B}{E_s} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{10 \cdot \sqrt{600}}{12{,}000} $$ $$ B = \sqrt{600} \approx 24.49 \text{ m} $$ $$ S = 1.2 \cdot \frac{10 \cdot 24.49}{12{,}000} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{244.9}{12{,}000} = 0.0245 \text{ m} = 24.5 \text{ mm} $$ > [!TIP] > **Practical Check:** 24.5 mm settlement is well within the permissible limit (typically 65 mm for rafts on clay as per IS 1904). Hence, the partially compensated design at 5 m depth is acceptable. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix | Case | $D_f$ (actual) | $q_{gross}$ | $\gamma D_f$ | $q_{net}$ | Settlement | Recommendation | |------|----|------|------|------|------|---------| | **A** (Over-compensated) | 8 m | 100 | 144 | –44 | Heave (rebound) | Not feasible — soil will swell | | **B** (Fully compensated) | 5.56 m | 100 | 100 | 0 | ≈ 0 | Ideal but practically difficult | | **C** (Partially compensated) | 5 m | 100 | 90 | 10 | 24.5 mm | **Acceptable** | | **D** (Conventional) | 2 m | 100 | 36 | 64 | 156.8 mm | Not acceptable for soft soil | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Diagram — Pressure Distribution in a Floating Foundation ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Original Ground Surface"] --> B["Soil Overburden removed
Weight = gamma x Df x A"] B --> C["Foundation Level at Depth Df"] C --> D["Net Stress Change = q_gross - gamma x Df"] D --> E{"q_net = 0?"} E -- "YES" --> F["Fully Compensated
Settlement approximately zero"] E -- "NO q_net small" --> G["Partially Compensated
Settlement within limits"] E -- "NO q_net large" --> H["Conventional Foundation
Excess Settlement Risk"] A --> I["Building Weight Q"] I --> J["Gross Pressure q_gross = Q / A"] J --> D F --> K["Buoyancy from removed soil
balances the structure"] G --> L["Partial Buoyancy
small net stress"] H --> M["No Buoyancy
full stress on weak soil"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Floating Foundation Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ABOVE["Above Ground"] S1["Superstructure Load Q"] end subgraph STRATA["Soil Profile"] L1["Layer 1: Soft Clay"] L2["Layer 2: Medium Clay"] L3["Layer 3: Stiff Clay"] end subgraph FOUND["Foundation System"] F1["Raft Foundation"] F2["Basement Walls"] F3["Basement Slab"] end S1 --> F1 F1 --> F2 F2 --> F3 F1 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 F3 -. "Buoyancy from excavation" .-> L1 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of Floating Foundation with Pressure Bulbs ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["Section View - Floating Foundation"] direction TB GL["GROUND LEVEL"] DP["DEPTH Df - Foundation Level"] BL["BEARING STRATUM (Soft Clay)"] GL --- DP DP --- BL RAFT["RAFT / BASEMENT FLOOR"] SOIL["REPLACED SOIL WEIGHT
equals STRUCTURE WEIGHT"] RAFT --- SOIL end Q1["q_gross acts on soil"] -.-> RAFT Q2["gamma x Df was the original overburden"] -.-> BL Q3["Net increase = q_gross - gamma x Df"] --> RESULT{"q_net = 0?"} RESULT -- "Yes" --> OK["Foundation Floats"] RESULT -- "No" --> ADJ["Adjust depth Df"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the Diagrams:** > - The **left branch** shows the **gross pressure** applied by the building. > - The **middle** represents the **original overburden** that the soil "used to" carry. > - The **right branch** shows the **net stress change** — which is the **design lever** for the engineer. > - A floating foundation shifts the operating point from the right (heavy loading) to the left (balanced loading). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Floating Foundation. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Model Answer:** A floating foundation is a type of foundation in which the weight of the excavated soil from the site is made approximately equal to the weight of the proposed structure, so that the net increase in effective stress at the foundation level is zero or negligible. **Mathematical Condition:** $$q_{gross} = \gamma \cdot D_f$$ The term *floating* is used because the structure is supported not by the bearing strength of the soil at the base, but by the **buoyancy effect** of the stress relief created by excavation — analogous to a ship floating in water. It is also called a **compensated** or **balanced foundation**. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Fully Compensated and Partially Compensated Floating Foundations. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Fully Compensated | Partially Compensated | |--------|-------------------|------------------------| | **Net pressure ($q_{net}$)** | Exactly **zero** | **Small but positive** ($q_{net} > 0$) | | **Theoretical settlement** | **Zero** | **Small**, within permissible limits | | **Practical feasibility** | Difficult to achieve | **Commonly used in practice** | | **Depth of excavation** | $D_f = \dfrac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$ | $D_f < \dfrac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$ | | **Soil rebound risk** | High (rebound must be controlled) | Moderate | | **Application** | Theoretical reference case | **Real building designs** | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain Terzaghi's concept of apparent pressure with reference to floating foundations. Derive the expression for the depth of a fully compensated floating foundation. [**7 Marks**] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(b)** A building has a total load of 120,000 kN (including the foundation) and is to be supported on a raft of dimensions 30 m × 20 m at a depth of 6 m. The soil has $\gamma = 20$ kN/m³ and the water table is at the ground surface. Determine: (i) Whether the foundation is fully, partially, or not compensated. (ii) The net pressure on the soil. (iii) The factor of safety against flotation if the weight of the structure alone is 100,000 kN. [**7 Marks**] #### Model Solution: **Part (a) Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Terzaghi's Concept:** Terzaghi (1943) introduced the concept of *apparent pressure* to explain why structures built on deep basements in soft soil did not fail, despite the very low bearing capacity of the soil. The "apparent" load on the soil is not the gross weight of the structure, but the **net difference** between the structural load and the weight of soil removed during excavation. **Step 2 — Derivation:** The vertical effective stress at depth $D_f$ in natural ground is $\gamma D_f$. After excavation, this stress is removed. The structure imposes $q_{gross} = Q/A$. The net stress increment is: $$q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f$$ For full compensation, $q_{net} = 0$, hence: $$D_f = \frac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$$ **Step 3 — Significance:** This depth is the **critical depth** below which the foundation "floats" — settlement is theoretically zero. --- **Part (b) Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $Q = 120{,}000$ kN - $A = 30 \times 20 = 600$ m² - $D_f = 6$ m - $\gamma = 20$ kN/m³ - Water table at ground surface - Weight of structure alone (excluding buoyancy) = 100,000 kN **Step 1: Calculate gross bearing pressure** $$q_{gross} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{120{,}000}{600} = 200 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 2: Calculate submerged unit weight (water table at surface)** $$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19 \text{ kN/m}^3$$ **Step 3: Calculate the overburden pressure (using $\gamma'$)** $$\gamma' \cdot D_f = 10.19 \times 6 = 61.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 4: Calculate net pressure** $$q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma' D_f = 200 - 61.14 = 138.86 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Classification** Since $q_{net} = 138.86$ kN/m² is **large and positive**, the foundation is **NOT compensated** (i.e., it behaves as a conventional foundation). This indicates the design is inadequate for a floating foundation. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Interpretation (if water table is below):** If the problem intends dry soil conditions (no WT), then $\gamma D_f = 20 \times 6 = 120$ kN/m², and $q_{net} = 80$ kN/m² — still large, so also **not compensated**. **Step 6: Factor of Safety against Flotation** The total uplift force on the foundation equals the weight of water displaced by the basement volume. $$U_{uplift} = \gamma_w \cdot A \cdot D_f = 9.81 \times 600 \times 6 = 35{,}316 \text{ kN}$$ $$FS = \frac{W_{structure}}{U_{uplift}} = \frac{100{,}000}{35{,}316} = 2.83$$ Since FS > 1.2, the structure is **safe against flotation**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the types of floating foundations. Discuss the conditions under which each type is preferred. [**7 Marks**] [KTU University Exam - May 2022] **(b)** A raft foundation 25 m × 20 m is proposed at a depth of 8 m in a soil with $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ and $E_s = 15{,}000$ kN/m². The column loads total 80,000 kN. The weight of the raft and soil above it is 10,000 kN. Calculate: (i) The gross and net bearing pressures. (ii) The expected elastic settlement. (iii) Comment on the type of foundation. Use $\mu_s \mu_g = 1.0$. [**7 Marks**] #### Model Solution: **Part (a) Model Answer:** Three types of floating foundations: 1. **Fully Compensated** — $q_{net} = 0$, theoretical zero settlement, used as a design ideal. 2. **Partially Compensated** — $q_{net}$ small and positive, practical design choice, settlement within limits. 3. **True Floating** — Structure subjected to buoyancy, used for dry docks, swimming pools; must design for uplift. (Refer to Section 2.2 of these notes for detailed types.) --- **Part (b) Model Solution:** **Given:** - $A = 25 \times 20 = 500$ m² - $D_f = 8$ m - $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ - $E_s = 15{,}000$ kN/m² - Column loads = 80,000 kN - Weight of raft + soil = 10,000 kN - $\mu_s \mu_g = 1.0$ **Step 1: Total load on foundation** $$Q_{total} = 80{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 90{,}000 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2: Gross bearing pressure** $$q_{gross} = \frac{90{,}000}{500} = 180 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Net bearing pressure** $$q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f = 180 - (18 \times 8) = 180 - 144 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 4: Equivalent width B** $$B = \sqrt{500} \approx 22.36 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Elastic settlement** $$ S = \mu_s \mu_g \cdot \frac{q_{net} \cdot B}{E_s} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{36 \times 22.36}{15{,}000} = \frac{804.96}{15{,}000} = 0.0537 \text{ m} = 53.7 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 6: Comment** Since $q_{net} = 36$ kN/m² > 0, this is a **partially compensated** floating foundation. The settlement of **53.7 mm** is within the IS 1904 permissible limit (65 mm for rafts on clay). Hence, the design is **acceptable**, but not ideal — full compensation would require: $$D_f^{required} = \frac{q_{gross}}{\gamma} = \frac{180}{18} = 10 \text{ m}$$ So the foundation should ideally be deepened by **2 m** for full compensation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $q_{gross}$ (instead of $q_{net}$) in the settlement formula. This gives **highly inflated settlement** and full mark deduction. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the weight of raft + soil above it (the 10,000 kN in this problem). Always include the *self-weight* of the foundation system. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not checking the water table condition. If the water table is at the foundation level, **$\gamma$ must be replaced by $\gamma'$**. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing "depth of compensation" with "depth of foundation." $D_f$ is the depth of the *raft*, not necessarily the depth of compensation. > - **Mark Loss Zone:** The "Comment on type of foundation" part carries 2 marks — students often skip classification (fully / partially / not compensated). Always explicitly state the type with $q_{net}$ justification. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:** - ✅ A **floating foundation** is also called a **compensated** or **balanced** foundation. - ✅ Design condition: $q_{gross} = \gamma D_f$ (full compensation). - ✅ Required depth: $D_f = \dfrac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$. - ✅ **Net pressure** $q_{net} = q_{gross} - \gamma D_f$ — this is the **only pressure** that causes settlement. - ✅ Settlement formula: $S = \mu_s \mu_g \cdot \dfrac{q_{net} \cdot B}{E_s}$ — **never use $q_{gross}$**. - ✅ **Three types:** Fully compensated, Partially compensated, True floating. - ✅ **Use $\gamma'$** (submerged unit weight) when water table is at or above foundation level. - ✅ **Check flotation:** $FS = \dfrac{W_{structure}}{U_{uplift}} \geq 1.2$. - ✅ Floating foundations are **economical in soft soils** where deep piling would be expensive. - ✅ **Soil rebound** after excavation must be considered — re-compaction may occur over time. - ✅ In KTU numericals, always show: (1) $q_{gross}$ calculation, (2) $q_{net}$ calculation, (3) Settlement using $q_{net}$ (not $q_{gross}$). - ✅ **Settlement in clay** is calculated using consolidation theory, while in **sand** using elastic theory (Bowles' equation as above). - ✅ The **concept of apparent pressure** (Terzaghi, 1943) is the theoretical foundation of floating foundation design. - ✅ **Critical depth** of excavation: $D_{crit} = \dfrac{q_{gross}}{\gamma}$ — below this, the foundation behaves as a "true" floating body. - ✅ **Permissible settlement** for rafts on clay (IS 1904): 40–65 mm depending on structural type. - ✅ **Unit conversion check:** $E_s$ in kN/m², $B$ in meters, gives $S$ in meters — convert to mm at the end.

topics.title: Uses and Classification of Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uses and Classification of Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **pile** is a slender, structural member made of timber, steel, concrete, or composite material, driven, drilled, or screwed into the ground to transfer structural loads through weak or compressible near-surface soil strata to deeper, stronger, and less compressible soil or rock. A **pile foundation** is the deep foundation system formed by a group of such piles, capped by a **pile cap** that distributes superstructure loads among them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Reproduce verbatim):**
> *"A pile is a relatively slender structural element installed into the ground to transmit loads to deeper, more competent strata. Piles are used when shallow foundations cannot provide adequate bearing capacity or where settlements would otherwise be excessive."*

## 1.2 Physical Principle at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Range | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Pile diameter (bored) | **0.3 m – 1.5 m** | IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 1) |
| Pile diameter (driven) | **0.2 m – 0.6 m** | IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 1) |
| Pile length | **5 m – 60 m** | Practical design range |
| Length-to-diameter ratio (L/D) | **10 – 60** | Drives the "long/slender" classification |
| Minimum pile spacing (centre-to-centre) | **2.5 D to 3.0 D** | IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 2) |

Where **D** = pile diameter and **L** = pile length.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bamboo in Soft Mud"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing on a thin layer of soft, wet mud beside a lake. The mud cannot support your weight — you simply sink. If you pick up a **bamboo pole** and push it firmly down through the mud until its tip rests firmly on the hard, rocky layer beneath, then transfer your weight onto the top of the bamboo, the load travels *through the soft mud* (which never feels your weight) and is delivered directly to the rock. The bamboo is acting exactly as a **pile** does for a building.
>
> - **Bamboo pole** → Pile
> - **Soft mud** → Weak, compressible topsoil
> - **Hard rock below** → Competent bearing stratum
> - **Your weight on top** → Superstructure load
> - **Hand on the bamboo** → Pile cap

## 1.4 When Piles Are Used — The Decision Logic

Pile foundations are mandated when any of the following conditions exist at the proposed foundation level:

1. **Inadequate bearing capacity** at shallow depth for the imposed structural load.
2. **Excessive estimated total/differential settlement** if a shallow footing is used.
3. **Presence of expansive or collapsible soils** (e.g., black cotton soil, loess) in the active zone.
4. **Presence of organic / peat / soft clay layers** of significant thickness.
5. **Sub-structure subject to uplift / tension forces** (overturning moments, hydrostatic uplift, buoyancy on basements).
6. **Significant horizontal / lateral loads** (bridges, wind turbines, offshore platforms, retaining structures).
7. **Scour-vulnerable sub-aqueous foundations** (bridge piers in rivers, jetties).
8. **Vibration / shock loading machinery** foundations where isolated stiff supports are needed.
9. **Constrained urban sites** where adjacent structures prevent spread footings.
10. **Offshore / marine structures** where seabed soils are extremely weak.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Classroom Mnemonic):** *"Piles are for PROBLEMS — Poor soil, Resistance to uplift, Offshore loading, Bearing strata deep, Lateral loads, Excessive settlement, Machine vibration, Scour."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Piles — The Four Pillars

KTU syllabus demands mastery of **four standard classification systems** for piles. Examiners expect students to draw and label the classification tree.

### Pillar 1 — Classification Based on **Function / Structural Use**

| Type | Functional Role | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **End-Bearing Piles** | Transfer entire load to a firm stratum at the tip | Bridges on rock-socketed piles |
| **Friction (Cohesion) Piles** | Transfer load entirely via skin friction along shaft | Piles in deep soft clay |
| **Compaction Piles** | Displace and densify loose granular soil (no load transfer design) | Sand densification |
| **Tension / Anchor Piles** | Resist uplift forces | Transmission towers, basement anchors |
| **Sheet Piles** | Retain earth or water (thin section, continuous wall) | Cofferdams, bulkheads |
| **Fender Piles** | Protect berthing structures from vessel impact | Wharves, jetties |
| **Dolphin Piles** | Mooring/breasting of ships (cluster of vertical + batter piles) | Harbour berths |
| **Batter (Raker) Piles** | Inclined to resist large horizontal loads | Bridge abutments, portals |
| **Soldier Piles** | H-piles with lagging for earth retention | Soldier-pile walls |
| **Load-Testing Piles** | Trial piles loaded to failure to confirm design | Site investigation |

### Pillar 2 — Classification Based on **Material of Construction**

| Type | Maximum Length | Key Properties | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timber Piles** | ~ 30 m | Cheap, light, easy to handle | Vulnerable to decay above GWL; limited capacity |
| **Precast Concrete Piles** | ~ 30 m | High capacity, factory quality control | Heavy; hard to splice |
| **Cast-in-situ Concrete Piles** (bored) | Up to 60 m | Any length, no vibration, large diameters | Requires casing/slurry; quality depends on workmanship |
| **Steel Piles** (H-pile, pipe) | Practically unlimited | High capacity, easy to splice, can be driven to rock | Corrosion in marine/aggressive soil |
| **Composite Piles** | Variable | Combine benefits (e.g., concrete + steel) | Joint detailing is critical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favourite:** *"Distinguish between driven and bored cast-in-situ piles."*
> - **Driven CIP** → Driven shell (e.g., Raymond, MacArthur) leaves a thin steel tube in ground; concrete is poured inside.
> - **Bored CIP** → Hole drilled first using auger/bucket; reinforcement cage lowered; concrete tremied.

### Pillar 3 — Classification Based on **Method of Installation**

This is the most heavily tested classification in KTU Module 4.

#### A) Driven (Displacement) Piles
- Pile is forced into the ground by a **drop hammer, hydraulic hammer, vibratory driver, or jacking**.
- Soil is **displaced laterally** (no soil removed).
- Sub-types: *precast concrete*, *timber*, *steel H*, *steel pipe (open or closed-ended)*.
- **Pros:** Soil around pile gets densified → higher capacity; quality of pile material can be inspected before driving.
- **Cons:** Noise and vibration; cannot penetrate hard strata; risk of damage during driving.

#### B) Driven and Cast-in-Place Piles
- A **steel shell** is driven first; soil inside is then **partially or fully extracted**; concrete is placed.
- Examples: *Raymond step-tapered pile*, *MacArthur corrugated pile*, *Franki pile*.
- Combines some advantages of driven and cast-in-situ piles.

#### C) Bored / Drilled (Replacement) Piles
- A **hole is first drilled/excavated**; soil is removed; reinforcement cage lowered; concrete placed by tremie.
- Sub-types: *straight-shaft bored*, *under-reamed (belled)*, *mini-pile (≤ 250 mm)*, *drilled shaft / caisson*.
- **Pros:** No vibration, can go to great depth, large diameters possible, can be socketed into rock.
- **Cons:** Disrupts in-situ stress; needs bentonite slurry or casing to support hole; quality depends heavily on site supervision.

#### D) Screw / Helical Piles
- A steel shaft with **helical plates** is rotated into the ground.
- Used for **tension / compression anchors**, especially in poor access sites and soft soils.

#### E) Jacked Piles
- Pushed into the ground using **hydraulic jacks reacting against the structure** or kentledge.
- **Zero-vibration** installation; used for underpinning existing buildings.

### Pillar 4 — Classification Based on **Load-Transfer Mechanism**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **mechanically most critical** classification — it dictates the analytical equation used for design.

#### A) Point (End) Bearing Piles
The pile tip is **rested on or socketed into** a hard stratum. Load is transferred predominantly through the **tip resistance**, $Q_b$.

$$Q_u = Q_b \quad \text{(with skin friction neglected or treated as small)}$$

#### B) Friction Piles (Cohesion / Floating Piles)
The pile does **not reach a hard stratum**. Load is transferred purely through **skin friction** along the embedded length.

$$Q_u = Q_s$$

#### C) Combined End-Bearing and Friction Piles (Most Common in Practice)
The pile is **embedded in a stiff/dense layer** but not socketed; *both* tip resistance and shaft friction contribute.

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$$

This is the **general equation** every KTU student must memorise.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Legend | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ultimate bearing capacity of single pile | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ | $Q_b$ = end bearing, $Q_s$ = skin friction | kN |
| 2 | Allowable load on single pile | $Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F_S}$ | $F_S$ = factor of safety (typ. **2.5–3.0** for driven, **3.0** for bored) | kN |
| 3 | End bearing in cohesive soil | $Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c^{*}$ | $A_b$ = tip area, $c$ = undrained cohesion, $N_c^{*}$ = bearing capacity factor ≈ **9** | kN |
| 4 | End bearing in cohesionless soil | $Q_b = A_b \cdot \sigma'_v \cdot N_q^{*}$ | $\sigma'_v$ = effective overburden at tip, $N_q^{*}$ = dimensionless factor (Berezantsev/Meyerhof chart) | kN |
| 5 | Skin friction in cohesive (alpha method) | $Q_s = \alpha \bar{c} \cdot A_s$ | $\alpha$ = adhesion factor (0.3–1.0), $A_s = \pi D L$ | kN |
| 6 | Skin friction in cohesionless (beta method) | $Q_s = \beta \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v \cdot A_s$ | $\beta = K_s \tan\delta$, $K_s$ = lateral earth pressure coeff., $\delta$ = pile–soil friction angle | kN |
| 7 | Group capacity (clay — block failure) | $Q_g = 2 D (B + L) c + B \cdot L \cdot c \cdot N_c$ | $B$, $L$ = plan dimensions of pile group | kN |
| 8 | Group efficiency | $\eta_g = \dfrac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_{u,\text{single}}}$ | $n$ = number of piles in group | – |
| 9 | Maximum design settlement (working) | $S_{max}$ ≈ **25 mm** for buildings, **50 mm** for bridges (IS 2911) | Serviceability limit | mm |
| 10 | Critical pile length | $L_c \approx 20 D$ (cohesionless), $L_c \approx 10 D$ (cohesive) | Beyond $L_c$ additional length adds negligible capacity | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Substitution Rule:** In the alpha method for soft clays, $\alpha$ is **not a constant**; it depends on $\bar{c}$ via a chart (e.g., API RP 2A). KTU expects you to **state the chart reference** during design.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters in Practice

- **Bridge & Highway Engineering:** Every major river-crossing bridge in Kerala (e.g., over Periyar, Bharathapuzha) uses **bored cast-in-situ piles** socketed into laterite/rock.
- **Coastal Engineering:** Marine berths (Vizhinjam, Kochi port) use **steel tubular driven piles** for fenders and dolphins.
- **High-Rise Buildings:** Kochi Metro viaducts rest on **rotary-bored, large-diameter (1.0–1.5 m) piles**.
- **Wind / Solar Farms:** Use **helical screw piles** for fast, vibration-free installation in remote sites.
- **Underpinning:** Old structures are stabilised using **jacked micropiles** to avoid disturbing adjacent foundations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Ultimate Pile Capacity — General Form

Every pile design in KTU exams begins from the **fundamental equilibrium equation** for an axially loaded single pile. We now derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Set Up Free Body Diagram

Consider a single vertical pile of length $L$ and diameter $D$ embedded in soil. A compressive load $Q$ is applied at the pile head. The pile is in **static equilibrium**.

### Step 2 — Resolve the Applied Load at the Pile Base

The load reaching the pile base is what remains after **shaft friction** along the entire embedded length has been mobilised. By Newton's third law, the soil exerts an **upward shear stress $\tau_s(z)$** on the cylindrical pile shaft, and an **upward normal stress $\sigma_b$** on the pile tip.

**Vertical equilibrium** of the pile:

$$\sum F_v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \;=\; Q_b + Q_s$$

### Step 3 — Express the Skin Friction Component

The skin friction is the **integral of the unit shaft friction** over the embedded cylindrical surface of area $A_s$:

$$Q_s \;=\; \int_{0}^{L} \tau_s(z) \cdot \pi D \, dz \;=\; \pi D \int_{0}^{L} \tau_s(z) \, dz$$

For a layered profile (n layers), this decomposes into a **sum of layer contributions**:

$$Q_s \;=\; \pi D \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tau_{s,i} \cdot \Delta z_i$$

### Step 4 — Express the End-Bearing Component

The end bearing is the product of the **average unit end resistance** and the **plan area of the pile tip**:

$$Q_b \;=\; A_b \cdot q_b \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot q_b$$

For **cohesive soil** (undrained, $\phi = 0$ analysis), bearing capacity theory gives:

$$q_b \;=\; c \cdot N_c^{*}$$

The factor $N_c^{*}$ is a **modification of the classical Terzaghi $N_c = 5.7$** (strip footing) to account for:
- Pile geometry (deep foundation, $L/D \gg 1$).
- Depth ratio ($L/D$).
- Soil type.

Theoretical and experimental values yield **$N_c^{*} \approx 9$** for typical driven piles in soft-to-medium clay (Skempton, 1951). For bored piles in stiff clay, the value can be **7–9** depending on construction method.

For **cohesionless soil** (drained analysis), the tip resistance is governed by **shear failure of a punching zone beneath the tip**:

$$q_b \;=\; \sigma'_{v}(\text{tip}) \cdot N_q^{*}$$

where $N_q^{*}$ depends on the **soil friction angle $\phi'$** and is read from **Meyerhof, Berezantsev, or IS 2911 charts**. Typical values:

| $\phi'$ (degrees) | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N_q^{*}$ (driven) | 12.4 | 25 | 55 | 130 | 280 |
| $N_q^{*}$ (bored) | 5.0 | 10 | 25 | 60 | 140 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Driven piles mobilise a *much* higher $N_q^{*}$** than bored piles, because driving densifies the soil around and beneath the tip. This is a frequent KTU viva and 14-marker point.

### Step 5 — Combine into the General Single-Pile Ultimate Equation

$$\boxed{\,Q_u \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot q_b \;+\; \pi D \int_{0}^{L} \tau_s(z) \, dz\,}$$

This is the **design equation** that the KTU 2024 syllabus expects every student to write from memory in the first two lines of a bearing-capacity answer.

### Step 6 — Introduce Factor of Safety → Allowable Load

$$Q_a \;=\; \dfrac{Q_u}{F_S}$$

The Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 2911 Part 1 Sec 2) recommends:

| Foundation Type | Minimum $F_S$ |
|---|---|
| Driven pile (compression) | **2.5** |
| Bored pile (compression) | **3.0** |
| Pile in tension / uplift | **3.0** (some codes 4.0) |
| Pile in lateritic / weak rock | **3.0 – 3.5** |

## 3.2 Worked Example — Cohesive Soil (Alpha Method, IS 2911)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 model style):**
> A 15 m long, 0.45 m diameter bored cast-in-situ pile is installed in a soft clay with the following profile:
> - Layer 1: 0 – 5 m, $\bar{c}_1 = 20$ kPa
> - Layer 2: 5 – 12 m, $\bar{c}_2 = 30$ kPa
> - Layer 3: 12 – 15 m, $\bar{c}_3 = 50$ kPa (bearing stratum at tip)
>
> Determine the **ultimate and allowable** pile load using the alpha method with $F_S = 3.0$.

**Step 1 — Identify tip area**
$$A_b = \frac{\pi (0.45)^{2}}{4} = 0.159 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — End bearing at tip (Layer 3, c = 50 kPa, Nc\* = 9)**
$$Q_b = 0.159 \times 50 \times 9 = 71.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Skin friction layer-by-layer (assume α₁ = 0.95, α₂ = 0.80, α₃ = 0.55)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s1} &= \alpha_1 \bar{c}_1 \cdot \pi D L_1 = 0.95 \times 20 \times \pi \times 0.45 \times 5 \\
&= 134.3 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{s2} &= 0.80 \times 30 \times \pi \times 0.45 \times 7 = 237.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

> Note: Layer 3 is a *bearing* stratum; its contribution to skin friction is **neglected** for conservative tip-bearing design (or capped). Take $\alpha_3 \bar{c}_3 = 0$ for $L_3 = 3$ m.

**Step 4 — Total skin friction**
$$Q_s = 134.3 + 237.5 = 371.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Ultimate load**
$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 71.6 + 371.8 = 443.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Allowable load**
$$Q_a = \frac{443.4}{3.0} = 147.8 \text{ kN} \approx 148 \text{ kN}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Scheme Note:** The examiner awards 2 marks for the formula, 3 marks for substituting values, 1 mark for unit, and 1 mark for the boxed final answer. Always **box** the final numerical result.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Driven vs Bored Piles

| Property | Driven (Displacement) Pile | Bored (Replacement) Pile |
|---|---|---|
| Soil removed from ground | **None** (displaced) | **Full column** |
| Ground heave at surface | Yes (potential) | No (slight settlement possible) |
| Vibration / noise | **High** | **Negligible** |
| Suitable in soft clay | Excellent | Good (with casing/slurry) |
| Suitable in hard strata / rock | Difficult (refusal) | **Excellent** (rock socket) |
| End-bearing factor $N_c^{*}$ / $N_q^{*}$ | Higher (denser soil) | Lower (loosened soil) |
| Adhesion factor $\alpha$ | Higher (≈ 1.0 for soft clay) | Lower (0.3–0.6) |
| Pile length | Limited by handling / driving energy | Theoretically unlimited |
| Quality control | High (precast) | Variable (site dependent) |
| Cost | High mobilisation, low unit | Low mobilisation, high unit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PILE CLASSIFICATION] --> B[By FUNCTION]
    A --> C[By MATERIAL]
    A --> D[By INSTALLATION]
    A --> E[By LOAD TRANSFER]

    B --> B1[End Bearing]
    B --> B2[Friction]
    B --> B3[Tension / Anchor]
    B --> B4[Sheet Fender Dolphin]
    B --> B5[Batter]
    B --> B6[Compaction]

    C --> C1[Timber]
    C --> C2[Concrete Precast]
    C --> C3[Concrete Cast In Situ]
    C --> C4[Steel H Pipe]
    C --> C5[Composite]

    D --> D1[Driven Displacement]
    D --> D2[Driven and Cast In Place]
    D --> D3[Bored Drilled Replacement]
    D --> D4[Screw Helical]
    D --> D5[Jacked]

    E --> E1[Point End Bearing]
    E --> E2[Friction Shaft]
    E --> E3[Combined End + Friction]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Load-Transfer Mechanism Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    Load[STRUCTURAL LOAD Q] --> Cap[PILE CAP]
    Cap --> P1[PILE 1]
    Cap --> P2[PILE 2]
    Cap --> P3[PILE 3]

    P1 --> S1[Skin Friction Qs along shaft]
    P1 --> T1[End Bearing Qb at tip]
    S1 --> Soil1[Adjacent Soil]
    T1 --> Rock[Bearing Stratum]

    P2 --> S2[Pure Shaft Friction - Friction Pile]

```

## 4.3 Driven vs Bored — Process Comparison Block

```mermaid

    P --> P2[BORED REPLACEMENT]

    P1 --> P1A[Position pile at ground level]
    P1A --> P1B[Apply hammer blows or vibration]
    P1B --> P1C[Soil displaced laterally]
    P1C --> P1D[Soil around pile densified]
    P1D --> P1E[Pile tip rests on bearing stratum]
    P1E --> P1F[Connect to pile cap]

    P2 --> P2A[Drill hole using auger or bucket]
    P2A --> P2B[Support hole with casing or slurry]
    P2B --> P2C[Lower reinforcement cage]
    P2C --> P2D[Pour concrete by tremie method]
    P2D --> P2E[Withdraw casing gradually]
    P2E --> P2F[Connect to pile cap]
```

> When asked to "sketch" a pile in your exam, always draw **four** essential elements:
> 1. The **pile cap** at top (rectangle).
> 2. The **pile shaft** (rectangle below cap).
> 3. The **soil strata** (horizontal lines with hatching on the left).
> 4. **Force arrows** — downward applied load $Q$ and upward $Q_s$ along shaft and $Q_b$ at tip.
