<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List any four situations where a pile foundation is preferred over a shallow foundation.**

**Model Answer:**

A pile foundation is adopted in preference to a shallow foundation in the following situations:

1. **Weak topsoil / strong deep stratum:** When the upper soil strata are of low bearing capacity and a firm stratum is available only at considerable depth (e.g., soft clay overlying rock).
2. **Excessive settlements:** When estimated total and differential settlements under a shallow footing would exceed IS-permissible limits.
3. **Lateral and uplift forces:** Structures subjected to large horizontal loads (bridges, retaining walls) or uplift (transmission towers, basement buoyancy).
4. **Scour-vulnerable sub-aqueous foundations:** Bridge piers in rivers where scour may expose shallow footings.
5. **Offshore / marine structures:** Seabed soils are very weak and waves impose large lateral loads.
6. **Vibration-sensitive machine foundations:** Where isolation via long flexible piles is required.

> **Mark Split:** 4 situations × 0.5 = 2 marks + 1 mark for coherence. Full sentence needed for each point.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between end-bearing piles and friction piles. (Any 4 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | End-Bearing Pile | Friction Pile |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pile tip rests on a hard stratum | Pile tip is in a soft stratum; not socketed to hard rock |
| 2 | Load transferred predominantly through **tip resistance** $Q_b$ | Load transferred predominantly through **shaft friction** $Q_s$ |
| 3 | Suitable when a hard stratum is available at reasonable depth | Suitable when hard stratum is too deep and stratum is moderately strong |
| 4 | Bearing capacity governed by $N_c^{*}$ or $N_q^{*}$ at the tip | Bearing capacity governed by $\alpha$ and $\bar{c}$ (clay) or $\beta$ (sand) |
| 5 | Settlement is small (rigid support at tip) | Larger settlement, governed by shaft compression and soil–pile interaction |

> **Mark Split:** Each valid difference = 1 mark × 3 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2 marks** for forgetting to state the **assumed factor of safety** before using it.
> - Students lose **1 mark** for using $N_c = 5.7$ (shallow footing value) instead of $N_c^{*} \approx 9$ (deep pile value) — *this is a classic trap*.
> - Forgetting to **convert pile diameter to metres** in unit-area calculations costs 1 mark.
> - **Never** present a final answer without units. **Box** the final value.

---

### **Question B1 — Option (A) (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Re-modelled for 2024 Scheme]

**A pre-cast concrete driven pile of 0.50 m diameter and 18 m length is driven into a uniform medium-stiff clay deposit with $\bar{c} = 60$ kPa and $\alpha = 0.7$. The unit weight of soil is 18 kN/m³. Groundwater table is at the ground surface.**

**(a)** Determine the **ultimate and allowable** load capacity of the pile using the alpha method. (7 marks)
**(b)** Comment on the load-transfer mechanism and identify whether this is essentially a friction or end-bearing pile. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Geometry and area**
- Diameter $D = 0.50$ m, Length $L = 18$ m
- Tip area $A_b = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5)^{2}}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2}$
- Shaft surface area $A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.5 \times 18 = 28.27 \text{ m}^{2}$

**Step 2: End bearing**

For a driven pile in clay, $N_c^{*} = 9$.

$$Q_b = A_b \cdot \bar{c} \cdot N_c^{*} = 0.1963 \times 60 \times 9 = 106.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Skin friction (alpha method)**

$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_s = 0.7 \times 60 \times 28.27 = 1187.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Ultimate load**

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 106.0 + 1187.5 = 1293.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Allowable load (use $F_S = 2.5$ for driven pile)**

$$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F_S} = \frac{1293.5}{2.5} = \boxed{517.4 \text{ kN}}$$

> **Mark Distribution:** Formulae (1) + Area calculations (1) + $Q_b$ (1) + $Q_s$ (1.5) + $Q_u$ (1) + $Q_a$ (1) + Unit & box (0.5) = 7 marks.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute contribution ratio**

$$\frac{Q_b}{Q_u} = \frac{106.0}{1293.5} = 8.2\%, \qquad \frac{Q_s}{Q_u} = \frac{1187.5}{1293.5} = 91.8\%$$

**Step 2: Interpretation**

Since the **shaft friction contributes over 90%** of the ultimate capacity and the pile is **not socketed into rock**, the pile behaves **predominantly as a friction pile** even though it carries some end bearing. Hence, it is classified as a **combined end-bearing-and-friction pile, with friction being the dominant mechanism**.

**Step 3: Comment on load-transfer mechanism**

- Load is applied at the pile head and propagates downward through the shaft as axial compression.
- **Mobilised shaft friction $\tau_s = \alpha \bar{c}$** acts upward along the embedded cylindrical surface, transferring the majority of the load to the surrounding clay.
- **Tip resistance** mobilises only when the relative displacement between the pile tip and the soil beneath is large enough (~ 5–10% of pile diameter, per IS 2911).
- Since the pile tip is in the **same clay layer** (not on rock), $Q_b$ is small.

**Step 4: Additional practical points**

- Driving **densifies the surrounding clay** and induces excess pore pressure; long-term capacity may be **higher** than short-term.
- Group effect in clays can **reduce** total group capacity if spacing < 3D (block failure mode).
- Recommended **minimum centre-to-centre spacing** = 2.5 D = 1.25 m.
- Static pile load test is **mandatory** for confirming $Q_a$ ≥ 2 × design load, per IS 2911.

> **Mark Distribution:** Ratio calculation (1.5) + classification (1.5) + load-transfer description (2) + IS code points (1) + conclusion (1) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B1 — Option (B) (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024 Remodel]

**A group of 9 bored cast-in-situ piles (3 × 3) of 0.60 m diameter and 20 m length is provided to support a column carrying 9 MN axial load. The pile cap is 4.5 m × 4.5 m. The subsoil is uniform medium clay with $\bar{c} = 50$ kPa, $m = 0$ (no depth correction), $N_c^{*} = 9$. Adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.6$.**

**(a)** Compute the **single-pile ultimate capacity** and the **group capacity using the block-failure formula** (per IS 2911). (7 marks)
**(b)** Determine the **group efficiency** and comment on whether the design is safe. Working load per pile = 1000 kN. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Single-pile tip area and shaft area**
- $A_b = \frac{\pi (0.6)^{2}}{4} = 0.2827 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $A_s = \pi \times 0.6 \times 20 = 37.70 \text{ m}^{2}$

**Step 2: Single-pile end bearing**
$$Q_b = A_b \cdot \bar{c} \cdot N_c^{*} = 0.2827 \times 50 \times 9 = 127.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Single-pile skin friction**
$$Q_s = \alpha \bar{c} A_s = 0.6 \times 50 \times 37.70 = 1131.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Single-pile ultimate capacity**
$$Q_{u,\text{single}} = 127.2 + 1131.0 = 1258.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Pile group plan dimensions**

Centre-to-centre spacing is $3D = 1.8$ m. With 3 piles in each direction (pitch + 2 spaces), the **outer-to-outer dimension** of the group:
$$B = L = 1.8 + 1.8 + 0.6 = 4.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Block-failure capacity of the group (clay)**

$$Q_{g,\text{ult}} = 2 D (B + L) \bar{c} + B L \bar{c} N_c^{*}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{g,\text{ult}} &= 2 \times 20 \times (4.2 + 4.2) \times 50 + 4.2 \times 4.2 \times 50 \times 9 \\
&= 2 \times 20 \times 8.4 \times 50 + 17.64 \times 50 \times 9 \\
&= 16{,}800 + 7{,}938 \\
&= 24{,}738 \text{ kN} \approx \boxed{24.74 \text{ MN}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Mark Distribution:** Pile area (1) + $Q_b$ (1) + $Q_s$ (1) + $Q_{u,single}$ (1) + Block formula (1) + Substitution (1) + Answer (1) = 7 marks.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Sum of individual pile capacities**

$$n \cdot Q_{u,\text{single}} = 9 \times 1258.2 = 11{,}323.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Group efficiency**

$$\eta_g = \frac{Q_{g,\text{ult}}}{n \cdot Q_{u,\text{single}}} = \frac{24{,}738}{11{,}323.8} = \boxed{2.18 \;\;(\text{or } 218\%)}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation — Why efficiency > 1?**

This counter-intuitive result is **physically real and well-documented in IS 2911 / Tomlinson**:
- In clays, the **soil enclosed within the pile group is confined** by the perimeter piles.
- The block behaves as a **larger equivalent pier**, mobilising higher average shear resistance along the entire block perimeter.
- Individual pile sum *under-estimates* the true capacity when piles are closely spaced and embedded in stiff clay.

**Step 4: Design check**

- Working load per pile: 1000 kN ⇒ total working load = 9 × 1000 = 9000 kN = **9 MN**.
- Factor of safety on group capacity: $F_S = 24{,}738 / 9000 = 2.75$.
- IS 2911 minimum required $F_S = 2.5$ for bored piles ⇒ **Design is safe**.

**Step 5: Settlement check (mention for full marks)**

- Estimated group settlement must be computed using the **equivalent pier method** or **Poulos elastic solution**.
- For a stiff clay and spacing 3D, settlement ≈ 1.1 × single-pile settlement × group factor. Must be ≤ 25 mm for buildings.

> **Mark Distribution:** $\eta_g$ formula (1) + substitution (1) + result (1) + physical reasoning (2) + $F_S$ check (1.5) + settlement note (0.5) = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Uses and Classification of Piles**

- [x] **Pile** = slender load-transfer element; **Pile cap** = block that ties the heads together.
- [x] **Four classification systems:** Function · Material · Installation · Load-Transfer.
- [x] **End-bearing** = tip on hard stratum; **Friction** = shaft in soft stratum; **Combined** = most common.
- [x] **Driven piles** displace soil (no removal); **Bored piles** remove soil (replacement).
- [x] **General design equation (memorise line 1 of every answer):**
$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} q_b + \pi D \int_0^L \tau_s(z)\,dz$$
- [x] **$N_c^{*} \approx 9$** for piles in clay — **NOT** 5.7 (shallow).
- [x] **$N_q^{*}$** for cohesionless soil → read from Meyerhof/Berezantsev chart.
- [x] **Alpha ($\alpha$) method** for skin friction in clay; **Beta ($\beta$) method** for sand.
- [x] **Factor of safety** = 2.5 (driven, compression); 3.0 (bored or tension).
- [x] **Minimum pile spacing** = 2.5 D to 3.0 D (IS 2911).
- [x] **Group efficiency > 1** is *physically valid* in stiff clays (block failure).
- [x] **Timber piles** decay above groundwater; max length ≈ 30 m.
- [x] **Steel H-piles** are best for dense granular soils; **bored piles** for rock socketing.
- [x] **Critical length $L_c$** ≈ 20 D (sand), 10 D (clay) — beyond this, length has no benefit.
- [x] **Common use-cases:** bridges, marine, high-rises, wind/solar, underpinning, transmission towers, machinery foundations.
- [x] **Always box final answer; always state units; always state $F_S$.**
- [x] **IS 2911 (Parts 1, 2, 3, 4)** is the governing code for pile design in India.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Liner Memory Aid for the Exam Hall:**
> *"Pile = Push it down; Foundation = Carry it up; Classification = Know the four ways; Design = Add the tip, add the shaft, divide by safety."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Type and Length of Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Type and Length of Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pile Selection** is the systematic engineering process of determining the most appropriate pile type (material, function, and installation method) and its required length to safely transfer superstructure loads to competent bearing strata, while satisfying serviceability, constructability, durability, and economic criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> Selection of type and length of piles involves the integrated assessment of subsoil profile, structural load magnitude, environmental exposure, installation constraints, and cost-benefit trade-offs to arrive at a technically optimal and economically viable pile foundation solution.

## 1.2 Three-Dimensional Classification of Piles

Before selection can be made, a KTU student must internalize the **three primary classification axes** of piles:

### (a) Classification Based on Material

| Material Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Timber Piles** | Sal, Teak, Deodar, Babool | Temporary works, light loads |
| **Concrete Piles** | Precast RCC, Prestressed, Cast-in-situ | Most common — heavy civil structures |
| **Steel Piles** | H-piles, Pipe piles, Sheet piles | Very heavy loads, deep penetrations |
| **Composite Piles** | Concrete + Steel, Timber + Concrete | Special applications (marine, splicing) |

### (b) Classification Based on Function

$$Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$$

where $Q_b$ is **end bearing** resistance and $Q_s$ is **skin friction** resistance. This yields the functional types:

- **End-Bearing Piles:** Transfer load to a hard stratum tip.
- **Friction (Floating) Piles:** Transfer load entirely via skin friction.
- **Compaction Piles:** Densify loose granular soils (no structural load).
- **Tension/Uplift Piles:** Resist pull-out forces.
- **Anchor Piles:** Resist horizontal/seismic forces.

### (c) Classification Based on Installation

- **Driven Piles** (displacement-type): Precast, driven with hammer.
- **Bored/Cast-in-Situ Piles** (replacement-type): Drilled, then filled with concrete.
- **Driven Cast-in-Situ Piles:** Driven with a steel casing, concrete filled, casing withdrawn.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Right Tool for the Job"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are a doctor and a patient arrives with an injury. Before prescribing a treatment, you must diagnose the injury type, severity, location, patient age, allergies, and budget. *You would never prescribe a cast for a sprain or a band-aid for a fracture.*
> 
> Similarly, a foundation engineer is a "doctor for structures." Choosing a pile is **never** a one-size-fits-all decision. A 15 m friction pile in soft clay is a "band-aid" for a 50-storey skyscraper. The pile type and length must be *prescribed* based on a thorough "geotechnical diagnosis" of the site.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic is a **favourite of KTU examiners** in Module 4 because it tests the student's ability to **integrate** knowledge from Soil Mechanics, Structural Analysis, and Construction Technology. Expect 14-mark analytical questions where you must justify your selection with soil data and back-calculate pile lengths.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Selection Criteria — The "8-Pillar Decision Framework"

A pile type and length selection is governed by the following **eight pillars** (must appear in your KTU answer):

### Pillar 1 — Subsoil Stratigraphy
- Identify the **bearing stratum** (depth, thickness, SPT N-value).
- Identify the **compressible/soft layers** above and below the pile tip.
- Locate the **water table** and any aggressive chemicals (sulphates, chlorides).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Trigger:** If rock/firm stratum is at shallow depth (≤ 15 m) → **End-bearing pile**. If soft clay extends to great depth → **Friction pile** (longer, larger diameter).

### Pillar 2 — Magnitude and Nature of Load
- **Vertical load (DL + LL):** Determines cross-section and length.
- **Horizontal/Seismic load:** Indicates need for **battered piles** or large diameter vertical piles.
- **Uplift load:** Requires **tension piles** with adequate skin friction.

### Pillar 3 — Pile Length (Depth to Firm Stratum)
The required pile length is calculated as:

$$L = D_b + S_{b}$$

where $D_b$ is the depth from ground level to the pile tip in the bearing stratum, and $S_b$ is the **socket length** (for piles socketed into rock).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Depth Rule:** In sands, end-bearing resistance reaches a maximum at a **critical depth** of about **10 to 20 times the pile diameter** (B). Below this depth, $q_b$ remains constant. For clays, skin friction is fully mobilized within the first **20 pile diameters**.

### Pillar 4 — Installation Constraints
- **Driven piles** require driving through dense layers or boulders — possible but noisy.
- **Bored piles** cannot penetrate dense strata or boulders easily.
- **Site access:** Headroom for piling rig, vibration restrictions near existing buildings.

### Pillar 5 — Groundwater Conditions
- High water table → prefer **bored cast-in-situ** (no dewatering needed).
- Artesian conditions → use **bored piles with tremie concrete**.
- Saline water → use **anti-sulphate resistant cement (SRC)**.

### Pillar 6 — Durability and Service Life
- **Marine/coastal:** Steel piles with epoxy coating; concrete with SRC.
- **Aggressive soils (sulphates > 0.2%):** Use **SRC or pile with sacrificial concrete cover**.
- **Design life:** 50 to 100 years for permanent structures.

### Pillar 7 — Cost-Benefit and Availability
- **Timber piles** — cheapest, but limited length (≤ 15 m) and decay-prone.
- **Precast concrete** — moderate cost, high quality control.
- **Bored cast-in-situ** — cost-effective for > 20 m depths and large diameters.
- **Steel H-piles** — expensive but reusable; high strength-to-weight ratio.

### Pillar 8 — Environmental Impact
- **Noise restrictions** (urban) → choose **bored piles** over driven.
- **Vibration restrictions** (near heritage structures) → **bored piles**.
- **Spoil disposal** (in bored piles) → environmental cost.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula table is high-yield for KTU 14-mark problems. Memorize the boundary conditions!**

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{u} = Q_{b} + Q_{s}$ | Ultimate pile capacity (static) | kN |
| 2 | $Q_{b} = A_b \cdot q_b = A_b \cdot \sigma'_{v,\text{tip}} \cdot N_q^*$ | End bearing (sands, Meyerhof) | kN |
| 3 | $Q_{s} = \sum (C_i \cdot \Delta L_i) \cdot \alpha$ | Skin friction in clay (alpha method) | kN |
| 4 | $Q_{s} = \sum (K \cdot \sigma'_{v} \cdot \tan\delta) \cdot A_{s,i}$ | Skin friction in sand (beta method) | kN |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{Q_{\text{req}} - Q_b}{f_s \cdot \pi \cdot D}$ | Length of **friction pile** in clay | m |
| 6 | $L_{\text{req}} = D_f + S_b$ | Length for end-bearing/socketed pile | m |
| 7 | $D_f^{\text{crit}} = 10D$ to $20D$ | Critical depth in sand | m |
| 8 | $S_b^{\text{min}} = 1.0$ to $1.5 \, D$ | Minimum rock socket length | m |
| 9 | $Q_{a} = \dfrac{Q_u}{F}$ | Allowable load, $F = 2.5$ (static), $3.0$ (dynamic) | kN |
| 10 | $N_q^* = f(\phi)$ | Meyerhof bearing capacity factor | dimensionless |

> **Notation Glossary:** $A_b$ = pile tip area, $q_b$ = unit end bearing, $C_i$ = average undrained shear strength of layer $i$, $\alpha$ = adhesion factor (0.3 to 1.0), $K$ = lateral earth pressure coefficient, $\delta$ = soil-pile interface friction angle, $D$ = pile diameter, $f_s$ = unit skin friction.

## 2.3 Decision Logic for Pile Type

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (Use in KTU answers):**
> - **Soft clay + shallow rock** → **Bored cast-in-situ end-bearing pile**.
> - **Soft clay + deep rock (> 30 m)** → **Driven precast concrete friction pile** (or **bored large-diameter pile**).
> - **Loose sand + water table high** → **Driven precast concrete** or **bored pile with casing**.
> - **Dense sand/gravel** → **Driven H-pile or driven precast**.
> - **Soft soil with boulders** → **Bored pile with chiselling** (NOT driven).
> - **Highly corrosive environment** → **SRC concrete pile or coated steel pile**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pile selection is at the heart of:
- **High-rise building foundations** (Burj Khalifa, Taipei 101 — bored friction piles).
- **Offshore wind turbines** (monopiles — large steel pipes).
- **Bridge piers** (driven precast or bored large-diameter piles).
- **Port and harbour structures** (steel tubular piles for dolphins, jetties).
- **Industrial chimneys and cooling towers** (subject to high lateral wind loads).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Length of a Pure Friction Pile in Cohesive Soil

### Starting Equation (Given)

A friction pile transfers its entire load through skin friction. The ultimate capacity is:

$$Q_u = Q_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot \bar{c}_a$$

where $\bar{c}_a$ is the **average unit adhesion** (skin friction) along the pile shaft. For clay, the alpha ($\alpha$) method gives:

$$\bar{c}_a = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u$$

### Step 1 — Apply Factor of Safety

The allowable (safe) load on the pile is:

$$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u}{F}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange for Pile Length

$$L = \frac{Q_a \cdot F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u}$$

> **Boundary Condition:** This equation is valid **only** when the pile tip rests in soft clay of **negligible end-bearing capacity**. If $q_b$ is non-negligible, the equation becomes:

$$L = \frac{Q_a \cdot F - A_b \cdot q_b}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u}$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (Typical KTU Style):** Design a friction pile to carry a working load of **600 kN** in a soft clay deposit. The pile diameter is **D = 0.5 m**, average undrained cohesion $\bar{c}_u = 30$ kPa, adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.7$, factor of safety $F = 2.5$. End bearing is negligible.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate ultimate skin friction per unit length:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u = 0.7 \times 30 = 21 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2:** Express ultimate load capacity in terms of $L$:

$$Q_u = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f_s = \pi \times 0.5 \times L \times 21 = 32.99 \, L \text{ (kN)}$$

**Step 3:** Set $Q_a = Q_u / F$ and solve for $L$:

$$L = \frac{Q_a \cdot F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot f_s} = \frac{600 \times 2.5}{32.99} = 45.5 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always **show units** in every step. A common mistake is omitting units in the final answer, which costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Length of an End-Bearing Pile Socketed in Rock

### Starting Equation (Given)

For piles socketed into rock, the ultimate capacity is the sum of:

$$Q_u = Q_b^{\text{rock}} + Q_s^{\text{rock socket}} = q_b^{\text{rock}} \cdot A_b + f_{s,\text{rock}} \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot S_b$$

### Step 1 — Identify Rock Properties

For a moderately weathered rock with unconfined compressive strength $q_u$:

$$q_b^{\text{rock}} = 0.5 \cdot q_u \quad \text{(commonly adopted, IS 14593)}$$

$$f_{s,\text{rock}} = 0.05 \cdot q_u \quad \text{(typical empirical value)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$Q_u = 0.5 \cdot q_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + 0.05 \cdot q_u \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot S_b$$

### Step 3 — Factor of Safety and Rearrangement

$$Q_a \cdot F = 0.5 \cdot q_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + 0.05 \cdot q_u \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot S_b$$

$$S_b = \frac{Q_a \cdot F - 0.5 \cdot q_u \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}}{0.05 \cdot q_u \cdot \pi \cdot D}$$

$$L = D_f + S_b$$

where $D_f$ is the depth from ground level to top of rock.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A pile of **D = 1.0 m** is to be socketed into rock with $q_u = 5$ MPa. Working load = **5000 kN**, $F = 3.0$, rock level is at **12 m** below ground level.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute end bearing capacity:

$$Q_b = 0.5 \times 5000 \times \frac{\pi \times 1.0^2}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Compute skin friction per unit length of socket:

$$f_{s,\text{rock}} = 0.05 \times 5000 = 250 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Required socket length:

$$S_b = \frac{5000 \times 3 - 1963.5}{250 \times \pi \times 1.0} = \frac{13036.5}{785.4} = 16.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Minimum socket check (must be $\geq 1.0D$):

$$S_b^{\min} = 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{OK (16.6 m > 1.0 m)}$$

**Step 5:** Total pile length:

$$L = 12 + 16.6 = 28.6 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Judgment Callout:** Very long sockets (> 10 m) are often uneconomical. In such cases, **increase pile diameter (D)** to reduce socket length, since $Q_b \propto D^2$ but $Q_s \propto D$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision-Making Summary

| Soil Profile | Recommended Pile Type | Installation Method | Typical Length Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soft clay over rock at 10 m | End-bearing | Bored cast-in-situ | 12 to 15 m |
| Soft clay over rock at 30 m | End-bearing (large D) | Bored cast-in-situ | 32 to 40 m |
| Soft clay extending > 50 m | Friction | Driven precast or bored | 30 to 50 m |
| Loose sand + GWT high | Friction + end bearing | Driven precast | 15 to 25 m |
| Dense sand/gravel | End-bearing | Driven H-pile or precast | 10 to 20 m |
| Fill over soft clay over rock | End-bearing with casing | Bored with temporary casing | 20 to 30 m |
| Marine / aggressive soil | Composite or SRC concrete | Bored with SRC | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pile Type Selection — Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **integrated decision logic** for selecting pile type and length as a function of the subsoil profile and loading.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Project Requirements] --> B[Conduct Subsoil Investigation]
    B --> C[Identify Bearing Stratum Depth and Type]
    C --> D{Bearing Stratum within 10 to 15 m?}
    D -- YES --> E[End-Bearing Pile Candidate]
    D -- NO --> F{Soft Stratum Extends > 30 m?}
    F -- YES --> G[Friction Pile Candidate]
    F -- NO --> H[Hybrid End-Bearing plus Friction]
    E --> I{High Lateral or Seismic Load?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- YES --> J[Use Battered Piles or Large Diameter Vertical]
    I -- NO --> K[Standard Vertical Pile]
    J --> L{Groundwater Aggressive?}
    K --> L
    L -- YES --> M[Use SRC Concrete or Coated Steel]
    L -- NO --> N{Available Rig and Access?}
    M --> N
    N -- YES --> O[Driven Pile Option]
    N -- NO --> P[Bored Cast-in-Situ Option]
    O --> Q[Calculate Length from Qa formula]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R{Critical Depth Criterion Met?}
    R -- YES --> S[Finalise L and D]
    R -- NO --> T[Increase L and Recheck]
    T --> S
    S --> U[End: Design Verified]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pile Length Calculation Engine

For analytical clarity, the pile length selection process can be viewed as a **functional pipeline**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Soil Profile Data] --> ENG1[Geotechnical Engine]
    IN2[Load Magnitude Qa] --> ENG1
    IN3[Groundwater and Chemistry] --> ENG1
    ENG1 --> OUT1[Pile Type Selected]
    OUT1 --> ENG2[Bearing Capacity Engine]
    ENG2 --> OUT2[Qb and Qs Computed]
    OUT2 --> ENG3[Length Solver Engine]
    ENG3 --> OUT3[Final Pile Length L]
    OUT3 --> VAL[Validation Module]
    VAL -- Pass --> FINAL[Design Output: L and D]
    VAL -- Fail --> ENG3
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Typical Pile Foundation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Superstructure Column Load] --> B[Pile Cap]
    B --> C1[Pile 1: Group Member]
    B --> C2[Pile 2: Group Member]
    B --> C3[Pile 3: Group Member]
    C1 --> D1[End Bearing on Rock]
    C2 --> D2[Skin Friction in Clay]
    C3 --> D3[End Bearing plus Skin Friction]
    D1 --> E[Safe Transfer to Firm Stratum]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and free of reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase text, with no markdown formatting or HTML tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any four factors that govern the selection of pile type for a given site.**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of pile type is governed by the following four key factors:

1. **Subsoil conditions** — bearing stratum depth, soil type, SPT N-value, and groundwater.
2. **Magnitude and type of load** — vertical, lateral, seismic, and uplift.
3. **Site constraints** — headroom, vibration limits, and proximity to existing structures.
4. **Cost and availability** — material cost, equipment availability, and ease of construction.

*Note: Students often write only 3 factors and lose 1 mark. Always write the **maximum** asked, with brief justification.*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the difference between an end-bearing pile and a friction pile? Under what soil condition is a friction pile preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | End-Bearing Pile | Friction Pile |
|---|---|---|
| **Load transfer** | Through pile tip to firm stratum | Through skin friction along shaft |
| **Tip condition** | Rests on rock / very dense sand | Embedded in soft/stiff clay |
| **Length** | Determined by depth to firm stratum | Determined by skin friction required |
| **End-bearing share** | Dominant | Negligible |

A **friction pile** is preferred when the soft compressible soil extends to a great depth (typically > 30 m) and no firm stratum is reachable within practical depths.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **eight criteria** that govern the selection of pile type for a foundation project. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** A **0.6 m diameter** bored cast-in-situ pile is to be installed in a **soft clay deposit of 40 m depth** with average undrained cohesion $\bar{c}_u = 40$ kPa. The adhesion factor is **0.6** and the factor of safety is **2.5**. The pile must carry a **working load of 800 kN**. Neglecting end bearing, calculate the **required length** of the pile. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eight Criteria for Pile Type Selection:**

1. **Subsoil stratigraphy** — depth and quality of bearing stratum, presence of soft layers, and groundwater.
2. **Magnitude of load** — determines cross-section and length; high loads need large bored piles.
3. **Nature of load** — vertical, lateral, seismic, and uplift dictate pile orientation (battered vs vertical).
4. **Installation constraints** — headroom, vibration restrictions, and rig access.
5. **Groundwater** — high water table prefers bored piles; sulphate attack requires SRC.
6. **Durability requirements** — design life, exposure conditions, and corrosion.
7. **Cost and material availability** — timber (cheap, short), precast (moderate), steel (expensive, high capacity).
8. **Environmental impact** — noise, vibration, and spoil disposal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write only "soil condition" and "load." You must list **eight** specific criteria with **at least one sentence** of justification each. Students who list bullet points without explanation lose 2-3 marks.

**(b) Pile Length Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute unit skin friction:**

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u = 0.6 \times 40 = 24 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ultimate skin friction per unit length:**

$$Q_s^{\text{per m}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot f_s = \pi \times 0.6 \times 24 = 45.24 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Set up capacity equation:**

$$Q_u = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f_s = 45.24 \times L \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Apply factor of safety:**

$$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F} = \frac{45.24 \times L}{2.5}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for L with $Q_a = 800$ kN:**

$$800 = \frac{45.24 \times L}{2.5}$$

$$L = \frac{800 \times 2.5}{45.24} = \frac{2000}{45.24} = 44.2 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Required pile length **L ≈ 44.2 m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Unit Consistency:** Do not mix kPa with kN/m² (they are equivalent, but write **kPa** for clarity). Also, ensure diameter is in **metres** for direct SI calculation. Many students substitute $D$ in mm and forget the conversion — this costs 1 full mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the **critical depth concept** for piles in sand. How does it influence the selection of pile length? (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** A **1.0 m diameter** end-bearing pile is to be socketed into rock with unconfined compressive strength $q_u = 4$ MPa. The rock level is **15 m** below the ground surface. The working load is **4500 kN** and the factor of safety is **3.0**. Determine the **total length of the pile** using IS 14593 recommendations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Critical Depth Concept:**

The critical depth in sand is the depth beyond which the **end-bearing resistance no longer increases** with depth, even though effective overburden stress continues to rise. This is due to the arching effect of soil around the pile tip.

- For **loose sand:** $D_{\text{crit}} \approx 10D$ (10 times pile diameter).
- For **dense sand:** $D_{\text{crit}} \approx 20D$ (20 times pile diameter).

**Influence on pile length selection:**

If the firm stratum lies **deeper than the critical depth**, the engineer should not blindly increase pile length to gain more end bearing. Instead, **skin friction** in the overlying sand must be relied upon, and the pile is designed as an end-bearing-plus-friction pile. The pile tip should be embedded **at least 1.0D to 1.5D** into the firm stratum for full mobilization of end bearing.

**(b) Pile Length in Rock:**

**Step 1 — End bearing capacity of pile tip:**

$$Q_b = 0.5 \cdot q_u \cdot A_b = 0.5 \times 4000 \times \frac{\pi \times 1.0^2}{4} = 1570.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Skin friction per unit length of socket:**

$$f_{s,\text{rock}} = 0.05 \cdot q_u = 0.05 \times 4000 = 200 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Total skin friction as a function of socket length $S_b$:**

$$Q_s = f_{s,\text{rock}} \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot S_b = 200 \times \pi \times 1.0 \times S_b = 628.3 \, S_b \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Apply capacity equation with factor of safety:**

$$Q_a \cdot F = Q_b + Q_s$$

$$4500 \times 3 = 1570.8 + 628.3 \cdot S_b$$

$$13500 - 1570.8 = 628.3 \cdot S_b$$

$$S_b = \frac{11929.2}{628.3} = 18.98 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Check minimum socket length criterion:**

$$S_b^{\min} = 1.0D = 1.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{OK (since 18.98 > 1.0 m)}$$

**Step 6 — Total pile length:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = D_f + S_b = 15 + 18.98 = 33.98 \text{ m} \approx 34 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** Total pile length **L ≈ 34 m**, with **19 m** socketed into rock.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Forgetting to check $S_b^{\min}$ is a **common 1-mark loss**. Also, do not forget the factor of safety — examiners often test this by giving $Q_u$ directly and asking for $L$, or vice versa.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Pile selection is governed by 8 criteria:** soil, load magnitude, load type, installation, groundwater, durability, cost, environment.

- **Critical depth in sand:** $10D$ (loose) to $20D$ (dense). Below this, $q_b$ is constant.

- **Critical depth in clay:** Skin friction is fully mobilized within the first $20D$ from the top.

- **Pure friction pile length equation:** $L = \dfrac{Q_a \cdot F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u}$.

- **Rock socket equations:**
  - $q_b^{\text{rock}} = 0.5 \, q_u$
  - $f_{s,\text{rock}} = 0.05 \, q_u$
  - $S_b^{\min} = 1.0D$ to $1.5D$

- **Pile length always = Depth to firm stratum + Socket length (or penetration length).**

- **Always state boundary conditions** in your derivation: state the assumption of end bearing being negligible (for friction piles) or skin friction being negligible (for pure end-bearing).

- **Factor of Safety values:** $F = 2.5$ for static load test-based, $F = 3.0$ for dynamic formula or soil parameter-based.

- **Decision matrix quick recall:**
  - **Driven piles:** Loose/medium sand, no noise restriction, shallow to medium depth.
  - **Bored piles:** Soft clay, deep bearing stratum, urban sites with noise/vibration limits.
  - **H-piles:** Very dense strata, heavy loads, limited space.

- **Common KTU mistake:** Writing "depth of pile" instead of "length of pile" — they are **not the same** for raked (battered) piles.

- **Always convert units to SI** before calculation. KTU examiners frequently provide mixed units to test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Capacity of Single Pile in Clay and Sand [I.S. Static Formulae] - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bearing Capacity of Single Pile in Clay and Sand [I.S. Static Formulae] - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultimate Bearing Capacity of a Single Pile (as per IS 2911 – Part 1 / Sec 2, Static Method)**
> The ultimate load a single pile can sustain is the sum of the ultimate end-bearing resistance developed at the pile tip and the ultimate skin friction (shaft resistance) mobilized along the embedded length of the pile.

The IS Static Formula treats the pile as a deep foundation where two distinct resistance mechanisms act:

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_f = (q_b \cdot A_b) + (f_s \cdot A_s)$$

Where:
- $Q_b$ = Ultimate end-bearing resistance at the pile tip (kN)
- $Q_f$ = Ultimate skin friction along the pile shaft (kN)
- $q_b$ = Unit end-bearing resistance (kN/m²)
- $f_s$ = Unit skin friction (kN/m²)
- $A_b$ = Cross-sectional area of pile base (m²)
- $A_s$ = Surface area of pile shaft (m²)

The **Allowable Load** is obtained by applying a Factor of Safety:

$$Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_u}{F \cdot S}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 2) – 2010 (Reaffirmed)**, the standard Factor of Safety (FoS) is **2.5** for piles resting on rock, **3.0** for piles in clay/sand, and **6.0** for piles subjected to uplift loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw in a Cake"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students**
> Imagine pushing a drinking straw vertically into a layered cake. The resistance you feel comes from **two sources**:
> 1. The **tip of the straw** (point resistance) — pressing against the dense cake layer at the bottom.
> 2. The **sides of the straw** (skin friction) — rubbing against the surrounding cake layers as you push.
> If the cake is **sticky (clay)**, the side resistance (adhesion) is huge, but the tip resistance is moderate.
> If the cake is **sandy & dry**, the side resistance is governed by friction and lateral pressure, but the tip resistance depends on the density of the sand layer it rests on.
> **The pile behaves exactly the same way** — a deep foundation that "rests + grips" simultaneously.

## 1.3 The Two Soil Regimes — Why They Need Different Formulas

| Soil Type | Failure Mode | Dominant Stress Parameter | Governing Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clay (Cohesive, $\phi_u = 0$ condition)** | Undrained | Total Stress | Cohesion ($c_u$), Adhesion factor ($\alpha$) |
| **Sand (Cohesionless, $c' = 0$)** | Drained | Effective Stress | Friction angle ($\phi'$), $K$, $\delta$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pile in Clay — IS Static Formula (Total Stress, $\alpha$-Method)

For undrained loading in cohesive soil, the IS Code recommends the **$\alpha$-method**:

$$Q_u = A_b \cdot N_c \cdot c_{ub} + \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s$$

Where:
- $A_b$ = Cross-sectional area of pile base
- $N_c$ = Bearing capacity factor = **9.0** (Skempton's value, IS 2911 standard)
- $c_{ub}$ = Undrained cohesion at pile tip level
- $\alpha$ = **Adhesion factor** (depends on $\bar{c}_u$)
- $\bar{c}_u$ = Average undrained cohesion along pile shaft
- $A_s$ = Pile shaft surface area = $\pi D L$ (for circular pile) or perimeter × L

### Adhesion Factor (α) — IS 2911 Chart Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For bored piles in stiff clay, IS recommends** $\alpha = 0.4$ to $0.5$ (constant).
> **For driven piles**, $\alpha$ varies with $\bar{c}_u$ and is taken from the IS 2911 chart:
> - $\bar{c}_u \leq 25$ kPa → $\alpha = 1.0$
> - $\bar{c}_u = 50$ kPa → $\alpha = 0.75$
> - $\bar{c}_u = 75$ kPa → $\alpha = 0.55$
> - $\bar{c}_u = 100$ kPa → $\alpha = 0.40$
> - $\bar{c}_u \geq 125$ kPa → $\alpha = 0.30$

A widely used empirical formula (useful in exams):

$$\alpha = 0.5 \left( \frac{c_{ua}}{\bar{c}_u} \right)^{0.45} \quad \text{(when } c_{ua} \leq \bar{c}_u \text{)}$$

A simpler textbook formula often used:

$$\alpha = 1.0 \quad \text{for } \bar{c}_u \leq 25 \text{ kPa}$$

## 2.2 Pile in Sand — IS Static Formula (Effective Stress, β-Method)

For drained loading in cohesionless soil, IS 2911 uses:

$$Q_u = q'_b \cdot A_b + P \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} (K_i \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_{vi} \cdot \tan\delta_i) \cdot \Delta L_i$$

Where:
- $q'_b$ = Unit end-bearing resistance = $\sigma'_{vL} \cdot N_q$
- $\sigma'_{vL}$ = Effective vertical overburden at pile tip
- $N_q$ = Bearing capacity factor (function of $\phi'$, IS 2911 Table)
- $P$ = Perimeter of pile shaft
- $K_i$ = Coefficient of lateral earth pressure (IS 2911: $K = 1.0$ for loose sand, up to $1.5$ for dense sand; for bored piles, $K = 0.5$ to $1.0$)
- $\bar{\sigma}'_{vi}$ = Average effective vertical stress in layer $i$
- $\delta_i$ = Angle of wall friction between pile and soil (typically $\delta = 0.5\phi'$ to $0.75\phi'$)
- $\Delta L_i$ = Thickness of layer $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Insight:** For **driven displacement piles**, IS 2911 recommends $K = 1.0$ (loose sand) to $1.5$ (dense sand). For **bored (replacement) piles**, $K = 0.5$ to $1.0$ since no displacement occurs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ultimate Pile Capacity (General) | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_f$ | kN | Always sum of tip + shaft |
| 2 | Allowable Load | $Q_{all} = Q_u / F.S.$ | kN | $F.S. = 2.5$ to $3.0$ (IS 2911) |
| 3 | Clay – End Bearing | $Q_b = A_b \cdot N_c \cdot c_{ub}$ | kN | $N_c = 9$ |
| 4 | Clay – Skin Friction | $Q_f = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s$ | kN | $\alpha$ from IS chart |
| 5 | Clay – Shaft Area | $A_s = \pi D L$ (circular) | m² | $D$ = diameter |
| 6 | Clay – Base Area | $A_b = \pi D^2 / 4$ (circular) | m² | |
| 7 | Sand – End Bearing | $Q_b = \sigma'_{vL} \cdot N_q \cdot A_b$ | kN | $N_q$ from IS Table (function of $\phi'$) |
| 8 | Sand – Skin Friction | $Q_f = P \cdot K \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v \cdot \tan\delta \cdot L$ | kN | Use average $\bar{\sigma}'_v$ if uniform soil |
| 9 | Effective Stress at Depth $z$ | $\sigma'_v = \sum (\gamma_i - \gamma_w) \cdot h_i$ (below WT) | kN/m² | Subtract $\gamma_w$ only below water table |
| 10 | Average $\bar{\sigma}'_v$ (uniform layer) | $\bar{\sigma}'_v = (\sigma'_{v0} + \sigma'_{vL})/2$ | kN/m² | Use this for one-layer sand |
| 11 | Critical Depth (Sand) | $L_c = 15 D$ (loose) to $20 D$ (dense) | m | $\sigma'_v$ capped at this depth |
| 12 | $\phi'$ vs $N_q$ (IS 2911) | $N_q = 10^{(N_q^{*})}$ approx, or Table 5-3 | – | E.g., $\phi'=30°$ → $N_q = 30$ (IS Table) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge piers & building foundations** over soft clay → use $\alpha$-method to size pile length and diameter.
- **Offshore platforms in sand** → use $\beta$-method (effective stress) since drainage occurs rapidly under wave loads.
- **Combined approach** (clay over sand or vice versa) → IS static formula handles layered profiles by integrating skin friction layer-by-layer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of End-Bearing in Clay ($\phi_u = 0$ Analysis)

Starting from the **general bearing capacity equation** for a strip footing under undrained conditions ($\phi_u = 0$):

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q$$

Under undrained clay, the surcharge term $q \cdot N_q$ is treated as **zero** because the soil at the pile tip displaces symmetrically (no surcharge influence at deep depth), and the $\gamma D_f N_\gamma$ term also vanishes because there is no unit weight contribution. The IS Code simplifies this to:

$$q_b = c_{ub} \cdot N_c \quad \text{with } N_c = 9$$

The total end-bearing force becomes:

$$Q_b = q_b \cdot A_b = 9 \cdot c_{ub} \cdot A_b$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Skin Friction in Clay

The unit skin friction in undrained clay is governed by **adhesion** between pile surface and clay:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u$$

The total shaft resistance integrates this along the pile length:

$$Q_f = P \cdot \int_0^L \alpha(z) \cdot c_u(z) \cdot dz$$

For a **homogeneous** clay profile, $\alpha$ and $c_u$ are constant, so:

$$Q_f = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot P \cdot L = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s$$

## 3.3 Derivation of End-Bearing in Sand

The end-bearing in sand comes from the **effective surcharge** at the pile tip multiplied by a bearing capacity factor:

$$q_b = \sigma'_{vL} \cdot N_q$$

Multiplying by base area:

$$Q_b = \sigma'_{vL} \cdot N_q \cdot A_b$$

The $N_q$ values per IS 2911 for driven piles:

$$N_q \text{ table: } \phi' = 25° \to 12, \quad 28° \to 20, \quad 30° \to 30, \quad 32° \to 45, \quad 35° \to 80, \quad 38° \to 150, \quad 40° \to 300$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **bored piles in sand**, IS 2911 recommends reducing $N_q$ by **one-third** (i.e., $N_q^{bored} = N_q^{driven}/3$) because the surrounding sand is loosened during boring.

## 3.4 Derivation of Skin Friction in Sand

The unit skin friction in sand follows **Coulomb's friction law** under effective stress:

$$f_s = K \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v \cdot \tan\delta$$

This is the **β-method** foundation. Multiplying by perimeter $P$ and integrating over length $L$:

$$Q_f = P \cdot \int_0^L K(z) \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v(z) \cdot \tan\delta(z) \cdot dz$$

For a **uniform sand layer** with constant $K$ and $\delta$, the average effective stress is the mid-depth value:

$$\bar{\sigma}'_v = \frac{\sigma'_{v0} + \sigma'_{vL}}{2}$$

So:

$$Q_f = P \cdot L \cdot K \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v \cdot \tan\delta = P \cdot L \cdot K \cdot \tan\delta \cdot \left( \frac{\sigma'_{v0} + \sigma'_{vL}}{2} \right)$$

## 3.5 Sample Numerical Problem 1 — Pile in Clay (α-Method)

**Problem:** A 30 cm diameter, 12 m long driven pile is embedded in a soft clay layer with the following data:
- Undrained cohesion at pile tip: $c_{ub} = 90$ kPa
- Average undrained cohesion along shaft: $\bar{c}_u = 60$ kPa
- Adhesion factor (from IS chart): $\alpha = 0.50$
- Factor of Safety: $F.S. = 3.0$

**Find the safe load capacity.**

### Step 1 — Geometric Properties

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 12 = 11.31 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — End-Bearing Resistance

$$Q_b = N_c \cdot c_{ub} \cdot A_b = 9 \times 90 \times 0.0707$$

$$Q_b = 810 \times 0.0707 = 57.27 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 3 — Skin Friction Resistance

$$Q_f = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s = 0.50 \times 60 \times 11.31$$

$$Q_f = 0.50 \times 60 \times 11.31 = 339.29 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 4 — Ultimate Load Capacity

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_f = 57.27 + 339.29 = 396.56 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 5 — Safe Load Capacity

$$Q_{safe} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{396.56}{3.0}$$

$$Q_{safe} = 132.19 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.6 Sample Numerical Problem 2 — Pile in Sand (Effective Stress Method)

**Problem:** A 40 cm diameter, 10 m long bored pile is installed in a homogeneous sand deposit. Data:
- Sand unit weight above water table: $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³
- Water table is at the ground surface (most conservative)
- Friction angle: $\phi' = 33°$ → $N_q = 55$ (IS 2911, for driven) → **bored: $N_q = 55/3 \approx 18.3$**
- $K = 0.7$ (bored pile, medium dense)
- $\delta = 0.75 \phi' = 0.75 \times 33° = 24.75°$
- $F.S. = 3.0$

**Find the safe load capacity.**

### Step 1 — Geometric Properties

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.40)^2 = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = \pi D = \pi \times 0.40 = 1.257 \text{ m}$$

$$A_s = P \times L = 1.257 \times 10 = 12.57 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Effective Stress at Pile Tip (Submerged)

$$\gamma' = \gamma - \gamma_w = 17 - 9.81 = 7.19 \text{ kN/m}^3 \text{ (or use 7.2)}$$

$$\sigma'_{vL} = \gamma' \times L = 7.19 \times 10 = 71.9 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3 — End-Bearing Resistance

$$Q_b = \sigma'_{vL} \cdot N_q \cdot A_b = 71.9 \times 18.3 \times 0.1257$$

$$Q_b = 71.9 \times 18.3 \times 0.1257 = 165.4 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 4 — Average Effective Stress (Uniform Sand, WT at GL)

$$\bar{\sigma}'_v = \frac{\sigma'_{v0} + \sigma'_{vL}}{2} = \frac{0 + 71.9}{2} = 35.95 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 5 — Skin Friction Resistance

$$Q_f = P \cdot L \cdot K \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v \cdot \tan\delta$$

$$Q_f = 1.257 \times 10 \times 0.7 \times 35.95 \times \tan(24.75°)$$

$$\tan(24.75°) = 0.461$$

$$Q_f = 1.257 \times 10 \times 0.7 \times 35.95 \times 0.461$$

$$Q_f = 145.9 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 6 — Ultimate Load Capacity

$$Q_u = 165.4 + 145.9 = 311.3 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 7 — Safe Load Capacity

$$Q_{safe} = \frac{311.3}{3.0} = 103.8 \text{ kN}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pile Load Transfer Mechanism (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LoadA["Applied Structural Load Q"] --> SplitNode["Pile Head Load Splitter"]
    SplitNode --> TipQ["Qb = End Bearing at Pile Tip"]
    SplitNode --> SkinQ["Qf = Skin Friction along Shaft"]
    TipQ --> TipSoil["Soil at Pile Tip"]
    TipSoil --> TipClay["Clay: 9 × cub × Ab"]
    TipSoil --> TipSand["Sand: Nq × sigma'vL × Ab"]
    SkinQ --> ShaftSoil["Soil along Pile Shaft"]
    ShaftSoil --> ShaftClay["Clay: alpha × cu-bar × As"]
    ShaftSoil --> ShaftSand["Sand: P × K × sigma'v-bar × tan delta × L"]
    TipClay --> SumQ["Qu = Qb + Qf"]
    TipSand --> SumQ
    ShaftClay --> SumQ
    ShaftSand --> SumQ
    SumQ --> FoSDivide["Qallow = Qu / F.S."]
    FoSDivide --> Output["Safe Load Capacity of Pile"]
```

## 4.2 Clay vs Sand — Decision Logic Flow (IS Static Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Pile in Soil Profile"] --> Q1{"Soil Type at Tip?"}
    Q1 -->|Clay / Cohesive| PathClay["Use alpha-Method"]
    Q1 -->|Sand / Cohesionless| PathSand["Use beta-Method"]
    Q1 -->|Mixed Layered| PathMix["Layer-by-Layer Integration"]
    PathClay --> StepC1["Find cu at tip, cu-bar along shaft"]
    StepC1 --> StepC2["Lookup alpha from IS chart"]
    StepC2 --> StepC3["Qb = 9 × cub × Ab"]
    StepC2 --> StepC4["Qf = alpha × cu-bar × As"]
    StepC3 --> StepC5["Qu = Qb + Qf"]
    StepC4 --> StepC5
    PathSand --> StepS1["Find phi-prime and gamma-prime"]
    StepS1 --> StepS2["Lookup Nq from IS Table"]
    StepS2 --> StepS3["Qb = sigma'vL × Nq × Ab"]
    StepS2 --> StepS4["Qf = P × K × sigma'v-bar × tan delta × L"]
    StepS3 --> StepS5["Qu = Qb + Qf"]
    StepS4 --> StepS5
    PathMix --> Mix1["Integrate Qf layer by layer"]
    Mix1 --> Mix2["Use tip parameters at final layer"]
    Mix2 --> StepC5
    StepC5 --> FoS["Qallow = Qu / F.S. (3.0 typical)"]
    StepS5 --> FoS
    FoS --> Final["Design Safe Load on Pile"]
```

## 4.3 Pile Geometry Schematic (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ground["Ground Level (GL)"] --> PileShaft["Pile Shaft: Length L, Diameter D"]
    PileShaft --> PileTip["Pile Tip: Area Ab = pi D^2 / 4"]
    PileShaft -.Shaft Area As.-> ShaftLabel["As = pi D L"]
    PileTip -.Base Area.-> BaseLabel["Ab = pi D^2 / 4"]
    WaterTable["Water Table (WT)"] -.Submerged.-> PileShaft
    Submerged["sigma'_v = gamma' × z (below WT)"] -.Stress.-> PileShaft
    EndForce["Qb acts upward at tip"] -.Reaction.-> PileTip
    SkinForce["Qf acts upward along shaft"] -.Reaction.-> PileShaft
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State the IS static formula for the ultimate bearing capacity of a single pile in clay. Define each term with its standard value.**

**Model Answer:**

The IS static formula for ultimate bearing capacity of a single pile in clay is:

$$Q_u = N_c \cdot c_{ub} \cdot A_b + \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s$$

Where:
- $N_c$ = Bearing capacity factor = **9.0** (undrained condition, IS 2911)
- $c_{ub}$ = Undrained cohesion at the pile tip
- $A_b$ = Cross-sectional area of pile base = $\pi D^2/4$
- $\alpha$ = Adhesion factor (depends on $\bar{c}_u$ per IS 2911 chart; typically 0.3 to 1.0)
- $\bar{c}_u$ = Average undrained cohesion along pile shaft
- $A_s$ = Surface area of pile shaft = $\pi D L$

The first term is the **end-bearing**, the second is the **skin friction**, and the safe load is $Q_u / F.S.$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List the factors affecting the unit skin friction of a pile in cohesionless soil per IS 2911.**

**Model Answer:**

The unit skin friction in sand per IS 2911 is:

$$f_s = K \cdot \bar{\sigma}'_v \cdot \tan\delta$$

Factors affecting it:
1. **Coefficient of lateral earth pressure ($K$)** — depends on pile type (driven: 1.0–1.5; bored: 0.5–1.0) and relative density.
2. **Average effective vertical stress ($\bar{\sigma}'_v$)** — depends on depth and water table location.
3. **Angle of wall friction ($\delta$)** — depends on pile material roughness; typically $0.5\phi'$ to $0.75\phi'$.
4. **Installation method** — driven vs. bored (bored piles have looser surrounding sand).
5. **Sand density (relative density)** — affects both $K$ and $\delta$.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** A **driven concrete pile** of **30 cm diameter** and **15 m length** is installed in a **soft clay deposit** with the following profile:
- 0 to 5 m: Clay with $c_u = 30$ kPa
- 5 to 10 m: Clay with $c_u = 50$ kPa
- 10 to 15 m: Clay with $c_u = 70$ kPa
- $c_{ub}$ at pile tip = 70 kPa
- Adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.50$ (uniform value given)
- F.S. = 3.0

**Compute the safe load on the pile using the IS static formula.** (7 marks)

**(b)** If the same pile is installed in **homogeneous sand** with $\phi' = 30°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, water table at **5 m depth**, $K = 1.0$, $\delta = 0.75\phi'$, and F.S. = 3.0, **calculate the safe load in sand** and **comment on whether the pile has more capacity in clay or sand.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Geometric Properties:**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 15 = 14.137 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — End-Bearing:**

$$Q_b = 9 \times c_{ub} \times A_b = 9 \times 70 \times 0.0707 = 44.54 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Average Shaft Cohesion (layer-wise weighted):**

$$\bar{c}_u = \frac{30 \times 5 + 50 \times 5 + 70 \times 5}{5+5+5} = \frac{150 + 250 + 350}{15} = 50 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Skin Friction:**

$$Q_f = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s = 0.50 \times 50 \times 14.137 = 353.43 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Ultimate & Safe Load:**

$$Q_u = 44.54 + 353.43 = 397.97 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{safe} = \frac{397.97}{3.0} = 132.66 \text{ kN}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1 — Geometric Properties (same as above):**

$$A_b = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2, \quad P = \pi \times 0.30 = 0.9425 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Stress at Pile Tip (15 m depth, WT at 5 m):**

$$\sigma'_{vL} = (18 \times 5) + (18-9.81) \times 10 = 90 + 81.9 = 171.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — End-Bearing (Driven pile, $N_q$ for $\phi'=30°$ from IS 2911 Table = 30):**

$$Q_b = \sigma'_{vL} \cdot N_q \cdot A_b = 171.9 \times 30 \times 0.0707 = 364.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Average Effective Stress along Shaft (Layer-wise approach for WT at 5 m):**

Above WT (0–5 m): $\sigma'_{v0 \to 5} = 18 \times 5 = 90$ kPa
Below WT (5–15 m): $\sigma'_{v5 \to 15} = 90 + 8.19 \times 10 = 171.9$ kPa

Average for upper 5 m: $\bar{\sigma}'_{v1} = (0+90)/2 = 45$ kPa
Average for lower 10 m: $\bar{\sigma}'_{v2} = (90+171.9)/2 = 130.95$ kPa

**Step 5 — Skin Friction (per layer):**

$$\delta = 0.75 \times 30° = 22.5° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\delta = 0.414$$

$$Q_{f1} = P \times L_1 \times K \times \bar{\sigma}'_{v1} \times \tan\delta = 0.9425 \times 5 \times 1.0 \times 45 \times 0.414 = 87.83 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{f2} = P \times L_2 \times K \times \bar{\sigma}'_{v2} \times \tan\delta = 0.9425 \times 10 \times 1.0 \times 130.95 \times 0.414 = 510.99 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_f = 87.83 + 510.99 = 598.82 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Ultimate & Safe Load:**

$$Q_u = 364.6 + 598.82 = 963.42 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{safe} = \frac{963.42}{3.0} = 321.14 \text{ kN}$$

**Comment:** The pile carries significantly higher safe load in sand (**321 kN**) than in clay (**133 kN**), as both end-bearing and skin friction are larger in dense, frictional soil under effective stress analysis.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A **bored pile** of **45 cm diameter** and **18 m length** is placed in a layered profile:
- 0 to 6 m: Loose sand, $\gamma = 16$ kN/m³, $\phi' = 28°$ (above WT)
- 6 to 18 m: Soft clay, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³, $c_u = 50$ kPa, $c_{ub} = 50$ kPa, $\alpha = 0.55$
- Water table is at **6 m depth**.

**Find the safe load capacity using IS static method. Take F.S. = 3.0.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss **three differences** between **driven and bored piles** in the context of IS 2911 bearing capacity calculations. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Geometric Properties:**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.45)^2 = 0.159 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = \pi \times 0.45 = 1.414 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Stress at Pile Tip (18 m, WT at 6 m):**

$$\sigma'_{vL} = (16 \times 6) + (19-9.81) \times 12 = 96 + 110.28 = 206.28 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — End-Bearing (Bored pile, soft clay tip, $N_c = 9$):**

$$Q_b = 9 \times c_{ub} \times A_b = 9 \times 50 \times 0.159 = 71.55 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Skin Friction in Sand (0 to 6 m, above WT, loose sand):**

For bored pile in sand: $K = 0.5$, $\phi' = 28°$ → IS $N_q$ for $\phi'=28°$ is 20.
$\delta = 0.75\phi' = 21°$, $\tan\delta = 0.384$

$$\bar{\sigma}'_{v,\text{sand}} = (0 + 96)/2 = 48 \text{ kPa}$$

$$Q_{f1} = P \times L_1 \times K \times \bar{\sigma}'_{v,\text{sand}} \times \tan\delta$$

$$Q_{f1} = 1.414 \times 6 \times 0.5 \times 48 \times 0.384 = 78.15 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Skin Friction in Clay (6 to 18 m, $\alpha$-method):**

$$A_{s,\text{clay}} = P \times 12 = 1.414 \times 12 = 16.97 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{f2} = \alpha \cdot c_u \cdot A_{s,\text{clay}} = 0.55 \times 50 \times 16.97 = 466.68 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Ultimate & Safe Load:**

$$Q_u = 71.55 + 78.15 + 466.68 = 616.38 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{safe} = \frac{616.38}{3.0} = 205.46 \text{ kN}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Three Differences Between Driven and Bored Piles

| # | Aspect | Driven Pile | Bored Pile |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lateral Earth Pressure ($K$)** | High (1.0 to 1.5) due to displacement of soil | Low (0.5 to 1.0) since no displacement, surrounding soil is loosened |
| 2 | **End-Bearing Factor ($N_q$)** | Full IS 2911 value (e.g., 30 for $\phi'=30°$) | Reduced to **one-third** of IS value (bored pile loosens sand at tip) |
| 3 | **Adhesion Factor ($\alpha$) in Clay** | Higher (0.4 to 1.0) since driving remolds clay and forms a strong bond | Lower (0.3 to 0.5) since the boring process smooths the pile-soil interface and may leave a slurry film |
| 4 | **Installation Disturbance** | Causes soil displacement, vibration, and densification of loose sand | Causes stress relief and loosening of sand; requires careful concreting |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide $N_q$ by 3 for bored piles in sand** — results in 200% over-estimation of end-bearing.
> 2. **Using $\sigma_v$ instead of $\sigma'_v$ (effective stress) below water table** — this is the most common error; always subtract $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ below the water table.
> 3. **Not averaging $\bar{c}_u$ for layered clay** — students often use $c_{ub}$ (tip value) for the entire shaft, leading to huge errors.
> 4. **Using $N_c = 6$ instead of 9** for end-bearing in clay — IS 2911 mandates $N_c = 9$ for piles.
> 5. **Mixing up $\delta$ and $\phi'$** — the wall friction $\delta$ is a fraction of $\phi'$ (typically $0.5\phi'$ for smooth concrete, $0.75\phi'$ for rough).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)**

- ✅ The **IS static formula** = $Q_u = Q_b + Q_f$ (end-bearing + skin friction) — **always** for any pile.
- ✅ For **clay** (undrained, $\alpha$-method): $Q_b = 9 c_{ub} A_b$; $Q_f = \alpha \bar{c}_u A_s$.
- ✅ For **sand** (drained, β-method): $Q_b = N_q \sigma'_{vL} A_b$; $Q_f = P K \bar{\sigma}'_v \tan\delta \cdot L$.
- ✅ **Bored piles** → reduce $N_q$ to **$1/3$** of IS table value (for sand tip).
- ✅ **$N_c = 9$** for piles in clay (IS 2911) — never 6.
- ✅ Use **effective stress** $\sigma'_v$ everywhere below the water table ($\sigma'_v = \sigma_v - u$).
- ✅ For **layered profiles**, integrate skin friction **layer by layer**; use the tip parameters for the layer at pile bottom.
- ✅ Average stress $\bar{\sigma}'_v = (\sigma'_{v0} + \sigma'_{vL})/2$ for uniform sand.
- ✅ **Wall friction $\delta$** is a fraction of $\phi'$: smooth concrete ≈ $0.5\phi'$, rough concrete ≈ $0.75\phi'$, steel ≈ $0.7\phi'$.
- ✅ **Standard F.S.** per IS 2911 = **2.5** (rock) to **3.0** (soil) to **6.0** (uplift).
- ✅ **Adhesion factor $\alpha$** decreases as $\bar{c}_u$ increases — high $c_u$ → low $\alpha$ (see IS 2911 chart).
- ✅ **$K$ values**: driven pile in loose sand = 1.0, dense sand = 1.5; bored pile = 0.5 to 1.0.
- ✅ **Critical depth** in sand = $15D$ (loose) to $20D$ (dense) — effective stress caps here.
- ✅ Always end with $Q_{safe} = Q_u / F.S.$ — never report $Q_u$ as the design load.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Formulae (Modified Hiley Formulae Only) - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT503 - FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Formulae (Modified Hiley Formulae Only) - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Modified Hiley Formula** is a semi-empirical dynamic pile-driving formula used to estimate the **ultimate static load-bearing capacity (Q_ult)** or **allowable load (Q_allow)** of a driven pile from observations recorded during pile installation. It is a refined adaptation of Hiley's original formula, incorporating an explicit **Factor of Safety (FS)** and refined energy-loss terms associated with the coefficient of restitution between the hammer and the pile cap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Modified Hiley Formula expresses the allowable load on a driven pile as the dynamic driving energy (corrected for impact losses) divided by the total deformation per blow (final set plus half the elastic rebound), divided by an appropriate Factor of Safety.

Mathematically, the Modified Hiley formula is stated as:

$$
Q_{allow} \;=\; \frac{Q_{ult}}{FS} \;=\; \frac{1}{FS} \cdot \frac{W_h \cdot H}{S + \frac{C}{2}} \cdot \frac{W_h + e^{2}\,W_p}{W_h + W_p}
$$

where each symbol carries the standard unit in the **SI system (kN and mm)** or the **CGS system (kgf and cm)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hammer-and-Nail" Intuition

Imagine hammering a long nail into a hard wooden plank. The "load" the nail can carry is determined by:

1. **How heavy the hammer is and how high you lift it** (this gives the input energy = $W_h \cdot H$).
2. **How deep the nail sinks with one final tap** (this is the *set* $S$).
3. **How much the nail and wood compress elastically and spring back** (this is $C/2$, since the elastic rebound helps the hammer bounce back, wasting energy).
4. **How bouncy the collision is** (the **coefficient of restitution** $e$ — a rubber mallet loses more energy than a dead-blow hammer).

If you hit harder (larger $W_h H$), the nail goes deeper — load capacity goes up. If the nail barely moves per blow (small $S$), the capacity is high. If the ground and pile compress a lot (large $C$), energy is wasted and capacity drops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in the Formula:**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² (≈ 9.806 m/s²)**
> - **Factor of Safety, FS = 3** (standard per IS 2911 / KTU reference)
> - **Coefficient of restitution, $e$** — empirical value (0 to 1)

---

## 1.3 Significance in Foundation Engineering

The Modified Hiley formula is classified as a **"Wave-Equation-Inspired" dynamic formula** — simpler than the full Smith/Case wave-equation analysis but more accurate than purely static formulas. It is widely used for:

- **Pile-driving contractor verification** at site
- **Quick preliminary design** of driven concrete, steel, and timber piles
- **Quality control** during pile installation (often called a *blow-count check*)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Whenever a KTU question says *"using Modified Hiley formula, find allowable load"*, the marker expects you to clearly show the efficiency factor $(\eta)$, the energy term $(W_h H)$, the set+elastic compression term $(S + C/2)$, and the chosen FS.

---

## 1.4 Boundary Conditions & Validity Range

| Condition | Acceptable Range (KTU convention) |
|---|---|
| Coefficient of restitution $e$ | 0.0 (no bounce) to 0.8 (steel on steel/concrete) |
| Factor of Safety $FS$ | 2 to 6 (KTU typically adopts $FS = 3$) |
| Final set per blow $S$ | > 0 (commonly reported in mm) |
| Elastic compression $C$ | 1 mm – 50 mm (typical) |
| Hammer efficiency | 70% – 100% (for drop/single-acting hammers) |

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcomes Mapped

| Course Outcome (CO) | Mapped Concept |
|---|---|
| **CO4** — Estimate pile load capacity using dynamic formulae | Directly addressed by this topic |
| **CO5** — Interpret field pile-driving data | Numerical problem-solving supports this CO |
| **CO6** — Apply IS code recommendations | Use of FS = 3 and standardized $e$ values |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why "Modified" Hiley?

Hiley's original 1922 derivation came from energy conservation:

> *Net energy delivered to pile = Energy used to penetrate set + Energy stored as elastic rebound*

The energy lost during the hammer–cap impact is captured through the **coefficient of restitution $e$**, which represents the ratio of relative velocity *after* impact to that *before* impact.

The **Modifications** introduced in the "Modified Hiley" form (per Indian/IS code practice) are:

1. **Explicit division by a Factor of Safety (FS)**: Converts the dynamic ultimate estimate to a working/allowable load.
2. **Inclusion of hammer efficiency $\eta_h$** in some variants (often folded into the energy term $W_h H$).
3. **Reorganized elastic compression term $C$**: Made explicit as the sum of:
   - Temporary elastic compression of the **pile itself** ($C_p$)
   - Temporary elastic compression of the **soil + cap + anvil** ($C_s + C_c$)

Hence:

$$
C \;=\; C_p \;+\; C_s \;+\; C_c
$$

---

## 2.2 Energy Balance Derivation — Hiley's Logic

Consider the pile-driving system at the moment of impact:

1. **Input energy** (from the falling hammer):
   $$E_{in} = W_h \cdot H$$

2. **Energy lost in impact** (due to inelastic collision between hammer weight $W_h$ and pile weight $W_p$):
   $$E_{lost} = W_h \cdot H \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{W_h + e^{2} W_p}{W_h + W_p} \right]$$

3. **Net energy delivered to the pile top**:
   $$E_{net} = W_h \cdot H \cdot \frac{W_h + e^{2} W_p}{W_h + W_p}$$

4. **Energy expended in driving the pile** (set $S$) **and stored in elastic rebound** $(C/2)$:
   $$E_{driving} = Q_{ult} \cdot \left( S + \frac{C}{2} \right)$$

Setting $E_{net} = E_{driving}$ and solving for $Q_{ult}$:

$$
Q_{ult} \;=\; \frac{W_h \cdot H}{S + \frac{C}{2}} \cdot \frac{W_h + e^{2} W_p}{W_h + W_p}
$$

Finally, applying Factor of Safety:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{allow} \;=\; \frac{1}{FS} \cdot \frac{W_h \cdot H}{S + \frac{C}{2}} \cdot \frac{W_h + e^{2} W_p}{W_h + W_p}\,}
$$

---

## 2.3 Efficiency Factor

The ratio:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{W_h + e^{2} W_p}{W_h + W_p}
$$

is called the **Hiley Efficiency Factor** (or **Hammer Efficiency**). It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Check:** If the problem says "drop hammer" → $e$ is small (≈ 0.4 – 0.5). If "double-acting / steam hammer" → $e$ is larger (≈ 0.5 – 0.8). The efficiency factor $\eta$ < 1 always.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Use in Exams)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Range / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_{ult}$ | Ultimate pile capacity (dynamic estimate) | kN | — |
| $Q_{allow}$ | Allowable load on pile | kN | $Q_{ult}/FS$ |
| $W_h$ | Weight of hammer (ram) | kN | 10 – 100 kN |
| $W_p$ | Weight of pile (+ cap, anvil) | kN | 5 – 200 kN |
| $H$ | Height of fall of hammer | m | 0.5 – 3 m |
| $S$ | Final set per blow (penetration) | mm | 2 – 25 mm |
| $C$ | Total temporary elastic compression | mm | 5 – 50 mm |
| $C_p$ | Elastic compression of pile | mm | — |
| $C_s$ | Elastic compression of soil | mm | — |
| $C_c$ | Elastic compression of cap + anvil | mm | — |
| $e$ | Coefficient of restitution | — | 0.0 to 0.8 |
| $\eta$ | Hiley efficiency factor | — | 0.4 to 1.0 |
| $FS$ | Factor of Safety | — | **3** (IS 2911 / KTU standard) |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | m/s² | **9.81** |

### Master Formula Card

$$
Q_{allow} \;=\; \frac{1}{FS} \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{W_h \cdot H}{S + \frac{C}{2}}
$$

where $\eta = \dfrac{W_h + e^{2} W_p}{W_h + W_p}$.

### Secondary Useful Relation — Set Determination

If $Q_{allow}$ is given and we need to find the **final set $S$**:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot W_h \cdot H}{FS \cdot Q_{allow}} \;-\; \frac{C}{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do **not** substitute $W_h$ and $W_p$ in different units. Either both in **kN** or both in **kgf** — *be consistent*. Also, the set $S$ should be expressed in the **same unit (mm or cm)** as $C$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Modified Hiley? |
|---|---|
| **Bored-cast-in-situ verification piles** | Quick estimate from driving record of test piles |
| **Precast concrete driven piles** | Site-ready formula, no extra instrumentation |
| **Steel H-piles and timber piles** | Widely applicable across materials |
| **Contractor payment schedules** | Number of blows × depth determines billable installation |
| **Refusal criteria** | Driving stops when $S$ becomes very small (capacity exceeds demand) |

---

## 2.6 Choice of $e$ (Coefficient of Restitution) — IS Code Guidance

| Pile Type / Cap Condition | Recommended $e$ |
|---|---|
| Concrete pile + timber dolly + cast iron cap | 0.40 – 0.50 |
| Concrete pile + timber dolly + steel cap | 0.50 – 0.60 |
| Steel pile + steel cap (direct) | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| Timber pile + steel cap | 0.45 – 0.55 |
